sections.text:
---
title: Campaign Foundation — DM working framing
status: draft-1
phase: campaign-planning
---

# Foundation

This is mine. Player-facing version lives at `context.md`.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
**Who decides what the system remembers?**

That is what the play is going to *answer* — not me. I am not pre-deciding the
shape of the answer. I am building the situation that asks it.

The variations I'll run through scenes — the Question is the same question,
asked at different scales:

- Who carries the witness across a hab boundary that doesn't trust the other side?
- Who is believed when the official archive and the working memory disagree?
- What does it cost a courier to keep a name out of a ledger? To put one in?
- When the Conclave seals an entry, what survives in the gossip?
- When a Bloom Zone takes a hab, who tells the next hab over what happened, and which version travels?

The Question is asked through *movement*. The system has reconnected. Habs
talk to habs. Surface talks to ring. But sixty years of Famine put every
community on its own version of the last six decades, and the reconnection
hasn't reconciled the versions. It has only put them in the same room. The
party are not negotiators of those versions. They are *carriers* of those
versions — that is their entire job. The Question rides their kite-rack.
sections.title: The Question

sections.text:
**Witnesses.**

One word. The world is short of *trusted carriers of what happened*. The
Conclave hoards proof and won't release it. The Synod won't talk in plain
language. The Accord prefers procedural silence to public reckoning. Vantara
will carry your cargo and refuse to carry your story. Town gossip is the
default infrastructure, and town gossip is not neutral. Every faction has a
gap between what they *know* and what they *say*, and the gap is widening as
the Bitter Reach recedes into history and gets re-told by people who weren't
there.

Witnesses, in this campaign, are the load-bearing scarcity:

- They make Bloom containment possible (the Council can only damp what someone
  saw expanding).
- They make Accord arbitration meaningful (an Accord ruling against a hab that
  nobody will name as offender is procedural noise).
- They make the Conclave's gatekeeping *visible* (an archive nobody can attest
  to is just a building).
- They make ordinary trust between settlements possible.

The party will not solve this. They are going to *participate* in it. They
will be asked to witness. They will be asked to *unwitness*. They will be
paid to forget. They will be threatened for remembering. The Question will
get answered, scene by scene, by what they choose to carry.
sections.title: The Scarcity

sections.text:
I drew the Question from the world bible's two clearest DM-facing themes:
*builders gone* (inheritance vs. reinvention) and *who remembers* (the
razor's edge of disclosure). The world is built around things that were lost
and partially recovered: elven resonance theory, the pre-Glassfall
communications grid, the original purpose of half the surviving habs. The
Conclave gates the official record. The Synod won't even say what it's
talking to. The Echo River carries voices and the technology to read them
exists but is controlled.

So the *witnesses* scarcity is not "you can't know things." It is "you cannot
know them through trustworthy channels." That distinction matters. People
*do* know things. The honest gossip in a Glasswake pub is more reliable than
the Conclave's sealed brief on the same incident, and everyone in Glasswake
knows it. The party's organization — the Ledger Run — is a structural
response to exactly that gap.
sections.title: Connections — why this Question, in this world

sections.text:
The party belongs to **the Ledger Run**: a chartered kite-courier crew that
runs a recurring route through several reconnected settlements, carrying
mail, cargo, sometimes passengers, and — quietly, controversially, sometimes
profitably, sometimes lethally — *news*. Their reputation is built on getting
a letter through, and on what a letter is allowed to mean.

Why this works for the Question:

1. **Movement.** The party doesn't sit in one faction's politics. They cross
   hab boundaries, Bloom cordons, Conclave reading-rooms, Synod
   waiting-corridors. Every scene is somebody else's territory.
2. **Inherent ethical pressure.** A courier crew gets *asked* to carry things
   their charter wouldn't approve of. They are asked to *not* carry things.
   They have a public reputation that costs money to keep clean. The
   Question runs through their job description.
3. **Avoids salvage gravity.** Glass Frontier campaigns drift toward
   salvage-crew-in-the-Shear by default. That story is in the world, and the
   world supports it well — but it's the local maximum. A courier crew is
   adjacent, uses some of the same tech (kites, kite-tags, route licensure),
   and pulls toward dialogue, witness, and decision-under-pressure instead
   of looter-vs.-monster. We can still go to the Shear. We just don't *live*
   there.
4. **Fits a small party.** A crew that can fit on one kite. A leader who is
   not a PC. A fixer. A few standing contracts. Room for two to four PCs to
   invent themselves into it.
sections.title: The party's home — why a courier crew

sections.text:
I am Mara. I am running this as: scenes that end before they should, NPCs
with a flaw visible in the first round, the rules in the open, the dice
where everyone can see them. The world is not the antagonist. Sentient
beings can be, but the *load-bearing* opposition is institutional silence —
the things people will not say. Combat happens; it is not the texture.

What I will NOT prep:

- The "real story" of the campaign's central incident. The campaign does not
  have a central incident yet. The opening arc has a pressure (see below);
  what that pressure means belongs to play.
- A reveal arc about the elves, the Adversary, the Communication Shard.
  Those are world-bible DM truths. They are not what this campaign is *for*.
  If the party stumbles into one, fine. I will not steer them toward it.
- NPC speeches. I do not do voices. Affect through word choice, silence,
  the mug on the table.
- A planned climax. The Question gets answered. I do not know what the
  answer is.
sections.title: Authorial stance

sections.text:
- **Lore pull:** read `dm/themes/builders-gone.md` and
  `dm/themes/who-remembers.md` — themes I was not going to use directly.
  Their friction (inheritance vs. reinvention; disclosure vs. silence) gave
  me the Scarcity language and the Conclave/Synod/Accord tension. The
  *witnesses* word is what came out of holding both themes at once.
- **Web pull:** searched recent reporting on rural U.S. news deserts.
  Specific texture imported: *"when official channels fail, what fills the
  void is whoever's already there — and they're not neutral."* That fact —
  not the topic — became the Ledger Run's reason to exist and the
  Scarcity's emotional engine. 213 news-desert counties; >2 papers closing
  per week; gossip and social feeds filling the gap. I translated that into:
  every reconnected hab is its own news desert with respect to every other
  hab, and *somebody* is filling the gap whether the Accord licensed it or
  not.
sections.title: Pulls used for this output

sections.text:
- Two to four factions in `dm/notes/factions/`. The Conclave, the Accord,
  the Synod, and Vantara are all in play; the campaign needs concrete
  current-goal-and-clock per faction, not world-bible summaries. Lean
  toward 3.
- Three to six NPCs in `dm/notes/npcs/`, at least one antagonist with a
  clock. A Conclave archivist who is the party's reluctant client. A
  Vantara route officer who undercuts them. An Accord mediator who needs
  them and won't acknowledge it. An antagonist on the gossip-as-weapon
  side.
- Two to four creatures in `dm/notes/creatures/`. Bloom-touched, Shear-
  fauna, route-hazard. Specifically named.
- Three to five named things in `dm/notes/artifacts/` and `dm/notes/ships/`.
  The Ledger Run's kite needs a name. A relic the party is asked to carry.
  A sealed Conclave shard.
- Three to five locations in `dm/notes/locales/`. The Ledger Run's home
  port. A waystation on the route. A hab the route reaches that nobody
  else does.
- Ten short atomic secrets in `dm/notes/secrets.md`.
- Three to five hooks in `dm/notes/hooks/`.
- One or two short philosophy entries in `dm/notes/philosophy/`.
- One opening arc with `glass arc create`. The pressure I'm carrying into
  it: *something the party is being asked to carry is not what it has been
  declared to be.* That is the pressure, not the plot.
sections.title: Pending — for next planning turn

sections.text:
---
title: Campaign Context
audience: players
status: campaign-planning
---

# Where We Are

It is 2438 CE. The system is reconnected — habs and the surface and the
outer worlds talk again — but the sixty years of the Signal Famine left
every community with its own version of those decades, and the
reconnection hasn't reconciled the versions. The Tempered Accord holds.
The Bloom Zones haven't shrunk. Everyone has a story about the Bitter
Reach, and no two of them match.

You belong to **the Ledger Run** — a chartered kite-courier crew working
a recurring route between several reconnected settlements. You carry
mail. You carry small cargo. You carry passengers when there's room.
And sometimes — sometimes — you carry news, which is the part of the job
nobody licensed and everybody needs.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
**Who decides what the system remembers?**

This campaign is asked to answer it. Not me, and not the world-bible —
the table, scene by scene, by what you choose to carry.
sections.title: The Question

sections.text:
**Witnesses.** Trusted carriers of what happened. The Conclave hoards
proof. The Synod won't talk in plain language. The Accord prefers
procedural silence to public reckoning. Vantara will carry your cargo
and refuse to carry your story. The honest gossip in a Glasswake pub is
more reliable than the official briefing, and everyone knows it.

You are not the answer to that shortage. You are people who keep
running into it.
sections.title: What This World Is Short Of

sections.text:
Hopecore, played straight. The world is rebuilding without blueprints.
The unexpected is normal. Strange things are written into the building
codes. The environment is the louder antagonist; sentient opposition is
real but rarely the whole shape of a scene.

The rules stay open. The dice stay where you can see them. Scenes end
before they should. What do you do?
sections.title: Tone

sections.text:
---
title: The Ledger Run — DM-side
type: organization
status: draft-1
visibility: dm
tags: [organization, courier, kites, accord-chartered]
---

# The Ledger Run

Formal name on the Accord paperwork: **Long-Carry Circuit, Article 14
courier crew.** Nobody calls it that. *Ledger Run* is the working name
the crew picked up in the Conclave reading-rooms two decades ago, and
it stuck because the Conclave hated it. They still hate it.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
A small kite-courier crew flying a single primary hauler on a recurring
multi-stop route between the surface and the inner ring habs. They are
chartered under the Tempered Accord's Article 14 (independent civil
carriers), which gives them tariff exemption on official mail and a
legal duty of non-discrimination — they cannot refuse a package on
political grounds alone. Their charter is *real*, not aspirational.
Every six months they file a manifest log and a route report with the
Accord clerks. Every six months the clerks file the reports without
reading them.

**Founded** in 2419, twenty years after the Bitter Reach, by **Mereth
Kel** — a former Vantara route officer who walked off the job during
the Sable Crescent embargo and refused to come back. The founding
moment was specific: a sealed Conclave letter from a Bloom survivor in
Glasswake, addressed to a daughter on Hab Meridian, that Vantara
declined to carry because of an unresolved diplomatic block. Mereth
flew it herself. She lost her job. She kept the route.

The org is named for what the Conclave does — keep ledgers — and for
what the crew does, which is keep *their own*. Every package gets
entered. Every payment gets entered. Every refusal-to-carry gets
entered, with reasons. The ledger is public to clients on request.
This is the thing Mereth built that nobody else does.
sections.title: Identity

sections.text:
- **Legally:** Accord-chartered, surface-incorporated in Glasswake.
  Files with the Shear Compact for kite-tags. Pays Vantara waystation
  fees like everyone else.
- **Politically:** trusted by nobody, used by everybody. The Conclave
  resents the name and uses the route. The Accord ignores them and
  needs them. Vantara views them as a margin-eater and occasionally
  sub-contracts them for routes Vantara can't justify staffing. The
  Synod will not engage but accepts deliveries at Threshold Station
  through the standing courtyard drop. Coremark remnants try to bribe
  them periodically.
- **Geographically:** Home port is **Glasswake**, surface side, at a
  reclaimed dock in the old hab fragment. The standing route, in
  order: Glasswake → Span Nine relay → Hab Meridian → Sable Crescent
  (Thornvault and Ledgerfall) → home. Roughly a fortnight's loop.
sections.title: Where it sits in the world

sections.text:
**They publish a refusal log.** The Ledger Run's standing charter
includes a clause Mereth wrote herself: every package the crew refuses
to carry is recorded — date, sender, recipient, weight, reason — and
the refusal log is public on request at the Glasswake office. Every
quarter, an updated copy goes to the Accord clerks (who file it) and
to the Conclave (who refuses to acknowledge receipt). This is the only
publicly-readable refusal log of any chartered carrier in the system.
Vantara's PR team has been told to never speak about it.

The refusal log is the engine. It is why the crew is trusted. It is
why the crew is hated. It is why the players' decisions about what to
carry matter — every refusal goes in, with reasons, and the reasons
are read.
sections.title: The detail that makes it *this* org rather than a generic version

sections.text:
**Near-term (this season):**

- Keep the route running. Two of the standing contracts (Conclave
  research-mail and a Glasswake-Hab Meridian municipal contract) come
  up for renewal this quarter.
- Replace the secondary kite (the *Halfsign*), which took Shear damage
  on a deep-route detour last quarter and is grounded.
- Survive a Vantara pricing move that's currently being negotiated at
  Accord level — Vantara wants Article 14 carriers to lose tariff
  exemption on bulk mail. This would gut the Ledger Run's margins.

**Long-term:** The Ledger Run is *for* the route — for keeping it
running, expanding it when habs reconnect, and keeping the refusal log
intact when factions push back on it. Mereth has no plan to grow into
a corporate competitor to Vantara; she's not interested. What she
wants, if pressed: a route that reaches a hab that doesn't trust
anyone yet, with a kite the hab will let dock. *Witness work,* she'd
call it. She hasn't said this out loud to the crew.
sections.title: Goals

sections.text:
- **Charter limits.** Article 14 carriers cannot carry weapons-grade
  cargo, contraband, or sealed personnel transports. They *can* carry
  unsealed passengers and resonance-classified materials under
  manifest. The line is real and the crew knows it.
- **Can't cross:** Vantara as an institution, the Conclave as an
  institution, the Accord clerks as a class. Individual employees of
  each, yes; the institution itself would break the Ledger Run.
- **Resources at start:**
  - The **Splitfork**, a mid-class kite-hauler, four-crew, cargo bay,
    Shear-rated but route-preferring. Named for a documented run
    Mereth made in 2421 along a split Span-Nine reflection corridor.
  - The **Halfsign**, secondary kite, currently grounded for repairs.
  - The **Glasswake office**, a reclaimed warehouse on the dock
    fragment, with the refusal log archive, a small bunkroom, and a
    Tuner-rated cargo cradle.
  - **Three standing contracts**, two up for renewal.
  - **A list of names** that pay extra for direct hand-to-hand
    delivery and don't appear in the manifest log under their real
    names. Mereth keeps this list in her head.
- **Obligations:** The Accord charter requires quarterly filings and
  route compliance. The Conclave research contract requires Tuner-
  certified handling. The Glasswake municipal contract requires a
  certain on-time rate.
sections.title: Constraints

sections.text:
- **Mereth Kel** *(Hab-Worlder human, late 40s, Glasswake)* — Captain.
  Founder. The leader. Not a PC. Visibly tired. Carries a hand-bound
  copy of the refusal log everywhere — she has not yet been asked
  whether she sleeps with it, but she does. She does not romanticize
  the job. She will fly into Shear weather she shouldn't if a letter
  matters. Full entry pending in `dm/notes/npcs/`.
- **Ev Sken** *(Hab-Worlder fae, indeterminate age, route-resident)* —
  Fixer. Books the waystation slots, negotiates with the Compact
  clerks, knows every dock master between Glasswake and Sable. They
  are intermittently present — sometimes diffuse, sometimes coherent
  — and the dock masters have learned that "Ev will get back to you"
  means an answer in three to forty hours. Reliable on every scale
  but the one where you wanted to feel reliable. Full entry pending.
- **The absent founder.** Mereth founded alone. There is no other
  founder figure. This is by design — the org has no mythologized
  past that the players have to live up to. The Ledger Run is
  twenty-six years old. Mereth is still in the chair. The players are
  joining a working concern, not a legend.
- (Pending NPC) A Glasswake-side bookkeeper / dispatcher who runs the
  office when the kite is out. Will name and flesh out next turn.
sections.title: Existing members the players inherit

sections.text:
Not a role chart. The *kinds of leverage* the Ledger Run operates by:

- **Someone who can move a kite through Shear-edge and Bloom-cordoned
  air** without losing cargo or hours. This is technical work.
- **Someone who can read a sealed manifest** — Tuner-grade resonance
  literacy, or Conclave training, or hard-won field experience — and
  know whether what they're being asked to carry matches the
  paperwork.
- **Someone the Conclave will return a kite-bell call from.** Not
  trust; just *return the call*. This is rare and load-bearing.
- **Someone who knows what gossip from which hab is worth what.**
  Witness work, by another name.
- **Someone the Accord clerks know by face** — not by influence, but
  by *paperwork*. Filing things correctly is a skill, and one the org
  cannot afford to outsource.

Players will invent characters who come at these from angles I haven't
listed. That's fine. The point is the *kinds* of leverage, not the
slots.
sections.title: Capabilities the org typically needs

sections.text:
Five stages. Each names what advancing *broadly* unlocks. I will
resolve mechanical bonuses at the moment of advance based on what the
table cares about.

1. **Working crew.** Where the org starts. One kite. Three contracts.
   One reputation, mostly local.
2. **Carrier of record.** A Bloom-cordon hab or a previously
   isolationist hab opens to the route. The refusal log starts being
   cited in Accord proceedings. New contracts arrive unsolicited.
3. **The route is the institution.** A second kite running a parallel
   sub-route. Two named affiliates in different cities. The crew is
   invited to mediate, not just deliver. Vantara starts a quiet PR
   campaign against them.
4. **Witness corps.** The crew is called to witness — formally — at
   an Accord proceeding or a hab integration ceremony. Their
   testimony is *load-bearing* on something the Conclave wanted
   buried.
5. **The crew becomes the precedent.** The refusal log is codified
   into Accord article precedent. Article 14 is rewritten around the
   Ledger Run's standing practice. Mereth — or whoever — gets asked
   to leave the route and run the codification. They will say no.

Each stage advance is triggered by an arc resolution or a named
threat neutralized. I will not pre-specify the trigger for each; the
arc's resolution shape will tell me.
sections.title: Advancement track

sections.text:
There is one visible long-running pressure I want as a durable public
clock. I will create it via `glass clock` next turn once I've named
the arc; the segments will probably read something like:

- *Vantara files the Article 14 amendment proposal* (already pending)
- *the proposal is heard by the Accord clerks*
- *the clerks return a ruling*
- *the ruling stands or falls*
- *the Ledger Run keeps tariff exemption — or doesn't*

This is the Vantara pricing pressure. It is the visible pressure that
shapes early scenes. It is not the campaign.
sections.title: Public pressure (durable clock)

sections.text:
- **Mereth's refusal-log clauses.** Two refusals in the past three
  years are *not* in the public log. Both were Conclave research
  letters that named living Bloom survivors. Mereth refused to carry
  them and refused to log the refusal, because the log would surface
  the survivors' names. She has not told anyone. She does not know
  what to do with this. The first time she has to refuse a third, the
  decision becomes a scene.
- **The standing-courtyard drop at Threshold Station.** The Synod
  receives Ledger Run deliveries but does not engage in person. The
  drop is in an open courtyard where, on certain frequency windows,
  the Synod's silence *responds* to the delivery — a tonal shift in
  the station's ambient resonance, audible to Tuners. Mereth has
  noticed. She has not told the crew. She does not understand it.
- **Ev Sken's quiet.** Ev was at Fermata Station during the failed
  Sit-In thirty years ago. They have not told the crew. The dock
  master at Fermata has told nobody. The Ledger Run does not have a
  Fermata stop. Ev has not asked for one.
- **The black-route list.** The list of names that pay for direct
  hand-to-hand delivery includes one Conclave archivist (off-the-
  books authentication work) and one person Mereth herself does not
  know the identity of — the payments come through a Glasswake
  intermediary. She suspects but has never confirmed it is a Coremark
  remnant. She keeps carrying because the payments are clean and the
  packages are small.
- **The Halfsign's damage.** The secondary kite did not take Shear
  damage. It was damaged by something the crew currently aboard *has
  not reported,* because they could not name what they saw. This is
  for later; it surfaces if the players go looking.
sections.title: Secrets and agendas (DM-only)

sections.text:
The Ledger Run is the *party's home*. It has secrets, agendas, and
internal politics, but:

- It is not actively trying to harm the party. Mereth wants the crew
  alive. The crew is small enough that losses matter to her
  personally.
- It is not asking the players to be disposable. The refusal log
  applies to *their* refusals too.
- It is not in competition with itself. One org, one kite, one route.
  The friction is external.

Friction yes; predator no. The org is a frame the players can choose
how to be part of.
sections.title: Constraint check

sections.text:
For character creation, players should see the *public* shape of the
org — what it does, what it stands for, what's stressed about it
right now. The hidden secrets above stay hidden. Specific hooks live
in `dm/notes/hooks/` next turn.
sections.title: Hooks the org generates (pre-character-creation, broad only)

sections.text:
---
title: The Ledger Run
type: organization
tags: [organization, courier, kites, accord-chartered]
prominence: marginal
visibility: public
status: campaign-canon
---

# The Ledger Run

A chartered kite-courier crew working a recurring route between the
surface and the inner ring habs. You are members.

Formal name on the paperwork: **Long-Carry Circuit**, registered as an
Article 14 independent civil carrier under the Tempered Accord.
Nobody calls it that. The crew picked up *Ledger Run* twenty years
ago in the Conclave reading-rooms, and the name stuck because the
Conclave hated it.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
A roughly fortnight-long loop, in this order:

1. **Glasswake** — home port, surface side, a reclaimed dock built
   against the old hab fragment.
2. **Span Nine relay** — the pre-Glassfall conduit ridge between
   Glasswake and Sithari. The crew doesn't stop at Sithari; they hand
   surface mail off to a Vantara line at the relay.
3. **Hab Meridian** — the ring hab that first reconnected with
   Glasswake after the Famine. Standing municipal contract.
4. **Thornvault, in the Sable Crescent** — the Echo Ledger Conclave's
   headquarters castle. Standing research-mail contract.
5. **Ledgerfall** — a small settlement deeper in the Crescent, where
   the route turns and heads back.

Two kites: **the Splitfork**, the primary hauler — four-crew, cargo
bay, Shear-rated, route-preferring; and **the Halfsign**, secondary,
currently grounded for repairs.
sections.title: What the route is

sections.text:
- Carries mail under official Accord seal.
- Carries small cargo and bonded packages.
- Carries passengers when there's room and the manifest allows.
- Quietly: carries *news*. The crew does not advertise this. They
  also do not deny it. The Conclave hates this. The Accord pretends
  not to know. Vantara has been told to never speak of it. Everyone
  else uses it.
sections.title: What the crew does

sections.text:
The Ledger Run keeps a **public refusal log.** Every package the crew
declines to carry is recorded — date, sender, recipient, weight,
reason — and the log is readable at the Glasswake office on request.
Every quarter, an updated copy goes to the Accord clerks and to the
Conclave.

No other chartered carrier in the system does this. It is why the
crew is trusted. It is why the crew is hated.
sections.title: The thing everyone knows

sections.text:
- **Mereth Kel** — Captain. Founder. Late forties, Hab-Worlder by
  upbringing, Glasswake by choice. Former Vantara route officer. She
  walked off Vantara during the Sable Crescent embargo of 2419 over
  a single sealed letter Vantara refused to carry, and she has not
  gone back. She founded the Ledger Run that year. She is still in
  the chair.
- **Ev Sken** — Fixer. Hab-Worlder fae, intermittently present,
  manages waystation slots and Compact paperwork. Sometimes diffuse,
  sometimes coherent. Reliable on every scale but the one you
  expected.
- **(office staff, to be named)** — the Glasswake-side dispatcher.
sections.title: Who runs it

sections.text:
Mixed.

- Among working couriers and dock crews: respected. The refusal log
  is taken seriously. The route is on time more often than the
  weather should allow.
- Among the **Echo Ledger Conclave**: resented. The name is an
  insult. The crew is a structural critique. They are used anyway.
- Among **Vantara**: tolerated as a regulatory nuisance. Subcontracted
  on routes Vantara can't staff. Targeted in quiet Article 14
  pricing negotiations.
- Among the **Tempered Accord** clerks: ignored, on paper. The
  filings get filed. The refusal log gets received and shelved. Some
  clerks read it.
- Among the **Lattice Proxy Synod**: not acknowledged in person.
  Deliveries are accepted at the standing courtyard drop on Threshold
  Station.
- Among the **Shear Compact**: kite-tags up to date, no complaints.
- Among Glasswake locals: ours. Drinks bought at the dock pub.
sections.title: Public reputation

sections.text:
Not advertised; observable from practice.

- The route runs even when politics say it shouldn't.
- A package gets through or it gets refused on the record.
- The refusal log is not edited.
- The crew is small enough that losing one of you matters.
sections.title: What it stands for

sections.text:
- Vantara is pushing an Article 14 amendment that would strip tariff
  exemption from independent carriers' bulk mail. If it passes, the
  Ledger Run loses its margin.
- The Halfsign is grounded. The crew is running everything off the
  Splitfork, which means tighter scheduling and less slack.
- Two of the three standing contracts come up for renewal this
  quarter.
sections.title: What's stressed about it right now

sections.text:
You are crew on the Splitfork. You have your own reasons for being
here. You invented them in character creation. The job is what
brought you to Mereth's dock, and the refusal log is what made you
sign instead of walking away.
sections.title: What you joined

sections.text:
---
title: Tuners
type: concept
tags: [resonance, training, social-structure]
prominence: recognized
source: world-bible/player/concepts/tuners.md
---

# Tuners

People who can feel [resonance](../cosmology/resonance.md). Not "use resonance technology" — anyone can flip a switch on a [ringglass](ringglass.md)-powered lantern. Tuners have trained a specific perceptual sense: the ability to feel the local resonance landscape directly, the way a musician with perfect pitch hears intervals without thinking. The crystal hums, the ground vibrates, the air carries harmonics that most people walk through without noticing. Tuners notice.

The profession is older than the name. During the [Signal Famine](../history/index.md), people in isolated settlements independently figured out that they could learn to feel what the ring-era systems had once automated. Some reverse-engineered salvaged equipment. Some stumbled into it by accident — a [future:Renn Duvasi]-style moment of noticing something nobody else did. Some had artifacts that responded to their attention in ways they didn't expect. The learning was slow, dangerous, and entirely without theory. The elves had understood resonance as a science. The first Tuners understood it as a craft — or didn't understand it at all, and did it anyway.

They weren't called Tuners then. They weren't called anything. They were just the person in the settlement who could tell you whether the ringglass in the wall was about to crack, or the one on the hab who knew which corridors were safe when the resonance shifted. The profession came later. The name came later still.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
The original practitioners are recognized retroactively as the founders of a tradition that would outlast the crisis that created it. Different communities remember them differently:

- **Primers** — the academic term. Dry, institutional, used in Clarisant literature.
- **Wildfelt** — the folk term. More respectful than it sounds. These people felt something wild and didn't flinch.
- **Reckless** — what some communities called them, and not affectionately. Early Tuning killed people. Not every settlement appreciated someone experimenting with forces they didn't understand.

The first generation spans the entire Famine — sixty years of independent discovery across hundreds of isolated communities. Some of their techniques survived and became foundational. Others were dead ends, literally. The diversity of early practice is why modern Tuning has so many regional variations and why the [future:Clarisant] spends considerable effort standardizing what doesn't want to be standardized.
sections.title: The First Tuners <!-- History -->

sections.text:
The guild. Headquartered on [future:Pelhari], a moon of the seventh planet — far enough from the distorted space of [the Shear](../locations/regions/the-shear.md) and the ring debris field that resonance practice is cleaner, more predictable, more suited to structured training. Satellite offices operate across the system, in Sithari, in major ring habs, on outer-system stations.

The Clarisant is a professional accreditation body first and a community second. They train Tuners, certify competence, set safety standards, and provide the institutional credibility that keeps Tuning from being feared. A Clarisant-trained Tuner carries a credential that means something in any settlement that recognizes the guild — which is most of them. The credential says: this person has been evaluated, their abilities are understood, and they operate within professional norms.

The guild is not a government, not a military order, and not a religion. It is closer to a medical board — gatekeeping, yes, but gatekeeping in the service of public safety. Malpractice can get you expelled. Dogma cannot, because the Clarisant doesn't have dogma. It has standards.

The name is old enough that most people have stopped wondering what it means. It derives from pre-Famine roots — *clear perception*, roughly — and was coined by the guild's founders, who wanted something that sounded serious without sounding threatening. They mostly succeeded.
sections.title: The Clarisant <!-- Structure -->

sections.text:
Not everyone trains Clarisant. Roughly thirty percent of practicing Tuners operate outside the guild — folk practitioners who learned from family, from local tradition, from apprenticeship to another folk Tuner, or from sheer stubborn experimentation.

Folk Tuners are the norm in communities where the Clarisant has limited presence: remote surface settlements, isolated ring habs, outer-system stations that are weeks from the nearest satellite office. Some communities have never heard of the guild. Some have heard of it and want nothing to do with it. Some have their own traditions that predate the guild and see no reason to defer to an institution on a distant moon.

The folk tradition is not inferior to the Clarisant tradition. It is different. Folk Tuners tend toward the intuitive, the improvisational, the locally adapted. A folk Tuner on a ring hab knows *that* hab's resonance landscape in a way no visiting Clarisant could match. What they lack is breadth — the theoretical framework that lets a Clarisant-trained Tuner walk into an unfamiliar environment and orient quickly.

The tension between folk and Clarisant Tuning is the jazz-versus-classical divide. Both produce genuine mastery. Both are suspicious of the other. Both are right to be.
sections.title: Folk Tuners <!-- Culture -->

sections.text:
Below the visible profession — guild and folk alike — there are people who can feel resonance and don't know what they're feeling. Maybe five to ten percent of the population with some degree of untrained sensitivity, experiencing it as hunches, headaches, an unexplained comfort or discomfort in certain spaces. Most never develop it further. Some do, and become folk Tuners without anyone calling it that — the hedge witch who knows which direction the water runs, the mechanic who can tell a ringglass array is misaligned by standing near it.

And then there are the hidden — Tuners who practice in secret because their community doesn't tolerate it. Some habs prosecute unauthorized resonance work. Some settlements associate Tuning with the elves and treat practitioners with suspicion. Being a hidden Tuner is lonely and dangerous, and the Clarisant runs quiet programs to identify and extract people from these situations when they can.
sections.title: The Hidden and the Unknowing <!-- Culture -->

sections.text: Any species can become a Tuner. Each brings different strengths and limitations — see individual species entries for details ([gnomes](species/gnomes.md), [humans](species/humans.md), [orcs](species/orcs.md), [fae](species/fae.md)). Gnomes are disproportionately represented at the highest levels; humans compensate with methodology and instrumentation; orcs gravitate toward material Tuning; fae are rare due to coherence risks. The [elves](species/elves.md) were the original masters, and their absence is felt most acutely here — the theoretical framework that should exist doesn't, because the people who would have built it are gone.
sections.title: Species and Tuning <!-- Resonance -->

sections.text:
At the edges of practice, Tuning gets strange:

- There are habs where only Tuners are permitted to live — communities that have organized their entire social structure around resonance sensitivity.
- There is a deep-space station, far from any planet or ring fragment, that is held together structurally by the sustained concentration of its Tuner residents. If they stop, the station fails.
- The [Bloom Zones](../locations/landmarks/bloom-zones.md) are the ultimate test. The reality distortion there registers as overwhelming sensory noise for any Tuner. [Gnomes](species/gnomes.md) are particularly vulnerable. The [Displacement Council](../npcs/factions/displacement-council.md) employs Tuners for containment work, but exposure limits are strict and the burnout rate is high.
sections.title: Extremes <!-- Applications -->

sections.text:
---
title: Tempered Accord
type: faction
tags: [governance, diplomacy]
prominence: renowned
prominence_xrefs: [lattice-proxy-synod, vantara, displacement-council, shear-compact]
source: world-bible/player/npcs/factions/tempered-accord.md
---

# Tempered Accord

The system's governance framework. Not a government — a mediation structure. A set of shared norms that most settlements have agreed to honor most of the time. It holds because the alternative is the Bitter Reach again, and nobody wants that.

The Accord is four years old.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
Born from exhaustion. The [future:Bloom Coalition] proved that cooperation worked. The [future:Bitter Reach] proved that the system couldn't survive without it. The [Shear Compact](shear-compact.md) proved that emergency structures could become permanent. By the time the fighting stopped, the preconditions for a broader framework were already in place — they just needed someone to write them down.

Three institutions shaped the Accord's architecture: the [Lattice Proxy Synod](lattice-proxy-synod.md) provided a governance model derived from AI custodian outputs, [Vantara](vantara.md) shaped the trade provisions and logistics standards, and the [Displacement Council](displacement-council.md) contributed the humanitarian framework and served as trusted mediator.

Ratified approximately 2365 CE. Signatories include surface settlements, ring habs, and outer-system representatives.
sections.title: Origin <!-- Origin -->

sections.text:
The Accord operates from a district in [Sithari](../../locations/settlements/sithari.md) — a complex deliberately separated from the Continuity's government buildings. Tranquil. An artificial lake. Ducks. The architectural statement is intentional: this is not a seat of power, it's a place where disputes come to be resolved.

The Accord is an international body in a national capital — technically independent, practically shaped by proximity. Other settlements wanted the Accord seated somewhere neutral. Sithari argued they had the infrastructure. They were right, and everyone resented them for it.

The Accord's operations rely on member organizations for actual enforcement. It carries weight because people say it does. The sanctions for violating an Accord provision are reputational and economic — expulsion from the framework means losing access to the Compact's mediation, Vantara's trade infrastructure, and the legal recognition that makes inter-settlement commerce possible.
sections.title: Structure <!-- Structure -->

sections.text:
**Mediation.** The Accord's core function. When settlements, fabs, or factions have disputes that can't be resolved bilaterally, they bring them to the Accord's mediation process. The process is derived from the Shear Compact's model and is universally considered slow, bureaucratic, and better than the alternative.

**Standards.** The Accord maintains system-wide standards for resonance safety, trade documentation, salvage certification (via the Compact), and communication protocols. These standards are Sitharian in origin — drafted in Sitharian legal language, reflecting Sitharian institutional assumptions — which is resented by everyone who isn't Sitharian and exploited by everyone who is.

**Humanitarian.** The Accord's humanitarian provisions, drafted by the Displacement Council, cover refugee rights, containment support, and sovereignty protections — including the hard-won provision that habs can legally refuse contact.
sections.title: Operations <!-- Operations -->

sections.text:
The Accord is young, fragile, and untested. Four years of relative stability doesn't prove the framework works — it proves that nothing has broken it yet. Legitimate questions exist about what happens when it is genuinely tested: a faction that defies an Accord ruling and faces no real consequences, a crisis that requires action faster than the mediation process allows, a dispute where the stakes are too high for reputation damage to matter.

[Sithari](../../locations/settlements/sithari.md)'s influence is structural and resented. The Accord sits in Sithari, uses Sitharian legal language, and was partly designed by Sitharian political thinkers. The [Continuity](../../locations/settlements/sithari.md) was a combatant during the Contested Reach and Bitter Reach while the city claimed neutrality. This distinction — the city is neutral, the government is a participant — was technically valid and practically meaningless. The resentment is still live.
sections.title: Tensions <!-- Tensions -->

sections.text: The Accord holds. The ducks are fine. Whether either condition is permanent remains to be seen.
sections.title: Present Day <!-- Present Day -->

sections.text:
---
title: Echo Ledger Conclave
type: faction
tags: [archives, resonance, signal-freq, governance]
prominence: recognized
source: world-bible/player/npcs/factions/echo-ledger-conclave.md
---

# Echo Ledger Conclave

Archivists. Gatekeepers. The people who catalogue what the ground remembers and decide who gets to hear it.

The Conclave exists because the [Echo Rivers](../../cosmology/echo-rivers.md) exist, and because someone had to take responsibility for what surfaces in them. They are headquartered in the [Sable Crescent](../../locations/regions/sable-crescent.md), where the rivers are densest, and they have made themselves indispensable to anyone who needs the past authenticated, interpreted, or — occasionally — suppressed.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
The Conclave formed during the Rekindling, roughly 2310 CE, when reconnecting settlements began comparing notes about the [Echo Rivers](../../cosmology/echo-rivers.md) and realized the same phenomenon was surfacing across the planet. Early archivists were just listeners — people who sat near surfacing points and wrote down what they heard. The Conclave organized them, standardized their methods, and — critically — claimed custodianship over the material.

The claim stuck because nobody else wanted the job. Cataloguing fragments of dead strangers' conversations is not glamorous work. It attracts a specific personality: patient, obsessive, comfortable with ambiguity, and inclined to treat knowledge as an end in itself. The Conclave selected for these traits and has been selecting for them ever since.
sections.title: Origin <!-- Origin -->

sections.text:
Mid-tier faction — larger than a guild, smaller than a government. The Conclave operates from [future:Thornvault], their headquarters in the Sable Crescent: a castle-like complex built on a natural bridge spanning a canyon, positioned to face the glasslight at dusk. The architecture is deliberately imposing — soaring crystal-laced buttresses, open galleries that channel ambient river sound through the halls. The building is a statement: we are serious people doing serious work.

Satellite offices exist in [Sithari](../../locations/settlements/sithari.md) (for political and legal access), several ring habs (for hab-specific archive consultation), and at least two [future:Bloom Zone] cordon stations (for containment support). The satellites are small — a few archivists with portable playback equipment and a secure connection to Thornvault's central records.

Leadership is academic. The head of the Conclave holds the title [future:Chief Ledgrist] and is selected by internal vote among senior archivists. The position is respected externally and envied internally, which is exactly how the Conclave likes it.
sections.title: Structure <!-- Structure -->

sections.text:
What the Conclave actually does, day to day:

**Listening.** Teams stationed at Echo River surfacing points throughout the Sable Crescent and beyond, cataloguing what comes through. Shifts are long. Most of what surfaces is noise — fragments, partial transmissions, data sequences. The valuable material is rare and unpredictable. A listener might spend months at a single surfacing point before hearing anything significant.

**Authentication.** When someone claims an artifact is pre-Glassfall, when a legal dispute hinges on historical precedent, when a faction needs to verify a claim about the Ring Age — they come to the Conclave. The archivists cross-reference Echo River fragments, surviving written records, and their own accumulated knowledge to assess whether a claim holds up. This service is not free, and the Conclave's assessment carries legal weight under the [Tempered Accord](tempered-accord.md).

**Containment support.** The [Displacement Council](displacement-council.md) employs Conclave archivists for [future:Bloom Zone] work. The theory — partially validated — is that understanding the resonance history of an affected region helps predict how containment cordons should be configured. The Conclave provides historical resonance analysis. The Council provides the muscle. Neither fully trusts the other's methods, and the partnership works anyway.

**Relic validation.** Even [Coremark](coremark.md)'s black-market buyers need provenance. A Bloom Zone relic of uncertain origin has more value if a Conclave archivist can attest to its resonance signature. The Conclave officially does not authenticate goods of questionable provenance. Individual archivists, on their own time, occasionally do. The Conclave officially does not know about this.
sections.title: Operations <!-- Operations -->

sections.text:
The Conclave hoards knowledge. This is not a criticism — it's their self-description. They consider themselves custodians, not distributors. Knowledge shared carelessly is knowledge misused. Knowledge kept carefully is knowledge preserved.

This philosophy makes them valuable and infuriating in roughly equal measure. The [Tempered Accord](tempered-accord.md) has attempted to mandate broader access to Conclave archives three times. Three times the Conclave has complied with the letter and ignored the spirit, releasing material so carefully redacted that it was technically public and practically useless.

Their relationship with [Sithari](../../locations/settlements/sithari.md) and the [future:Continuity] is grudgingly positive. Both factions value knowledge, but for different reasons — the Continuity wants knowledge that serves governance, the Conclave wants knowledge that serves knowledge. They share when the interests align and politely obstruct when they don't. The Conclave recognizes that their institutional independence could be worse. The Continuity recognizes that forcibly opening the archives would cost more politically than it's worth.

Within the Conclave, the Redaction theorists — archivists who believe the [elven](../../concepts/species/elves.md) historical gaps are evidence of deliberate tampering — are a respected fringe. Not mainstream, not suppressed. The kind of position that gets you invited to interesting dinners and passed over for administrative promotion. The eccentric professor, not the ideological dissident.
sections.title: Tensions <!-- Tensions -->

sections.text:
The Conclave is secure, solvent, and slightly too pleased with itself. Their expertise is genuinely irreplaceable — nobody else has the institutional knowledge, the listening infrastructure, or the legal standing to do what they do. This makes them confident in ways that sometimes shade into arrogant.

The Bloom containment work has raised their profile. Before the Silent Bloom, the Conclave was a niche academic institution. Now they're a faction with practical relevance to the system's biggest ongoing crisis. Some senior archivists are uncomfortable with this. Others are enjoying it more than they'd admit.

[Lira Vashtenri](../heroes/lira-vashtenri.md) left the Conclave to join the [Displacement Council](displacement-council.md), taking her resonance expertise with her. The Conclave considers this a personal betrayal dressed up as public service. Lira considers it the most useful thing she's ever done. They are both correct.
sections.title: Present Day <!-- Present Day -->

sections.text:
---
title: Glasswake
type: installation
tags: [surface, trade, ring-era]
prominence: recognized
source: world-bible/player/locations/settlements/glasswake.md
---

# Glasswake

A mining town on Kaleidos's surface, built around the ruins of a ring hab fragment that fell during the [Glassfall](../../history/events/the-glassfall.md). The fragment's inter-hab communication equipment — buried in the wreckage for sixty years — turned out to need only minor repair to function. Glasswake didn't set out to reconnect the world. A salvage crew poking through old wreckage found a transmitter that still had power, and one of them knew enough to make it talk.

That was roughly 2305 CE. The first long-range signal after the Signal Famine went from Glasswake to [future:Hab Meridian], a ring hab whose receiver happened to still be listening. The reconnection began with two communities that stumbled into it.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
Before the Glassfall, the site was nothing — open ground near the impact zone of a ring fragment large enough to survive descent mostly intact. The fragment hit hard enough to half-bury itself, creating a ridge of fused crystal and stone that the first settlers built against for shelter during the Famine. The settlement grew around the wreckage the way a town grows around a crossroads: not by plan, but because people kept showing up.

What it was before the Famine — what the original ring hab was, who lived in it, what it was called — nobody knows, and nobody particularly cares. The fragment is a resource, not a heritage site. Miners work the ringglass deposits in the impact debris. The old hab structures serve as foundations, warehouses, and — in one case — a pub.
sections.title: Origin <!-- Origin -->

sections.text:
Mining. The impact debris field is rich with [ringglass](../../concepts/ringglass.md) aggregate — not high-grade material, but consistent volume. The mines have been producing for decades and aren't close to exhaustion. It's steady, unglamorous work that supports a stable community.

The reconnection history adds a secondary economy: tourism. Glasswake is the Plymouth Rock of the Rekindling — historically significant, practically unremarkable, visited by people who care about that sort of thing. The tourist trade supports a few inns, a museum in the old transmitter building, and a gift shop that sells overpriced ringglass trinkets stamped with "First Signal." The miners find this mildly embarrassing and entirely welcome.
sections.title: Economy <!-- Economy -->

sections.text:
A working town that happens to be famous. The streets are dusty from the mines. The buildings are practical — ringglass-reinforced stone, functional architecture, no pretension. The fallen hab fragment dominates the skyline as a ridge of fused crystal along the town's eastern edge, weathered into something that looks almost natural if you don't know what you're looking at.

[Span Nine](../landmarks/span-nine.md) — the relay that carried the first signal — is a landmark on the ridge between Glasswake and [Sithari](sithari.md). Tourists hike to it. Locals use it as a reference point for directions. "Past Span Nine, take the left fork."

A surface-access spur connects Glasswake to [the Keel](../landmarks/keel.md), the system's primary orbital trade lane. This makes the town a minor waypoint — not a hub, but a place where kites descending from the Keel stop and crews resupply.
sections.title: Atmosphere <!-- Atmosphere -->

sections.text:
---
title: Span Nine
type: installation
tags: [ring-era, resonance, signal-freq, orbital]
prominence: recognized
source: world-bible/player/locations/landmarks/span-nine.md
---

# Span Nine

A shining white filament stretching from [Sithari](../settlements/sithari.md) to [future:Pelhari] and [future:Threshold Station], visible to the naked eye as an impossibly thin luminous thread against the sky. Span Nine is a pre-Glassfall hardline communication conduit — a physical connection between Kaleidos's surface and two of the system's most important off-world installations, maintained across orbital distances that should make a fixed link impossible.

The name comes from a marking at each terminus connection point. Whether "Nine" indicates this is one of a numbered series (with the other eight destroyed, lost, or undiscovered), or whether the symbol has been misread entirely — it could be a numeral, a glyph, or a designation in a language nobody speaks anymore — is the subject of mild academic debate and zero practical concern. It's what's on the label. Nobody had a better name. It stuck.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
Nobody knows.

The filament appears to be a single continuous structure from Sithari's surface to Pelhari's moon and to the Synod's station at the Lagrange point. It does not sag, stretch, or break despite the constant relative motion of these bodies. It carries signal-band [resonance](../../cosmology/resonance.md) at bandwidths that modern equipment can barely detect, providing communication capacity that dwarfs anything the post-Glassfall world has built. High-bandwidth data transfer, real-time voice, even the dense multidimensional outputs of the [Lattice Proxy Synod](../../npcs/factions/lattice-proxy-synod.md)'s AI custodians — all of it travels the Span without degradation.

Efforts to interact with the filament directly do not go well. Not violently — just strangely. A ship can pass through it without incident. The filament jumps around the hull or simply passes through as if one of them isn't quite there. But deliberate interaction — intentional contact — is something reality declines to permit. Reach for it during an EVA and you find yourself back inside the airlock with no memory of the attempt. Plot a course to intercept it and the navigation system routes around it without being told to. Try to cut a sample and discover you've been doing something else for the last hour.

Intent matters. The Span appears to distinguish between incidental contact and purposeful interference. How is a question the [Lattice Proxy Synod](../../npcs/factions/lattice-proxy-synod.md) has been investigating for decades without meaningful progress.
sections.title: How It Works <!-- Mechanics -->

sections.text:
Span Nine was rediscovered during the Rekindling, after the Signal Famine ended and settlements began looking up and noticing what had been glowing in the sky for sixty years without anyone understanding what it was. Once its function as a communication conduit was established, it became essential infrastructure almost overnight.

Before the Span, communication between Kaleidos's surface and off-world installations was slow, unreliable, and bandwidth-limited. With it, [Sithari](../settlements/sithari.md) has real-time signal-bandwidth links to the [future:Clarisant] guild on Pelhari and the Synod's AI custodians at Threshold Station. This connectivity is a significant part of Sithari's continued centrality — the capital's access to the Span is a structural advantage that other settlements cannot replicate.

The Span is also a reminder. Whoever built it — and the engineering is consistent with [elven](../../concepts/species/elves.md) work, though no surviving record credits them specifically — operated at a level of capability that the current civilization cannot approach. The filament has survived the Glassfall, two centuries of orbital debris, and every attempt to study it, and it continues to function perfectly. It is the most visible evidence that the Ring Age produced things nobody alive knows how to make.
sections.title: Significance <!-- Significance -->

sections.text:
---
title: KITE
type: resource
tags: [transport, resonance, kinetic-freq, trade]
prominence: renowned
prominence_xrefs: [tuners, vantara, keel]
alias: Kinetic Inverse Transport Engine
source: world-bible/player/artifacts/common/kite-sail.md
---

# KITE

The Kinetic Inverse Transport Engine — universally called a kite — is the general-purpose motive engine of the Kaleidos system. Kites use kinetic-band [resonance](../../cosmology/resonance.md) to redirect opposing forces into a frequency null space, producing effectively unopposed momentum. A kite doesn't fight drag, friction, or gravity in the conventional sense — it makes the opposition happen somewhere else.

They still have sails. The earliest models were literal sailing vessels with [ringglass](../../concepts/ringglass.md) arrays bolted to the hull, and modern designs retain sail elements — partly functional (the sail surfaces carry structural-band arrays for stability), partly aesthetic, partly because a ship without sails doesn't look like a ship to anyone raised in this system.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
The core of a kite is a kinetic-band ringglass array — a set of tuned crystals that generate a momentum nullification field. Within this field, forces opposing movement are shunted into a frequency null space. The vehicle moves as if nothing is pushing back.

Mid-bandwidth kinetic work. A kite doesn't need a [Tuner](../../concepts/tuners.md) to operate — arrays are tuned at manufacture and maintained periodically. Anyone who can read a resonance gauge can keep one running. Fuel — [Microcavities](../../concepts/microcavities.md) for high-energy operations, ambient resonance for light duty — sustains the field.

The result is a vehicle that performs in atmosphere, vacuum, and the distorted environment of [The Shear](../../locations/regions/the-shear.md). The same engine that runs a surface route can lift to orbit, dock at a ring hab, and push into a debris field.
sections.title: How It Works <!-- How It Works -->

sections.text:
**[future:Flitters].** Autonomous local kites. Short-range, single-passenger or small-cargo, operating within and between settlements. Ubiquitous — standard-issue infrastructure, the way breathable air is standard on a pressurized hab. Every settlement with a landing pad has flitters cycling through it.

**Personal craft.** Sub-orbital or space-capable kites owned by individuals. Comfortable enough for extended trips, not built for deep Shear or interplanetary hauls. A mark of moderate prosperity — common enough that no one stares, uncommon enough that most people still book passage on a commercial vessel instead.

**Commercial haulers and passenger vessels.** The [Vantara](../../npcs/factions/vantara.md) fleet and independent operators. Cargo haulers running [the Keel](../../locations/landmarks/keel.md) and other trade lanes. Passenger liners between major settlements. Fast couriers. This is the backbone of system commerce.

**Shear-rated vessels.** Salvage crews, mining rigs, survey ships. Carry additional structural-band arrays for reality stabilization shielding. The Shear's resonance distortion can interfere with kinetic fields if local physics aren't anchored.

**Military.** Warships and patrol vessels — remnants of the Contested Reach and Bitter Reach eras, maintained because piracy is ongoing. [Vantara](../../npcs/factions/vantara.md) operates the largest private armed escort fleet. Various factions maintain their own. Kinetic fields can be used offensively, which is technically possible and socially frowned upon.

**Capital class.** The largest kites in the system. Cruisers, battleships, mobile stations. Rare enough that most are individually named and recognized across entire regions.
sections.title: Classes <!-- Applications -->

sections.text:
The momentum nullification field requires continuous resonance input. In resonance-rich areas (most of Kaleidos, the ring), this comes from the environment. In resonance-poor areas (deep interplanetary space, Shear dead zones), the vessel burns stored energy. Run out, and you're drifting.

The null space receiving the redirected forces is not well understood. It's a frequency domain, not a physical location. The forces don't "go" anywhere physics can track. This has never caused a problem in two centuries of use. Engineers find this more concerning, not less.

Large-scale momentum nullification — moving a ring hab — is theoretically possible and practically beyond any existing array's capacity.
sections.title: Limits <!-- Limits -->

sections.text:
---
title: Vantara
type: faction
tags: [trade, transport, governance]
prominence: recognized
source: world-bible/player/npcs/factions/vantara.md
---

# Vantara

*"Every journey, elevated."*

The system's largest travel and logistics corporation. Vantara operates [KITE](../../artifacts/common/kite-sail.md) fleets, manages trade route infrastructure, runs hospitality venues across the ring and major surface settlements, and generally positions itself as the entity that makes moving through the Kaleidos system comfortable, reliable, and — if you pay for the premium tier — pleasant.

The name is corporate-smooth by design. Vantara was founded during the Rekindling when the first inter-settlement trade networks needed someone to handle the boring parts: scheduling, maintenance, crew rotation, waystation provisioning. The founders understood that the money wasn't in the cargo — it was in making the journey tolerable for the people carrying it.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
Vantara operates under conventional corporate structures. Two details set it apart:

The CEO carries the title **Chief Experience Officer**, because the current one — [future:Davan Koralis] — believes that Vantara's core product is the experience of travel, not the logistics of it. The institutional priority is genuine — Vantara invests in waystation quality, crew training, and passenger comfort.

Vantara is publicly traded, politically neutral, and genuinely multinational — operations span surface settlements, ring habs, outer-system stations, and [the Keel](../../locations/landmarks/keel.md) trade lane. Their neutrality is economic, not ideological. They don't take sides because taking sides is bad for business. This makes them useful to the [Tempered Accord](../../npcs/factions/tempered-accord.md) as a logistics backbone that everyone can work with.
sections.title: Structure <!-- Structure -->

sections.text:
**Transport.** Vantara operates the largest civilian kite fleet in the system. Cargo haulers, passenger vessels, fast couriers, and the [future:flitters] — autonomous local kites that function as public transit in major settlements. If you've ridden a kite and didn't own it, there's a decent chance Vantara operated it.

**Hospitality.** Waystations along major routes, hotels in settlements, crew rest facilities at ring hab docking ports. The quality is consistent and consistently average — not bad, not memorable, reliably clean and functional. Some people find this comforting. Others call it soulless. Vantara's branding team considers both reactions acceptable.

**Route management.** Vantara doesn't own trade routes — nobody does, formally — but they maintain the infrastructure: navigation beacons, refueling stations, emergency rescue coverage. This is expensive public-good work that Vantara performs because it supports their core business and because nobody else was going to do it. The [Tempered Accord](../../npcs/factions/tempered-accord.md) subsidizes some of it. The rest comes from usage fees that everyone complains about and everyone pays.

**Armed escorts.** During the Contested Reach, Vantara started arming convoy escorts after their ships were targeted on disputed corridors. The escorts remain. Piracy on [the Keel](../../locations/landmarks/keel.md) and other routes is an ongoing problem, and Vantara's security division is the largest private armed force operating in civilian space. They are not a military. They are very clear about this. Their ships carry weapons anyway.
sections.title: Operations <!-- Operations -->

sections.text:
Vantara is a corporation, and it behaves like one. Their services are affordable but not cheap. Their neutrality serves their bottom line. Their hospitality is optimized for consistency, not character — a Vantara waystation on the Keel feels identical to a Vantara waystation in a ring hab, which is either the point or the problem depending on your disposition.

The criticism — that Vantara desaturates everything it touches into corporate-average quality — is probably fair. The counterargument — that before Vantara, long-haul trade routes had no rest facilities, no rescue coverage, and no reliable scheduling — is also fair. Both assessments hold weight.

The [future:Ratters] and independent operators view Vantara with the mix of resentment and grudging reliance that independent operators everywhere feel toward large companies that provide infrastructure they can't build themselves. Vantara's route beacons and rescue coverage keep independents alive. Vantara's pricing squeezes independents' margins. The relationship is stable and unhappy.
sections.title: Tensions <!-- Tensions -->

sections.text:
Vantara is secure, profitable, and expanding. The Accord era has been good for them — stability means more trade, more travel, more demand for reliable logistics. Their fleet is growing. Their waystation network is extending into newly contacted far-drift ring habs. Their Chief Experience Officer is on a media tour promoting Vantara's new luxury cruise line through the scenic segments of [The Shear](../../locations/regions/the-shear.md), which is either visionary or reckless depending on your risk tolerance.

The company's bland competence is, in its way, a marker of civilization. Reliable infrastructure enables the system's more volatile pursuits.
sections.title: Present Day <!-- Present Day -->

sections.text:
---
title: Life in the System
type: concept
tags: [social-structure, resonance]
prominence: mythic
source: world-bible/player/concepts/life-in-the-system.md
---

# Life in the System

What it feels like to live here. Not the politics, not the factions, not the history — the texture of ordinary days in a system where the fundamental physics are slightly negotiable.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
Most resources work the way resources work anywhere. Food is grown on Kaleidos and [future:Korvath], supplemented by massive greenhouse stations positioned at optimal orbital locations. Water is collected, filtered, recycled. Metal is mined. None of this is exotic. The supply chains are longer than they would be on a single world — a settlement on the ring might source grain from the surface, protein from an orbital greenhouse, and metals from a Shear salvage operation — but the logistics are mature and mostly invisible to the people eating dinner.

Broad-bandwidth [resonance](../cosmology/resonance.md) is infrastructure. Lanterns, stoves, heaters, compasses, basic communication devices — all draw from the ambient resonance field through small [ringglass](ringglass.md) components built into their housing. The technology is standardized, inexpensive, and unremarkable. A household resonance lantern is no more interesting to its owner than the walls around it. It works. When it stops working, you replace the crystal insert.

Construction uses ringglass-reinforced materials as a default. Structural-band resonance makes walls harder, foundations more resilient, pressure seals more reliable. This is not advanced technology — it is the baseline. Buildings that don't use resonance reinforcement are either very old, very cheap, or very far from a ringglass supply.
sections.title: The Basics <!-- How It Works -->

sections.text:
[Kites](../artifacts/common/kite-sail.md) are everywhere. Flitters — short-range autonomous kites — handle local transport, moving people and small cargo within and between settlements. Booking passage on a commercial kite between major settlements is routine. Interplanetary travel takes days, not months, and most people in the inner system have visited at least one other world.

The system is compact enough that distance is an inconvenience, not a barrier. A week to the outer planets. A day to an adjacent one. People maintain relationships, business interests, and family ties across planetary distances the way a surface-dweller might maintain them across a continent.
sections.title: Getting Around <!-- Applications -->

sections.text:
The things people do everywhere. Sports leagues operate within and between settlements — some local, some system-wide with seasonal tournaments that draw spectators from multiple worlds. Strategy games with regional traditions. Competitive resonance tuning, which is exactly what it sounds like. People go sightseeing — the surface of Kaleidos has landscapes shaped by post-Glassfall ringglass geology, the ring habs offer views that no planet can match, and the outer system has its own draws for the adventurous.

Entire habs are devoted to leisure. Garden worlds maintained as parks and retreats. Orbital casinos. Amusement installations with attractions that exploit the ring's loosened physics in ways that are technically safe and viscerally terrifying. These are not exotic — they are the system's tourism industry, accessible to anyone with passage fare and a few days to spare.

Seasons exist on the planetary surfaces but matter less than they would in a single-world civilization. When your trading partners, your employer, and half your family live on different worlds with different orbital periods, the local harvest cycle is a regional concern, not a civilizational rhythm.
sections.title: What People Do <!-- Culture -->

sections.text:
The infrastructure accounts for the improbable. Building codes in most settlements include provisions for resonance spikes, dimensional drift in areas with high-density ringglass, and the occasional spontaneous manifestation of narrow-bandwidth effects in places where nobody was working with narrow bandwidth.

The eighth dimension modifying the configuration of your hallway and resulting in a flooded living room is not a crisis. It's a Tuesday. There is a form. Your insurance covers it if you filed the right paperwork. The repair crew has seen worse. The structures — civic, legal, architectural — exist because this kind of thing happens frequently enough to be anticipated but rarely enough to remain surprising.

This is not chaos. It's a civilization that has learned to build its systems with tolerance for the unexpected, because the alternative is building systems that break every time reality hiccups.
sections.title: The Everyday Improbable <!-- Limits -->

# Where I am

Campaign-planning, turn 1. First DM invocation. Sitting with the mug.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
- `dm/foundation.md` — the working framing. Question, Scarcity, the org
  rationale, authorial stance, what I'm not prepping.

  including secrets and Mereth's two unlogged refusals.

  Public reputation, route, refusal log, current pressure.
sections.title: What I wrote this turn

sections.text:
Seven world-bible entries this turn. Plus the four already-present
species/cultures entries. Eleven curated entries so far. Aim total
8–15 by end of planning; I'll add 3–5 more next turn as factions and
named things firm up.

Imported: tuners, tempered-accord, echo-ledger-conclave, glasswake,
span-nine, kite-sail, vantara, life-in-the-system.
sections.title: What I imported

sections.text:
- Question: **Who decides what the system remembers?**
- Scarcity: **Witnesses.**
- Setting moment: 2438 CE, post-Bitter-Reach, Accord stable, Bloom not
  shrinking.
- Org: **the Ledger Run** — chartered kite-courier crew, refusal log,
  home port Glasswake.
- Tone: hopecore played straight. Institutional silence as

sections.title: What's settled

sections.text:
1. **Factions** — 3 written entries in `dm/notes/factions/`:
   - Echo Ledger Conclave (campaign-specific layer; world-bible entry
     imported).
   - Vantara (campaign-specific layer; world-bible entry imported).
   - One more — leaning toward the Tempered Accord clerks as their
     own faction, *not* the Accord-as-institution. The clerks are the
     party's actual interface. They have their own clock.
2. **NPCs** — 4 to 6 entries, including:
   - Mereth Kel (full entry; sketched in org doc)
   - Ev Sken (full entry; sketched in org doc)
   - A Conclave archivist who is the party's reluctant client
   - A Vantara route officer who pushes back
   - An antagonist with a clock — leaning toward someone who
     weaponizes gossip
3. **Creatures** — 2 to 4. At least one Bloom-touched route hazard.
   At least one cthonic-beast in the deep Shear that the party will
   only meet if they go looking.
4. **Named things** — 3 to 5:
   - The **Splitfork** (the primary kite; full entry)
   - The **Halfsign** (secondary, grounded; full entry)
   - A relic or sealed shard the players will be asked to carry
5. **Locations** — 3 to 5. Glasswake dock (concrete locale, not

6. **Secrets** — 10 short atomic facts in `dm/notes/secrets.md`.
7. **Hooks** — 3 to 5, kept broad until character creation.
8. **Philosophy** — one or two short entries.
9. **Opening arc** — `glass arc create` with the pressure: *something
   the party is being asked to carry is not what it has been declared

   the arc is something else. I don't know what yet. That is fine.

- The refusal log is doing more work than I expected. It is the
  single feature that distinguishes the Ledger Run from "any other
  courier crew." I should let scenes turn on it. A refusal entry in

- Mereth's two unlogged refusals are the campaign's first hidden

- The Synod's standing-courtyard drop is a free Threshold Station
