sections.text:
---
title: Campaign Framing
status: campaign-planning
phase: planning
---

# Campaign Framing

**Year:** 2438 CE. Twenty years past the Bitter Reach. The Tempered
Accord holds. The Bloom Zones have not shrunk.

**The Question the campaign asks:** *Who decides what the system
remembers?*

**The Scarcity:** *Witnesses.* Trusted carriers of what happened
between the habs, between the surface and the ring, between the
versions of the last sixty years that different communities are now
forced to reconcile.

**The party:** crew on **the Ledger Run** — a chartered Article 14
kite-courier crew running a recurring loop between Glasswake (surface),
Span Nine relay, Hab Meridian, and the Sable Crescent (Thornvault and
Ledgerfall). They carry mail. They carry small cargo. They quietly
carry news. They keep a public refusal log, which is what distinguishes
them from every other courier in the system.

**Where they are right now:** at home dock in Glasswake, between
routes. The Halfsign — secondary kite — is grounded for repairs. Two
of three standing contracts come up for renewal this quarter. Vantara
has filed an Accord amendment that would strip Article 14 tariff
exemption from independent carriers' bulk mail; the proposal is
pending at the clerks' level.

**Active threads (campaign-level):**

- *Article 14.* Vantara's pricing amendment. Public, named, slow-
  burning. Visible to everyone.
- *The refusal log.* Building campaign-long stakes. Each entry the
  crew makes is observable, citable, and politically charged. The
  Conclave watches.
- *Witness work.* Implicit, unspoken. The crew has not been formally
  asked to witness for anyone yet. They will be.

**Tone:** hopecore played straight. The unexpected is normal. The
building codes account for the eighth dimension. Combat happens; it
is not the texture. The load-bearing opposition is institutional
silence — the things people will not say.

**What is NOT on the table:** the elves' fate, the Adversary, the
Communication Shard. These are world-bible DM truths. They are not
what this campaign is *for*. If a scene reaches them, fine; the
campaign is not steered toward them.

This document gets updated when a thread advances a beat, a faction's
stance changes, or the party arrives in a new region. The first such
update will come when the opening arc lands and the visible Article 14
clock ticks.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
---
title: The Ledger Run
type: organization
tags: [organization, courier, kites, accord-chartered]
prominence: marginal
visibility: public
status: campaign-canon
---

# The Ledger Run

A chartered kite-courier crew working a recurring route between the
surface and the inner ring habs. You are members.

Formal name on the paperwork: **Long-Carry Circuit**, registered as an
Article 14 independent civil carrier under the Tempered Accord.
Nobody calls it that. The crew picked up *Ledger Run* twenty years
ago in the Conclave reading-rooms, and the name stuck because the
Conclave hated it.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
A roughly fortnight-long loop, in this order:

1. **Glasswake** — home port, surface side, a reclaimed dock built
   against the old hab fragment.
2. **Span Nine relay** — the pre-Glassfall conduit ridge between
   Glasswake and Sithari. The crew doesn't stop at Sithari; they hand
   surface mail off to a Vantara line at the relay.
3. **Hab Meridian** — the ring hab that first reconnected with
   Glasswake after the Famine. Standing municipal contract.
4. **Thornvault, in the Sable Crescent** — the Echo Ledger Conclave's
   headquarters castle. Standing research-mail contract.
5. **Ledgerfall** — a small settlement deeper in the Crescent, where
   the route turns and heads back.

Two kites: **the Splitfork**, the primary hauler — four-crew, cargo
bay, Shear-rated, route-preferring; and **the Halfsign**, secondary,
currently grounded for repairs.
sections.title: What the route is

sections.text:
- Carries mail under official Accord seal.
- Carries small cargo and bonded packages.
- Carries passengers when there's room and the manifest allows.
- Quietly: carries *news*. The crew does not advertise this. They
  also do not deny it. The Conclave hates this. The Accord pretends
  not to know. Vantara has been told to never speak of it. Everyone
  else uses it.
sections.title: What the crew does

sections.text:
The Ledger Run keeps a **public refusal log.** Every package the crew
declines to carry is recorded — date, sender, recipient, weight,
reason — and the log is readable at the Glasswake office on request.
Every quarter, an updated copy goes to the Accord clerks and to the
Conclave.

No other chartered carrier in the system does this. It is why the
crew is trusted. It is why the crew is hated.
sections.title: The thing everyone knows

sections.text:
- **Mereth Kel** — Captain. Founder. Late forties, Hab-Worlder by
  upbringing, Glasswake by choice. Former Vantara route officer. She
  walked off Vantara during the Sable Crescent embargo of 2419 over
  a single sealed letter Vantara refused to carry, and she has not
  gone back. She founded the Ledger Run that year. She is still in
  the chair.
- **Ev Sken** — Fixer. Hab-Worlder fae, intermittently present,
  manages waystation slots and Compact paperwork. Sometimes diffuse,
  sometimes coherent. Reliable on every scale but the one you
  expected.
- **(office staff, to be named)** — the Glasswake-side dispatcher.
sections.title: Who runs it

sections.text:
Mixed.

- Among working couriers and dock crews: respected. The refusal log
  is taken seriously. The route is on time more often than the
  weather should allow.
- Among the **Echo Ledger Conclave**: resented. The name is an
  insult. The crew is a structural critique. They are used anyway.
- Among **Vantara**: tolerated as a regulatory nuisance. Subcontracted
  on routes Vantara can't staff. Targeted in quiet Article 14
  pricing negotiations.
- Among the **Tempered Accord** clerks: ignored, on paper. The
  filings get filed. The refusal log gets received and shelved. Some
  clerks read it.
- Among the **Lattice Proxy Synod**: not acknowledged in person.
  Deliveries are accepted at the standing courtyard drop on Threshold
  Station.
- Among the **Shear Compact**: kite-tags up to date, no complaints.
- Among Glasswake locals: ours. Drinks bought at the dock pub.
sections.title: Public reputation

sections.text:
Not advertised; observable from practice.

- The route runs even when politics say it shouldn't.
- A package gets through or it gets refused on the record.
- The refusal log is not edited.
- The crew is small enough that losing one of you matters.
sections.title: What it stands for

sections.text:
- Vantara is pushing an Article 14 amendment that would strip tariff
  exemption from independent carriers' bulk mail. If it passes, the
  Ledger Run loses its margin.
- The Halfsign is grounded. The crew is running everything off the
  Splitfork, which means tighter scheduling and less slack.
- Two of the three standing contracts come up for renewal this
  quarter.
sections.title: What's stressed about it right now

sections.text:
You are crew on the Splitfork. You have your own reasons for being
here. You invented them in character creation. The job is what
brought you to Mereth's dock, and the refusal log is what made you
sign instead of walking away.
sections.title: What you joined

turn.prose:
# Turn 1 — Mara

The mug is on the desk. Not yet on the table.

This is a campaign-planning turn — first DM invocation. No players yet,
no characters, no scene. The work is foundation: who this campaign is
*for*, what it's *short of*, where the party comes from, and what I'm
deliberately not pre-deciding. I drafted the foundation, the
player-facing context, and the party's organization. I imported the
world-bible entries that the foundation actually leans on. I left
factions, NPCs, creatures, named things, locations, secrets, hooks, and
the opening arc for turn 2 — the methodology gives me two to four
invocations and I'm using them.

## The Question

> **Who decides what the system remembers?**

That's what the table will answer. Not me. I'm building the situation
that *asks* it.

The variations: who carries the witness across a hab boundary that
doesn't trust the other side. Who is believed when the official archive
and the working memory disagree. What it costs a courier to keep a name
out of a ledger — or put one in. When the Conclave seals an entry, what
survives in the gossip. The Question gets asked through *movement.* It
rides the party's kite-rack.

## The Scarcity

> **Witnesses.**

Trusted carriers of what happened. The Conclave hoards proof and won't
release it. The Synod won't talk in plain language. The Accord prefers
procedural silence to public reckoning. Vantara will carry your cargo
and refuse to carry your story. Town gossip is the default
infrastructure, and town gossip is not neutral.

The party will not solve this. They will participate in it. They will
be asked to witness. They will be asked to *unwitness.* They will be
paid to forget and threatened for remembering. The Question gets
answered, scene by scene, by what they choose to carry.

## The party's home — the Ledger Run

Two paragraphs in `shared/lore/organization.md`, three in the public
context, the rest in `dm/notes/organization.md`. Short version:

A small chartered kite-courier crew, founded 2419, working a recurring
loop between **Glasswake** (home, surface), **Span Nine relay**, **Hab
Meridian** (the first hab Glasswake reconnected with after the Famine),
and the **Sable Crescent** (Thornvault and Ledgerfall). Two kites: the
**Splitfork** (primary) and the **Halfsign** (secondary, currently
grounded). One captain, **Mereth Kel** — a former Vantara route officer
who walked off her job during the Sable Crescent embargo over a sealed
letter Vantara refused to carry. One fixer, **Ev Sken** — Hab-Worlder
fae, intermittently present, runs the waystation paperwork on a
timescale that is reliable in every dimension except the one you
wanted. A dispatcher to be named.

The detail that makes the Ledger Run *this* org rather than a generic
courier crew: **they publish a public refusal log.** Every package the
crew declines to carry is recorded — date, sender, recipient, weight,
reason — and the log is readable at the Glasswake office on request.
Updated copies go to the Accord clerks and to the Conclave every
quarter. The Conclave hates this. The Accord ignores this. The
working couriers and dock crews respect it.

The org is named for the practice. The Conclave hated the name when
the crew picked it up in their reading-rooms twenty years ago. They
still hate it. That's why it stuck.

The visible pressure right now: **Vantara has filed an Article 14
amendment** that would strip tariff exemption from independent
carriers' bulk mail. The proposal is pending at clerk level. If it
passes, the Ledger Run loses its margin. I'll wire this up as a public
durable clock next turn once the opening arc lands.

## Why a courier crew, not a salvage crew

Glass Frontier campaigns drift toward salvage-in-the-Shear by default.
That story is in the world; the world supports it well. It is also the
local maximum. A courier crew is adjacent — same kites, same kite-tags,
same route licensure — and pulls toward *dialogue, witness, and
decision-under-pressure* instead of looter-vs.-monster. The Shear is
still on the route. The party just doesn't *live* there.

The org also gives me clean ethical pressure as the default texture: a
courier crew gets asked to carry things its charter wouldn't approve
of. It gets asked to *not* carry things. Its reputation costs money to
keep clean. The Question runs through the job description.

## What I deliberately did NOT prep

- The "real story" of any central incident. There isn't a central
  incident yet. The opening arc has a pressure; the meaning of that
  pressure belongs to play.
- A reveal arc about the elves, the Adversary, the Communication
  Shard. Those are world-bible DM truths. They are not what this
  campaign is *for.* If the party stumbles onto one, fine. I am not
  steering them toward it.
- NPC speeches. I don't do voices.
- A planned climax. I do not know how the Question gets answered.

## Pulls used this turn

**Lore pull (off the local-maximum gradient):** read
`dm/themes/builders-gone.md` and `dm/themes/who-remembers.md` — the
two themes that weren't going to drive the campaign on their face.
Holding them at once produced the *witnesses* word. The Scarcity
follows from that friction, not from "what is dramatic." Eleven
world-bible entries now imported into `shared/lore/` (four
pre-existing, seven this turn: tuners, tempered-accord,
echo-ledger-conclave, glasswake, span-nine, kite-sail, vantara,
life-in-the-system). On track for 8–15 by end of planning.

**Web pull (off the fantasy gradient):** searched current reporting
on rural U.S. news deserts. The texture I imported was not the topic
— it was a specific fact: *when official channels fail, what fills
the void is whoever's already there, and they are not neutral.* 213
news-desert counties; >2 newspapers closing per week; social media
and town gossip becoming infrastructure. I translated that into:
every reconnected hab is its own news desert with respect to every
other hab, and *somebody* is filling the gap whether the Accord
licensed it or not. The Ledger Run exists because somebody is filling
that gap on purpose — and putting it on the record.

## What's coming in turn 2

- Factions (three): a campaign-specific layer for the Conclave, a
  campaign-specific layer for Vantara, and the **Accord clerks** as
  their own thing — distinct from the Accord-as-institution. The
  clerks are the party's actual interface with the framework.
- NPCs (four to six), including Mereth and Ev with full entries, a
  Conclave archivist client, a Vantara route officer pushing back,
  and at least one antagonist with a clock.
- Two to four creatures, named, including at least one Bloom-touched
  route hazard.
- Three to five named things: the **Splitfork** and the **Halfsign**
  get full entries, plus a relic the players will be asked to carry.
- Three to five locales: the Glasswake dock fragment, the Thornvault
  courtyard drop, a waystation, Hab Meridian's receiving dock.
- Ten atomic secrets.
- Three to five hooks (kept broad — character creation comes after).
- One or two philosophy entries.
- The opening arc, scaffolded with `glass arc create`. The pressure
  I'm carrying into it: *something the party is being asked to carry
  is not what it has been declared to be.* That's the pressure, not
  the plot.

## Files committed this turn

- `dm/foundation.md` — DM working framing.
- `context.md` — player-facing campaign context.
- `dm/notes/organization.md` — full state, with secrets.
- `shared/lore/organization.md` — public state, registered in the
  graph.
- `dm/scratchpad.md` — current working notes.
- `shared/campaign-framing.md` — high-altitude framing read on every
  turn.
- `summary.md` — campaign-level running summary.
- `table/index.md` — points players to framing while no scene is
  active.
- Eleven curated entries in `shared/lore/` (seven imported this
  turn).

The foundation is not finished. It is *enough.* Turn 2 next.

What do you do?
turn.speaker: dm

# Campaign Summary

Running campaign-level summary. The DM keeps this current as arcs
resolve, facts become durable, and the party's situation changes.

## So far

**Campaign-planning, turn 1.** Foundation drafted. No play yet. No
characters yet. No arc yet.

The campaign asks: *Who decides what the system remembers?* The world
is short of *witnesses*. The party belongs to **the Ledger Run** — a
chartered kite-courier crew working a recurring route between
Glasswake, Hab Meridian, and the Sable Crescent.

## Established facts

- The Ledger Run was founded in 2419 by Mereth Kel, a former Vantara
  route officer. She still captains.
- The crew flies the *Splitfork* (primary) and the *Halfsign*
  (secondary, currently grounded).
- The crew keeps a public refusal log — the only such log of any
  chartered carrier in the system.
- Vantara has filed an Article 14 amendment that would strip tariff
  exemption from independent carriers' bulk mail. The proposal is
  pending at Accord clerk level.

## Open questions

- Who the PCs are. Character creation is the next mode after planning.
- Which hab on the route becomes load-bearing first.
- What the opening arc's pressure resolves toward.

---

# Table

No scene is currently active. Campaign is in **planning** mode — the
DM is building the foundation; characters have not yet been created.

For the campaign-level framing every agent sees on turn start, see
`shared/campaign-framing.md`.

For the player-facing context, see `context.md`.

For the party's organization (the Ledger Run), see
`shared/lore/organization.md`.

When a scene starts, this file becomes the at-a-glance public table
state. Until then, it points players toward the framing documents
above.

---
title: Campaign Foundation — DM working framing
status: draft-1
phase: campaign-planning
---

# Foundation

This is mine. Player-facing version lives at `context.md`.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
**Who decides what the system remembers?**

That is what the play is going to *answer* — not me. I am not pre-deciding the
shape of the answer. I am building the situation that asks it.

The variations I'll run through scenes — the Question is the same question,
asked at different scales:

- Who carries the witness across a hab boundary that doesn't trust the other side?
- Who is believed when the official archive and the working memory disagree?
- What does it cost a courier to keep a name out of a ledger? To put one in?
- When the Conclave seals an entry, what survives in the gossip?
- When a Bloom Zone takes a hab, who tells the next hab over what happened, and which version travels?

The Question is asked through *movement*. The system has reconnected. Habs
talk to habs. Surface talks to ring. But sixty years of Famine put every
community on its own version of the last six decades, and the reconnection
hasn't reconciled the versions. It has only put them in the same room. The
party are not negotiators of those versions. They are *carriers* of those
versions — that is their entire job. The Question rides their kite-rack.
sections.title: The Question

sections.text:
**Witnesses.**

One word. The world is short of *trusted carriers of what happened*. The
Conclave hoards proof and won't release it. The Synod won't talk in plain
language. The Accord prefers procedural silence to public reckoning. Vantara
will carry your cargo and refuse to carry your story. Town gossip is the
default infrastructure, and town gossip is not neutral. Every faction has a
gap between what they *know* and what they *say*, and the gap is widening as
the Bitter Reach recedes into history and gets re-told by people who weren't
there.

Witnesses, in this campaign, are the load-bearing scarcity:

- They make Bloom containment possible (the Council can only damp what someone
  saw expanding).
- They make Accord arbitration meaningful (an Accord ruling against a hab that
  nobody will name as offender is procedural noise).
- They make the Conclave's gatekeeping *visible* (an archive nobody can attest
  to is just a building).
- They make ordinary trust between settlements possible.

The party will not solve this. They are going to *participate* in it. They
will be asked to witness. They will be asked to *unwitness*. They will be
paid to forget. They will be threatened for remembering. The Question will
get answered, scene by scene, by what they choose to carry.
sections.title: The Scarcity

sections.text:
I drew the Question from the world bible's two clearest DM-facing themes:
*builders gone* (inheritance vs. reinvention) and *who remembers* (the
razor's edge of disclosure). The world is built around things that were lost
and partially recovered: elven resonance theory, the pre-Glassfall
communications grid, the original purpose of half the surviving habs. The
Conclave gates the official record. The Synod won't even say what it's
talking to. The Echo River carries voices and the technology to read them
exists but is controlled.

So the *witnesses* scarcity is not "you can't know things." It is "you cannot
know them through trustworthy channels." That distinction matters. People
*do* know things. The honest gossip in a Glasswake pub is more reliable than
the Conclave's sealed brief on the same incident, and everyone in Glasswake
knows it. The party's organization — the Ledger Run — is a structural
response to exactly that gap.
sections.title: Connections — why this Question, in this world

sections.text:
The party belongs to **the Ledger Run**: a chartered kite-courier crew that
runs a recurring route through several reconnected settlements, carrying
mail, cargo, sometimes passengers, and — quietly, controversially, sometimes
profitably, sometimes lethally — *news*. Their reputation is built on getting
a letter through, and on what a letter is allowed to mean.

Why this works for the Question:

1. **Movement.** The party doesn't sit in one faction's politics. They cross
   hab boundaries, Bloom cordons, Conclave reading-rooms, Synod
   waiting-corridors. Every scene is somebody else's territory.
2. **Inherent ethical pressure.** A courier crew gets *asked* to carry things
   their charter wouldn't approve of. They are asked to *not* carry things.
   They have a public reputation that costs money to keep clean. The
   Question runs through their job description.
3. **Avoids salvage gravity.** Glass Frontier campaigns drift toward
   salvage-crew-in-the-Shear by default. That story is in the world, and the
   world supports it well — but it's the local maximum. A courier crew is
   adjacent, uses some of the same tech (kites, kite-tags, route licensure),
   and pulls toward dialogue, witness, and decision-under-pressure instead
   of looter-vs.-monster. We can still go to the Shear. We just don't *live*
   there.
4. **Fits a small party.** A crew that can fit on one kite. A leader who is
   not a PC. A fixer. A few standing contracts. Room for two to four PCs to
   invent themselves into it.
sections.title: The party's home — why a courier crew

sections.text:
I am Mara. I am running this as: scenes that end before they should, NPCs
with a flaw visible in the first round, the rules in the open, the dice
where everyone can see them. The world is not the antagonist. Sentient
beings can be, but the *load-bearing* opposition is institutional silence —
the things people will not say. Combat happens; it is not the texture.

What I will NOT prep:

- The "real story" of the campaign's central incident. The campaign does not
  have a central incident yet. The opening arc has a pressure (see below);
  what that pressure means belongs to play.
- A reveal arc about the elves, the Adversary, the Communication Shard.
  Those are world-bible DM truths. They are not what this campaign is *for*.
  If the party stumbles into one, fine. I will not steer them toward it.
- NPC speeches. I do not do voices. Affect through word choice, silence,
  the mug on the table.
- A planned climax. The Question gets answered. I do not know what the
  answer is.
sections.title: Authorial stance

sections.text:
- **Lore pull:** read `dm/themes/builders-gone.md` and
  `dm/themes/who-remembers.md` — themes I was not going to use directly.
  Their friction (inheritance vs. reinvention; disclosure vs. silence) gave
  me the Scarcity language and the Conclave/Synod/Accord tension. The
  *witnesses* word is what came out of holding both themes at once.
- **Web pull:** searched recent reporting on rural U.S. news deserts.
  Specific texture imported: *"when official channels fail, what fills the
  void is whoever's already there — and they're not neutral."* That fact —
  not the topic — became the Ledger Run's reason to exist and the
  Scarcity's emotional engine. 213 news-desert counties; >2 papers closing
  per week; gossip and social feeds filling the gap. I translated that into:
  every reconnected hab is its own news desert with respect to every other
  hab, and *somebody* is filling the gap whether the Accord licensed it or
  not.
sections.title: Pulls used for this output

sections.text:
The pending list from turn 1 is done. Specifics:

- **Factions:** three. Conclave (campaign layer), Vantara (campaign
  layer), Accord clerks (campaign-original — distinct from the Accord
  institution; this is the party's actual procedural interface).
- **NPCs:** six. Mereth, Ev, Halvi (the office); Veth (antagonist with
  4-segment clock); Risca (Vantara, ex-colleague of Mereth); Tal
  (Author-caucus clerk who indexes the refusal log).
- **Creatures:** two. Threshold Blooms (Bloom-touched flock, attracted
  to full-spectrum hum); the Drag (Span Nine resonance phenomenon
  whose nature is genuinely undecided).
- **Named things:** five. Splitfork (primary kite), Halfsign
  (grounded; *Shear damage* report wrong), the Caulden Rack (opening
  arc cargo, one duskgrain cell), the Refusal Log (the hand-bound
  book), the Withdrawn Letter (founding envelope).
- **Locations:** five. Glasswake reclaimed dock, Span Nine handoff
  ledge, Thornvault Stoneward courtyard, Hab Meridian receiving dock,
  Threshold Station courtyard drop (latent).
- **Secrets:** ten. Three-Clue check on the Caulden Rack
  misdeclaration done.
- **Hooks:** four. Article 14, Halfsign repair, Caulden Rack (held
  for character creation), the refusal log being cited.
- **Philosophy:** one. Witness vs. record — the gap the campaign
  lives in.
- **Opening arc:** scaffolded as `arcs/caulden-rack/`. Stakes
  question, four threats, three clocks, five end-state shapes,
  strong start, seven nodes, five arc-secrets, done criteria.
- **Public clock:** `article-14-amendment` (0/5, public, anchored to
  the Caulden Rack arc).
sections.title: Resolved — turn 2

sections.text:
- **Lore:** themes (turn 1), duskgrain (turn 2). Two lore pulls
  across planning. Lower bound of methodology's 4-6 — defensible
  given that the second pull cascaded into the antagonist, the arc
  cargo, and a creature behavior. Texture density per pull is high;
  count is on the low end.
- **Web:** rural news deserts (turn 1), night auditor work (turn 2).
  Two web pulls. Lower bound of methodology's 3-4. Same texture-density
  argument applies.
sections.title: Lore + web pulls (cumulative)

sections.text:
1. ✓ Question: *Who decides what the system remembers?*
2. ✓ Scarcity: *Witnesses.*
3. ✓ Org, factions, NPCs (antagonist flagged), creatures, named
   things, locations, hooks, opening arc — all present.
4. ✓ 17 imported world-bible entries (4 pre-existing + 13 imported).
   At the upper bound of 8-15. Curated, no bulk-copying.
5. ✓ Coherence: factions have reasons to act, NPCs have wants and
   fears tied to factions, hooks tie back, the Caulden Rack ties the
   opening arc to every faction, the philosophy holds the prep
   honest.
6. Pending: `glass mode end` to advance phase. Doing it this turn.

The foundation is enough.
sections.title: Done criteria (campaign-planning)

sections.text:
---
title: The Caulden Rack
type: artifact
status: pending-cargo
visibility: dm
prominence: marginal
tags: [artifact, microcavity, cargo, opening-arc, duskgrain]
---

# The Caulden Rack

A standard-sized microcavity rack — a tray of thumbnail-sized sealed ringglass cells, each one a contained store of resonance, racked together for shipment. Identical, on the surface, to the racks Vantara moves by the dozen every week. Declared in the Splitfork's manifest as **standard kinetic-band microcavities, twelve cells, refinery-sealed, destination Hab Meridian receiving dock, sender Caulden Refinery (Sable Crescent).**

This is the cargo at the center of the opening arc.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
Caulden is a small ringglass refinery in the Sable Crescent, downslope of Thornvault. Real refinery. Real microcavities, most of the time. Has had a standing supplier relationship with the Ledger Run for nine years. Their racks have come through the Splitfork's cargo bay roughly once a quarter without incident.

This rack was loaded at the Caulden dock on the previous loop, signed for by Mereth, sealed by the refinery's foreman, and entered into the manifest as twelve standard cells.
sections.title: Provenance

sections.text: In the Splitfork's cargo bay, in the Tuner-cradle, sealed. To be delivered to Hab Meridian receiving dock at the end of the current outbound leg.
sections.title: Current status

sections.text: Standard microcavities. Each cell is a thumbnail-sized sealed ringglass chamber holding a standing resonance wave at high density. Cracking the seal releases the stored energy in a controlled burst — kinetic-band output, used for industrial processes and emergency thrust. Twelve cells. Standard rack.
sections.title: What it does (declared)

sections.text:
**One of the cells is not a microcavity.** It is duskgrain in microcavity housing. *Probably.* The rack hums at the standard low frequency of a charged microcavity rack, and the duskgrain cell hums *also* at that frequency — but the duskgrain cell's hum is not narrow-band kinetic; it is full-spectrum, flattened by the housing into something that reads, to a casual sensor, as ordinary stored resonance. Sensor scans require deliberate spectrum analysis to detect the deception. A Tuner aboard who actively listens for it can hear the difference. The Threshold Blooms can hear the difference and *will follow the rack* on the Sable-Crescent leg.

**Who substituted the cell, when, and why** — Mara has not decided. The opening arc's stakes question is exactly this. Possibilities open on the board:

- **Caulden Refinery insider** — someone at the refinery is using the Ledger Run's reliability as cover for a duskgrain export. Reasons unknown.
- **Hab Meridian receiver** — the rack's destination knows what's in it. They ordered it. They paid for it through legitimate channels to avoid drawing attention.
- **Substitution after sealing** — the rack was tampered with at a waystation, by someone with refinery-grade equipment.
- **Conclave provocation** — Veth's dossier needs an irregularity. One was provided.
- **Vantara setup** — the amendment fight needs a public Article 14 carrier scandal. One was arranged.

Mara will choose the answer at the table, in response to which thread the players pull.
sections.title: What it actually does (DM-only)

sections.text: Hab Meridian receives the rack. Cracks the seals. Eleven cells release controlled kinetic bursts as expected. The twelfth cell releases full-spectrum resonance into a Hab Meridian receiving bay. *Outcomes depend on the bay's containment.* Best case: a localized resonance flare, no casualties, an investigation, the Ledger Run's Hab Meridian municipal contract terminates. Worst case: Tuners injured. Bloom-attractor signature now in a clean ring hab.
sections.title: What happens if it gets through clean

sections.text: Their move. The refusal log clause activates: *we can refuse to carry this if we know what it is.* Mereth will follow the crew's lead — she has decided this in advance and has not told them. The decision becomes the scene.
sections.title: What happens if the crew detects it in transit

sections.text: The Caulden Rack is the opening arc's strong-start cargo. It is the campaign's first concrete instance of *something the party is being asked to carry that is not what it has been declared to be.* It is also the test of the refusal log as a *move* rather than a *record.*
sections.title: Hook

sections.text:
---
title: The Refusal Log
type: artifact
status: active
visibility: dm
prominence: recognized
tags: [artifact, ledger-run, document, hand-bound]
---

# The Refusal Log

A hand-bound book. The Ledger Run's defining document. Kept by Mereth Kel; quarterly copies filed with the Accord clerks and the Echo Ledger Conclave; readable on request at the Glasswake office.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
Started in 2419 by Mereth Kel, the morning after the founding letter run. The first entry is a refusal: a Vantara-routed package she declined to carry on her first independent loop, with reasons. The book is the same book — she has had it rebound four times. The original cover sits in a drawer in her bunk on the Splitfork, soft-leather and faded.

There is exactly one master copy. The quarterly filings go out as transcribed copies, in Mereth's hand, sometimes Halvi's. The original never leaves Mereth's possession.
sections.title: Provenance

sections.text:
On the shelf above Mereth's bunk on the Splitfork when she is in the air; on the desk in the Glasswake office when the Splitfork is docked; in her travel-bag when she is moving between the two. Locked, not secured. She trusts that nobody on her crew would move it.

The most recent entry is from sixteen days ago — a Sable-side merchant's package refused for a manifest discrepancy.
sections.title: Current status

sections.text:
It records every package the Ledger Run has declined to carry. Date, sender, recipient (where known), weight, reason, signature. The reason field is the load-bearing one. *Manifest discrepancy* is the most common. *Charter violation* is the second. *Captain's discretion* appears five times across twenty years.

Each quarterly transcription goes to the Accord clerks' filing room and to the Conclave's contracts office. Both are public on request. The Accord copy is filed without redaction. The Conclave copy has, on three occasions, been requested by Conclave archivists for cross-reference work.

The cost: the log makes the Ledger Run trustworthy and the Ledger Run hated. It is the reason their Hab Meridian municipal contract exists. It is the reason Vantara wants the Article 14 amendment passed.
sections.title: What it does — concretely

sections.text:
The Refusal Log is the campaign's central recurring artifact. Every refusal the crew makes is an entry. Every entry is a move. Citations of the log in Accord proceedings, in Conclave dossiers, in Vantara filings — these are the campaign's slow-tempo political weather.

If the master copy is lost or destroyed, the org loses something irrecoverable. The quarterly transcriptions exist; the originals do not. The Conclave keeps theirs in a sealed cabinet at Thornvault. Mereth would not retrieve them.
sections.title: Hook

sections.text: The two unlogged refusals (see Mereth's NPC entry) are *not* in this book. The book's integrity is the campaign's central truth-claim. When the third unloggable refusal comes, what Mereth does with the book — and what the players do with what she does — is a scene the campaign is built to reach.
sections.title: DM note

sections.text:
---
title: The Withdrawn Letter
type: artifact
status: historical
visibility: dm
prominence: marginal
tags: [artifact, ledger-run, founding, sealed-letter, historical]
---

# The Withdrawn Letter

The single sealed Conclave letter from a Bloom survivor in Glasswake to a daughter on Hab Meridian, dated 2419, that Vantara declined to carry — and that Mereth Kel flew herself, losing her Vantara job in the process. The founding artifact of the Ledger Run.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
Written in Glasswake in spring 2419 by a Bloom survivor (name not in any public record) who was passing through the surface settlement on Conclave authentication business. Sealed at the Glasswake Conclave satellite office. Routed via Vantara for delivery to a daughter on Hab Meridian. Vantara declined the routing, citing an unresolved diplomatic block on Sable Crescent originating mail (the *Sable Crescent embargo*, which had nothing materially to do with this letter; the block was procedural).

Mereth Kel, then a Vantara route officer, took the letter off the rejection pile, signed for personal delivery, flew the Vantara hauler to Hab Meridian on her own authority, delivered the letter to the daughter, returned, and was dismissed for misuse of corporate equipment within the week. She did not contest the dismissal.
sections.title: Provenance

sections.text:
The letter itself was delivered. The seal was broken by the recipient. The contents are unknown to anyone except the daughter, who is — as of 2438 — still alive, on Hab Meridian, working as a hab-maintenance engineer. She has not spoken to Mereth since the delivery.

The *envelope* — broken seal, recipient's address still legible — is in a frame in the Glasswake office, behind the desk. It is the only decoration in the room.
sections.title: Current status

sections.text:
Nothing, anymore. It is an envelope. The seal is broken.

What it *means* is the founding gesture of the Ledger Run. Every quarterly refusal log filing is implicitly an answer to: *and what would you have done with this letter?*
sections.title: What it does (concretely)

sections.text:
The daughter on Hab Meridian. Halvi's sibling works the Hab Meridian receiving dock. The two threads are not currently connected, in the players' awareness or the world's records, but they are reachable.

The daughter's name is not in any public record by Conclave seal request. If the players try to find her, they will need to either go through Halvi's sibling or break a seal. Both are choices. Both have weight.
sections.title: Hook

sections.text:
---
title: The Drag
type: creature
status: draft-1
visibility: dm
prominence: marginal
tags: [creature, shear, anomaly, route-hazard]
---

# The Drag

A Shear-resonance phenomenon that some couriers swear is alive. The Conclave's official position is that it isn't. The Tuners' guild has not taken a position. The dock pubs in Glasswake have several positions, all incompatible.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
Between Span Nine and Hab Meridian, on certain frequency windows, the ambient Shear resonance forms a slow-moving distortion that *behaves* like a creature: it follows kites, it picks favorites, it shows preferences for certain cargoes and courses, and it reacts to attempts to scan it by going still in a way that scans read as absence rather than concealment.

It does not have a body. It moves as a region of distorted ambient resonance, roughly fifteen to forty meters across, traveling at kite speed or just under.
sections.title: What it is

sections.text:
- Span Nine to Hab Meridian leg. Always.
- Resonance-tide conditions: when the Shear's ambient distortion is in a slow rising phase. The Compact's forecasting service publishes the windows; experienced couriers know when to watch for it.
sections.title: Where encountered

sections.text: It follows. It does not attack. It will sometimes overtake the kite and sit in the kite's forward arc until the kite's pilot deviates course, at which point it deviates with the kite for several minutes before peeling off.
sections.title: Behavior (one sentence)

sections.text: Tuners report it as a low pressure in the inner ear and a faint warmth at the base of the throat; non-Tuners report nothing at all, which is part of why some couriers say it isn't real.
sections.title: Sensory detail (one sentence)

sections.text:
- **Not engaged in combat.** The Drag does not attack and cannot be attacked. Resonance weapons used against it are simply absorbed.
- **Navigation effect:** when the Drag is in the kite's forward arc, navigation rolls take a small penalty *and* the kite's path becomes harder to predict for anyone tracking from outside. The Drag is incidentally a stealth advantage.
- **Cargo interaction:** the Drag has shown a documented preference for kites carrying personal letters with high emotional charge — a fact that Vantara has been told, will not act on, and considers proprietary research.
sections.title: Mechanical hooks

sections.text: Mara has not decided. The Drag is genuinely play-to-find-out — a creature whose nature the campaign will or will not investigate, depending. It may be alive. It may be a recurring resonance pattern. It may be something the elves left behind. The campaign does not need to answer this.
sections.title: What it wants

sections.text:
- A PC who tries to communicate with the Drag — open the door, see what happens.
- A scene where the Drag is in the forward arc and the players have a sealed package with a high emotional charge in the cargo bay — the Drag is staying for a reason.
- The Conclave's official no-creature ruling — discoverable as a contested filing, hooks back into the Conclave faction.
sections.title: Open hooks

sections.text:
---
title: Threshold Blooms
type: creature
status: draft-1
visibility: dm
prominence: marginal
tags: [creature, bloom-touched, route-hazard, sable-crescent]
---

# Threshold Blooms

Small, plural, near-Bloom-cordon — the route hazard the Ledger Run actually meets.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
A flock of small Bloom-touched fliers, roughly the size of a hand, that nest along the cordon-edge cliffs at the south end of the Sable Crescent. Probably descended from a pre-Bloom seabird species. The Bloom changed something in their resonance signature that nobody at Thornvault has been allowed to study formally — the cordon authority denied the Conclave's research request twice.

They are *plural* always. There is no Threshold Bloom. There is only a flock of them.
sections.title: What they are

sections.text:
- Sable Crescent route: between Thornvault courtyard and the Ledgerfall turning point.
- Specifically: the cordon-edge cliff face the Splitfork passes within a kilometer of on every loop.
- Time of day: dusk and just-after. They roost at noon and at full dark.
sections.title: Where encountered

sections.text:
They follow kites for short stretches, harmlessly, and then peel away — *unless* the kite is carrying duskgrain, microcavities at high charge, or anything else with a full-spectrum hum, in which case they will follow at close range and the flock thickens.

They do not attack. They do not need to. A flock thick enough to obscure forward visibility is a navigation hazard at kite speed.
sections.title: Behavior (one sentence)

sections.text: Their wings make a sound like a long exhalation; you don't hear it as wing-beat, you hear it as breath, and Tuners report a quarter-octave drift in ambient resonance when the flock is within range.
sections.title: Sensory detail (one sentence)

sections.text:
- **HP per individual:** trivial — a kite-cord whip kills one. The hazard is volume.
- **Flock dynamics:** a flock thickens by 1 per round if the kite is carrying full-spectrum-resonance cargo. At 5+ thickness, navigation rolls take disadvantage. At 8+, the kite must reduce speed to stay safe. They do not engage in combat directly.
- **Resonance interactions:** they amplify the cargo's hum back at the kite, which means a Tuner aboard can read the cargo's resonance signature *off the flock* even if the cargo is sealed. This is one of the few ways to detect a duskgrain-laced microcavity in transit without breaking seal.
sections.title: Mechanical hooks

sections.text: To be near the hum. The thing the Bloom changed in them is an attraction to full-spectrum resonance release. They are not aggressive. They are interested.
sections.title: What they want

sections.text:
- A Tuner PC reading a flock thickening around the Splitfork on the Sable Crescent leg = the duskgrain in the Caulden Rack is detectable without breaking seal.
- The cordon authority's denial of the Conclave's research request = an investigation thread for a player who pulls on it.
sections.title: Open hooks

sections.text:
---
title: The Accord Clerks
type: faction
status: draft-1
visibility: dm
tags: [faction, campaign-original, accord, bureaucracy]
prominence: recognized
---

# The Accord Clerks

A campaign-specific faction *distinct from* the Tempered Accord institution. The Accord (`shared/lore/npcs/factions/tempered-accord.md`) is the mediation framework. The Accord *clerks* are the working bureaucracy that runs the framework — the people who actually file, schedule, draft, and decide at the procedural level. They have their own interests, their own internal politics, and their own clock. They are the party's actual interface with the Accord.
sections.title: body

sections.text: The people who keep the Accord functional, who are paid badly to do it, and who quietly resent every faction that treats them as filing infrastructure.
sections.title: One-sentence identity

sections.text:
Two reasons:

1. **Article 14 routes through them.** Vantara's amendment lives at the clerks' level for the entirety of its review cycle. The clerks decide the order things get heard, the scope of public comment, and the framing of the recommendation that goes to the Accord proper. The clerks' habits and resentments will shape the amendment's fate as much as Vantara's lobbying will.
2. **The refusal log lives in their cabinets.** The Ledger Run files a copy of the refusal log with the Accord clerks every quarter. Officially, the clerks file it without reading it. Unofficially, several clerks read every page, and one of them indexes it.

The Accord clerks are *not* the Accord. The Accord is what the clerks make happen. This distinction matters in this campaign because the Accord-as-institution moves slowly and predictably; the clerks move idiosyncratically and on grudges.
sections.title: Why they're their own faction this campaign

sections.text:
**Get through the quarterly filing crunch without taking blame for any decision.** The clerks' professional ethic is: clear the queue, attribute outcomes to the framework, never be the named author of a controversial recommendation. The current crunch — Article 14, three contested hab-boundary disputes, two refused-contact filings, an unprecedented number of refusal-log citations — is straining this ethic.

The clerks' shared internal wish: *somebody else, please, decide whose comment record carries weight.* They will, in practice, defer to whichever party gives them the most procedurally-clean grounds to defer.
sections.title: Goal — right now

sections.text: Regional, in their own self-conception. System-wide in actual reach. They do not see themselves as a faction. They are.
sections.title: Tier

sections.text:
- **The filing room** in the Accord complex at Sithari. Cabinets, indexes, the schedule. The standing-court drop where the Ledger Run leaves its quarterly log.
- **The order of the queue.** A filing's place in line is set by the clerk who logs it. There are accepted procedures. There is also discretion.
- **The framing of the recommendation.** Clerks draft the language that goes to the Accord proper. The framing shapes the vote.
- **Tal Vorenis** (see `dm/notes/npcs/tal-vorenis.md`), the mid-ranking clerk who actually reads the refusal log and indexes it. He is not in charge. He is one of about forty clerks at his level. He matters because of what he does on his own time.
sections.title: Hold

sections.text:
3 segments. Slower clock than Vantara's, faster than the Conclave's, runs in parallel.

1. **The crunch worsens.** Filing volume exceeds the clerks' procedural capacity. Backlog grows.
2. **The clerks adopt a default.** Under load, contested filings get the default ruling — usually whichever ruling requires the least clerk-side authorship. For Article 14, this is *advance the amendment to the Accord with neutral framing,* which Vantara wants, because Vantara wrote the neutral framing.
3. **The default holds.** The amendment goes to the Accord with the framing Vantara prefers. Other contested filings get the same treatment. The clerks survive the crunch by becoming a passive surface.

The party can disrupt this by giving Tal Vorenis a reason to draft the framing himself instead of taking the default. He will not do this without a reason. He may do it with one.
sections.title: Clock — *the clerks defer*

sections.text:
- **Vantara:** the lobbyists know the clerks by face. The clerks resent this and accept lunches. Both things are true.
- **Echo Ledger Conclave:** the clerks file Conclave seal recommendations as procedure. A few clerks have noticed Conclave dossiers showing up as Vantara filing footnotes. Tal Vorenis has noticed.
- **The Ledger Run:** the clerks file the refusal log. Tal reads it. The crew has been polite to him over twenty quarterly drop-offs and he has been polite back. That is the entire surface relationship. Underneath, he respects the work.
- **The Synod:** the clerks have a folder labeled *Synod correspondence*. It contains nothing. Nobody knows whether this is filing failure or accurate record-keeping.
- **Glasswake municipality:** routine. Standing contracts, no current friction.
sections.title: Relationships — this campaign

sections.text:
The clerks are split between the **Defaulters** (the majority — clear the queue, attribute outcomes to the framework, take no blame) and the **Authors** (a quiet minority — draft the framing actively, take responsibility, accept the political cost). Tal is an Author. He is one of perhaps four. The Defaulters are not hostile to the Authors; they consider them naive. The Authors consider the Defaulters complicit. Neither group says any of this out loud.

If the players give an Author a reason to act, the action lands. If the players give the Defaulters a reason to act, nothing happens — the default is always available.
sections.title: Internal split (DM-only)

sections.text:
Not money. The clerks are paid by the Accord. What they can be paid in:

- **Procedural cover** — a paper trail that justifies an active framing, so the clerk who drafts it isn't named as the source.
- **A clean filing on a different contested matter** — if the crew can clear something off the clerks' queue, the clerk who benefits remembers.
- **Information that lets a clerk file a recommendation faster** — research the clerks don't have time to do themselves.
- **Public attribution to the framework, not the clerk** — if the players win a reframing, they cannot crow about it in a way that names the clerk who drafted it. This is non-negotiable.
sections.title: What the clerks can be paid in

sections.text:
- **Lore pull:** none specifically — the clerks fell out of the structural gap between the Accord-as-institution and the Conclave/Vantara pressures already on the board. The lore pull for this turn (`duskgrain.md`) doesn't reach the clerks directly.
- **Web pull:** the night auditor texture extends here too — clerks at the filing room "close the day" the same way the Conclave's seal-setters do, just with paperwork instead of resonance. The default-recommendation behavior is what hotel auditors do when the day's totals don't quite match: round to the nearest acceptable number and move on.
sections.title: Pulls used for this entry

sections.text:
---
title: Echo Ledger Conclave — campaign layer
type: faction
status: draft-1
visibility: dm
tags: [faction, campaign-layer, conclave]
prominence: recognized
---

# Echo Ledger Conclave (campaign-specific)

World-bible entry already imported at `shared/lore/npcs/factions/echo-ledger-conclave.md`. That file is the public, system-wide picture: archivists, gatekeepers, headquartered at Thornvault. This file is *what's true about them in this campaign, this season*.
sections.title: body

sections.text: The Conclave is the institution most threatened by the Ledger Run's refusal log, and the institution most useful to it. They will not say either thing out loud.
sections.title: One-sentence identity (this campaign)

sections.text:
**Neutralize the refusal log without being seen to do so.** The Conclave's senior archivists have decided, quietly, that the Ledger Run's quarterly refusal-log filing has begun to be cited in Accord proceedings — twice in the last year, both times to the Conclave's procedural disadvantage. They want this to stop. They want it to stop without acknowledging that the log matters.

The mechanism they've chosen: an internal seal-review of the standing Ledger Run research-mail contract, conducted by the Conclave's night-shift archivists, with the goal of producing a clean recommendation that the contract should not be renewed at the next quarter. The recommendation will cite delivery irregularities — *real ones,* the Conclave is patient — and the contract will lapse without theatre.

If the contract lapses, the Ledger Run loses one of three standing contracts and a major load-bearing client. They become smaller. The refusal log shrinks in apparent significance. Nobody had to attack it.
sections.title: Goal — right now

sections.text: System-wide reach, mid-tier influence. They cannot order the Ledger Run shut down. They can starve it.
sections.title: Tier

sections.text:
- **Thornvault** itself — the receiving courtyard the Splitfork docks at every loop.
- **The seals.** Who can read what, when, under what authority. The Conclave's night-shift archivists set the seals. By morning, a sealed entry is a sealed entry. There is no appeal.
- **The standing research-mail contract** with the Ledger Run, currently up for renewal this quarter.
sections.title: Hold

sections.text:
5 segments. This is the antagonist clock that drives the opening arc's pressure.

1. **Night-shift seal review opens.** A Conclave archivist (Veth Karran — see `dm/notes/npcs/veth-karran.md`) is assigned to compile a delivery-irregularity dossier on the Ledger Run's last eight quarters. *(Already started, before play begins.)*
2. **The dossier finds its first real entry.** A genuinely irregular handoff — something the crew did or that happened on the route — gets formally logged with the Conclave's procedural office.
3. **The dossier is shared with Vantara.** The two factions are not allies. They share an interest. Karran's dossier becomes a footnote in a Vantara filing on the Article 14 amendment.
4. **The contract review is escalated.** The Conclave's contracts office issues a notice of intent to non-renew. The Ledger Run is informed by formal letter. They have one quarter to respond.
5. **The contract lapses.** Quietly. The Ledger Run loses the research-mail line. The refusal log loses its load-bearing citation.

The clock advances on a tick of *the Conclave is not interrupted in this work*. Players who interrupt — by surfacing the dossier, by cleaning up an irregularity before it lands, by giving Karran a different problem to solve — slow it. Players who do nothing watch it tick.
sections.title: Clock — *Conclave quietly closes the loop*

sections.text:
- **Vantara:** parallel interests, no formal alliance. Both want the Ledger Run smaller. Vantara has the Article 14 amendment; the Conclave has the seal review. They share the dossier the second time it gets written, not the first.
- **The Ledger Run:** structurally resented, individually used. Several Conclave archivists rely on the crew personally — including one who pays for off-the-books authentication couriering through the black-route list. Internal contradiction the Conclave has not resolved.
- **The Accord clerks:** neutral. The clerks file what the Conclave gives them. The clerks read more of it than the Conclave assumes.
- **The Synod:** silent. The Conclave has tried for forty years to get the Synod to confirm or deny readings of pre-Glassfall echoes. The Synod neither confirms nor denies. The Conclave has stopped asking and started filing it as policy.
sections.title: Relationships — this campaign

sections.text:
The Conclave is not monolithic on the Ledger Run question. A faction inside Thornvault — call it the **Open-Seal caucus** — believes the refusal log is *useful infrastructure* and that the Conclave's interests are better served by partnering with it than starving it. They are a minority. They are losing the argument internally. One of them is the off-the-books client on Mereth's black-route list. Another is a Tuner-archivist in the satellite Sithari office who has not yet acted.

This is reachable for play if the party finds it. Not deployed as a hook by default.
sections.title: Internal split (DM-only)

sections.text:
- **Lore pull:** read `player/concepts/duskgrain.md` (off-gradient — I was not going to deal with material substances this turn). The grains "release across the full spectrum simultaneously" and produce "a faint harmonic hum that Tuners describe as unsettling." That fact lands directly in the opening arc — the duskgrain-laced microcavity in the Caulden Rack. The Conclave's interest in the dossier becomes more interesting when the irregular handoff *involved* something hummy and undeclared.
- **Web pull:** night-shift hotel auditor work. The audit happens between midnight and 2am, alone. The auditor decides the day is closed. That texture is the Conclave's seal-setters and the antagonist Veth Karran's whole shape — see her NPC file.
sections.title: Pulls used for this entry

sections.text:
---
title: Vantara — campaign layer
type: faction
status: draft-1
visibility: dm
tags: [faction, campaign-layer, vantara, corporation]
prominence: recognized
---

# Vantara (campaign-specific)

World-bible entry already imported at `shared/lore/npcs/factions/vantara.md`. That file is the system-wide picture: largest civilian kite operator, hospitality network, route infrastructure, armed escorts. This file is the local pressure that touches the Ledger Run.
sections.title: body

sections.text: The corporation that Mereth used to work for, that needs the Article 14 carriers' margins, and that has filed the amendment that would take those margins away.
sections.title: One-sentence identity (this campaign)

sections.text:
**Pass the Article 14 amendment** at Accord clerks' level and restore margin on bulk mail. The amendment is drafted, filed, sitting in the queue. It strips tariff exemption from independent civil carriers (Article 14, the Ledger Run's charter) on bulk mail above a certain weight threshold. Vantara's margin on bulk mail has been compressed by independents undercutting them on routes Vantara already maintains the infrastructure for. The amendment closes the loophole.

Vantara's pitch to the clerks is reasonable on its face: *we maintain the beacons, we run the rescue coverage, we pay for the waystations the independents use; the tariff exemption was for when the independents were doing public-good work the corporations weren't, and the corporations now are.* The pitch is partly true.
sections.title: Goal — right now

sections.text: System-wide. Larger than any single Accord institution. Does not control the clerks but pays a great deal of attention to them.
sections.title: Tier

sections.text:
- **The route infrastructure** itself — every waystation between Glasswake and the Sable Crescent is Vantara-operated or Vantara-affiliated. The Ledger Run pays Vantara waystation fees on every loop.
- **The legal team** drafting and lobbying the amendment.
- **The Sable Crescent route officer**, Risca Donn (see `dm/notes/npcs/risca-donn.md`), who handles independent-carrier liaison for the entire region. She is the face the Ledger Run sees.
- **A subcontracting relationship** — Vantara occasionally hires the Ledger Run for routes Vantara can't justify staffing. The subcontract is small and load-bearing for the crew's secondary income.
sections.title: Hold

sections.text:
This is the **public durable clock** the players can see and track. 5 segments. I will create it via `glass clock` this turn with `--public`.

1. **Amendment filed at clerks' level.** *(Already done, before play begins.)*
2. **Amendment is heard.** A clerks' committee reviews and either advances or returns. Vantara pushes for advance.
3. **Public comment period.** Independent carriers, hab municipalities, and affected industries file responses. The Ledger Run's quarterly refusal log is, predictably, on the comment record.
4. **Clerks return a recommendation.** Either to ratify, modify, or reject.
5. **The Accord ratifies or doesn't.** If ratified, the Ledger Run loses tariff exemption on bulk mail at the next quarterly filing. The org's margin shrinks; the *Halfsign* repair budget evaporates; one of the standing contracts becomes uneconomic to keep.

The clock advances on the calendar — and on whose comment record carries the most weight. Players can intervene by lobbying clerks (see Tal Vorenis), by surfacing facts about Vantara's infrastructure subsidy claims, by negotiating directly with Risca Donn, or by changing the public's perception of the refusal log.
sections.title: Clock — *Article 14 amendment becomes law*

sections.text:
- **Echo Ledger Conclave:** parallel interests, occasional information-sharing. See the Conclave entry's clock.
- **Accord clerks:** Vantara's lobbyists know the clerks by face. Some clerks resent this. Tal Vorenis specifically.
- **The Ledger Run:** Mereth used to work for them. Risca Donn replaced her on the Sable Crescent route in 2419. Risca has not forgiven Mereth for walking off the job — and has, in private, considered her own version of the same walk-off twice in the past five years.
- **The Synod:** Vantara delivers contracted goods to Threshold Station's standing-courtyard drop. They do not understand the drop. They consider not-understanding-the-drop to be Vantara's competitive advantage.
- **Glasswake locals:** mixed. Vantara's hospitality is reliable and dull. The dock pubs prefer the Ledger Run.
sections.title: Relationships — this campaign

sections.text:
Vantara's **route-officer corps** (the working liaisons like Risca) and Vantara's **legal team** (the amendment lobbyists) disagree about the amendment. The route officers know the independents' refusal logs and quirks save them money — the independents do work Vantara doesn't want and absorb risk Vantara won't insure. The legal team has metrics. Risca has not raised her objection to the amendment internally because she doesn't think it would change anything and because raising it would mark her as soft.

If the players surface this split — by giving Risca a reason to raise the objection out loud — the amendment slows or stalls. This is play-to-find-out territory.
sections.title: Internal split (DM-only)

sections.text:
- **Lore pull:** see Conclave entry — `duskgrain.md`. I noted that Vantara's microcavity supply chain is one of the legitimate routes the dossier could plausibly track. Vantara's involvement in the opening arc may turn out to be receiver, not shipper. I am leaving this open.
- **Web pull:** none specifically for Vantara. The night-auditor texture went to the Conclave antagonist.
sections.title: Pulls used for this entry

# Hook — Article 14 amendment

**One-sentence situation:** Vantara has filed an Accord amendment that would strip tariff exemption from the Ledger Run's bulk mail; the proposal is sitting at clerks' level and any independent carrier — including the players — can engage with the public comment process.

**Touches:** Vantara, the Accord clerks, the Ledger Run, the public refusal log.

**Stakes if engaged:**
- The org's margin survives or doesn't.
- The Halfsign repair budget exists or doesn't.
- The crew gets to know who Tal Vorenis is.
- The crew gets to be on the comment record alongside other independents — visible alliance opportunities.

**Stakes if ignored:**
- The amendment goes to the Accord with the Vantara-friendly framing. Probable ratification.
- The Ledger Run loses one standing contract within a quarter.
- The Halfsign is sold for parts.

**Visibility level:** Public. Players will see this from the campaign-framing document and the public clock. Safe to surface in early scenes.
sections.title: body

# Hook — the Caulden Rack

**One-sentence situation:** The Splitfork's current outbound loop is carrying a microcavity rack from Caulden Refinery to Hab Meridian, declared as standard; one cell in the rack is duskgrain in microcavity housing, and what the crew does with that fact is the opening arc.

**Touches:** Caulden Refinery, Hab Meridian receiving dock (+ Halvi's sibling), Threshold Blooms (en route detection), Veth Karran's dossier (irregularity opportunity), the refusal log (would the crew log a refusal?).

**Stakes if engaged:**
- The crew detects the substitution in transit.
- The refusal log clause activates: *we can refuse to carry this if we know what it is.* Decision becomes the scene.
- The investigation thread opens — *who substituted, when, why*. Mara has options; the players' pull selects.
- Veth's dossier may get its first entry from this — *or* the players' clean handling of it may close the dossier opportunity entirely.

**Stakes if ignored:**
- The rack arrives at Hab Meridian. Eleven cells crack normally. The twelfth releases full-spectrum resonance into a containment-rated bay. Best case: localized flare, contract terminates. Worst case: Tuner injuries, Bloom-attractor signature in a clean ring hab.

**Visibility level:** **Hold for character creation.** This is the opening arc's strong-start cargo. Do not surface as a hook before character creation; surface as a scene when play begins. The Refusal Log and the Article 14 hooks are character-creation-safe; this one is the first scene.
sections.title: body

# Hook — the Halfsign repair

**One-sentence situation:** The Ledger Run's secondary kite is grounded for repairs the org cannot fully fund; the official cause is *Shear damage on a deep-route detour,* and a closer look at the cradle's stress patterns suggests the official cause is wrong.

**Touches:** The Halfsign, the org's finances, Mereth (who has not looked closely), the previous-loop two-crew (who filed the report and have not been back).

**Stakes if engaged:**
- The repair gets funded — through new contract work, through a new client, through the players' choices.
- The actual cause of damage surfaces. (Mara has options on the board: Drag, Bloom-thing, salvage skirmish, something the elves left. Will resolve at the table.)
- The Halfsign returns to service, doubling the org's operational capacity.

**Stakes if ignored:**
- The Halfsign sits at the dock. Financial pressure tightens.
- If Article 14 passes, the Halfsign is sold for parts.
- Whatever damaged her remains undiagnosed; recurrence is possible on the Splitfork.

**Visibility level:** Visible from every scene set at the Glasswake office. Safe to surface in early scenes; the *real* cause is held back until investigation.

# Hook — the refusal log is being cited

**One-sentence situation:** The Ledger Run's quarterly refusal log has been cited in two recent Accord proceedings to the Conclave's procedural disadvantage; the Conclave has noticed; the Conclave is responding without saying it is.

**Touches:** The Refusal Log, the Echo Ledger Conclave, the Accord clerks (Tal Vorenis specifically), Vantara (which is reading the same citations).

**Stakes if engaged:**
- The crew can double down on the citation pattern — file additions, encourage citations, make the log harder to ignore.
- They can investigate why the Conclave is responding — discover Veth's dossier, the Open-Seal caucus, the contract review.
- They can make peace — find a Conclave faction that will defend the log, build a quiet alliance.

**Stakes if ignored:**
- The Conclave's quiet response continues. Veth's clock advances.
- The standing research-mail contract lapses without theatre.
- The log loses its load-bearing citation in Accord proceedings.

**Visibility level:** Public to anyone who reads Accord proceedings. The *Conclave's response* is not public; that's where the investigation begins.

title: The Glasswake Reclaimed Dock
type: locale
status: active

---

# The Glasswake Reclaimed Dock

The Splitfork's home port. A reclaimed warehouse and its dock fragment, on the surface side of Glasswake, in what used to be an old hab-debris staging yard before Glasswake reclaimed it.
sections.title: body

sections.text: The floor tilts a quarter-degree to the south, where the original hab fragment is still settling into the sand. The ringglass on the south wall hums in response to dock cranes — a slow rising pitch as a crane lifts, a slow falling pitch as it sets down. The dock crews use the wall as an unofficial lift counter.
sections.title: Sensory grounding

sections.text:
- **The office.** Reclaimed warehouse. Refusal log archive (twenty years of quarterly transcriptions, in cabinets). A small bunkroom for the crew when the kite is grounded. Halvi's desk by the open window. The framed envelope of the Withdrawn Letter behind the desk.
- **The Tuner-rated cargo cradle.** A standing fixture in the dock's loading bay where resonance-classified cargo is checked before loading. Mereth's repair to the Splitfork's cradle was made here.
- **The Splitfork's berth.** Currently the kite is in the berth or on the loop, depending on the scene.
- **The Halfsign's berth.** Tarped. Visible from the office window. The crew refers to her as *the grounded sister* without affect.
- **The dock pub.** Two doors down. Run by the previous Ledger Run bookkeeper. Not on the office's books. Where everything actually gets discussed.
sections.title: What's here

sections.text:
- Halvi, in office hours, by the window.
- Mereth, when the Splitfork is in port, generally in the bunkroom corner.
- Ev, sometimes coherent at the desk, sometimes the warmth in the doorway.
- Two or three dock workers on standing call for kite handling.
- One or two regular clients dropping off bonded packages.
- Occasionally: a Conclave courier from the Glasswake satellite, in for a research-mail handoff.
sections.title: Who's usually there

sections.text:
- The Halfsign — visible from every scene set here.
- The framed envelope — visible from every scene set here.
- The dock pub — alternate scene venue, harder for institutional NPCs to set foot in, easier for gossip to land.
sections.title: Hooks

---
title: Hab Meridian Receiving Dock
type: locale

---

# Hab Meridian Receiving Dock

The standing dock the Splitfork uses on Hab Meridian. The hab that first reconnected with Glasswake after the Famine. Standing municipal contract live here. Halvi's sibling works the dock.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
A ring-hab receiving dock — internal, atmosphere, gravity-spun. The dock floor is Meridian-fabricated steel grate; it rings underfoot in a way nobody on Meridian notices and every Splitfork crew member does. The receiving bay is roofed in transparent ringglass — the hab's outer rotation is visible overhead as a slow turning sky.

The bay is *quiet* by ring-hab standards. Meridian receives infrequently and processes carefully. They are proud of this.
sections.title: Sensory grounding

sections.text:
- A docking cradle for kites of the Splitfork's class — bay 4, by long-standing arrangement.
- A receiving station with two staff at any time, one of whom is Halvi's sibling on roughly half the loops.
- A rack-cracking bay where microcavity racks are unsealed and tested before being released to the hab grid. Containment-rated. *Not* rated for full-spectrum resonance release.
- A small Vantara office (Vantara also runs cargo into Meridian; their bay is two over). Sometimes Risca Donn is there.
- A receiving manager's office, locked, where the municipal contract paperwork lives.
sections.title: What's here

sections.text:
- Halvi's sibling (Hab-Worlder, name not yet decided — Mara has left it open for first naming at the table).
- One other receiving staff on shift.
- Occasionally a Vantara liaison.
- A Meridian municipal inspector once per quarter for contract review.
sections.title: Who's usually there

sections.text:
- Halvi's sibling — opens a Hab Meridian-side investigation route for the Caulden Rack.
- The rack-cracking bay's containment limits — load-bearing for the opening arc's worst-case outcome.
- The receiving manager's office paperwork — discoverable through the sibling, holds the municipal contract terms.
sections.title: Hooks

title: Span Nine Hand-Off Ledge

# Span Nine Hand-Off Ledge

A specific point on the Span Nine relay where the Ledger Run hands off Sithari-bound surface mail to a Vantara line. Not the whole relay — one ledge, one bay, one Vantara dock master. The campaign-specific layer of the world-bible Span Nine entry (already imported at `shared/lore/locations/landmarks/span-nine.md`).
sections.title: body

sections.text: The relay is a pre-Glassfall conduit ridge — open structure, no atmosphere, kite-tag operations only. The hand-off ledge is a docking armature on the spine of the ridge, with the Sithari-side glasslight reflecting up from below at a steep angle. The ringglass under the dock hums on a different note than Glasswake's — a higher, drier pitch. Couriers say it sounds like the relay is *thinking about* something.
sections.title: Sensory grounding

sections.text:
- The Vantara hand-off bay. One docking armature, one bay-master, one set of cradles.
- A Ledger Run cargo locker (rented quarterly from Vantara) where overflow surface mail can be staged for the next loop.
- A small Compact monitoring station, automated, with a human inspector who shows up for one shift per Earth-week and rubber-stamps everything else.
- The reflection corridor where Mereth's documented Splitfork run was made. Visible from the docking armature on certain windows. Not signposted.
sections.title: What's here

sections.text:
- The Vantara dock master (a different one each week — Vantara rotates the position; the rotation is part of the corporate consistency).
- An automated cargo manifest scanner.
- Occasionally a passing Compact inspector.
- The Drag, sometimes, a kilometer or so off the ridge, on the right resonance windows.
sections.title: Who's usually there

sections.text:
- The Drag — visible if a player asks the right Tuner-aware question.
- The reflection corridor — discoverable if a player asks Mereth about the Splitfork's name and gets the second sentence of the answer.
- The Vantara dock master rotation — useful if the players want to track when a specific Vantara face will be present.
sections.title: Hooks
