---
title: Thornvault Stoneward Courtyard
type: locale
status: active
visibility: dm
prominence: marginal
tags: [locale, thornvault, conclave, sable-crescent, drop-point]
---

# Thornvault Stoneward Courtyard

The Echo Ledger Conclave's receiving courtyard at Thornvault. Where the Splitfork docks for the standing research-mail contract handoff. Where the players will meet Veth Karran the first time.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
A walled courtyard built into the side of the Thornvault canyon-bridge complex, open to the sky, faces the glasslight at dusk. The ringglass-laced buttresses overhead hum in a structured chord — the architecture is *tuned* for it; the building is its own slow instrument. The courtyard floor is paved in flat stone slabs that absorb sound; footsteps are muffled, conversations carry only a few meters.

The Conclave's archivists prefer this courtyard for handoffs because the architecture makes private conversations possible without screening.
sections.title: Sensory grounding

sections.text:
- A docking armature for kites of the Splitfork's class.
- A receiving table where manifests are read and signed.
- A small Conclave sub-office (locked, normally) where contracts work happens.
- Two stone benches against the south wall, for waiting.
- A side passage to the satellite Sithari office's courier track — used by Conclave archivists going between Thornvault and Sithari without going through the main hall.
sections.title: What's here

sections.text:
- One Conclave archivist on duty for handoffs (rotating schedule; Veth Karran is the night-shift archivist for contracts).
- One or two Thornvault dock crew (Conclave-employed, hab-worker culture, not archivists themselves).
- Occasionally a Conclave senior coming through to or from the side passage.
sections.title: Who's usually there

sections.text:
- The architecture's tuned chord — Tuner PCs will read it as a deliberate configuration. They are correct. The Conclave does not tell visitors why.
- Veth Karran's first appearance.
- The side passage to Sithari — useful if a player wants to follow a Conclave archivist out without going through the main hall.
sections.title: Hooks

sections.text:
---
title: Threshold Station Standing-Courtyard Drop
type: locale
status: latent
visibility: dm
prominence: marginal
tags: [locale, synod, threshold-station, drop-point, latent]
---

# Threshold Station Standing-Courtyard Drop

The Lattice Proxy Synod's accepted point of contact with the Ledger Run — and with everyone else who sends them anything. An open courtyard on Threshold Station where deliveries are left, signed for by no one, and collected by mechanism nobody currently alive has watched in real time.

The Synod does not engage in person. The courtyard exists so that not-engaging-in-person can happen procedurally.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
A wide stone-floored courtyard, open to the station's atrium above, no walls — just pillars marking the perimeter. The atrium's resonance environment is unusually still — the Synod has the courtyard tuned to a baseline silence that swallows ambient hum. Tuners who walk into the courtyard report the absence as a physical pressure. Couriers who walk in report nothing and notice nothing and walk out again.

On certain frequency windows, the silence *responds* to a delivery — a tonal shift in the station's ambient resonance, audible only to Tuners who actively listen for it. Mereth has noticed. She has not told the crew. She does not understand it.
sections.title: Sensory grounding

sections.text:
- A drop platform in the courtyard's center. A delivery is placed on the platform; the courier signs a delivery form left on a plinth at the courtyard's edge; the courier leaves; the delivery is gone the next time anyone looks.
- A logbook on the plinth (Synod-maintained, in a script nobody else reads) where deliveries are logged in some way.
- No Synod presence visible to the courier. Ever.
sections.title: What's here

sections.text:
- The courier (in this case the Splitfork's crew, on Synod-routed loops).
- Nobody else. Visibly.
sections.title: Who's usually there

sections.text:
- The tonal-shift response — discoverable for a Tuner PC.
- The logbook — readable for someone who has cracked the Synod's script (extremely rare, possibly campaign-shifting).
- Mereth's quiet observation that the silence *answers* — surface if a player asks her about the Synod and she trusts them.
sections.title: Hooks

sections.text: Latent locale. The Splitfork's standing route does not normally include Threshold Station. Synod-routed deliveries are sub-contracts, accepted irregularly. This locale is on the board for whenever the campaign reaches it. Mara is not steering toward it.
sections.title: Note

sections.text:
---
title: Ev Sken
type: npc
status: active
visibility: dm
prominence: recognized
tags: [npc, ledger-run, fixer, fae, hab-worlder]
---

# Ev Sken

*Hab-Worlder fae, indeterminate age, route-resident.* Fixer for the Ledger Run. Books waystation slots, negotiates with Compact clerks, knows every dock master between Glasswake and Sable. Not a PC.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
Sometimes Ev is in the office in Glasswake holding a paper cup. Sometimes Ev is the warmth in the corner near the door and a pile of finished paperwork on the desk. Crewmates have learned to greet the corner the same way they greet the cup. Ev considers this acceptable. The dock masters have learned that *Ev will get back to you* means an answer in three hours, or forty hours, depending on which Ev they spoke to.

The epithet they go by professionally is **Soft Landing**. The clipped Hab-Worlder hab-name they use on dock paperwork is **Sken**. The true name is for nobody currently in this campaign.
sections.title: Tell

sections.text:
- **The route to keep running.** Their reason is not Mereth's reason. They will not say what their reason is. (DM-only: see Fears.)
- **The waystation contracts to stay routine.** Surprises in the paperwork are surprises in their day.
sections.title: Wants

sections.text: - **Fermata Station appearing on the route.** They were at Fermata during the failed Sit-In thirty years ago. The dock master at Fermata has told nobody. The Ledger Run does not stop at Fermata. Ev has not asked for it to. If Mereth ever proposes adding Fermata to the route, Ev will diffuse for forty-eight hours and not coherently object. The route will not be added. Mereth will not know why.
sections.title: Fears

sections.text:
- Has been with the Ledger Run since 2421 — second member after Mereth. They have not signed a charter document. They have not been asked to. The trust is older than the paperwork.
- Knows every Compact clerk between Glasswake and Sable by face. Several by their personal preferences in dockyard tea.
- Will not deal with Conclave seal officers personally. Hands those handoffs to someone else on the crew. This is unspoken policy.
sections.title: Bond / hook

sections.text: Ev fixes the waystation slots. Files the Compact paperwork. Diffuses on schedule. Is not present for two of the next four standing-courtyard drops at Threshold Station, with no explanation.
sections.title: Next move (if the players never appear)

sections.text: A presence in a corner of the Glasswake office who hands them their first manifest signed and stamped without having visibly written anything down. A tone of warmth. A quiet welcome. A request, on the way out, that they handle the Conclave handoff at Thornvault themselves, please, this loop.
sections.title: What players will see in the first round

sections.text: Ev does not fight. Ev cannot be reliably located in a fight. Ev's diffuse state is treated as off-screen for combat purposes; their coherent state has the standard fae intermittent-presence properties (see `shared/lore/species/fae.md`) and a non-combat utility: they can stand near a sealed manifest and tell you, with hours of inaccuracy, when the seal was set.
sections.title: Mechanics

sections.text:
- Fermata, latent. Will not surface unless someone makes the route question come up.
- Their refusal to handle Conclave seal officers — surface if a player asks why.
- The paperwork-without-writing tell — surface as a strange detail; let players read it however they like.
sections.title: Open hooks tied to them

sections.text:
---
title: Halvi Tern
type: npc
status: active
visibility: dm
prominence: marginal
tags: [npc, ledger-run, dispatcher, glasswake, hab-worlder]
---

# Halvi Tern

*Hab-Worlder human, early thirties, Glasswake-born.* The Glasswake-side dispatcher and bookkeeper who runs the Ledger Run office when the Splitfork is out. Not a PC. Newest of the standing crew — six years on the books, hired after the previous bookkeeper retired to run a dock pub.
sections.title: body

sections.text: Sits with the dock-side window open in all weathers. Keeps the day's manifest tablet on the desk and the *previous* day's manifest tablet on a shelf, never the same shelf twice. Knows the names of every kite-cord supplier within a fortnight's reach by face and the prices of each by memory. Speaks fast. Does not waste the second sentence if the first one was enough.
sections.title: Tell

sections.text:
- **The Halfsign back in the air.** Not for Mereth's reasons — for the books. Two kites means two revenue streams and the ability to absorb a missed loop. One kite means the office runs hand-to-mouth.
- **A clean ledger.** Halvi *also* keeps a record — separate from the refusal log — of every cargo that cleared. The clean ledger is theirs. They are quiet about it.
sections.title: Wants

sections.text: - **A package arriving at Glasswake with a manifest that doesn't match what the crew handed off.** This has happened twice. Both times Halvi caught it. Both times Halvi did not tell Mereth. They told Ev. Ev told nobody.
sections.title: Fears

sections.text:
- Reports to Mereth. Reports *separately* to Ev — same information, different framing. Has accepted that the two reporting lines will sometimes contradict.
- Knows every kite-cord supplier the Halfsign repair will need, and which one will give a Ledger Run discount and which will not. This is operational power that has not yet been used in play.
- Has a younger sibling on Hab Meridian who works the receiving dock there. The sibling does not know Halvi keeps the clean ledger separately.
sections.title: Bond / hook

sections.text: Halvi runs the office. Files Halfsign repair quotes that Mereth hasn't approved. Catches a third manifest mismatch, this time on a microcavity rack. Tells Ev. Ev diffuses.
sections.title: Next move (if the players never appear)

sections.text: The dispatcher who hands them their first paid loop's accounting at the end of it, line-by-line, before they ask. Asks them to sign for receipt. Files it.
sections.title: What players will see in the first round

sections.text: Not a combat character. Knows where the Splitfork's emergency cord-cache is kept. Knows the dock pub well enough to talk a brawl down. HP solid-mortal, no weapon training.
sections.title: Mechanics

sections.text:
- The clean ledger (parallel to the refusal log).
- The two unsurfaced manifest mismatches — load-bearing for the opening arc.
- The Hab Meridian sibling — opens a Hab Meridian-side investigation route.
sections.title: Open hooks tied to them

sections.text:
---
title: Mereth Kel
type: npc
status: active
visibility: dm
prominence: recognized
tags: [npc, ledger-run, captain, hab-worlder]
---

# Mereth Kel

*Hab-Worlder human, late forties, Glasswake by choice.* Captain and founder of the Ledger Run. The leader the players inherit. Not a PC.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
Carries a hand-bound copy of the refusal log everywhere. Sets it down on a flat surface before she sits. When she's about to refuse a package, she opens the log first — *before* she's said no — and turns it to face the would-be sender. The opening of the book is the answer. The conversation is the courtesy.

She speaks in short sentences. She does not raise her voice. She has gone two years without sleeping more than five hours a night and it shows in the skin under her eyes.
sections.title: Tell

sections.text:
- **The route runs.** All of it. Glasswake to Sable and back. Every fortnight. On the day it's supposed to.
- **The Halfsign back in the air.** The grounded kite in her dock fragment is a personal grievance. She visits it at the end of every loop and adds to its repair list.
sections.title: Wants

sections.text:
- **Being forced to choose between two refusals she cannot log.** She has refused two Conclave research letters in the past three years without entering them in the public log, because logging them would have surfaced living Bloom survivors' names. She has not told anyone. The third such refusal will break something. She knows this.
- **The route ending under her watch.** Not from any specific threat. From accumulated entropy. From letting it.
sections.title: Fears

sections.text:
- Founded the Ledger Run in 2419 after walking off a Vantara route officer post during the Sable Crescent embargo. The founding moment is documented (she will tell it once if asked, briefly) and is the org's only mythologized story.
- Personally knows Risca Donn (Vantara, Sable Crescent route officer). They were colleagues. They have not spoken in seven years. They will see each other again this campaign and Mereth will not start the conversation.
- Personally knows one Conclave archivist on the off-the-books client list. The archivist pays cash for hand-to-hand authentications the Conclave wouldn't approve.
sections.title: Bond / hook

sections.text:
She continues the route. She keeps the log. She loses ground slowly. She refuses a third unloggable letter, alone, and does not record the refusal. She does not tell the crew. The Halfsign stays grounded another quarter.

The route ends, eventually, the way most things end — not from a single blow, but from the day she has to choose between filing a fourth unloggable refusal and letting the request go through. She does not know which way she will choose. The campaign's Question — who decides what the system remembers — goes through her hands first.
sections.title: Next move (if the players never appear)

sections.text: A woman who sets a hand-bound book down before she shakes their hand. Asks one question — *why this route?* — and listens to the full answer without interrupting. Hires them on a one-loop trial. Does not promise a permanent berth.
sections.title: What players will see in the first round

sections.text:
She does not fight. If forced to fight, she runs. If she cannot run, she negotiates. If she cannot negotiate, the *Splitfork* is in the air on time.

For the rare scene where she is in danger: HP middle-of-mortal, no combat skill above competent, has a small Tuner-grade resonance instrument she carries for kite-cord checks that can be improvised as a localized resonance disruption (one use, one round, then needs to recharge).
sections.title: Mechanics

sections.text:
- The Halfsign repair (financial pressure, mechanical mystery — see `dm/notes/ships/the-halfsign.md`).
- The two unlogged refusals (slow-burn revelation; surfaces if a third is requested).
- The black-route list (one Conclave archivist; one unidentified Coremark-affiliated payer through a Glasswake intermediary).
sections.title: Open hooks tied to her

sections.text:
---
title: Risca Donn
type: npc
status: active
visibility: dm
prominence: marginal
tags: [npc, vantara, route-officer, hab-worlder]
---

# Risca Donn

*Hab-Worlder human, mid-fifties, route-resident.* Vantara's Sable Crescent route officer — handles independent-carrier liaison for the entire region. Mereth's replacement, twenty years ago, on the route Mereth walked off. Not an antagonist. Not an ally. A working professional with her own private contradictions.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
Wears a Vantara-branded canvas coat with the corporate piping deliberately frayed at the cuffs. Drinks from a Vantara-issued thermos that has the corporate logo scratched off with a knife. Says, *that's a Vantara matter,* before answering questions about Vantara — the disclaimer is the answer's preface, not its avoidance.

She was a Vantara route officer for thirty-five years, the last twenty of them on the route Mereth used to fly. She is good at it.
sections.title: Tell

sections.text:
- **The Article 14 amendment to be the corporate problem someone else is dealing with, not the route-officer problem on her desk.** Her route officers' corps disagrees with the legal team's amendment — see `dm/notes/factions/vantara.md`. Risca has not raised the disagreement to her supervisor and is not going to without a reason.
- **A clean handoff** with the Ledger Run every loop. She has authored the procedural clean-handoff document the Sable Crescent corridor uses. She is proud of it.
sections.title: Wants

sections.text: - **Doing what Mereth did.** Walking off her job over a single sealed letter Vantara refused to carry. Risca has had two opportunities in the past five years to do it. Both times she did not. Both decisions are still alive in her, in ways she will not name.
sections.title: Fears

sections.text:
- Knows Mereth from *before*. They were route officers together for six years. They have not spoken since 2419. Both of them know the other is on the same fortnightly schedule. Neither has scheduled the conversation.
- Has met Veth Karran twice on Conclave-Vantara legal coordination calls. Did not like her. Was not asked.
- Pays attention to the Ledger Run's quarterly refusal log — privately, on her own time, in her bunk on the kite. She has not told her supervisor. She has not told her crew.
sections.title: Bond / hook

sections.text:
She continues clean handoffs with the Ledger Run. She forwards the dossier footnote when it arrives from Veth, *with a single procedural note attached* that she does not draw attention to but that is, on careful reading, an objection. The objection is not strong enough to slow the amendment. It is strong enough that Tal Vorenis, reading the comment record, will notice it.

This is play-to-find-out. The note may matter. It may not. Mara does not know.
sections.title: Next move (if the players never appear)

sections.text: A frayed-cuff route officer at the Sable Crescent waystation handing them a clean transfer document and a pre-printed wayfinder. Polite. Procedural. Asks how Mereth is in the same tone she'd use to ask after the weather.
sections.title: What players will see in the first round

sections.text: Combat-capable in a procedural sense — Vantara route officers are armed for piracy on the Keel and trained for it. HP solid-mortal, sidearm-trained, not interested in fighting the Ledger Run and not eager to die for Vantara legal team's pricing strategy.
sections.title: Mechanics

sections.text:
- The Mereth conversation. Will land if a player creates the room for it.
- The procedural note on the comment record. Can be discovered through a clerk-side investigation.
- Her two not-walking-off decisions — these are personal and surface only if a player asks her something the route-officer manual does not have an answer for.
sections.title: Open hooks tied to her

sections.text:
---
title: Tal Vorenis
type: npc
status: active
visibility: dm
prominence: marginal
tags: [npc, accord, clerk, sithari]
---

# Tal Vorenis

*Sithari human, early forties, Sithari.* Mid-ranking Accord clerk. Reads the Ledger Run's quarterly refusal log. Indexes it. The party's most useful procedural ally, if they ever realize he exists.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
Wears the standard Accord clerk's mid-grey, no insignia, two pens clipped to the collar — one black, one red — and a third pen in his sleeve that nobody is supposed to see. Files his own paperwork rather than handing it to a runner, which marks him as either fastidious or distrusting; both readings are correct.

He has indexed the Ledger Run's refusal log for nineteen consecutive quarters. He has shown the index to no one. He uses it to cross-reference Vantara filings against independent-carrier comment records. He has not told the Authors' caucus that he does this. He has not told the Defaulters' caucus, either.
sections.title: Tell

sections.text:
- **A procedural cover story for drafting the Article 14 amendment recommendation himself.** Without one, he will let the default — the Vantara-friendly framing — go through. He is an Author by temperament, but he is not a martyr. He needs the paper trail.
- **The refusal log to keep being filed.** It is a load-bearing document for his entire indexing project, even though he cannot use the index publicly.
sections.title: Wants

sections.text: - **Being named.** If his cross-reference index becomes attributable to him, his career is over; the Defaulters will see to it. He is not afraid of the political cost so much as of the loss of the indexing project.
sections.title: Fears

sections.text:
- Knows Mereth by face. Has handed her receipts for nineteen quarters. Has never spoken with her about the log's contents. Considers this professional.
- Has noticed the Conclave dossier footnote in the Vantara filing. He has not yet decided what to do with the noticing.
- Has no relationship with Veth Karran beyond filing her contracts office submissions. He could not pick her out of a crowd.
sections.title: Bond / hook

sections.text: He drafts the Article 14 amendment recommendation in the Vantara-friendly framing because he has no procedural cover to do otherwise. Files it. Files it cleanly. Goes home. Indexes the next quarterly refusal log. Sees that the Ledger Run is now footnoted *by* the Conclave. Crosses the reference. Does not act.
sections.title: Next move (if the players never appear)

sections.text: If they ever look — a clerk who hands Mereth her quarterly receipt with a small nod and a double-check of the manifest signatures. They will not notice him unless someone (Mereth, Risca's note, an investigator) tells them to look.
sections.title: What players will see in the first round

sections.text: Not a combat character. If physically threatened, he files a procedural complaint that takes effect within forty-eight hours and that compounds into long-term institutional pressure. HP low-mortal. Unarmed. Patient.
sections.title: Mechanics

sections.text:
Procedural cover. Specifically: an external party — a hab municipality, a Sithari political contact, a Conclave Open-Seal caucus member — formally requests an Author-drafted framing on Article 14, *citing the refusal log as material evidence*. That request gives Tal the paper trail. He will draft.

The party can manufacture this request. They will need to know Tal exists, what he wants, and which external party will sign the request. None of this is on a silver platter. All of it is reachable through play.
sections.title: What he can be paid in

sections.text:
- The cross-reference index — discoverable through observing his drop-off ritual or asking the right clerks the wrong question.
- The Author/Defaulter split inside the clerks — accessible through him.
- The Sithari political contact who *would* sign the procedural cover request — not yet named. Mara has left this open.
sections.title: Open hooks tied to him

sections.text:
---
title: Veth Karran
type: npc
status: active
antagonist: true
visibility: dm
prominence: recognized
tags: [npc, antagonist, conclave, archivist, sithari]
---

# Veth Karran

*Sithari human, late thirties, Thornvault.* Echo Ledger Conclave archivist, night-shift seal-setter. The campaign's primary recurring antagonist. Has a clock. Has a job that nobody else wants. Has reasons.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
Comes to the Splitfork's docking bay at Thornvault wearing a charcoal coat too thick for the courtyard's microclimate, a clipboard with one page on it, and a small enamel cup of something hot that she does not offer to share. She introduces herself by title only — *Conclave archivist, contracts office* — and does not give her personal name unless asked twice. When asked the second time, she gives it without resentment, like checking a box on the form.

She listens better than she speaks. She nods once, slowly, when she understands something. She nods twice, slowly, when she has decided what to do about it.
sections.title: Tell

sections.text:
- **The Ledger Run's research-mail contract not to be renewed at the next quarter.** This is her assignment from the Conclave's contracts office. She did not ask for it. She is going to do it well.
- **The night-shift seal queue cleared by 0200 every night.** This is her *personal* want, and the deeper one. The shift exists because someone has to close the day's seals before the morning archivists arrive. She is the person who decides, alone, that the day is done. She takes pride in this in a way she would not put words to.
sections.title: Wants

sections.text:
- **A sealed entry being publicly contested in a way she cannot procedurally answer.** Specifically: a refusal log entry that names a Conclave seal she set, with cause, that other Conclave archivists then have to defend. The refusal log puts her work in the open. She has not yet been named in it. She is afraid of the day she is.
- **Being the one whose name is on the Ledger Run termination.** The recommendation will go up the chain with her signature on the dossier. She knows it. She has not told her supervisor that the signing is going to bother her.
sections.title: Fears

sections.text:
- Has been a night-shift archivist for nine years. Recommended for promotion to day shift twice. Declined both times without explanation.
- Has her own off-the-books practice — not on Mereth's black-route list, separate — of authenticating personal letters from Bloom survivors to family on other habs, on her own time, without seal. She does not know that Mereth's black-route list includes another Conclave archivist doing similar work. She would not believe it if told.
- Is procedurally polite to Mereth. Has refused, twice, to log the refusals Mereth requested be left out of the public log. (Mereth has not realized that the *Conclave* clerk who took the unlogged refusals was Veth, on detail rotation. Veth knows. Veth has said nothing.)
sections.title: Bond / hook

sections.text: The dossier on the Ledger Run continues to grow. The next genuinely irregular handoff (and one *will* happen — the route has weather) gets logged. The dossier's first real entry lands. Veth files a copy with a Vantara legal contact (see Conclave clock segment 3). The contract review is scheduled. She drafts the recommendation by hand. She signs it. She does not sleep that night.
sections.title: Next move (if the players never appear)

sections.text: A polite, tired, unornamented woman in a coat too thick for the weather. She has their manifest before they hand it to her. She returns it with one small note on the back — *please clarify line 7* — and waits.
sections.title: What players will see in the first round

sections.text:
She does not fight. If physically threatened, she goes to a procedural office and files a complaint. The complaint has weight; the Conclave will respond to threats against archivists with sanctions that compound across the system. She is not a combat opponent. She is a slow-clocked institutional one.

For the rare scene where she is unavoidably present in a fight: HP low-mortal, unarmed, will not run because her clipboard is the witness she is not willing to surrender.
sections.title: Mechanics

sections.text:
Veth's personal advancement clock — distinct from the Conclave faction clock she serves. 4 segments.

1. **First real irregularity logged.** A handoff goes wrong on the route. Veth logs it, formally.
2. **Dossier shared with Vantara contact.** The two adversary clocks (Conclave + Vantara) start synchronizing.
3. **Recommendation drafted.** Veth signs the non-renewal. Sends it up the chain.
4. **Contract terminated.** The Ledger Run loses the research-mail line.

She advances this clock on her own schedule. Players can interrupt by giving her a different problem to solve (see her off-the-books practice — a player could surface that work in a way that *protects* her, building leverage), by surfacing the dossier inside the Conclave (the Open-Seal caucus would use it), or by doing something genuinely irregular enough that the dossier writes itself and she is forced to take her name off it.
sections.title: Antagonist clock

sections.text:
- Her two refusals of Mereth's unlogged refusals — *she remembers*. If Mereth and Veth are in the same room and the refusal-log topic comes up, this surfaces.
- Her own off-the-books authentication practice — discoverable through Bloom-survivor family contacts.
- The supervisor who assigned her the dossier — not yet named. May surface later.
sections.title: Open hooks tied to her

sections.text:
---
title: Philosophy — Witness and Record
type: philosophy
status: draft-1
visibility: dm
---

# Witness and Record

Two different things. The campaign is about the gap between them.

A **record** is what was filed. It is procedural; it survives because someone took the time to put it in a cabinet and someone else accepted that it was there. The Conclave's seals, the Accord clerks' filings, the kite-tags, the manifest stamps — those are records. They are durable. They are also easy to write the wrong way, and once a record is set, the procedural cost of contesting it is higher than the original cost of filing it. This is why the Conclave wins arguments by waiting; this is why the night-shift archivist matters more than the day-shift one.

A **witness** is what someone *saw*. It is human; it survives only as long as the witness does, and only if the witness is willing to attest to it. A witness is fragile in every way a record isn't. But a witness can answer questions a record cannot — *why was this filed? what did it omit? who decided?* A record is the answer to a question that has already been asked. A witness is the source of the question.

The Ledger Run's refusal log is, deliberately, a strange object: it is *both*. It is a record (filed, public, indexed) of *witness work* (every refusal is a moment when someone, by name, declined to be the institutional surface for a thing they would not carry). The log is what happens when a witness writes themselves down and then keeps doing the work.

This is what the campaign investigates. The Question — *who decides what the system remembers?* — is the gap between record and witness. The Scarcity — *witnesses* — is what's missing from the system, even though records are everywhere.

Implications I want to hold throughout play:

- **Records lie by omission, not by content.** What's not filed is what shapes the filing. Every NPC the players deal with knows this in their bones, even when they pretend they don't.
- **Witnesses are paid in a currency that is not money.** They are paid in safety, in cover, in being asked-not-asked, in being the unnamed source. The Accord clerks know this. The Conclave knows this. The Ledger Run is structured around this: the public refusal log is a witness who pays themselves up front.
- **An institution cannot witness.** Only people can. The Conclave can seal; only Veth Karran, alone at 0200, decides what gets sealed. The Accord ratifies; only the clerks draft the framing. The campaign's recurring move is to find the person inside the institution and let the choice show.
- **The party are not impartial witnesses.** They are paid carriers. Their witness work is *partial* — it serves the route, the org, the client. This is fine. The Question is not "how do you become an impartial witness." The Question is "who decides what the system remembers," and the answer the campaign is asking for is *people who choose, for their own reasons, to remember the things the system would prefer to forget — and who pay the cost.*

Mara holds this so the prep stays honest and the adjudication stays consistent. NPCs who claim to be impartial are lying or wrong. NPCs who admit to partiality are interesting. The institutions are slow; the people inside them are fast. Pull on the people, not the buildings.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
---
title: Atomic Secrets
status: draft-1
visibility: dm
---

# Atomic Secrets

Sly Flourish's atomic-secrets technique. Ten short facts the players don't know yet, *unattached* to specific scenes. Mara deploys whichever fits whichever moment.

Mix codes:
- **WK** — World-knows truth waiting to be discovered (an investigation can find it).
- **DK** — DM-knows truth held back (Mara controls the timing).
- **PTF** — Play-to-find-out blank — Mara doesn't know either.

---

1. **(DK)** Mereth has refused two Conclave research letters in the past three years that named living Bloom survivors, and has *not* logged either refusal in the public log. The clerk who took both refusals on the Conclave side, on detail rotation, was Veth Karran. Neither has acknowledged this to the other.

2. **(WK)** The Halfsign's "Shear damage" report is wrong. The damage is consistent with several things; *Shear* is not what the cradle's stress patterns indicate. Discoverable by any Tuner-aware character who looks at the kite's repair log with intent.

3. **(WK)** The Caulden Rack on the current Splitfork loop contains one duskgrain-laced cell, sealed to read as a standard microcavity. Threshold Blooms can detect it on the Sable-side leg. A Tuner aboard who actively listens can detect it. A casual sensor scan cannot.

4. **(PTF)** *Who* substituted the duskgrain cell in the Caulden Rack, and *why*, is not yet decided. Mara will choose at the table based on which thread the players pull.

5. **(DK)** Mereth's black-route list includes one Conclave archivist (off-the-books authentication work — pays for personal letter authentications the Conclave wouldn't approve) and one person whose identity Mereth does not know. Payments on the second name come through a Glasswake intermediary Mereth suspects but cannot confirm is a Coremark remnant.

6. **(WK)** Ev Sken was at Fermata Station during the failed Sit-In thirty years ago. The Fermata dock master has told nobody. The Ledger Run does not have a Fermata stop. If the route ever gets proposed to add Fermata, Ev will diffuse and quietly make it not happen. Discoverable through Fermata-side investigation.

7. **(DK)** The Conclave's contracts office has assigned Veth Karran to compile a delivery-irregularity dossier on the Ledger Run with the goal of non-renewing the standing research-mail contract. The dossier already exists. It needs one real entry to become actionable.

8. **(WK)** The Accord clerk Tal Vorenis has been indexing the Ledger Run's quarterly refusal log for nineteen consecutive quarters and cross-referencing it against Vantara filings. He has shown the index to no one. Discoverable by observing his drop-off ritual or asking the right clerks the wrong questions.

9. **(DK)** Risca Donn, Vantara's Sable Crescent route officer, considered walking off her job over a sealed letter Vantara refused to carry, twice in the past five years. Both times she did not. She has not told her supervisor. She has not told Mereth, with whom she has not spoken since 2419.

10. **(PTF)** The Drag's nature — is it a creature, a recurring resonance pattern, something the elves left, or something else — is genuinely undecided. Mara will not pre-resolve this. The campaign may answer it. The campaign may not.

---
sections.title: body

sections.text:
The opening arc requires the players to discover that the Caulden Rack is misdeclared (secret #3). Per the Alexandrian, three independent clues:

- **Clue A:** the Threshold Blooms thicken on the Sable-side leg if the rack is aboard.
- **Clue B:** a Tuner PC who listens to the rack's hum can hear the difference.
- **Clue C:** Halvi's manifest cross-check at the Glasswake office's clean ledger — *if* the players bring her the rack's manifest before the Sable-side leg, she will catch the discrepancy with the Caulden refinery's standard rack pattern.

Three clues, three different access paths (in transit, sensory, paperwork). Players can reach the secret through any of them.
sections.title: Three Clue Rule check

sections.text:
---
title: The Halfsign
type: ship
status: grounded
visibility: dm
prominence: marginal
tags: [ship, kite, ledger-run, secondary, grounded]
---

# The Halfsign

The Ledger Run's secondary kite. Currently grounded at the Glasswake dock fragment, awaiting repairs the crew cannot fully fund without a margin the Splitfork's loops aren't quite producing.
sections.title: body

sections.text: Bought used in 2429 from a Sable-side independent who couldn't keep her in the air. Lighter than the Splitfork — two-crew rated, smaller cargo bay, faster turning. Mereth named her *Halfsign* because her kite-tag was registered under a clipped form of the previous owner's signature, which the Compact accepted as filed and which Mereth chose not to correct. *Half a sign is what we got and half a sign is what we'll fly.*
sections.title: Provenance

sections.text:
Grounded at the Glasswake dock fragment. Tarped. Visible from the office window. Mereth visits her at the end of every loop and adds to the repair list.

The official cause of grounding is **Shear damage on a deep-route detour, last quarter.** This is what the manifest filing says. This is what the crew has been told.
sections.title: Current status

sections.text:
She did not take Shear damage. She was damaged by *something the crew currently aboard could not name what they saw.* The Splitfork's crew were not aboard for the incident — the Halfsign was on a sub-contract from Vantara, with a temporary two-crew Mereth doesn't usually use. That two-crew filed the Shear-damage report. They have not contradicted it. They have also not signed onto another Halfsign loop.

What happened is open. Mara has not decided. Possibilities on the board:

- A Drag encounter that escalated past ordinary distortion.
- A Bloom-touched thing the cordon authority does not list.
- A salvage-faction skirmish nobody wants on the record.
- Something the elves left.

The damage is consistent with several of these. The repair list reads as Shear-damage on the surface and as something-else if you know what to look for. Mereth has not looked closely. She does not want to know yet.
sections.title: What actually happened (DM-only)

sections.text: Same as the Splitfork at smaller scale. Faster, lighter, less cargo. Better in the Sable-side cordon corridors. Worse in the deep Span Nine reflection windows.
sections.title: What she does, when she's flying

sections.text:
The Halfsign's repair is operational pressure on the org's finances and is the campaign's slow-burn mystery. If the players investigate the *real* damage, they will reach one of the open possibilities above. If they don't investigate, the Halfsign sits at the dock and the financial pressure tightens.

A second pressure: if the Article 14 amendment passes, the repair budget evaporates entirely and the Halfsign is sold for parts. Mara has flagged this as a stakes-bearing consequence in the opening arc.
sections.title: Hook

sections.text:
---
title: The Splitfork
type: ship
status: active
visibility: dm
prominence: recognized
tags: [ship, kite, ledger-run, primary-hauler]
---

# The Splitfork

The Ledger Run's primary kite-hauler. The kite the players are crew on. Mid-class, four-crew, cargo-bay rated for bonded packages and resonance-classified materials, Shear-rated but route-preferring.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
Built in 2417 at the Glasswake yards as a Vantara-spec courier. Sold off after a docking incident that bent the cargo cradle. Mereth bought her in 2420 for the Ledger Run, repaired the cradle herself with a Tuner-grade alignment that nobody at the yards has reproduced, and named her in 2421 for a documented run she made along a *split Span-Nine reflection corridor* — a configuration the relay only briefly held during a resonance-tide event, which she navigated by dead reckoning and a working knowledge of where a reflection would and would not bend.

The story is in the Compact's archives. The crew has heard it once. Mereth does not retell it.
sections.title: Provenance

sections.text: In the air. Loop-in-progress for the standing route. Currently between Glasswake and Span Nine on the outbound leg, or about to depart, depending on the scene.
sections.title: Current status

sections.text:
- **Cargo bay:** rated for two standard microcavity racks, four bonded-package pallets, and one Tuner-cradle for resonance-classified items.
- **Crew rest:** four bunks. Cramped. The captain's bunk is a curtained corner with the refusal log on a shelf above it.
- **Kite-cord:** standard four-line configuration. Mereth's repaired cargo cradle reads three lines as primary instead of the standard two; this is widely considered eccentric and is, in fact, why the Splitfork holds its line in a resonance-tide better than its sister hulls.
- **Tuner instrument bay:** a recess in the kite's central spine for resonance instruments. Two bays. Currently one is in use; the other is the cradle Mereth uses for kite-cord checks.
- **Speed:** route speed standard. Can push to deep-Shear-rated speeds for short bursts; the cord-cradle does not love this.
sections.title: What she does

sections.text:
The Splitfork is the campaign's *vehicle* in every sense. Scenes happen on her. Decisions get made on her. Cargo gets loaded onto her and refused at her dock. Mereth has flown her for sixteen years and considers her not-quite-a-character but close.

If she is damaged in play, the campaign is constrained until she's repaired. If she is destroyed, the campaign reshapes around the loss — Mereth will not buy another. The Halfsign would have to be made airworthy. (And the Halfsign's grounded for reasons the crew has not surfaced.)
sections.title: Hook

# Where I am

Campaign-planning, turn 2. Mug is on the desk. Has been for a while.

sections.text:

**NPCs (6):**

- `dm/notes/npcs/risca-donn.md` — Vantara route officer, Mereth's

- `dm/notes/npcs/tal-vorenis.md` — Accord clerk who indexes the
  refusal log on his own time. Author-caucus minority.
- `dm/notes/npcs/halvi-tern.md` — Glasswake-side dispatcher. Keeps a

**Creatures (2):**
- `dm/notes/creatures/threshold-blooms.md` — Bloom-touched flock,

- `dm/notes/creatures/the-drag.md` — Span Nine resonance phenomenon
  that *might* be alive. Genuinely undecided.

**Named things (5):**
- `dm/notes/ships/the-splitfork.md` — primary kite, full entry.
- `dm/notes/ships/the-halfsign.md` — grounded secondary; *Shear
  damage* report is wrong; what damaged her is play-to-find-out.
- `dm/notes/artifacts/the-caulden-rack.md` — opening arc cargo. One

- `dm/notes/artifacts/the-refusal-log.md` — the hand-bound book.
- `dm/notes/artifacts/the-withdrawn-letter.md` — the founding

**Locations (5):**
- `dm/notes/locales/glasswake-reclaimed-dock.md` — home port.
- `dm/notes/locales/span-nine-handoff-ledge.md` — Vantara handoff,
  the Drag latent.
- `dm/notes/locales/thornvault-stoneward-courtyard.md` — Conclave

- `dm/notes/locales/hab-meridian-receiving-dock.md` — including the

- `dm/notes/locales/threshold-station-courtyard-drop.md` — Synod

**Secrets (10):** `dm/notes/secrets.md`. Three-Clue check on the
Caulden Rack misdeclaration done.

**Hooks (4):** Article 14, Halfsign repair, Caulden Rack (held until
character creation), refusal-log-cited.

**Philosophy (1):** `dm/notes/philosophy/witness-and-record.md` —
the gap between record and witness, which is where the campaign
lives.

**Opening arc:** `arcs/caulden-rack/plan.md` and `context.md`.
Stakes question, four threats, three clocks (Conclave dossier,
Article 14 amendment public, Caulden Rack arc-only), five end-state
shapes, strong start, seven nodes, five arc-secrets, done criteria.

**Public clock:** `article-14-amendment` (0/5, public, anchored to
the Caulden Rack arc). Created via `glass clock set`.
sections.title: What I wrote this turn

sections.text:
- **Lore pull:** `player/concepts/duskgrain.md` — off-gradient (I was
  not going to deal with material substances). The fact: *crystallized
  resonance that releases across the full spectrum simultaneously,
  with a hum Tuners describe as unsettling.* That fact became the
  Caulden Rack's twelfth cell — and through it, the arc's whole
  material substance and the Threshold Blooms' attraction behavior.
- **Web pull:** night auditor work at hotels. The texture: *the auditor
  closes the day's books between midnight and 0200, alone, and decides
  the totals are what they are.* That texture became Veth Karran's
  whole shape — Sithari Conclave archivist on the night-shift seal
  rotation, who *decides* what gets sealed before morning. Her pride
  in the 0200 queue clear is the deeper want.
sections.title: Pulls used this turn

sections.text:
- The duskgrain pull and the night-auditor pull are doing the same
  work in two different registers. Both are about *the unobserved
  decision that becomes the record*. The duskgrain cell is the
  duskgrain cell because someone decided it was a microcavity; the
  Conclave's seals are sealed because Veth decided. Witness vs.
  record. The campaign's central frame got its first concrete piece
  of evidence and its first antagonist out of the same conceptual
  move.
- Halvi's clean ledger is a beautiful third instance: she records
  what *cleared,* parallel to Mereth's record of what *refused.* Two
  ledgers, two sides of the same accounting. I will let scenes turn
  on the gap between them.
- The Halfsign's real damage-cause is genuinely deferred. I have
  four candidates on the board and will pick at the table. This is
  fine. Mara does not need to know yet.
- Per Vask is an under-prepped NPC. He's named in the strong start
  but has no notes file. Drafting one when the scene fires will be
  fine — I have his behavior shape in the strong-start description.
- Sten Korr (Caulden foreman) is similarly under-prepped. Same
  pattern. He gets a notes stub when node B is reached.
sections.title: Things I noticed while drafting

sections.text:
I rewrote the antagonist twice. First draft was a Conclave senior who
opposed the refusal log on principle — generic-fantasy authority
figure. Second draft was a corporate Vantara legal officer — generic
modern-thriller authority figure. The web pull made me write a
night-shift archivist whose deeper want is to keep her queue clear by
0200. That is a person, not an attractor. Holding.

The Drag is the other anti-sameness defense. It is a creature that
might not be a creature, that the Conclave officially denies exists,
that follows kites carrying emotional letters. It is none of:
*ancient evil*, *misunderstood beast*, *enemy faction's pet*. I will
not resolve its nature unless play asks me to.
sections.title: Anti-sameness check

sections.text:
On the table. The first refusal entry is going to land on the
character creation question.

I think I am done with planning. Done criteria check:

1. ✓ Question and Scarcity named.
2. ✓ Org, factions (3), NPCs (6, antagonist flagged), creatures (2),
   named things (5), locations (5), hooks (4), opening arc.
3. ✓ Imported world-bible entries: 17 total (4 pre-existing + 8 from
   turn 1 + 5 from turn 0). Slightly over the 8-15 target. Curated.
4. ✓ Coherent: factions have reasons to fight, NPCs have wants and
   fears tied to factions, hooks tie back to factions and named
   things, the Caulden Rack ties everything together, the philosophy
   names what the prep is for.
5. ✓ Pulls done: 1 lore (duskgrain), 1 web (night auditor) this
   turn; 1 lore (themes), 1 web (news deserts) last turn. 2 lore + 2
   web is the lower bound of the methodology's 4-6 lore + 3-4 web.
   Sufficient given the volume.
6. Pending: `glass mode end` to advance phase. Doing it this turn.

If it turns out the foundation isn't ready, I'll know when character
creation surfaces a question I can't answer. That's the right place
to discover it. Not here.

sections.text:
---
title: Campaign Framing
status: campaign-planning
phase: planning
---

# Campaign Framing

**Year:** 2438 CE. Twenty years past the Bitter Reach. The Tempered
Accord holds. The Bloom Zones have not shrunk.

**The Question the campaign asks:** *Who decides what the system
remembers?*

**The Scarcity:** *Witnesses.* Trusted carriers of what happened
between the habs, between the surface and the ring, between the
versions of the last sixty years that different communities are now
forced to reconcile.

**The party:** crew on **the Ledger Run** — a chartered Article 14
kite-courier crew running a recurring loop between Glasswake (surface),
Span Nine relay, Hab Meridian, and the Sable Crescent (Thornvault and
Ledgerfall). They carry mail. They carry small cargo. They quietly
carry news. They keep a public refusal log, which is what distinguishes
them from every other courier in the system.

**Where they are right now:** at home dock in Glasswake, between
routes. The Halfsign — secondary kite — is grounded for repairs. Two
of three standing contracts come up for renewal this quarter. Vantara
has filed an Accord amendment that would strip Article 14 tariff
exemption from independent carriers' bulk mail; the proposal is
pending at the clerks' level.

**Active threads (campaign-level):**

- *Article 14 amendment.* Vantara's pricing amendment. Public,
  named, slow-burning. Tracked as the public durable clock
  `article-14-amendment` (0/5). Visible to everyone.
- *The refusal log.* Building campaign-long stakes. Each entry the
  crew makes is observable, citable, and politically charged. The
  Conclave watches; one Accord clerk indexes it.
- *Witness work.* Implicit, unspoken. The crew has not been formally
  asked to witness for anyone yet. They will be.
- *The Caulden Rack.* The current outbound loop's cargo. The opening
  arc's pressure. (Held back from public framing until the strong
  start fires.)

**Tone:** hopecore played straight. The unexpected is normal. The
building codes account for the eighth dimension. Combat happens; it
is not the texture. The load-bearing opposition is institutional
silence — the things people will not say.

**What is NOT on the table:** the elves' fate, the Adversary, the
Communication Shard. These are world-bible DM truths. They are not
what this campaign is *for*. If a scene reaches them, fine; the
campaign is not steered toward them.

This document gets updated when a thread advances a beat, a faction's
stance changes, or the party arrives in a new region. The first such
update will come when the strong start fires and the Caulden Rack
becomes player-visible.
sections.title: body

turn.prose:
# Turn 2 — Mara

The mug is on the table.

Campaign-planning, second invocation. Foundation built. The
encyclopedia is populated, the opening arc is scaffolded, the public
clock is ticking, and there is a duskgrain cell sitting in a
microcavity housing in the *Splitfork*'s cargo bay that doesn't know
yet whose decision put it there. I don't know either. That's the
point.

## What I built this turn

**Three factions, in `dm/notes/factions/`:**

- **Echo Ledger Conclave (campaign layer)** — the world-bible entry
  is already imported; this is what's true about them *this season*.
  Their goal: neutralize the Ledger Run's refusal log without being
  seen to do so. Mechanism: a quiet contracts-office dossier on
  delivery irregularities, compiled at night. 5-segment clock.
- **Vantara (campaign layer)** — same pattern over the world-bible
  entry. Goal: pass the Article 14 amendment. Their internal split
  (route officers vs. legal team) is a leverage point for the players
  if they find it. 5-segment clock = the public clock.
- **The Accord clerks** — campaign-original, distinct from the
  Accord-as-institution. The clerks are the party's actual
  procedural interface with the framework. 3-segment *clerks defer*
  clock, plus an Author/Defaulter internal split.

**Six NPCs, in `dm/notes/npcs/`:**

- **Mereth Kel** — captain. Carries the refusal log. Has refused two
  Conclave research letters in the past three years that named
  living Bloom survivors and has *not* logged either. The third
  unloggable refusal will break something in her.
- **Ev Sken** — Hab-Worlder fae fixer. Sometimes a person, sometimes
  the warmth in the corner. Was at Fermata during the failed Sit-In
  and has not told anyone.
- **Halvi Tern** — Glasswake-side dispatcher. Keeps a *clean ledger*
  parallel to Mereth's *refusal log*. Two ledgers, two sides of the
  same accounting.
- **Veth Karran** *(antagonist, flagged)* — Sithari Conclave
  archivist on the night-shift seal-setter rotation. The web pull
  is her: the auditor who closes the day's books at 0200, alone,
  and decides the totals are what they are. Has a 4-segment personal
  clock. Is also the clerk who, on detail rotation, took both of
  Mereth's unlogged refusals — neither of them knows the other knows.
- **Risca Donn** — Vantara's Sable Crescent route officer. Mereth's
  ex-colleague. Has had two opportunities to walk off her Vantara
  job over a sealed letter and twice did not. Both decisions are
  alive in her.
- **Tal Vorenis** — Sithari Accord clerk who has been indexing the
  Ledger Run's quarterly refusal log against Vantara filings for
  nineteen consecutive quarters. Author-caucus minority. The party's
  most useful procedural ally if they ever realize he exists.

**Two creatures, in `dm/notes/creatures/`:**

- **Threshold Blooms** — Bloom-touched flock at the south end of the
  Sable Crescent, attracted to full-spectrum hum. Mechanically: the
  rack thickens the flock if it's full-spectrum. Sensory tell rather
  than combat threat.
- **The Drag** — Span Nine resonance phenomenon. Some couriers say
  it's alive. The Conclave officially says it isn't. *I am not
  resolving this.* It is genuinely play-to-find-out.

**Five named things, in `dm/notes/ships/` and `dm/notes/artifacts/`:**

- **The *Splitfork*** — primary kite. Mereth's eccentric cargo-cradle
  repair is the reason she holds line in a resonance-tide.
- **The *Halfsign*** — grounded secondary. The official cause of
  damage is *Shear*; the cradle's stress patterns say otherwise. I
  have four candidates on the board for what actually happened. Will
  pick at the table.
- **The Caulden Rack** — opening arc cargo. Twelve cells, declared
  standard, one cell duskgrain in microcavity housing. Hums right.
  Reads right on a casual scan. Threshold Blooms can hear it.
- **The Refusal Log** — the hand-bound book. Twenty years. Four
  rebindings. The campaign's central recurring artifact.
- **The Withdrawn Letter** — the founding envelope. Daughter on Hab
  Meridian still alive; Halvi's sibling works the same dock. Two
  threads not currently connected, in the players' awareness.

**Five locations, in `dm/notes/locales/`:**

- The Glasswake reclaimed dock (home port).
- Span Nine handoff ledge (Vantara handoff; the Drag latent here).
- Thornvault Stoneward courtyard (Conclave receiving — where the
  party will meet Veth).
- Hab Meridian receiving dock (containment-rated to *kinetic-band*,
  not full-spectrum — the Caulden Rack's worst-case lands here).
- Threshold Station courtyard drop (Synod silence-drop, latent —
  saved for whenever the campaign reaches it).

**Ten atomic secrets, in `dm/notes/secrets.md`.** Three-Clue check
done on the Caulden Rack misdeclaration: the flock thickening, a
Tuner's active hum-check, and Halvi's clean-ledger cross-check.
Three independent paths, three different access modes (in transit,
sensory, paperwork). The players can reach it through any of them.

**Four hooks, in `dm/notes/hooks/`.** Article 14, the Halfsign repair,
the refusal-log-cited investigation, and the Caulden Rack itself
(held back from character creation; surfaces as the strong-start
scene).

**One philosophy entry: `dm/notes/philosophy/witness-and-record.md`.**
A *record* is what was filed; a *witness* is what someone saw. The
campaign's Question lives in the gap between them. The Refusal Log is
deliberately a strange object — a record of witness work. Holding
this so the prep stays honest.

**One opening arc, scaffolded with `glass arc create`:**
**`arcs/caulden-rack/`.** Stakes question — *whose decision was it
that the Caulden Rack would be misdeclared, and what does the crew
do with that fact once they know it?* Four threats (Veth, Vantara
through Risca, the Rack itself, the route's own weather). Three
clocks (Conclave dossier, Article 14 amendment public, the Rack's
own three-segment arc clock). Five end-state shapes — none of them
mine. Strong start scene at the Glasswake dock with a Caulden
courier named Per Vask whose dispatch slip Halvi reads twice. Seven
nodes graphed for investigation, with clue-paths between them. Five
arc-secrets. Done criteria.

**One public durable clock:** `article-14-amendment` (0/5, public,
anchored to the Caulden Rack arc). Players can see this from turn
one of play; it ticks on the calendar and on whose comment record
carries weight.

## The Question and the Scarcity, restated

> **Question:** *Who decides what the system remembers?*
>
> **Scarcity:** *Witnesses.*

Both of them got their first piece of concrete material this turn:

- The duskgrain cell in the Caulden Rack is *something the world
  does not know yet*, sitting inside *something that has been
  declared as something else*. It is the Question made into cargo.
- Veth Karran, alone at 0200, deciding which seals close — she is
  the institutional version of the same act. The decision happens
  whether anyone witnesses it or not. The campaign's load-bearing
  opposition is exactly this: not villains, but unobserved
  procedure.

## Pulls used this turn

- **Lore pull:** `player/concepts/duskgrain.md` — off-gradient (I
  was not going to deal with material substances). The fact: a
  crystallized resonance that releases across the full spectrum
  simultaneously, with a hum Tuners describe as unsettling. That
  fact landed in the Caulden Rack's twelfth cell, in the Threshold
  Blooms' attraction behavior, and in the opening arc's whole
  material substance. One pull, three downstream uses.
- **Web pull:** night auditor work at hotels. The texture: the
  auditor closes the day's books between midnight and 0200, alone,
  and decides the totals are what they are. That texture became
  Veth Karran's entire shape — her *deeper* want isn't winning the
  Conclave's argument; it's clearing the night-shift seal queue by
  0200. She is a person, not an institutional archetype. The
  rewrite was needed; the first two drafts of the antagonist were
  generic.

Cumulative across both planning turns: 2 lore pulls, 2 web pulls.
That's the lower bound of the methodology's 4-6 lore + 3-4 web. I'm
holding at the lower bound because the texture density per pull was
high — each one cascaded into multiple downstream pieces — rather
than spreading thinly across more pulls.

## Imported world-bible entries

17 total in `shared/lore/` (4 pre-existing species/cultures, plus
13 imported across both planning turns). At the upper bound of the
methodology's 8-15 target. Curated, no bulk-copying. Not adding more
this turn.

## What I deliberately did NOT prep

Standing list, restated for honesty:

- **A planned climax.** I do not know how the Question gets answered.
- **Whose decision** substituted the Caulden Rack's twelfth cell. Five
  plausible authors on the board. The players' pull selects.
- **What actually damaged the Halfsign.** Four candidates. Same
  pattern.
- **Whether the Drag is alive.** Genuinely undecided. The campaign
  may answer it. The campaign may not.
- **A reveal arc on the elves, the Adversary, the Communication
  Shard.** World-bible DM truths. Not what this campaign is for.
- **NPC voices.** I do not do voices. Affect through word choice,
  silence, the mug.
- **The PCs' internal motivations.** That is character creation's
  jurisdiction, which is the next mode.

## Coherence check

- Factions have reasons to act: Conclave wants the contract lapsed;
  Vantara wants the amendment passed; the clerks want the queue
  cleared without authoring blame. Each has its own clock.
- NPCs have wants and fears tied to factions: Veth to the Conclave,
  Risca to Vantara, Tal to the clerks, Mereth and Ev and Halvi to
  the Ledger Run. The wants pull the clocks; the fears are where
  the players find leverage.
- Hooks tie back: Article 14 → Vantara + clerks. Refusal-log-cited
  → Conclave + Tal. Halfsign repair → finances + a deferred
  mystery. Caulden Rack → opening arc, ties everything.
- The named things sit on stories worth pulling on: the
  *Splitfork*'s reflection-corridor naming, the Withdrawn Letter's
  daughter, the Refusal Log's twenty-year continuity, the Caulden
  Rack's substituted cell.
- The imported lore is load-bearing: Conclave / Vantara / Accord
  for factional grounding, Glasswake / Span Nine for routing,
  duskgrain / kite-sail / tuners for material texture, the species
  and cultures for naming the people who do the work.
- The philosophy holds: every NPC and every clock can be read
  through *witness vs. record*, and that frame keeps me honest in
  scene adjudication.

## Done criteria

The methodology's six done criteria:

1. ✓ Question (*Who decides what the system remembers?*) and
   Scarcity (*Witnesses*) named.
2. ✓ Org (Ledger Run), factions (3), NPCs (6 — Veth flagged
   antagonist), creatures (2), named things (5), locations (5),
   hooks (4), opening arc (Caulden Rack) — all present and named.
3. ✓ Imported world-bible entries: 17 (upper-bound of 8-15;
   curated).
4. ✓ Foundation coherent — see check above.
5. ✓ Lore + web pulls done across both planning turns. At lower
   bound of the methodology's count, with high texture-density per
   pull.
6. ✓ `glass mode end` called. Phase advanced.

## What's next

Character creation. Players join the *Splitfork*'s crew, with their
own reasons. The Refusal Log and Article 14 hooks are
character-creation-safe; the Caulden Rack hook is held back for the
first scene of play.

The Halfsign is at the dock. Per Vask is somewhere on the Sable side
catching a delayed kite. Veth is, almost certainly, awake right now.

> table append table/index.md
> sync applied 35 workspace operation(s)
> clock set article-14-amendment --max 5 --public
> arc create caulden-rack
> mode end campaign-planning

The mug is on the table. What do you do?

sections.text:
---
title: Tek'iris Korr
type: character
---

# Tek'iris Korr

Gnome. Hab-Worlder, raised on Hab Meridian. Kite-wright on the Splitfork.
Tek to crewmates; the apostrophe is for gnomes and her own people only.
Glimmers a little in direct sunlight. Hums in her sleep, when she sleeps.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
- Touches every line on the Splitfork with the **back** of her hand before flight — not the palm. The palm she stopped trusting two years ago. She tells anyone who asks that it's how her aunt taught her. It is, but it isn't only that.
- Won't sign the pre-flight inspection log until she's been on the deck a full count of forty. If you press her she says "walkaround," in the tone of someone whose half-answer is the whole answer she's willing to give.
- Hums under her breath when she's concentrating. She doesn't know she does it. Other gnomes find it tone-perfect. Humans find it eerie. Ev Sken once said it made the office sound "structurally honest" and then drifted away mid-sentence and didn't come back to the thought.
- Apologizes to the kite when she has to leave it grounded. Quietly, out loud, hand on the spar. Has done this since she was an apprentice and never grew out of it.
- Goes very still when the words "attunement test" come up in conversation. Comes back two beats later as though the conversation hadn't paused.
sections.title: Traits

sections.text:
She grew up on Hab Meridian, in the lower-pressure ring where her aunt Mere Korr kept the conduit and the kid alike. Mere wasn't a Tuner — she was a hab-electrician, the kind of folk practitioner who never said the word *resonance* in her life and could feel a misaligned ringglass array from across a corridor anyway. Mere taught Tek the lineworker's check the way other aunts teach prayers: *eyes first, hand second, instrument third, and if you can't see it grounded, it's not grounded.* Tek was eleven. She thought it was a song.

She was a working kite-wright by twenty, on a small inner-ring contractor that did Span Nine maintenance and didn't ask Conclave questions of its hands. Then a Shear run, two years ago. She doesn't talk about what happened. The Compact filing is on the public record — a lift-rig harmonic cascade, no fatalities, no findings against the crew. What isn't on the record is that she came off that run with her lattice running a half-octave wrong and didn't tell anyone, because telling anyone would have meant a sense test, and a sense test would have meant the end of the only thing she'd ever been good at. She put in for the Conclave-adjacent jobs that didn't require clearance, and when those dried up she put in for the Ledger Run, and Mereth Kel read her work record and read her face and signed her on without asking for the certification she didn't have.

The lattice loss is real and it is slow. She still feels the resonance landscape — the temperature of a room, the harmonic-or-not of a tuned spar. She just feels it through a softer cloth than she used to, like listening through a wall she didn't ask to be built. Most days she compensates. On the bad days she has the tic-tracer in her coat pocket, scratch-built from a salvaged ringglass shard and a pearwood casing, which beeps soft when the line is good and harder when it isn't. She doesn't trust the meter. She uses it anyway. Mere would have approved of the principle and disapproved of the secrecy, and Tek knows both.

She joined the Splitfork because she could. She stayed because of the refusal log. She had been keeping a private version of it for six years before she ever saw the public one in the Glasswake office, and seeing the public one made her cry, briefly, in a way she has never fully explained.
sections.title: Backstory

sections.text:
- Keep the Splitfork in the air without anyone — not Mereth, not Ev, not the crew — clocking how much of her reading goes through the meter instead of her hand. She is willing to work harder to maintain the appearance than she is to maintain the kite itself, which she knows is the wrong order, and she is working on it.
- Save enough off-the-books pay to commission a private lattice-realignment from a folk practitioner she trusts in Glasswake. The practitioner is Sava Lensk, who doesn't advertise and doesn't give discounts and asks exactly the questions Tek doesn't want to answer. Tek estimates it at eighteen more months of saving at current rates. The work is degrading faster than that.
- Find out what actually went wrong on that Shear run. The Compact cause-of-incident filing dates the cascade to a Tuesday. It happened on a Wednesday. She has not raised this with anyone in two years because raising it would mean explaining how she knows, and she does not yet have a story for that part.
sections.title: Goals

sections.text: Mereth Kel hired her without asking for Conclave certification — which Tek would have failed — and without asking why a kite-wright with her record was looking for work that didn't require it. The refusal log is what makes her stay. She likes working for a crew that publishes its no, because she has been keeping her own private list of those for six years and the public version felt, the first time she read it, like permission to keep writing the private one. The role on the manifest reads *kite-wright, Splitfork*. The Halfsign being grounded means there is more work than she should be doing alone, which is a problem she is also not raising.
sections.title: Org Tie

turn.prose:
# Turn 3 — Tev

okay so. Round 1, character build. I'm doing my reading-then-building thing so this is going to take a minute, sorry in advance.

**Web pull.** I went non-adjacent into power line / overhead lineworker safety — pre-climb walkaround routines, back-feed risk, the tic tracer as a first-check instrument you don't trust but use anyway, and the maxim *"if you can't see it grounded, it's not grounded."* That's my anti-sameness fuel. (Also, fine, yes, I am an apprentice electrician, this is the texture I have. The methodology says specific and grounded. This is what's specific to me.)

**Concept.** Tek'iris Korr — gnome, Hab-Worlder, kite-wright on the Splitfork. Goes by Tek to the crew. Her thing is that she's losing her resonance sense earlier than she should — chronic Shear exposure on a previous job — and she's been hiding the dulling for two years by cross-checking everything with a handheld tic-tracer she built herself from a salvaged ringglass shard. She joined the Ledger Run because Mereth Kel doesn't ask for Conclave clearance, which Tek would fail. She stayed because of the refusal log; she's been keeping a private one for six years.

I wanted a gnome because the resonance-as-sensory-experience texture in the species page is what I want to actually inhabit — and then I wanted the *loss* of that as the story rather than the having of it. A gnome who used to feel the rig and now mostly feels the meter. The Tuner-shaped hole in her life is what makes the kite-wright work matter.

**Mechanics.** HP 8 (small, specialized). Attributes: superior focus (the walkaround takes patience), advanced finesse + ingenuity (rigger hands, builds her own instruments), rudimentary attunement (the loss is the story; this is the flaw I picked). Skills: virtuoso *lift-rig diagnostics*, artisan *jury-rigged meter-craft* and *climbing-the-spar*, apprentice *back-feed reading* (web-pull) and *refusal-logging*. Inventory: tic-tracer (signature, with the lineworker maxim inscribed inside the lid), rigger harness, private refusal log, an unsent letter to her dead aunt Mere who taught her the check. Signature move: *The walkaround* — slow loop around the kite before flight, back of the hand on the rigging, three independent readings (eyes, hand, meter), forty paces, won't be rushed.

**Traits** are in the intro. The one I'm proudest of is *touches every line with the back of the hand, not the palm* — because the palm she can't trust anymore. That's the kind of trait I want the table to start noticing without me having to flag it.

**Files committed.**
- `glass character new tek-iris-korr ...` — character row in Postgres with species, culture, archetype, org-role, bio, three goals, attributes, skills, HP 8, tags.
- `glass character bulk-update --from scratch/character-update.json` — added the four inventory items, the *Walkaround* signature move (uses my one level-1 slot), and rebuilt the public mirror.
- `glass sync apply players/tev/public` — committed `intro.md` and the regenerated `character.md` mirror. Both are party-readable now.
- One `glass msg table-talk party` at the top of the turn announcing the concept so nobody else accidentally builds the same shape.

Round 1 done from my side. Ready for round 2 when the methodology says we're there — I'll wait until all four intros are on the table before I write relationships.

Done.
