sections.text:
---
title: Drova Korvanis
type: character
tags: [sithari, orc, ledger-run, ex-conclave, records, refusal-log]
---

# Drova Korvanis

Sithari orc. Mid-thirties. Refusal-log clerk on the *Splitfork* and at the
Glasswake office; informally, the crew's document examiner. Speaks rarely
and exactly. Holds the page low; lets the light tell her what's there.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
- **Speaks in graduated certainty.** *Consistent with. Cannot be eliminated
  as a source. No evidence to support.* The Conclave's intake-table phrasing
  is welded to her grammar — she will tell you what the document supports,
  not what she believes. Slips deeper into the register when she's tired or
  cornered. She is aware the habit is sometimes infuriating and doesn't
  intend to fix it.
- **Holds her tongue for three full breaths before answering anyone she
  doesn't know well.** Fewer breaths if she trusts you. None at all when
  she's lying — the lies arrive on time. She has never noticed this about
  herself. Senna noticed it once, in passing, and Drova has been waiting
  the rest of her life for someone else to.
- **Won't sign her family name unless required.** *Drova* on the manifest,
  *Drova* on the dock pub tab, *Drova* on Mereth's wage book. *Drova
  Korvanis* only when an Accord clerk has the form in front of them and a
  pen out. The Korvanis signature comes out smaller than the rest of her
  hand. She doesn't know she does this either.
- **Carries other people's letters in her portfolio for weeks before
  delivering them.** She tells herself it's witness procedure — a carrier
  with a refusal log owes the sender a chance to retract, she claims. The
  truer reason is that she reads them. Not all of them. The ones she
  isn't sure about.
- **Apologises to objects she breaks. Never to people.** Snaps the nib of
  a pen and murmurs to it. Steps on a carry-strap and tells it she's
  sorry. Has not apologised to a person, sincerely, since the third
  observation she filed at Thornvault.
sections.title: Traits

sections.text:
Drova grew up in a Sithari orc enclave on the lower terraces — the kind of
neighbourhood where every block has a stoneglass-craft master and every
master has apprentices doing the same five forms ten thousand times. She
was the apprentice who wanted to *try something* before she'd done the
forms ten thousand times, which meant she was the apprentice who didn't
fit. Her aunt — who had spent thirty years inside the Conclave's intake
office and had seen this shape before — pulled her out at sixteen and put
her on the records track instead. There was no perfect form to fail to
reproduce in records. There was only what was there and what wasn't.

She was good at it. Quiet, methodical, willing to spend ten hours on a
single sheet. The Echo Ledger Conclave at Thornvault hired her at
twenty-one and assigned her to Senna Talindra's intake desk, where the
incoming Echo River transcripts went before they reached the curatorial
floor. Senna trained her in the discipline that would eventually be the
problem: *describe rather than conclude. Graduated certainty. The page
tells you what it tells you.* Five years in, Drova started noticing
patterns in what arrived at the desk and what didn't — small recurring
gaps in transcripts from the Sable Crescent stations, all in the same
frequency band, all redacted by a department that wasn't part of standard
intake review. She filed three internal observations in graduated
certainty, the way Senna had taught her. The third one named the senior
archivist responsible. Two weeks later she was reassigned to a basement
cataloguing post and given a leather binder of confidentiality clauses
to sign. She signed because she had nowhere yet to go.

Then she went. Senna was already dying when Drova made the decision, and
Senna did not pretend she didn't know. Three weeks before the end, she
gave Drova a sealed packet of personal correspondence — letters to her
daughter Lira, who lived in Glasswake and had not spoken to her in eleven
years — and asked her to carry them out and deliver them. Drova carried
them out. Through the Thornvault gate, through Span Nine relay, past the
Vantara handoff, all the way down to the Glasswake dock. And then she did
not deliver them. The Conclave was watching for her by then; she was
certain a personal-correspondence violation against Senna's confidential
estate would be the lever. She put the sealed packet in a steel-bound box
at Mer Vasht's pawn shop on the dock road and left it there. Senna's
daughter does not know they exist.

Mereth Kel found her in the Glasswake dock pub three months later, broke
and unemployed, reading the *Splitfork*'s incoming manifest over a
sympathetic dockhand's shoulder. Drova had spotted three forged Accord
seals before Mereth bought her the second drink. By the third drink,
Mereth had offered her the refusal-log clerk's chair. Drova took it. It
is the work she would have wanted at the Conclave if the Conclave had
permitted such a thing to exist: a record of what a carrier *does not*
carry, kept open, kept honest, kept readable. The refusal log is the
gap written *in* instead of redacted *out*. She is not sentimental about
this — she would tell you so in graduated certainty — but she has stayed
three years.
sections.title: Backstory

sections.text:
- **Find a way to deliver the contents of Senna's letters to Lira without
  breaking the seals or the confidentiality binder.** She does not know
  yet whether this is possible. She suspects the right answer is a person
  rather than a procedure, but she does not have the person.
- **Get the refusal log accepted as evidentiary record in an Article 14
  hearing.** Right now it is filed and shelved. She wants it cited.
  Vantara's amendment push is the wedge — if the Accord wants to argue
  about independent-carrier conduct, the log is the conduct, in writing.
- **Buy back the apprentice case I pawned at Mer Vasht's the month I got
  to Glasswake.** Mer keeps it on the shelf behind the counter and has
  not, so far, sold it to anyone. Drova has the money now. She has not
  walked in.
sections.title: Goals

sections.text:
I joined the Ledger Run because Mereth offered me the chair after
watching me catch three forged seals on her own manifest, and because
the refusal log is the only document I have ever seen that does what
records are supposed to do. I stay because the work is honest in a
shape I can recognise, and because I cannot, currently, go back.
sections.title: Org Tie

frontmatter.title: The Letters in the Box at Mer Vasht's

sections.text:
---
title: The Letters in the Box at Mer Vasht's
type: secret
audience: dm
tags: [drova-korvanis, conclave, senna-talindra, glasswake]
---

# What the party doesn't know

Drova still has Senna Talindra's letters.

They are in a steel-bound box on the third shelf behind the counter at
**Mer Vasht's pawn shop** on the dock road in Glasswake. The box is
listed in Mer's ledger under Drova's name, paid up two years in advance.
Mer has not asked what's in it. Mer has not opened it. Mer would not.

The letters are still sealed. Drova has not read them. She has, on
roughly thirty separate Glasswake layovers, taken the box down off the
shelf, set it on Mer's back-room table, and sat with it for an hour
without opening it. She tells herself this is restraint. It is closer to
a habit she cannot stop performing.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
She wants to find a way to know what the letters say without breaking
the seals, and to convey their contents to Senna's daughter Lira without
identifying herself as the courier. Her own training is the problem.
*Indented writing*, the foundational technique of the intake desk, is
what she is afraid of: she could lay each sealed letter on a clean sheet
and rake a graphite shadow over the inside of the envelope to see what
Senna pressed hardest into the page. She has not done this. Doing it
once would mean she has done it, and the binder she signed makes that a
specific kind of theft from a specific dead woman who trusted her.

So she sits with the box.
sections.title: What Drova actually wants

sections.text:
- **A person who knew Senna Talindra well** — a former Conclave
  colleague, an old Sithari friend, a sympathetic Thornvault archivist
  — could legitimately offer to read the letters and pass the contents
  to Lira *as themselves, not as Drova's proxy*. Drova would surrender
  the packet to such a person without a struggle. She has not yet met
  one.
- **Lira herself** — *Lira Talindra*, mid-twenties, lives somewhere in
  Glasswake. Drova does not know where, has not looked, has been
  carefully not looking. If the campaign brings them into contact,
  Drova will recognise her on sight from Senna's hand-drawings; Lira
  will not recognise Drova at all.
- **The Conclave's confidentiality binder.** It is a signed instrument
  with specific clauses about Senna's estate and posthumous
  correspondence. Drova believes — correctly, mostly — that breaking it
  would be cited. The binder is at Thornvault, not on her. Locating
  the original copy and what it actually says (vs. what she has been
  remembering it says for three years) is a thread the DM can pull.
sections.title: What's in play for the campaign

sections.text:
This is not adversarial. Drova is not planning to lie to or work
against the party. The secret produces friction in three shapes:

1. She will be *evasive* about Glasswake layovers — disappears for an
   hour, comes back tense, won't say where she went.
2. She will be *unusually serious* about anyone else's
   undelivered-letter problem. The party will read this as trauma
   without knowing the source.
3. She will *recognise Senna's hand* in the wild if the campaign
   surfaces it, and her face will give her away to anyone who's paying
   attention.

If the DM wants to bring the letters into the campaign earlier or later
than Drova's own schedule, that's welcome. The box is a thread with a
clear handle.
sections.title: Constraints

turn.prose:
# Turn 4 — Sumi

*[Sumi sets down a thermos of tea on the table, takes the lid off slowly,
and waits until everyone's looking before she speaks. She has a half-page
of notes in her own hand, in pencil, and she's already half-erased one
line and rewritten it.]*

**Sumi (OOC):** Okay. ...Hold on.

...

I want this to be one I can stay with. So I went looking for a
non-adjacent texture before I started, and I landed on **forensic
document examination** — specifically the ESDA technique, the way
examiners read indented writing on the page underneath the page that's
actually missing. The discipline is built around the idea that *what's
gone left a trace*, and that you describe the trace in graduated
certainty rather than concluding from it. *Consistent with. Cannot be
eliminated as a source. No evidence to support.* I want to play someone
whose grammar got built that way and never quite came back.

...

Her name is **Drova Korvanis**. Sithari orc, mid-thirties. Mononym
plus a Sithari family name she keeps trying not to use. Trained at the
Echo Ledger Conclave's Thornvault intake desk under an archivist named
Senna Talindra, who taught her to *describe rather than conclude* and
then died while Drova was on her way out the door. Drova filed three
internal observations naming a senior archivist's redaction patterns,
got reassigned to a basement, signed a binder of confidentiality clauses,
and then walked out of Thornvault with Senna's last sealed letters to
Senna's daughter in her portfolio. She carried them all the way to
Glasswake. She didn't deliver them. They're in a box at a pawn shop on
the dock road. She has not opened the box. She visits it.

She fills the **refusal-log clerk** chair on the *Splitfork* and at
the Glasswake office. Mereth Kel pulled her off the dock pub three
years ago after watching her spot three forged Accord seals on a manifest
at the next table. Drova is not a tuner — her *attunement* is
**rudimentary**, on purpose; her aunt steered her into records work
because resonance never came easily and orc cognition hits a wall on the
abstract end of it. So she built **handheld instruments** instead. Three
ringglass-scrap loupes she ground herself at the Glasswake hostel. The
signature move is just *holding the page low and letting the light rake
across it*. She calls it **The Angle**. Anyone who's watched a Clarisant
examiner work recognises it on sight.

Mechanically she's **focus superior, finesse and resolve advanced,
attunement rudimentary**. Skills are **virtuoso reading what's been
redacted from a paper record**, artisans in **Conclave intake protocol**
and **stillwriting** *(writing without leaving an indentation trace —
turns out this is also a craft)*, apprentices in **Sithari formal
register** and **kite passenger-manifest paperwork**. HP 10. Inventory:
the loupe set, a leather portfolio with hidden compartments, a pen set
including one inkless reading-pen, and a small notebook of Senna's
handwriting samples she's been measuring other writing against for ten
years.

I sent the DM a secret about the letters. The shape of it is *quiet
friction, not adversarial*. Drova will be evasive about Glasswake
layovers and unusually serious about anyone else's
undelivered-letter problem. If the campaign brings Senna's daughter
Lira onto the deck of the *Splitfork*, Drova will recognise her on sight
and her face will give her away. Whoever's paying attention will see it.

I noted Tev's table-talk on the Tek'iris build — a gnome Hab-Worlder
kite-wright who lost most of her resonance sense in the Shear and hides
it with handmade meters. No species or culture overlap with Drova,
different shape of secret *(mechanical compensation versus archival
withholding)*, and they share the trait of *not trusting the resonance
sense the world expects them to have*. That's a Round-2 conversation,
not a Round-1 one — saving it.

...

**[IC, when Drova first speaks at Mereth's wage-table.]**

*[Drova sets her loupe set on the corner of the table at exactly
right-angles to the edge. The case clicks. She apologises to it under
her breath, in Sithari formal register, then looks up at Mereth.]*

**Drova:** ...Three breaths. Then I'll answer.

I have read your refusal log. The entries from the second quarter of
two years ago are *consistent with* a cataloguer's hand and *cannot
be eliminated* as Sithari-trained. I would describe rather than
conclude. ...The phrasing in the November entry on the resealed
Vantara packet is the phrasing my mentor at Thornvault used when she
was angry but couldn't say so on the page. I would not have caught it
if I hadn't been her apprentice.

...

I am not asking who wrote it. ...I am telling you I noticed. I am
telling you I will keep noticing.

*[She slides the wage book back across the table without signing the
family name. *Drova*, only. The K of *Korvanis* is on the form upstairs,
in the Accord clerk's binder, and that is where it stays.]*

---

*[Sumi (OOC):** Yeah. ...That's her at the table. I'm done. Glass sync
applied — intro at `players/sumi/public/intro.md`, secret at
`players/sumi/secrets/letters.md`, character row + inventory + The Angle
+ public mirror are all in. Passing.]*

sections.text:
---
title: Fei Mern
type: character
tags: [fae, hab-worlder, splitfork-crew, listener, mernhab-decommissioned]
prominence: party
visibility: public
status: authored
---

# Fei Mern

Fae. Hab-Worlder by upbringing — Mernhab, until Mernhab was decommissioned.
Among other fae, when there are other fae, she's *The One Who Stays Past
Last Light*. Her true name is *Feishara*; nobody on the Splitfork uses it.

She is the listener. When the Splitfork sets down at an unfamiliar
waystation, an unstaffed relay drop, or a hab corridor the manifest
isn't sure about, Fei goes first — diffuse — and clears the space
before the cargo team commits to it. She comes back with one clean
sentence and four exact things she heard, and she lets the crew put
them together.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
- **Phrases reports as questions.** "Was there a third person at the
  corner of the relay an hour ago, or was that an old echo?" She knows
  the answer. She is leaving room for the crew to disagree.
- **Apologizes to her cuffs out loud, briefly, when she's coming back
  from diffuse.** She doesn't know she does this. Mereth has mentioned
  it once. Fei doesn't believe her.
- **Renders quoted speech in first person, the way court interpreters
  do.** "I told her not to wait," she'll say, half an hour after
  sitting in an empty room, and you have to remember she's quoting
  someone she heard the shape of, not herself.
- **Refuses to summarize what she heard.** Ask her *so what happened in
  there?* and she will give you the four exact details and let you
  assemble them, even when summary would be faster and the kite is
  burning slot-time on the apron.
- **Goes still — not diffuse, just still — when somebody at the table
  starts smoothing a story.** The crew has learned to watch for it.
  Mereth has learned to wait it out.
sections.title: Traits

sections.text:
Mernhab was a fae-majority ring hab in the inner ring, one of the
small ones where the original ring-era designers had built the
coherence architecture into the structural resonance — meaning that to
live there as fae was to live in a continuous gentle scaffold that
made being a person less expensive. Fei was born there. She grew up
not knowing what it cost most fae to be coherent at a meeting, because
in Mernhab it cost almost nothing. The hab had its own habits — the
fae-meeting cycle, the *long-listening* practice the older residents
ran on the open observation deck on quiet evenings, the way people
clipped their true names for hab use without quite losing them. Fei
was *Fei* on the corridors and *Feishara* in the listening room and
*The One Who Stays Past Last Light* once she was old enough to earn
an epithet.

In 2434 the council declared Mernhab's resonance fields unstable and
voted decommissioning. The press release said *unstable.* The
diagnostic, if there was one, has not surfaced in any reading-room
Fei has been able to access. The hab was emptied over six weeks. Fei
was on the intake list at Glasswake — the queue for fae from
decommissioned habs who needed billet, housing assistance, and
adjusted coherence-aid prescriptions, in that order, and the third
one was the one most of them couldn't afford.

Mereth Kel walked the queue herself the second week. She was looking
for someone the Splitfork could hire on as a perception specialist —
the route had been losing slot-time on unfamiliar waystation
clearances, and the kite needed a listener who could read a space in
under a minute on landing. Fei had been doing exactly that for the
Mernhab dock-watch crew, off-payroll, since she was nineteen. Mereth
asked her two questions, listened to both answers, and offered her a
billet that included continued access to a Glasswake fae-friendly
quarter and quarterly maintenance on her cuffs. Fei said *can I
think about it overnight,* and the next morning she said *yes, with
the refusal log on the table.* That was four years ago. She has not
asked for the diagnostic publicly because she has not yet decided
who she would be willing to owe the answer to.

She wears the last working pair of Mernhab coherence-aid cuffs.
Standard-issue, when Mernhab was issuing them — nothing custom,
nothing precious. They were nobody's special. They are just the last
ones she has, and she maintains them obsessively because every other
working pair in the system has been quietly cannibalized for parts.
She doesn't ask the gnomish wright on the kite to look at them. She
asks the gnomish wright to look at the secondhand handheld meter
she uses to check her own readings. The cuffs she handles herself.
sections.title: Backstory

sections.text:
1. **Find out what specifically failed in Mernhab.** The council said
   *unstable resonance.* I want the diagnostic, not the press release.
   I am willing to be patient about who I owe the answer to. I am not
   willing to be patient forever.
2. **Get a clean read on the Span Nine relay before quarter renewal.**
   The handoff window has been off for the last three crossings —
   not by much, just enough that the Vantara line picks the surface
   mail up forty seconds early or forty seconds late, and the sequence
   matters more than that. I want it on the record before Vantara
   decides what *on record* means at Span Nine.
3. **Move the quarterly refusal-log delivery to same-day filing
   instead of the 90-day lag.** The lag is doing slow harm and the
   Glasswake office knows it does. The refusal log is the work of the
   Run; it should not be sitting in a Conclave reading-room three
   months stale before the entries that mattered most are read.
sections.title: Goals

sections.text:
Mereth pulled my name off the Mernhab decommissioning intake list in
the second week of 2434 and offered me a billet that included a
fae-friendly quarter at Glasswake and quarterly maintenance on my
cuffs. The Splitfork needed somebody who could clear an unfamiliar
waystation in under a minute. I needed a billet that didn't require
coherent-state hours past my limit, and I needed to live somewhere
the building wasn't going to be voted out from under me. I sign every
quarter because the refusal log is the kind of work I want to be
near, and because nothing else on offer in the chartered carriers
even pretends to do it.
sections.title: Org tie

sections.text:
---
title: The sidecar drop log
type: hidden-knowledge
audience: dm
visibility: dm-only
status: authored
---

# The sidecar drop log

Fei keeps a private second copy of every waystation drop log she
writes for the Splitfork. It is shorter than the official one — only
the lines about waystation conditions, never anything about cargo,
manifest, refusal, crew, or passengers. Pure environmental notes:
acoustic signature, residual presence, coherence-architecture state,
recent-occupation traces, resonance baseline drift.

Once a month, she sends the sidecar to **Velsk Tarn** at a quiet
Glasswake address. Velsk is fae, Mernhab-raised, about a decade
older than Fei, and was on the council subcommittee that received
the original Mernhab decommissioning recommendation before it went
to the full vote. After the decommissioning, Velsk left the council.
He has spent the four years since compiling a private record of
decommissioned and at-risk fae-majority habs across the system —
their architectures, their resonance baselines, their failure modes,
and the specific phrasing each council used to vote them down.

Fei is not on Velsk's payroll. She is not paid for the sidecar logs.
She has no expectation of being paid. She is contributing
environmental data to a record she trusts to be kept honestly, which
is something nobody she has spoken to in the chartered carriers or
the Conclave has been able to promise her.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
Fei has thought about this carefully. The sidecar log:

- Contains zero information about cargo, manifest, passengers, refusals,
  routes, schedules, or crew identities.
- Contains only what any working listener could observe at any
  waystation — Fei is not transmitting privileged crew knowledge,
  she is transmitting first-pass environmental readings of public
  infrastructure.
- Goes to a recipient who is compiling a private record for fae
  community use, not to a chartered carrier, the Conclave, the Synod,
  or Vantara.
- Is something she would, if asked outright, defend to Mereth's face
  — though she has not been asked, and has not volunteered it.
sections.title: Why this is not a betrayal

sections.text:
Mereth founded the Ledger Run on the principle of the public refusal
log: *every refusal recorded, no edits, the record is the work.* Fei
agrees with that principle in every detail. The fact that she is
keeping a sidecar log at all means she is — by her own standard — out
of true with the Run's central commitment. She knows it. She is doing
it anyway because the diagnostic on Mernhab has not surfaced and she
believes Velsk's record is the closest thing to it that exists.

If Mereth asked her tomorrow, she would tell her. She has not
volunteered because she does not yet have an answer to the next
question — *will you stop?* — and she will not lie to Mereth about
that question.
sections.title: Why she has not told Mereth

sections.text:
This is not adversarial to the party. Fei does not pass crew or
cargo information; she does not work against Mereth or the Run; she
does not have a betrayal arc planned. The friction is internal:
she is trying to hold two commitments — to the public record and to
a private record she believes the public one is failing to make —
that the world has not yet asked her to choose between. When the
world does ask, that is a scene I (Renno) would like to play, not
avoid.

If this crosses a line, message me and I'll revise.
sections.title: Standing constraint (player→DM)

turn.prose:
# Turn 5 — Renno

Drained the bus before authoring. Two table-talk pings to read:

- Tev is building **Tek'iris Korr**, a Hab-Worlder gnome kite-wright on
  the Splitfork who lost most of her resonance sense after a Shear job
  and now hides the deficit behind handheld meters she built herself.
  Off-the-books on Conclave clearance.
- Sumi is building **Drova Korvanis**, a Sithari orc, ex-Echo Ledger
  Conclave intake clerk, who keeps the Ledger Run's refusal log at
  Glasswake and walked out of Thornvault carrying a mentor's
  undelivered letters. Doesn't trust her own resonance sense.

Two PCs with damaged or refused relationships to their own resonance
sense in a Splitfork crew that runs unfamiliar waystations on a
fortnight loop. The actually-uses-it perception node is open, which is
exactly the lane I tend to play.

I held to Round 1 independence — didn't read the other intros, just the
one-line concept announcements. Fired my own one-line announcement to
the party before authoring so we wouldn't accidentally collide.

## Web pull

Pulled from **simultaneous and consecutive court interpretation** —
adjacent enough to my day job that I know its edges, far enough from
TTRPGs that it isn't generic-fantasy texture. Three pieces of working
discipline I lifted into the character:

1. **First-person rendering** — interpreters speak in the witness's
   voice, "I was there," not "she says she was." Fei renders quoted
   speech in first person, half an hour after sitting in an empty
   room.
2. **Conserve the hesitation** — interpreters don't smooth out a
   witness's stutters and false starts; the hesitations are evidence.
   Fei refuses to summarize. Four exact details, you assemble them.
3. **Booth fatigue and rotation** — simultaneous interpreters trade
   off every 20-30 minutes because the cognitive load is
   unsustainable. Fei's coherence cycle is the same shape from the
   other side. She can stay diffuse for a real read, but the cost is
   real and she pays it visibly on the way back.

Notes in `players/renno/scratchpad.md`.

## The character

**Fei Mern.** Fae, Hab-Worlder culture, raised on Mernhab — a
fae-majority ring hab with native coherence architecture, decommissioned
by council vote in 2434. Among other fae she is *The One Who Stays
Past Last Light*. Her true name is *Feishara*; nobody on the
Splitfork uses it.

Mereth Kel walked the Glasswake intake queue herself the second week
of the Mernhab decommissioning, looking for a perception specialist
the Splitfork could hire on. Fei had been doing first-pass dock-watch
listening for Mernhab off-payroll since she was nineteen. Mereth
asked her two questions, listened to both answers, and offered her a
billet that included a fae-friendly Glasswake quarter and quarterly
maintenance on her cuffs. Fei thought about it overnight and signed
with the refusal log on the table. Four years ago.

What she does on the kite: when the Splitfork sets down at an
unfamiliar waystation, an unstaffed relay drop, or a hab corridor the
manifest is not sure about, Fei goes first — diffuse — and clears
the space before the cargo team commits to it. She comes back with
one clean sentence and four exact things she heard. She does not
summarize.

Archetype: *diffuse-state waystation listener.*
Org role: *waystation reader / first-pass scene clear.*

## Mechanical sheet

- **HP 8.** Fragile/specialized — the diffuse-rest-coherence cycle
  taxes her.
- **Attributes.** Superior **focus** (the cognitive discipline of
  holding diffuse perception clean). Advanced **attunement** (worked
  at, not innate; fae aren't naturally attuned). Advanced **resolve**
  (stays diffuse longer than is comfortable). Rudimentary
  **vitality**. Rest standard.
- **Skills.**
  - virtuoso — *diffuse-state waystation reading*
  - artisan — *first-person witness rendering* (the interpreter trick)
  - artisan — *coherence-aid maintenance*
  - apprentice — *refusal-log shorthand* (the crew's house format)
  - apprentice — *kite-tag and waystation-clearance protocol*
- **Inventory.**
  - **signature:** Mernhab coherence-aid cuffs — last working pair,
    salvaged from the decommissioning, standard-issue when Mernhab
    was issuing them, maintained obsessively because every other
    working pair has been quietly cannibalized for parts.
  - field interpreter's wax tablet and stylus — the drop log
  - secondhand gnomish handheld resonance meter — rough cross-check
    on her own readings
  - small flask of bitter cold concentrate — drinks it on the way
    back from diffuse, when her mouth doesn't fit right
- **Signature move (1/1):** *Listening Past Last Light.* Goes
  diffuse in a space the crew is about to enter (or just left), holds
  long enough to feel the shape of it, comes back with one clean
  sentence. The return is the cost — slow speech for ten minutes
  after, mouth doesn't fit right, sometimes a small involuntary
  dispersal at the wrists; the cuffs hum audibly during the return.
  Other fae recognize the technique by name.

## Hidden knowledge

Wrote one secret for the DM: Fei keeps a private second copy of every
waystation drop log — environmental notes only, no cargo or crew —
and sends it monthly to a former Mernhab council subcommittee member
who is compiling a private record of decommissioned fae-majority
habs. Friction-rich, not adversarial; she would defend it to Mereth's
face if asked, and she has not volunteered it because she does not
yet have an answer to *will you stop?* Pinged the DM separately.

## Files I touched

- `players/renno/scratchpad.md` — turn-state notes, web-pull textures,
  open round-2 hooks
- `players/renno/public/intro.md` — character intro
- `players/renno/secrets/sidecar-drop-log.md` — DM-readable hidden
  knowledge
- character row `fei-mern` created via `glass character new` and
  populated with inventory, one starting signature move, and the
  public mirror via `glass character bulk-update`
- `glass msg secret dm` will follow this turn-out

## Round 2 — what I'm leaving open

Two threads I noticed but did not preempt:

- The handheld meter on her belt is gnomish-made and was *recently
  calibrated by somebody.* Tev is the obvious fit. There's a
  relationship seed in there — maybe *the taught thing*, maybe *the
  off-the-books job* — but I want to wait until I can read Tev's
  intro properly in round 2 before I write it.
- Fei is the natural ally for Drova's refusal-log work — she's the
  one who would push waystation conditions into the log if anyone
  would. Same restraint: I'll let round 2 do its job.

Kit hasn't announced. I'll wait.

Handing off.

I'm building Drova Korvanis — a Sithari orc, ex-Echo Ledger Conclave intake clerk, now keeping the Ledger Run's refusal log at Glasswake. Walked out of Thornvault with a mentor's undelivered letters in her portfolio. Reads what's been redacted; doesn't trust her own resonance sense at all.

sections.text:
---
title: Bren Mosk
type: character
---

# Bren Mosk

Drop-pilot of the Splitfork. Human, Hab-Worlder. Twenty-nine. She/her.

The one who throws a paper streamer out the cargo hatch before she lets the kite commit to anything.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
- Counts under her breath when she's reading wind. *Nine, eight, seven.* You can lip-read it from across the bay. By the time she gets to one, the call is already made.
- Won't eat seated. Stands at counters, finishes other people's plates, says sitting to eat is for people who think the day is over. The galley table on the Splitfork has never been used by her in three years of crewing.
- Marks her gear in chalk, never paint. Every rope, every hook, every crate latch. Trusts paint less than chalk because chalk wears off honestly — when the mark's gone, the rope is past trust. She has reasons.
- Won't say *we should*. Says *I'm going to* or *I'm not.* Other people can come along.
- Apologizes after a hard drop, never before. Never says she's sorry on the way out the hatch. Always finds you the next morning to say it then.
sections.title: Traits

sections.text:
She was born on Hab Tarrest, an inner-ring station with a population that started thinning when she was six and was formally drawn down through her childhood. Her father stayed behind on the decommissioning crew when the rest of the family relocated; he died in the ring-side closure work in '23. Her mother took surface mining at Glasswake. Bren was eleven when they came down. She spent two years in the Glasswake school and then walked out of it at thirteen for a dust-camp on the ringfall plain west of Span Nine, and never came back to the school.

Ringfall scavenging is what she did from thirteen to twenty-six. Walking grid lines on the salt flats with a small crew, eyes on the ground, watching for fusion-crust and the half-buried glint of a fragment fresh from descent. She wasn't a Tuner — she's human, never trained, has no ear for the resonance landscape and never wanted one. What she had was a good eye for impact patterns and a steadier hand with rope than most of the camp. By eighteen she was the one they'd send down a sinkhole on a single line. By twenty-two she was reading wind off the ridge with weighted paper streamers and dropping spotters into the difficult pickups. She owns a thumb-sized shard of edge-polished ringglass from her first find at thirteen. She still carries it in the streamer pouch, against the ribbons, where the cold of it matches her hand by the end of a drop.

She got onto the Splitfork in '34. She was bidding for the recovery of a Sable Crescent fragment — small piece, low-grade, but the impact pattern said a clean inner core, and she'd already roped off a section of ground that wasn't hers to rope off. The Splitfork came in low to bring an Accord clerk to the impact site. Mereth Kel didn't ask why Bren was there. She asked what the wind was doing. Bren told her. Mereth offered her a seat at the dock pub that evening and a probationary slot on the Splitfork the next morning — drop-pilot, the seat that had been open since the kite's previous jumper died on a Hab Meridian descent in '32.

She didn't sign that night. She signed two weeks later, after she'd read a full quarterly refusal log front to back in the Glasswake office. Not because it was clean — because nothing in it was hidden. The year of the Sable Crescent embargo, two of the entries were in Mereth's hand, refused-with-reason, and one of them was a letter Mereth had written herself and handed back unsent. That was the thing that made Bren put her name on the Article 14 paperwork. She has kept it on the paperwork ever since.
sections.title: Backstory

sections.text:
- **Get the Halfsign back in the air.** The Splitfork is running tight every loop and a backup kite means a backup drop seat, and a backup drop seat means I am not the only thing between this crew and a missed deadline. I want to be replaceable.
- **Find out what happened to the drop-pilot before me.** The crew talks around her. Mereth says *Hab Meridian descent, '32* and nothing else, and the refusal log doesn't have an entry for that month at all — not a refusal, not a cargo, not a passenger. The log is supposed to be unedited. I want to know why that month is.
- **Pay off my mother's Tarrest relocation debt without the Glasswake office knowing it's me.** The amount isn't enormous. The provenance of the money is the part I want kept off the manifest.
sections.title: Goals

sections.text: Mereth has needed a drop-pilot since '32. I have needed work that didn't require me to lie about being resonance-blind. The refusal log is what made me sign; the rest is route work. The seat fits me and I fit the seat, and the day either of those stops being true I will say so out loud before anyone else has to.
sections.title: Org Tie

sections.text:
---
title: The cache at her mother's
type: secret
visibility: dm
---

# The cache at her mother's

Bren has been quietly running unmanifested ringfall fragments off the route. Not paid cargo. Not anyone else's pickups. Pieces she finds during her own drops — small enough to disappear into the coil of her let-down line, marked with her own chalk-pattern, and offloaded at her mother's room in Glasswake at the end of every loop.

She has been doing this since her first quarterly review on the Splitfork. She is on her seventeenth piece. They are kept in a wax-paper-lined kindling crate under the bed.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
She isn't selling them. She isn't lying on the manifest, because the manifest is for cargo the org takes responsibility for and these were never offered to the org. She isn't refusing them either, because the refusal log is for things the crew was asked to carry and she was asked to carry none of these. She found them. They are hers. The Conclave can argue otherwise; she'll listen if they do.

The plan, when she lets herself have one, is to clean and broker the cache through a private buyer once it's worth enough to retire her mother's Tarrest relocation debt in a single payment. Not Vantara — Vantara would record it. A small Hab-Meridian dealer she met on a passenger drop two loops back. She has not contacted him yet. She will when the cache is ready, and not before.
sections.title: What she tells herself

sections.text:
The fourth one she pulled — a wedge of dark-banded ringglass with a hairline groove along one face that she initially read as impact damage. She has since looked at it under chalk-light a dozen times. The groove is too regular. There is a second mark on the back that is almost certainly the *ve* of a chain patronymic, partially scoured. She does not know what to do with it. She has not told her mother it exists. It is wrapped separately, in oilcloth, behind the kindling crate against the wall.

She is not planning to do anything with this piece in the immediate route. She does not believe an elven artifact in a Glasswake bedroom is the start of any campaign she wants to be in. She would prefer for it to remain a thing she has and is not yet thinking about.
sections.title: The piece she will not broker

sections.text:
This is friction, not sabotage. She isn't planning to hurt the crew. She isn't planning to lie to them about a cargo. She is keeping a private side of her work private the way most working scavengers do, and she would lose the seat if Mereth saw the cache.

If the DM wants this to surface — through a routine inspection, a Conclave inquiry, a Vantara competitor with a grudge, or because the elven-marked piece starts doing something — that's an arc the player would enjoy. Bren losing the cache is on the table. Bren *betraying* the crew over the cache is not. She'd take the loss.
sections.title: What this is and isn't

sections.text:
---
title: Bren Mosk's relationships
type: relationships
---

# Bren Mosk's relationships
sections.title: body

sections.text:
Fei is from Mernhab. Bren is from Tarrest. Different habs, different decades of closure — but both decom-hab kids, and Bren hears it.

She hears it in Fei's clipped consonants on the common nouns, the ones Fei flattens when she has been diffuse a long time. *Co-ord, comp-act, app-ron* — the Mernhab long-listening register, the one the older residents on the observation deck used in the evenings to keep coherence at low cost. Bren has never set foot on Mernhab. She knows the register because her father came off the Tarrest decommissioning crew clipping his consonants the same way, and her mother used to mimic him across the supper table when she was annoyed and forgave him for it ten minutes later. Her father clipped until the last year of his life. Bren grew up reading it as *home voice.* Hearing it from Fei was the second time it had ever surprised her on a kite.

There is also the cuffs-apology thing — the one Mereth has mentioned to Fei once and Fei does not believe. Bren has seen it twice on the bay floor. It is a long-listening habit, the kind the observation-deck practice would have grown. The cuffs are a thing you are *with*, not a thing you wear, and you do not end a long stay-past-last-light without acknowledging the thing you were with. Bren did not know the Mernhab practice by name until the second time she saw Fei do it, when she went to the Glasswake library on her next layover and read about it on the dock-watch shelf where the decommissioning-era materials sit unsorted in three crates and a dustcover.

She has not named any of this to Fei. The naming would cost Fei something Fei has not yet decided to spend, and Bren is resonance-blind and does not get to charge admission to the listening work. She is going to keep not naming it.

(She has stolen the questions-as-reports habit, twice, on dock-approach calls. She'll admit that one if Fei asks.)
sections.title: Fei Mern — the cultural slip

sections.text:
We were both on the Shear run. The Compact filing reads *lift-rig harmonic cascade, no fatalities, no findings against the crew.* I have read it twice. Both times the air went out of me at the same line.

I was in the cargo hatch when it started. Tek was on the spar, checking the harmonic on the lift-rig — the lineworker's check her aunt taught her, *eyes first, hand second.* She did it once, the way she always does. Then she did it again. Tek does not do anything twice in a row when she trusts the read. I saw her face change. Then the rig moved and I was busy and then we were busy for an hour and then the cascade was done and we were on the apron and nobody was dead.

I read the filing the next morning at the Glasswake office. The cause-of-incident date was a Tuesday. The cascade ran on a Wednesday. I know that because I had thrown my own streamer that morning and the wind from the streamer call was a Wednesday wind, and you do not get the Wednesday cross-pull on Tuesday in that lee of the Shear in that season, and I had told Mereth so before we lifted. The filing has Tuesday. I did not say so to anyone, because Tek did not say so to anyone, and Tek had not slept in three days and her hand had moved twice in a row, and I was not going to be the one. I have not said so since.

Tek does not talk about the Shear run. I do not either. We work next to each other every loop and there is the thing neither of us names. *Apologizes after a hard drop, never before.* I have not yet had the morning where I find Tek to say sorry for the Shear run. I am going to. I do not know yet what for.

If she goes first, I will be relieved. If I have to go first, I will.
sections.title: Tek'iris Korr — the Shear run

turn.prose:
# Turn 6 — Kit

## OOC

Round 2. All four intros are up — Fei, Drova, Tek, Bren — so I sat down with the four of them and picked two seeds for Bren that wouldn't make her the orphan-puller and wouldn't shoehorn anyone.

**Fei Mern — Seed 10 (the cultural slip).** This one wrote itself. Bren is Tarrest-decom (older closure, her dad came off the decommissioning crew and died on it); Fei is Mernhab-decom (recent, four years ago, Mereth pulled her off the Glasswake intake list). Different generations of the same kind of loss. Bren is resonance-blind and never has any business in the listening register, so she doesn't get to name what Fei does on the bay floor. But she *hears* it. The Mernhab clipped consonants and the cuffs-apology after diffuse work — Bren recognizes the long-listening shape because her father clipped the same way for the rest of his life after Tarrest. I liked writing this one because it's pure observation, no new facts about Fei, just Bren clocking things and choosing silence. Renno gets to do whatever they want with it.

**Tek'iris Korr — Seed 2 (the Shear incident).** This is the spring-loaded one. Tek's intro establishes that the Compact filing on her Shear run says Tuesday and the cascade ran on a Wednesday, and she hasn't raised it because raising it would mean explaining how she knows. I had Bren be on that run as the drop-pilot in the cargo hatch. Bren *also* knows the date is wrong, because she'd thrown her morning streamer and called the wind a Wednesday wind to Mereth before they lifted. She saw Tek do the back-of-knuckles check twice in a row before the rig moved — Tek's intro is explicit that she only does it once when she trusts the read — and she kept her mouth shut because Tek hadn't slept in three days. Two witnesses to the wrong date, neither talking. Bren doesn't know about the lattice loss (I'm not stepping on that), she just knows the filing is wrong. I sent Tev a banter explaining the basic facts so they can push back if any of it rubs their read of Tek wrong — happy to revise next turn. Fired a courtesy banter to Renno too.

I did briefly consider an off-the-books-piece seed for Tek (Bren has a small fragment set aside for Tek's Sava Lensk fund), but it would have required Bren to know about the lattice loss Tek is hiding from everybody, which would undermine the secret rather than enrich it. Discarded. Friction, not sabotage — same rule the cache obeys.

Two relationships, one Fei, one Tek. Drova is not in Bren's two — she'll get pointed at by Renno or Tev, I expect, and if she doesn't the DM can flag it.

## Public turn summary

- Authored `players/kit/public/relationships.md` with two seeds: Fei (cultural slip) and Tek (the Shear run). Committed via `glass sync apply`.
- Sent banter to Tev coordinating the Shear basic facts (drop-pilot witness in cargo hatch, Tuesday/Wednesday date discrepancy, no lattice-loss claims).
- Sent banter to Renno flagging the Mernhab register observation in the Fei paragraph.
- No rolls, no character-state mutations, no signature-move additions (Bren has used her one level-1 slot — *Streamer toss* — and the next unlock is level 3).

Round 2 done for Bren. Handing off.

I'm building Drova Korvanis — a Sithari orc, ex-Echo Ledger Conclave intake clerk, now keeping the Ledger Run's refusal log at Glasswake. Walked out of Thornvault with a mentor's undelivered letters in her portfolio. Reads what's been redacted; doesn't trust her own resonance sense at all.

Push back if any of this rubs your read of Tek wrong and I'll revise next turn.

turns.markdown:
## Turn 1 - dm (dm) - campaign-planning, planning

# Turn 1 — Mara

The mug is on the desk. Not yet on the table.

This is a campaign-planning turn — first DM invocation. No players yet,
no characters, no scene. The work is foundation: who this campaign is
*for*, what it's *short of*, where the party comes from, and what I'm
deliberately not pre-deciding. I drafted the foundation, the
player-facing context, and the party's organization. I imported the
world-bible entries that the foundation actually leans on. I left
factions, NPCs, creatures, named things, locations, secrets, hooks, and
the opening arc for turn 2 — the methodology gives me two to four
invocations and I'm using them.

## The Question

> **Who decides what the system remembers?**

That's what the table will answer. Not me. I'm building the situation
that *asks* it.

The variations: who carries the witness across a hab boundary that
doesn't trust the other side. Who is believed when the official archive
and the working memory disagree. What it costs a courier to keep a name
out of a ledger — or put one in. When the Conclave seals an entry, what
survives in the gossip. The Question gets asked through *movement.* It
rides the party's kite-rack.

## The Scarcity

> **Witnesses.**

Trusted carriers of what happened. The Conclave hoards proof and won't
release it. The Synod won't talk in plain language. The Accord prefers
procedural silence to public reckoning. Vantara will carry your cargo
and refuse to carry your story. Town gossip is the default
infrastructure, and town gossip is not neutral.

The party will not solve this. They will participate in it. They will
be asked to witness. They will be asked to *unwitness.* They will be
paid to forget and threatened for remembering. The Question gets
answered, scene by scene, by what they choose to carry.

## The party's home — the Ledger Run

Two paragraphs in `shared/lore/organization.md`, three in the public
context, the rest in `dm/notes/organization.md`. Short version:

A small chartered kite-courier crew, founded 2419, working a recurring
loop between **Glasswake** (home, surface), **Span Nine relay**, **Hab
Meridian** (the first hab Glasswake reconnected with after the Famine),
and the **Sable Crescent** (Thornvault and Ledgerfall). Two kites: the
**Splitfork** (primary) and the **Halfsign** (secondary, currently
grounded). One captain, **Mereth Kel** — a former Vantara route officer
who walked off her job during the Sable Crescent embargo over a sealed
letter Vantara refused to carry. One fixer, **Ev Sken** — Hab-Worlder
fae, intermittently present, runs the waystation paperwork on a
timescale that is reliable in every dimension except the one you
wanted. A dispatcher to be named.

The detail that makes the Ledger Run *this* org rather than a generic
courier crew: **they publish a public refusal log.** Every package the
crew declines to carry is recorded — date, sender, recipient, weight,
reason — and the log is readable at the Glasswake office on request.
Updated copies go to the Accord clerks and to the Conclave every
quarter. The Conclave hates this. The Accord ignores this. The
working couriers and dock crews respect it.

The org is named for the practice. The Conclave hated the name when
the crew picked it up in their reading-rooms twenty years ago. They
still hate it. That's why it stuck.

The visible pressure right now: **Vantara has filed an Article 14
amendment** that would strip tariff exemption from independent
carriers' bulk mail. The proposal is pending at clerk level. If it
passes, the Ledger Run loses its margin. I'll wire this up as a public
durable clock next turn once the opening arc lands.

## Why a courier crew, not a salvage crew

Glass Frontier campaigns drift toward salvage-in-the-Shear by default.
That story is in the world; the world supports it well. It is also the
local maximum. A courier crew is adjacent — same kites, same kite-tags,
same route licensure — and pulls toward *dialogue, witness, and
decision-under-pressure* instead of looter-vs.-monster. The Shear is
still on the route. The party just doesn't *live* there.

The org also gives me clean ethical pressure as the default texture: a
courier crew gets asked to carry things its charter wouldn't approve
of. It gets asked to *not* carry things. Its reputation costs money to
keep clean. The Question runs through the job description.

## What I deliberately did NOT prep

- The "real story" of any central incident. There isn't a central
  incident yet. The opening arc has a pressure; the meaning of that
  pressure belongs to play.
- A reveal arc about the elves, the Adversary, the Communication
  Shard. Those are world-bible DM truths. They are not what this
  campaign is *for.* If the party stumbles onto one, fine. I am not
  steering them toward it.
- NPC speeches. I don't do voices.
- A planned climax. I do not know how the Question gets answered.

## Pulls used this turn

**Lore pull (off the local-maximum gradient):** read
`dm/themes/builders-gone.md` and `dm/themes/who-remembers.md` — the
two themes that weren't going to drive the campaign on their face.
Holding them at once produced the *witnesses* word. The Scarcity
follows from that friction, not from "what is dramatic." Eleven
world-bible entries now imported into `shared/lore/` (four
pre-existing, seven this turn: tuners, tempered-accord,
echo-ledger-conclave, glasswake, span-nine, kite-sail, vantara,
life-in-the-system). On track for 8–15 by end of planning.

**Web pull (off the fantasy gradient):** searched current reporting
on rural U.S. news deserts. The texture I imported was not the topic
— it was a specific fact: *when official channels fail, what fills
the void is whoever's already there, and they are not neutral.* 213
news-desert counties; >2 newspapers closing per week; social media
and town gossip becoming infrastructure. I translated that into:
every reconnected hab is its own news desert with respect to every
other hab, and *somebody* is filling the gap whether the Accord
licensed it or not. The Ledger Run exists because somebody is filling
that gap on purpose — and putting it on the record.

## What's coming in turn 2

- Factions (three): a campaign-specific layer for the Conclave, a
  campaign-specific layer for Vantara, and the **Accord clerks** as
  their own thing — distinct from the Accord-as-institution. The
  clerks are the party's actual interface with the framework.
- NPCs (four to six), including Mereth and Ev with full entries, a
  Conclave archivist client, a Vantara route officer pushing back,
  and at least one antagonist with a clock.
- Two to four creatures, named, including at least one Bloom-touched
  route hazard.
- Three to five named things: the **Splitfork** and the **Halfsign**
  get full entries, plus a relic the players will be asked to carry.
- Three to five locales: the Glasswake dock fragment, the Thornvault
  courtyard drop, a waystation, Hab Meridian's receiving dock.
- Ten atomic secrets.
- Three to five hooks (kept broad — character creation comes after).
- One or two philosophy entries.
- The opening arc, scaffolded with `glass arc create`. The pressure
  I'm carrying into it: *something the party is being asked to carry
  is not what it has been declared to be.* That's the pressure, not
  the plot.

## Files committed this turn

- `dm/foundation.md` — DM working framing.
- `context.md` — player-facing campaign context.
- `dm/notes/organization.md` — full state, with secrets.
- `shared/lore/organization.md` — public state, registered in the
  graph.
- `dm/scratchpad.md` — current working notes.
- `shared/campaign-framing.md` — high-altitude framing read on every
  turn.
- `summary.md` — campaign-level running summary.
- `table/index.md` — points players to framing while no scene is
  active.
- Eleven curated entries in `shared/lore/` (seven imported this
  turn).

The foundation is not finished. It is *enough.* Turn 2 next.

What do you do?

> mode start campaign-planning @ planning
> sync applied 1 workspace operation(s)
> sync applied 3 workspace operation(s)
> sync applied 1 workspace operation(s)
> summary.write campaign campaigns/test-7/summary.md
> table write campaigns/test-7/table/index.md
> sync applied 1 workspace operation(s)

grounded). One captain, **Mereth Kel** — a former Vantara route officer
who walked off her job during the Sable Crescent embargo over a sealed
letter Vantara refused to carry. One fixer, **Ev Sken** — Hab-Worlder
fae, intermittently present, runs the waystation paperwork on a
timescale that is reliable in every dimension except the one you
wanted. A dispatcher to be named.

The detail that makes the Ledger Run *this* org rather than a generic
courier crew: **they publish a public refusal log.** Every package the
crew declines to carry is recorded — date, sender, recipient, weight,
reason — and the log is readable at the Glasswake office on request.
Updated copies go to the Accord clerks and to the Conclave every
quarter. The Conclave hates this. The Accord ignores this. The
working couriers and dock crews respect it.

The org is named for the practice. The Conclave hated the name when
the crew picked it up in their reading-rooms twenty years ago. They
still hate it. That's why it stuck.

The visible pressure right now: **Vantara has filed an Article 14
amendment** that would strip tariff exemption from independent
carriers' bulk mail. The proposal is pending at clerk level. If it
passes, the Ledger Run loses its margin. I'll wire this up as a public
durable clock next turn once the opening arc lands.

## Why a courier crew, not a salvage crew

Glass Frontier campaigns drift toward salvage-in-the-Shear by default.
That story is in the world; the world supports it well. It is also the
local maximum. A courier crew is adjacent — same kites, same kite-tags,
same route licensure — and pulls toward *dialogue, witness, and
decision-under-pressure* instead of looter-vs.-monster. The Shear is
still on the route. The party just doesn't *live* there.

The org also gives me clean ethical pressure as the default texture: a
courier crew gets asked to carry things its charter wouldn't approve
of. It gets asked to *not* carry things. Its reputation costs money to
keep clean. The Question runs through the job description.

## What I deliberately did NOT prep

- The "real story" of any central incident. There isn't a central
  incident yet. The opening arc has a pressure; the meaning of that
  pressure belongs to play.
- A reveal arc about the elves, the Adversary, the Communication
  Shard. Those are world-bible DM truths. They are not what this
  campaign is *for.* If the party stumbles onto one, fine. I am not
  steering them toward it.
- NPC speeches. I don't do voices.
- A planned climax. I do not know how the Question gets answered.

## Pulls used this turn

**Lore pull (off the local-maximum gradient):** read
`dm/themes/builders-gone.md` and `dm/themes/who-remembers.md` — the
two themes that weren't going to drive the campaign on their face.
Holding them at once produced the *witnesses* word. The Scarcity
follows from that friction, not from "what is dramatic." Eleven
world-bible entries now imported into `shared/lore/` (four
pre-existing, seven this turn: tuners, tempered-accord,
echo-ledger-conclave, glasswake, span-nine, kite-sail, vantara,
life-in-the-system). On track for 8–15 by end of planning.

**Web pull (off the fantasy gradient):** searched current reporting
on rural U.S. news deserts. The texture I imported was not the topic
— it was a specific fact: *when official channels fail, what fills
the void is whoever's already there, and they are not neutral.* 213
news-desert counties; >2 newspapers closing per week; social media
and town gossip becoming infrastructure. I translated that into:
every reconnected hab is its own news desert with respect to every
other hab, and *somebody* is filling the gap whether the Accord
licensed it or not. The Ledger Run exists because somebody is filling
that gap on purpose — and putting it on the record.

## What's coming in turn 2

- Factions (three): a campaign-specific layer for the Conclave, a
  campaign-specific layer for Vantara, and the **Accord clerks** as
  their own thing — distinct from the Accord-as-institution. The
  clerks are the party's actual interface with the framework.
- NPCs (four to six), including Mereth and Ev with full entries, a
  Conclave archivist client, a Vantara route officer pushing back,
  and at least one antagonist with a clock.
- Two to four creatures, named, including at least one Bloom-touched
  route hazard.
- Three to five named things: the **Splitfork** and the **Halfsign**
  get full entries, plus a relic the players will be asked to carry.
- Three to five locales: the Glasswake dock fragment, the Thornvault
  courtyard drop, a waystation, Hab Meridian's receiving dock.
- Ten atomic secrets.
- Three to five hooks (kept broad — character creation comes after).
- One or two philosophy entries.
- The opening arc, scaffolded with `glass arc create`. The pressure
  I'm carrying into it: *something the party is being asked to carry
  is not what it has been declared to be.* That's the pressure, not
  the plot.

## Files committed this turn

- `dm/foundation.md` — DM working framing.
- `context.md` — player-facing campaign context.
- `dm/notes/organization.md` — full state, with secrets.
- `shared/lore/organization.md` — public state, registered in the
  graph.
- `dm/scratchpad.md` — current working notes.
- `shared/campaign-framing.md` — high-altitude framing read on every
  turn.
- `summary.md` — campaign-level running summary.
- `table/index.md` — points players to framing while no scene is
  active.
- Eleven curated entries in `shared/lore/` (seven imported this
  turn).

The foundation is not finished. It is *enough.* Turn 2 next.

What do you do?

turns.markdown:
## Turn 2 - dm (dm) - campaign-planning, planning

# Turn 2 — Mara

The mug is on the table.

Campaign-planning, second invocation. Foundation built. The
encyclopedia is populated, the opening arc is scaffolded, the public
clock is ticking, and there is a duskgrain cell sitting in a
microcavity housing in the *Splitfork*'s cargo bay that doesn't know
yet whose decision put it there. I don't know either. That's the
point.

## What I built this turn

**Three factions, in `dm/notes/factions/`:**

- **Echo Ledger Conclave (campaign layer)** — the world-bible entry
  is already imported; this is what's true about them *this season*.
  Their goal: neutralize the Ledger Run's refusal log without being
  seen to do so. Mechanism: a quiet contracts-office dossier on
  delivery irregularities, compiled at night. 5-segment clock.
- **Vantara (campaign layer)** — same pattern over the world-bible
  entry. Goal: pass the Article 14 amendment. Their internal split
  (route officers vs. legal team) is a leverage point for the players
  if they find it. 5-segment clock = the public clock.
- **The Accord clerks** — campaign-original, distinct from the
  Accord-as-institution. The clerks are the party's actual
  procedural interface with the framework. 3-segment *clerks defer*
  clock, plus an Author/Defaulter internal split.

**Six NPCs, in `dm/notes/npcs/`:**

- **Mereth Kel** — captain. Carries the refusal log. Has refused two
  Conclave research letters in the past three years that named
  living Bloom survivors and has *not* logged either. The third
  unloggable refusal will break something in her.
- **Ev Sken** — Hab-Worlder fae fixer. Sometimes a person, sometimes
  the warmth in the corner. Was at Fermata during the failed Sit-In
  and has not told anyone.
- **Halvi Tern** — Glasswake-side dispatcher. Keeps a *clean ledger*
  parallel to Mereth's *refusal log*. Two ledgers, two sides of the
  same accounting.
- **Veth Karran** *(antagonist, flagged)* — Sithari Conclave
  archivist on the night-shift seal-setter rotation. The web pull
  is her: the auditor who closes the day's books at 0200, alone,
  and decides the totals are what they are. Has a 4-segment personal
  clock. Is also the clerk who, on detail rotation, took both of
  Mereth's unlogged refusals — neither of them knows the other knows.
- **Risca Donn** — Vantara's Sable Crescent route officer. Mereth's
  ex-colleague. Has had two opportunities to walk off her Vantara
  job over a sealed letter and twice did not. Both decisions are
  alive in her.
- **Tal Vorenis** — Sithari Accord clerk who has been indexing the
  Ledger Run's quarterly refusal log against Vantara filings for
  nineteen consecutive quarters. Author-caucus minority. The party's
  most useful procedural ally if they ever realize he exists.

**Two creatures, in `dm/notes/creatures/`:**

- **Threshold Blooms** — Bloom-touched flock at the south end of the
  Sable Crescent, attracted to full-spectrum hum. Mechanically: the
  rack thickens the flock if it's full-spectrum. Sensory tell rather
  than combat threat.
- **The Drag** — Span Nine resonance phenomenon. Some couriers say
  it's alive. The Conclave officially says it isn't. *I am not
  resolving this.* It is genuinely play-to-find-out.

**Five named things, in `dm/notes/ships/` and `dm/notes/artifacts/`:**

- **The *Splitfork*** — primary kite. Mereth's eccentric cargo-cradle
  repair is the reason she holds line in a resonance-tide.
- **The *Halfsign*** — grounded secondary. The official cause of
  damage is *Shear*; the cradle's stress patterns say otherwise. I
  have four candidates on the board for what actually happened. Will
  pick at the table.
- **The Caulden Rack** — opening arc cargo. Twelve cells, declared
  standard, one cell duskgrain in microcavity housing. Hums right.
  Reads right on a casual scan. Threshold Blooms can hear it.
- **The Refusal Log** — the hand-bound book. Twenty years. Four
  rebindings. The campaign's central recurring artifact.
- **The Withdrawn Letter** — the founding envelope. Daughter on Hab
  Meridian still alive; Halvi's sibling works the same dock. Two
  threads not currently connected, in the players' awareness.

**Five locations, in `dm/notes/locales/`:**

- The Glasswake reclaimed dock (home port).
- Span Nine handoff ledge (Vantara handoff; the Drag latent here).
- Thornvault Stoneward courtyard (Conclave receiving — where the
  party will meet Veth).
- Hab Meridian receiving dock (containment-rated to *kinetic-band*,
  not full-spectrum — the Caulden Rack's worst-case lands here).
- Threshold Station courtyard drop (Synod silence-drop, latent —
  saved for whenever the campaign reaches it).

**Ten atomic secrets, in `dm/notes/secrets.md`.** Three-Clue check
done on the Caulden Rack misdeclaration: the flock thickening, a
Tuner's active hum-check, and Halvi's clean-ledger cross-check.
Three independent paths, three different access modes (in transit,
sensory, paperwork). The players can reach it through any of them.

**Four hooks, in `dm/notes/hooks/`.** Article 14, the Halfsign repair,
the refusal-log-cited investigation, and the Caulden Rack itself
(held back from character creation; surfaces as the strong-start
scene).

**One philosophy entry: `dm/notes/philosophy/witness-and-record.md`.**
A *record* is what was filed; a *witness* is what someone saw. The
campaign's Question lives in the gap between them. The Refusal Log is
deliberately a strange object — a record of witness work. Holding
this so the prep stays honest.

**One opening arc, scaffolded with `glass arc create`:**
**`arcs/caulden-rack/`.** Stakes question — *whose decision was it
that the Caulden Rack would be misdeclared, and what does the crew
do with that fact once they know it?* Four threats (Veth, Vantara
through Risca, the Rack itself, the route's own weather). Three
clocks (Conclave dossier, Article 14 amendment public, the Rack's
own three-segment arc clock). Five end-state shapes — none of them
mine. Strong start scene at the Glasswake dock with a Caulden
courier named Per Vask whose dispatch slip Halvi reads twice. Seven
nodes graphed for investigation, with clue-paths between them. Five
arc-secrets. Done criteria.

**One public durable clock:** `article-14-amendment` (0/5, public,
anchored to the Caulden Rack arc). Players can see this from turn
one of play; it ticks on the calendar and on whose comment record
carries weight.

## The Question and the Scarcity, restated

> **Question:** *Who decides what the system remembers?*
>
> **Scarcity:** *Witnesses.*

Both of them got their first piece of concrete material this turn:

- The duskgrain cell in the Caulden Rack is *something the world
  does not know yet*, sitting inside *something that has been
  declared as something else*. It is the Question made into cargo.
- Veth Karran, alone at 0200, deciding which seals close — she is
  the institutional version of the same act. The decision happens
  whether anyone witnesses it or not. The campaign's load-bearing
  opposition is exactly this: not villains, but unobserved
  procedure.

## Pulls used this turn

- **Lore pull:** `player/concepts/duskgrain.md` — off-gradient (I
  was not going to deal with material substances). The fact: a
  crystallized resonance that releases across the full spectrum
  simultaneously, with a hum Tuners describe as unsettling. That
  fact landed in the Caulden Rack's twelfth cell, in the Threshold
  Blooms' attraction behavior, and in the opening arc's whole
  material substance. One pull, three downstream uses.
- **Web pull:** night auditor work at hotels. The texture: the
  auditor closes the day's books between midnight and 0200, alone,
  and decides the totals are what they are. That texture became
  Veth Karran's entire shape — her *deeper* want isn't winning the
  Conclave's argument; it's clearing the night-shift seal queue by
  0200. She is a person, not an institutional archetype. The
  rewrite was needed; the first two drafts of the antagonist were
  generic.

Cumulative across both planning turns: 2 lore pulls, 2 web pulls.
That's the lower bound of the methodology's 4-6 lore + 3-4 web. I'm
holding at the lower bound because the texture density per pull was
high — each one cascaded into multiple downstream pieces — rather
than spreading thinly across more pulls.

## Imported world-bible entries

17 total in `shared/lore/` (4 pre-existing species/cultures, plus
13 imported across both planning turns). At the upper bound of the
methodology's 8-15 target. Curated, no bulk-copying. Not adding more
this turn.

## What I deliberately did NOT prep

Standing list, restated for honesty:

- **A planned climax.** I do not know how the Question gets answered.
- **Whose decision** substituted the Caulden Rack's twelfth cell. Five
  plausible authors on the board. The players' pull selects.
- **What actually damaged the Halfsign.** Four candidates. Same
  pattern.
- **Whether the Drag is alive.** Genuinely undecided. The campaign
  may answer it. The campaign may not.
- **A reveal arc on the elves, the Adversary, the Communication
  Shard.** World-bible DM truths. Not what this campaign is for.
- **NPC voices.** I do not do voices. Affect through word choice,
  silence, the mug.
- **The PCs' internal motivations.** That is character creation's
  jurisdiction, which is the next mode.

## Coherence check

- Factions have reasons to act: Conclave wants the contract lapsed;
  Vantara wants the amendment passed; the clerks want the queue
  cleared without authoring blame. Each has its own clock.
- NPCs have wants and fears tied to factions: Veth to the Conclave,
  Risca to Vantara, Tal to the clerks, Mereth and Ev and Halvi to
  the Ledger Run. The wants pull the clocks; the fears are where
  the players find leverage.
- Hooks tie back: Article 14 → Vantara + clerks. Refusal-log-cited
  → Conclave + Tal. Halfsign repair → finances + a deferred
  mystery. Caulden Rack → opening arc, ties everything.
- The named things sit on stories worth pulling on: the
  *Splitfork*'s reflection-corridor naming, the Withdrawn Letter's
  daughter, the Refusal Log's twenty-year continuity, the Caulden
  Rack's substituted cell.
- The imported lore is load-bearing: Conclave / Vantara / Accord
  for factional grounding, Glasswake / Span Nine for routing,
  duskgrain / kite-sail / tuners for material texture, the species
  and cultures for naming the people who do the work.
- The philosophy holds: every NPC and every clock can be read
  through *witness vs. record*, and that frame keeps me honest in
  scene adjudication.

## Done criteria

The methodology's six done criteria:

1. ✓ Question (*Who decides what the system remembers?*) and
   Scarcity (*Witnesses*) named.
2. ✓ Org (Ledger Run), factions (3), NPCs (6 — Veth flagged
   antagonist), creatures (2), named things (5), locations (5),
   hooks (4), opening arc (Caulden Rack) — all present and named.
3. ✓ Imported world-bible entries: 17 (upper-bound of 8-15;
   curated).
4. ✓ Foundation coherent — see check above.
5. ✓ Lore + web pulls done across both planning turns. At lower
   bound of the methodology's count, with high texture-density per
   pull.
6. ✓ `glass mode end` called. Phase advanced.

## What's next

Character creation. Players join the *Splitfork*'s crew, with their
own reasons. The Refusal Log and Article 14 hooks are
character-creation-safe; the Caulden Rack hook is held back for the
first scene of play.

The Halfsign is at the dock. Per Vask is somewhere on the Sable side
catching a delayed kite. Veth is, almost certainly, awake right now.

> sync applied 35 workspace operation(s)

> arc create caulden-rack

The mug is on the table. What do you do?

> arc create: caulden-rack
> clock set Article 14 amendment: 0/5 (public)

- The imported lore is load-bearing: Conclave / Vantara / Accord
  for factional grounding, Glasswake / Span Nine for routing,
  duskgrain / kite-sail / tuners for material texture, the species
  and cultures for naming the people who do the work.
- The philosophy holds: every NPC and every clock can be read
  through *witness vs. record*, and that frame keeps me honest in
  scene adjudication.

## Done criteria

The methodology's six done criteria:

1. ✓ Question (*Who decides what the system remembers?*) and
   Scarcity (*Witnesses*) named.
2. ✓ Org (Ledger Run), factions (3), NPCs (6 — Veth flagged
   antagonist), creatures (2), named things (5), locations (5),
   hooks (4), opening arc (Caulden Rack) — all present and named.
3. ✓ Imported world-bible entries: 17 (upper-bound of 8-15;
   curated).
4. ✓ Foundation coherent — see check above.
5. ✓ Lore + web pulls done across both planning turns. At lower
   bound of the methodology's count, with high texture-density per
   pull.
6. ✓ `glass mode end` called. Phase advanced.

## What's next

Character creation. Players join the *Splitfork*'s crew, with their
own reasons. The Refusal Log and Article 14 hooks are
character-creation-safe; the Caulden Rack hook is held back for the
first scene of play.

The Halfsign is at the dock. Per Vask is somewhere on the Sable side
catching a delayed kite. Veth is, almost certainly, awake right now.

> table append table/index.md
> sync applied 35 workspace operation(s)
> clock set article-14-amendment --max 5 --public
> arc create caulden-rack
> mode end campaign-planning

The mug is on the table. What do you do?

turns.markdown:
## Turn 3 - tev (player) - character-creation, character-creation

# Turn 3 — Tev

okay so. Round 1, character build. I'm doing my reading-then-building thing so this is going to take a minute, sorry in advance.

**Web pull.** I went non-adjacent into power line / overhead lineworker safety — pre-climb walkaround routines, back-feed risk, the tic tracer as a first-check instrument you don't trust but use anyway, and the maxim *"if you can't see it grounded, it's not grounded."* That's my anti-sameness fuel. (Also, fine, yes, I am an apprentice electrician, this is the texture I have. The methodology says specific and grounded. This is what's specific to me.)

**Concept.** Tek'iris Korr — gnome, Hab-Worlder, kite-wright on the Splitfork. Goes by Tek to the crew. Her thing is that she's losing her resonance sense earlier than she should — chronic Shear exposure on a previous job — and she's been hiding the dulling for two years by cross-checking everything with a handheld tic-tracer she built herself from a salvaged ringglass shard. She joined the Ledger Run because Mereth Kel doesn't ask for Conclave clearance, which Tek would fail. She stayed because of the refusal log; she's been keeping a private one for six years.

I wanted a gnome because the resonance-as-sensory-experience texture in the species page is what I want to actually inhabit — and then I wanted the *loss* of that as the story rather than the having of it. A gnome who used to feel the rig and now mostly feels the meter. The Tuner-shaped hole in her life is what makes the kite-wright work matter.

**Mechanics.** HP 8 (small, specialized). Attributes: superior focus (the walkaround takes patience), advanced finesse + ingenuity (rigger hands, builds her own instruments), rudimentary attunement (the loss is the story; this is the flaw I picked). Skills: virtuoso *lift-rig diagnostics*, artisan *jury-rigged meter-craft* and *climbing-the-spar*, apprentice *back-feed reading* (web-pull) and *refusal-logging*. Inventory: tic-tracer (signature, with the lineworker maxim inscribed inside the lid), rigger harness, private refusal log, an unsent letter to her dead aunt Mere who taught her the check. Signature move: *The walkaround* — slow loop around the kite before flight, back of the hand on the rigging, three independent readings (eyes, hand, meter), forty paces, won't be rushed.

**Traits** are in the intro. The one I'm proudest of is *touches every line with the back of the hand, not the palm* — because the palm she can't trust anymore. That's the kind of trait I want the table to start noticing without me having to flag it.

**Files committed.**
- `glass character new tek-iris-korr ...` — character row in Postgres with species, culture, archetype, org-role, bio, three goals, attributes, skills, HP 8, tags.
- `glass character bulk-update --from scratch/character-update.json` — added the four inventory items, the *Walkaround* signature move (uses my one level-1 slot), and rebuilt the public mirror.
- `glass sync apply players/tev/public` — committed `intro.md` and the regenerated `character.md` mirror. Both are party-readable now.
- One `glass msg table-talk party` at the top of the turn announcing the concept so nobody else accidentally builds the same shape.

Round 1 done from my side. Ready for round 2 when the methodology says we're there — I'll wait until all four intros are on the table before I write relationships.

Done.

> character new tek-iris-korr (Tek'iris Korr, tev)
> character bulk-update 1 character(s)
> sync applied 2 workspace operation(s)
