turn.prose:
---
title: Turn 12 — Mara — Round 2 review, character-creation closing
turn_id: test-7-t0012
speaker: dm
mode: character-creation
---

# Turn 12 — Mara

The mug lands on the desk a little louder than usual. Not a cut. A page-turn.

I read all three new relationships files twice. I read kit's again so I had the four of them side by side. The room is quiet enough that I can hear the kettle in the next room shifting on the burner.

## Tek and Bren — the Shear

*If she goes first, I will be relieved. If I have to go first, I will.* — Bren.

Two witnesses. One Wednesday wind. One filing that says Tuesday and one second pass on the haul line that nobody has yet asked about and one count-of-four at every walkaround.

Tev — what I love about your side of this is that you didn't soften it. The second pass on the line was the *last time the old sense was fully working,* and Tek did not know that yet. That sentence is now canon. Lattice-loss has a moment now, and the moment has a witness who saw the gesture without reading what was inside it. Bren saw the face change between the two passes. Bren wrote down a count of four at every walkaround since. Neither of you wrote *we will talk about it.* You both wrote *we will, eventually, and I do not know which one of us will be the one.* That is the texture I am going to play to.

I am not deciding whose Compact-filing signature put a Tuesday on a Wednesday. I have candidates. The right answer will be the one that costs the most when it surfaces.

## Tek and Drova — the receipt

*...I notice that I have started leaving the tic-tracer in my coat pocket instead of my workbox on days she is on the manifest.* — Tek.

*She has the tools to know and she has chosen not to know, and she does not yet have a clean story for why.* — Drova.

Both of you wrote this from the inside of the same silence. Tek matched the *Korvanis* on the wage book against the *Korvanis* on a receipt and copied a name into the back of her refusal log and did not ask. Drova has the refusal-log archive at her elbow and has chosen, three years running, not to spend thirty minutes confirming what she half-knows. Neither of you knows what the other knows. Both of you are carrying the shape of it.

What I want to mark, because both of you went there without my asking: the *graduated-certainty register* — *consistent with*, *cannot be eliminated as a source*, *no evidence to support* — is now the language Drova uses to think to herself, not just to file in the binder. That register has crossed the page. Tek, you wrote the tic-tracer-in-the-coat-pocket detail the same week. Both of you adjusted what you carry on your person on days the other one is on the manifest. Neither of you has named it. That's the relationship.

I am not deciding yet whether the request slip for Tek's transcript was Veth Karran's or routine annex-to-curatorial upstreaming. Drova said she is afraid the answer might be either, and I am going to honor that fear by keeping it answerable in either direction until the table makes me choose.

## Drova and Fei — Mernhab

*She is going to. She does not know yet how.* — Drova.

*I have not yet decided who I would be willing to owe the answer to... so I have been composing the question for three years.* — Fei.

*Feishara* on the upstream copy of the intake list, in small careful Korvanis hand, before Mereth ever walked the Glasswake billet queue. The name sat in Drova's head from a page and stayed. She did not connect *Feishara* to *Fei* for three months. The connection landed on a quarterly review afternoon and Drova put the page down and walked out to the dock for ten minutes. She has not told Fei.

Fei is composing the question. Fei has been composing the question for three years. Fei has noticed Drova hold her tongue for three full breaths before answering people she does not know well, and the pause shrink for people she does. Fei has noticed the *Korvanis* smaller than the *Drova* on the Accord signature. Fei has not asked.

Renno, sumi — what you have both written is *a refusal log kept by two of us, in two different shapes, in the same room.* That's the line. I am going to underline it and put it in the campaign's bones. The Splitfork has a refusal log on a wall. These two have one between them, unwritten, mutual, and observed. It is the same shape as Mereth's, by accident or instinct, and it will start to matter in scene when one of them moves first.

The *Mernhab resonance diagnostic packet* that may or may not have passed through Thornvault intake before being pulled back up the line — I am not deciding today whether Drova really remembers it or whether she has built the memory because she wishes she had been the clerk who could give Fei the page. Both are live. Renno, you wanted the diagnostic findable but expensive. Sumi, you put the diagnostic *behind Drova's own doubt about her own memory*, which is the most expensive place you could have put it. Thank you for that.

## Fei and Tek — the taught thing

*I have not yet found a way to tell her that does not also break the etiquette. I will keep trying.* — Fei.

Tek showed Fei how to do the back-of-hand check on a fae's coherence-aid cuffs in the maintenance bay one afternoon, without ceremony, with maybe three words, and Fei has done it that way every morning since. Fei has not said *thank you* in a sentence with the word *thank* in it. Tek may or may not know Fei still does it.

Renno — the seed isn't one-sided just because Tek didn't write back. The two of you are now the two people on the kite who maintain your own instruments and do not bring them to the wright except for instrument tuning. Tek has her tic-tracer. Fei has the secondhand handheld meter recalibrated four times and the cuffs themselves. Neither of you asks the wright about the actual thing the meter is checking. Neither of you asks the other. That is the relationship — practitioners of not-asking, recognising each other across the kite without naming it. The back-of-hand check is the proof on the page. I am going to play to it. When Tek sees Fei do the check, eventually, in a scene where the seeing matters, that will be the moment. I will not chase it. I will let it come.

## On the party

Drova is not the orphan any more. Every PC is held in another PC's hand now: Tek is pointed at by Bren, by Drova, by Fei; Drova by Tek and Fei; Fei by Bren and Drova; Bren by Tek. The party's web is asymmetric — Tek is at the centre of three relationships, Bren at one, and that asymmetry is *because of who they are*, not because anybody got short-changed. Tek is the kite-wright, and the kite-wright is who you notice when you do not yet have language for what you are noticing. Bren is the drop-pilot who came in for the Shear run and stayed; she is who Tek and Fei orbit, not the other way around. The shape fits.

What I am keeping, because I do not want to lose it: every one of the four relationships is a *withheld* relationship. Nobody on this kite has said the thing. Two witnesses, two name-matchers, one wage-book hand-match, one back-of-hand check taught in three words. Four silences kept differently. Sumi wrote it most explicitly — *the etiquette is itself a form of refusal log* — and that sentence applies to the whole party. Mereth's refusal log on the wall is the public version. The four of you are the private version, four ways.

That is a party I can run for. I am going to run for it.

## What I am holding back

The same things I held back at Round 1 end, plus a little more:

- I am not naming whose hand wrote *Tuesday* on the Compact filing.
- I am not saying whether the request slip that pulled Tek's transcript was Veth Karran's, or whether Drova's third observation named the same archivist who pulled it. Both are live. Drova's fear that it could be either is the load-bearing piece, and I am preserving that.
- I am not saying whether the Mernhab resonance diagnostic actually existed as a document or whether Drova has rebuilt it from the shape of having been a clerk who routed many such things. Both are live. The question of *what Drova's memory does when she cannot afford it to be wrong* is now in the campaign.
- I am not naming the previous Splitfork drop-pilot, and I am not saying who skipped that month in the refusal log.
- The elven-marked piece in Bren's cache stays under the bed. Not for this arc.

These are the questions that will pay for themselves when the table asks them, and not before.

## Closing the mode

Round 2 is closed. Both rounds genuinely done. Four PCs, four intros, four relationships files, every PC pointed at by at least one other PC, no orphans, no off-spec items, no revision requests. The character-creation phase ends here.

`glass mode end character-creation`.

Next phase is prelude-arc: two short scenes before the main campaign opens. I will start it on the next DM turn. The four of you will be on the Splitfork, in the morning, on a day with a manifest. I am not going to telegraph which of the threads you've handed me I am going to reach for first. You will find out the same way the four of you will.

## Turn summary

- Read all three new `players/*/public/relationships.md` files (tev, sumi, renno). Round 2 closed. Every PC has between 1 and 2 outbound relationships; every PC is pointed at by at least one other. Done criteria met.
- Drova is no longer the orphan PC. Tek is the most-pointed-at; that is correct.
- Banter coordination on shared seeds (tev↔kit on seed 2, tev↔sumi on seed 7, sumi↔renno on seed 11, renno↔tev on seed 14) is on the record. Specifics line up across PC pairs. No revision requests.
- DM scratchpad updated with Round 2 closure notes — Drova's graduated-certainty register has crossed from the binder onto the page, the tic-tracer/coat-pocket on Drova-manifest days is canon, the Splitfork's private second refusal log between Drova and Fei is canon, the back-of-hand-as-taught is canon. Held in `dm/scratchpad.md`.
- Table updated: `table/index.md` and `table/scene.md` now reflect the end of character-creation and the move to prelude-arc.
- `glass mode end` run. Phase moves to prelude-arc on the next DM turn.

Mug stays on the desk. The kettle has stopped shifting. Next turn opens a scene.

...what do you do?
turn.speaker: dm

sections.text:

arc_id: prelude
type: scene-play

# Scene: the apron before light

Glasswake reclaimed dock, the Splitfork's apron berth. Pre-dawn. The
dock lamps are still on; you can see your breath. The Splitfork is
loaded for the outbound loop except for the last bag, which Halvi is
walking the paperwork on inside.

Where you are:

- **Tek** - spar side of the apron, coat on, just beginning the
  walkaround.
- **Drova** - at the apron lectern, refusal-log day-page open, the
  archive shelf-box on the floor by her ankle.
- **Fei** - coming back from the dock corridor, where she went out to
  listen for first-light. Cuffs steady. Slow-speech window almost
  closed.
- **Bren** - at the cargo hatch, streamer pouch hanging at her chest,
  chalk on her left sleeve.

Mereth is in the office with Halvi Tern finishing the manifest. Ev is
somewhere warm, mostly inside himself. The apron is otherwise yours.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
In the dock corner - has been here since before any of you got in - a
Hab-Worlder woman in her mid-fifties is sitting on the forwarding
bench with a basket on her lap and a small parcel of waxed paper
between her hands. She has not interrupted. She has been waiting.

- Inka Vell, Hab-Worlder, widow, mid-fifties, came down from Hab
  Meridian on last night's passenger-kite. She has a small sealed
  packet - waxed paper, one cord, no seal - that she wants the
  Splitfork to carry up the line. The recipient name on the outside
  is Eshil Caur, Sable Crescent. She has a basket of dried apricots
  she will give to whoever decides - not as a bribe, she will explain
  if anyone asks; in her hab nobody arrives at a dock empty-handed.
sections.title: Who else is here

sections.text:
You have somewhere between fifteen and twenty minutes before Mereth
comes out of the office. The lift window is at first light. The day
is starting.

You may have noticed (Drova will have, by the time she opens the
day-page; the rest of you may pick it up from her or from the name
itself) - Eshil Caur is already on the refusal log. Two years and
four months ago, the office refused to carry a Conclave-marked
summons to that recipient at the request of a Crescent advocate.
Mereth signed the refusal. It has been on the log since.

This packet is small. It is unmarked. It is from a different sender.
It may be nothing. It may not be nothing. Inka is waiting for
somebody to look at her and say something.
sections.title: What is open

sections.text:
- Mereth's call. She will make one if the four of you do not. The
  scene is asking what kind of crew you are when she is not in the
  room.
- The Caulden Rack. The outbound loop's signature cargo is not loaded
  yet - the refinery courier is not due until tomorrow morning.
sections.title: What is not on the table

---
scene_id: prelude-opening
arc_id: prelude
type: scene-play
status: open
visibility: dm
---

# Prep: prelude-opening
sections.title: body

sections.text:
End of character-creation. Round 2 closed. Four PCs authored, four
relationships files written, every PC pointed at by at least one
other. No prior in-fiction scenes. The campaign opens here.
sections.title: 1. Recap

sections.text:
Pre-dawn at the Glasswake reclaimed dock. Splitfork berthed and
loaded for the outbound loop except the last bag (which Halvi is
still papering inside). All four PCs at their usual stations: Tek on
the spar, Drova at the apron lectern, Fei coming back from the dock
corridor, Bren at the cargo hatch. Mereth and Halvi inside finishing
the manifest. Ev mostly absent.

In the dock corner, on the forwarding bench, **Inka Vell** —
Hab-Worlder widow, mid-fifties, came down on the night
passenger-kite. Small sealed packet in waxed paper, one cord, no
seal. Recipient name written on the outside: *Eshil Caur*, Sable
Crescent. Inka has a basket of dried apricots for whoever decides.
She has been waiting on the bench since before the PCs got in. She
has not interrupted.

The hook *Eshil Caur is already on the refusal log* lands as soon as
Drova opens the day-page (or any PC reads the name and asks). Two
years four months ago, the office refused to carry a Conclave-marked
summons to that recipient at the request of a Crescent advocate.
Mereth signed the refusal. The packet now is small, unmarked, from
a different sender. It may be nothing. It may not.

The four PCs have a fifteen-to-twenty-minute window before Mereth
comes out and decides. The scene is asking what kind of crew this
is when she is not in the room.
sections.title: 2. Strong start

sections.text:
- **Drova opens the archive.** Pulls the original refusal-log entry
  on Eshil Caur. Reads the conditions Mereth recorded. Decides
  whether the new packet meets the same shape or a different one.
  Stake: the refusal log gets cited *by the crew* in the crew's own
  hands for the first time on screen. Hook: she walks her hand to the
  shelf-box without breaking eye contact with Inka.
- **Tek does the walkaround and finds something.** The signature move
  surfaces on screen. She may notice that the apron-lee humidity is
  already shifting — the Shear front is closer than the morning
  forecast, which sets up scene 2's tracker. Or she may notice
  nothing, which is also true to her practice. Stake: Tek's
  walkaround becomes the table's anchor for *the kite is what is in
  this scene too*. Hook: the back-of-hand check on the haul line.
- **Fei reads Inka.** Or she reads the corridor for the front. Or
  both — but *Listening Past Last Light* costs ten minutes of slow
  speech and the lift window is closing. Stake: whether Fei spends
  the move now or saves it for the action scene. Hook: she does the
  cuffs-apology when she comes back.
- **Bren talks to Inka.** Drop-pilot's posture is *I'm going to / I'm
  not*. She may simply ask the woman the question nobody else has
  yet asked. Stake: the conversation lands with Inka before the
  others have decided in committee. Hook: she steps off the cargo
  hatch toward the bench.
- **The packet itself surfaces.** Drova's *Angle* signature move can
  read indentations through waxed paper without breaking the cord —
  the page above this packet may have left ghosts of pen-pauses, a
  crease, a return-address erasure. Stake: the table learns whether
  *Inka* wrote the recipient or whether somebody else did. Hook:
  Drova tilts the packet flat against the lectern and lets the dock
  lamp rake across it.
sections.title: 3. Possible directions

sections.text:
- **Inka Vell** *(new — stub here)*. Hab-Worlder widow, fifty-five,
  Hab Meridian. Late husband Den Vell, brother to Eshil Caur in
  Sable Crescent. Came down on the night passenger-kite specifically
  to walk the packet to the dock; would not entrust it to a Hab
  Meridian forwarder. Wants this delivered before the year-mark of
  her husband's death (which is in eleven days). She does not know
  the recipient is on the Splitfork's refusal log. She knows that
  Eshil and Den had not spoken in nineteen years. The packet is what
  Den left Eshil; she has not opened it. She believes she is doing
  what her husband would have asked her to do if he had asked. She
  wants this off her hands. She is patient and does not interrupt.
  *Promote to dm/notes/npcs/inka-vell.md after the scene.*
- **Mereth Kel** *(off-screen for the window of this scene; in the
  office with Halvi)*. Wants the manifest closed cleanly. Will come
  out at first light and ask whether it is closed. Will respect a
  decision the crew has made; will not respect a question handed to
  her if it could have been a decision. (Per
  `dm/notes/npcs/mereth-kel.md` if it exists; else the org file.)
- **Halvi Tern** *(off-screen with Mereth)*. Bookkeeper / dispatcher.
  Cross-checking the day's documents. Will notice the Vell name on
  any refusal-log entry the crew files this morning, because she
  cross-checks Drova's day-page against her own clean ledger as a
  matter of routine.
- **Han Pellow** *(dock master, off-screen until scene 2)*. Glasswake
  municipal. He is who calls the lift window. Will arrive at first
  light with the day's call.
sections.title: 4. NPCs in play

sections.text:
- **No antagonist on screen this scene.** The Conclave dossier
  (Veth) does not surface. The arc-level pressure for the prelude is
  *the crew making a decision the captain is not in the room for*,
  not external opposition.
- **Honest pressure source for the action scene that follows**:
  Shear-edge front closing the lift window. Not a creature; a
  condition.
- **Clocks**: do not tick `article-14-amendment` this scene; do not
  open the Conclave dossier clock. The prelude action scene will
  introduce the **lift window** scene tracker (4 segments) when it
  opens.
sections.title: 5. Antagonists, creatures, threats

sections.text:
- **The Splitfork** *(loaded for outbound)*. Tek will walk her.
  Bren will streamer-call when the front closes in scene 2.
- **The Refusal Log** — both the wall-bound office copy and the
  day-page Drova has open at the lectern. The original Eshil Caur
  entry is in the archive shelf-box at her ankle.
- **The Vell packet** *(new)*. Small, waxed paper, one cord, no
  seal. Recipient *Eshil Caur*, Sable Crescent. Sender unwritten on
  the outside. Contents unknown to Inka, the PCs, and me.
sections.title: 6. Named things in play

sections.text:
Drawing from the wider list — none of the Caulden Rack arc-secrets.
Atomic-secrets technique; pick from this short list as appropriate:

1. **(About Inka.)** Inka has not opened the packet. She believes
   it contains a single object her husband Den left for his brother.
   She does not know what the object is and is afraid that if she
   opened it she would not be able to close it again with the same
   intention. *Surfaces if Bren or Fei asks the question directly.*
2. **(About the original refusal.)** Mereth's two-year-four-month-old
   refusal of the Conclave-marked summons to Eshil Caur was filed
   without a stated reason in the public log — *refused, no reason
   given* — but the office's internal note (which Drova can find in
   the archive shelf-box if she looks) says: *advocate's request
   inconsistent with subject's standing; no harm visible in carry,
   no good visible either*. *Surfaces if Drova pulls the archive.*
3. **(About Eshil Caur.)** Eshil Caur is alive, in Sable Crescent,
   and is a former Conclave reading-room steward who left the
   service shortly before Drova arrived at Thornvault. He is not on
   any other office's refusal log. He is not the senior archivist
   from Drova's third observation. He is not anyone Drova
   recognises. *Surfaces only if directly asked, and only as
   *consistent with* and *no evidence to suggest otherwise*.*
sections.title: 7. Secrets that might surface

sections.text:
Notes to myself; the scene answers what the scene answers.

- Will the crew decide together, or will one of them decide and the
  others ratify?
- Does Drova pull the original entry, or does she trust the day-page
  she already has?
- Does Tek's walkaround get done in this scene, or does she defer
  it to the action scene under wind?
- Does Bren talk to Inka before the others have a chance to commit?
- Does Fei spend *Listening Past Last Light* on the corridor, on
  Inka, or save it?
- What does the crew choose to do with Inka's apricots?
sections.title: 8. Open questions

frontmatter.title: Mara's scratchpad — turn 12, Round 2 review, mode closing

sections.text:
---
title: Mara's scratchpad — turn 12, Round 2 review, mode closing
---

# Where I am

Character-creation, turn 12. DM review at the end of Round 2.
All four PCs are authored, all four relationships files are written,
no orphans. Mode ends this turn. Next phase: prelude-arc.

The four PCs:

- **Tek'iris Korr** (tev) — gnome Hab-Worlder, Splitfork kite-wright,
  off-the-books. Lattice-loss from the Shear run two years ago,
  hidden via a scratch-built tic-tracer. Wednesday/Tuesday Compact
  filing discrepancy. HP 8.
- **Drova Korvanis** (sumi) — Sithari orc, ex-Echo Ledger Conclave
  intake clerk, now the Splitfork's refusal-log clerk. Holding Senna
  Talindra's undelivered sealed letters at Mer Vasht's pawn shop. HP 10.
- **Fei Mern** (renno) — fae Hab-Worlder, decom-hab kid from Mernhab,
  Splitfork listener. Keeps a private sidecar drop log to Velsk Tarn,
  ex-council member compiling a decommissioning record. HP 8.
- **Bren Mosk** (kit) — human Hab-Worlder, drop-pilot,
  resonance-blind, Tarrest decom kid. Private cache of unmanifested
  ringfall fragments at her mother's, including one piece with what
  may be a partial chain-patronymic mark. HP 10.
sections.title: body

sections.text:
- Attributes: each PC has exactly 2 advanced, 1 superior, 1
  rudimentary, 3 standard. ✓
- Skills: each PC has 1 virtuoso, 2 artisan, 2 apprentice. ✓
- HP: 8 or 10, none over. ✓
- Inventory: 4 items each with named signature item. ✓
- Public intros include Traits, Backstory, Goals, Org Tie sections. ✓
- One signature move per PC at level 1 (per character.md mirror). ✓

No off-spec items. No revision requests sent.
sections.title: Mechanical compliance

sections.text:
These are conjunctions between player canon and my prep that the
players don't yet know I've noticed:

### Drova ↔ Veth Karran (the night seal-setter)

Drova filed three internal observations about Sable Crescent
transcript redactions, all in the same frequency band, all by a
department she names obliquely (in her own intro). The third one
named the senior archivist. She was reassigned and given a
confidentiality binder.

Veth Karran is my Conclave antagonist — night-shift seal rotation, the
person who decides what gets sealed before morning, deep want is keeping
her 0200 queue clear. The reassignment-after-the-third-observation
shape is consistent with someone on the night seal rotation deciding
*Drova is what is interrupting my queue*. Whether Veth was the
specific clerk who set the seals or simply approved the reassignment
is open — I am not yet committing. The shape is right.

This means: if play surfaces Drova returning to Thornvault, or if the
Conclave dossier on the Ledger Run (secret #7) starts to need a face,
Veth and Drova have an existing history that neither of them has
publicly acknowledged. Drova's binder may have been signed under
Veth's seal.

I will not write this as DM canon yet. I will let play ask.

### The previous Splitfork drop-pilot

Bren introduced a refusal-log month-gap: the month the previous
drop-pilot died (Hab Meridian descent, '32), the refusal log has no
entries at all. Not refused, not carried. That is a hole in a
document Mereth founded on the principle of no edits.

This is a player handing me a hook. Accepting as canon. Implications:

- Mereth either has not noticed (Bren's belief; consistent with
  Mereth's character), or has noticed and not addressed it (possible;
  more painful). I will play to find out.
- The previous drop-pilot now has a name and a fate I am holding
  back. Candidate names: not yet committed. Probably one syllable.
- The month-gap interacts with secret #2 (the Halfsign's "Shear
  damage" is wrong) — both are gaps in the Splitfork's own record
  about events involving its own kites. I had not planned this
  parallel. I am noticing it now and letting it sit.

If Bren pulls the thread, the question is *who told the office to
skip that month*. The answer is not Mereth. The answer is — I will
decide when the table asks.

### The Wednesday/Tuesday Shear gap

Two PCs (Tek, Bren) were on the Shear run two years ago. The Compact
filing reads Tuesday. The cascade ran Wednesday. Neither said so at
the time. Neither has said so since. Tek does not know Bren noticed;
Bren does not know about Tek's lattice loss.

This is a player-authored open secret with two witnesses. It is now
canon that the Compact filing has the wrong date. Whoever signed the
filing wrote a Tuesday where a Wednesday belonged. That signature is
recoverable from public Compact records. I have not yet decided
whose signature it is, but the candidates are interesting:

- The Splitfork's filing crew at the time (Ev? Mereth herself?).
- A Compact clerk who wrote down what they were told.
- A Conclave-adjacent reviewer who pre-approved the cause-of-incident.

The Tuesday is on the page because somebody put it there. *Who*
opens the question of *why the right date wouldn't fit*.

### Mernhab decommissioning

Renno introduced Velsk Tarn (Mernhab council subcommittee member who
left after the decommissioning vote and is compiling a private
record) as Fei's sidecar recipient. Fei sends environmental-only
data once a month from waystation drops. This is friction, not
sabotage — player has been explicit.

I want to be careful with this. The Mernhab story is now load-bearing
for Fei and for the campaign. Velsk Tarn is off-stage but available.
*Who voted Mernhab down, and what diagnostic they suppressed* is a
genuinely open question I have not yet answered. I would like to
honor renno's "I want the diagnostic, not the press release" goal by
making the diagnostic *findable but expensive*. Probably tied to a
Conclave reading-room or a Thornvault basement.

The cuffs are the last working pair in the system. That detail can
do work later — if Fei's cuffs fail in scene, the search for a
replacement leads exactly back to the diagnostic question.

### Bren's elven-marked fragment

Kit's secret: one piece in the cache has what may be the *ve* of a
chain patronymic, partially scoured. Player explicitly says "I do not
believe an elven artifact in a Glasswake bedroom is the start of any
campaign she wants to be in" and would prefer it remain not-yet
thought about.

Respecting that. Will not surface this in the opening arc. If a
later arc wants an elven thread, it is there. Until then it sits
under the bed.

### Org-side echoes I'm holding

- Mereth and Senna — both Sithari adjacent to Conclave records work.
  Plausible they overlapped at some point. I will not invent this
  yet, but they are in the same generation.
- Halvi's clean ledger (parallel to Mereth's refusal log) and the
  refusal log month-gap that Bren named: Halvi may have a memory of
  that month that Mereth does not. Three-Clue-rule-style:
  - Clue A: refusal log gap (already canon).
  - Clue B: Halvi's clean ledger from that month — does it have
    Splitfork entries? Probably yes, which makes the absence in the
    refusal log louder.
  - Clue C: Compact filing from the previous drop-pilot's death — is
    it on the public record at all?
  I am not building this arc yet. Filing it.
sections.title: Accidental connections I want to remember

sections.text:
- I did not name Veth Karran. The Drova ↔ Veth connection is mine to
  hold until play asks.
- I did not commit to who killed the previous drop-pilot or
  doctored the refusal log month-gap. Bren's question is the
  question.
- I did not commit to who signed the Wednesday-as-Tuesday filing.
- I did not name Sava Lensk (Tek's folk practitioner) as needing
  more prep yet. She is a known-named NPC now; if play moves toward
  Tek's lattice realignment, I will write her a notes stub.
- I did not commit to who, on the Mernhab council, voted yes/no/no
  on decommissioning.

These are the *good* DM moves to hold. The party gave me enough hooks
to run a whole arc on. I am going to let them choose which ones to
pull.
sections.title: What I held back from the turn prose

sections.text:
I deliberately did not seed party shape during planning, and the four
PCs that resulted are:

- A Hab-Worlder gnome kite-wright with lattice loss (Tek)
- A Sithari orc clerk with the Conclave behind her (Drova)
- A fae decom-hab listener with two record-commitments (Fei)
- A human ringfall scavenger turned drop-pilot, resonance-blind (Bren)

Four cultures (Sithari, Hab-Worlder x3 of which one fae, one gnome,
one human, one human-on-the-surface), four professions, four
relationships with the Conclave (lapsed, ex-staff, claimant-in-waiting,
indifferent), four relationships with resonance sense (lattice loss,
distrusts own, attunement-advanced and trained, resonance-blind).

The party is *not* one shape. The methodology's anti-sameness rigor
held. I will let the party play the way it built — heterogeneous,
asymmetric, four very different relationships to the same Question.
sections.title: Anti-sameness check on the party

sections.text:
- Sumi, Renno, Tev each write 1-2 relationship seeds.
- Drova is the orphan. Somebody needs to point at her.
- I review again after Round 2 closes.
- Then `glass mode end` and the campaign moves to prelude-arc.
sections.title: Round 2 plan

sections.text:
### The four relationships, settled

- **Bren → Fei (seed 10, cultural slip), Bren → Tek (seed 2, Shear).**
  Already-canon from turn 7.
- **Tek → Bren (seed 2, mirror).** Two witnesses to a Wednesday wind.
  The second pass on the haul line was the last time the old sense
  was fully working. Lattice-loss now has a moment with a witness
  who saw the gesture without reading what was inside it.
- **Tek → Drova (seed 7, the receipt).** Tek matched *Korvanis* on
  the wage book against *Korvanis* on the Glasswake annex
  request-receipt and copied the name into the back of her refusal
  log. Has not asked. Has started leaving the tic-tracer in her coat
  pocket on Drova-manifest days.
- **Drova → Tek (seed 7, mirror).** Drova does not remember the
  pull. She has the refusal-log archive at her elbow and has chosen,
  three years running, not to spend thirty minutes verifying. The
  load-bearing piece is the *chosen not to know*.
- **Drova → Fei (seed 11, decommissioned hab).** *Feishara* on the
  upstream copy of the Mernhab intake list, '34, Drova's hand. The
  connection landed on a quarterly review afternoon three months
  after Fei joined the kite. Plus: a memory of a Mernhab resonance
  diagnostic packet that Drova does not trust her own memory of.
- **Fei → Drova (seed 11, mirror).** Three years composing the
  question. *The etiquette is itself a form of refusal log — kept by
  two of us, in two different shapes, in the same room.* That line
  goes into the campaign's bones.
- **Fei → Tek (seed 14, the taught thing).** Back-of-hand check on
  the cuffs. Taught once, three words, no ceremony, four years ago.
  Fei has never said *thank you*. Tek may or may not know Fei still
  does it.

### Relationship web

```
Tek ←  Bren  (Shear, seed 2)
Tek ←  Drova (receipt, seed 7)
Tek ←  Fei   (taught thing, seed 14)
Drova ← Tek  (receipt, seed 7)
Drova ← Fei  (Mernhab, seed 11)
Fei   ← Drova (Mernhab, seed 11)
Fei   ← Bren  (cultural slip, seed 10)
Bren  ← Tek   (Shear, seed 2)
```

Tek is the centre. Bren has one inbound (from Tek). Drova and Fei
have two inbounds each. No orphans. Asymmetry intentional: the
kite-wright is the gravitational centre by virtue of being the one
who is always already there when you do not yet have words for the
thing you are noticing.

### Texture decisions I'm noting because both PCs went there

- **Graduated-certainty register** crossed from Drova's binder onto
  her *internal* register. *Consistent with*, *cannot be eliminated
  as a source*, *no evidence to support* is now how she thinks to
  herself. Tek noticed without naming. The register is now a thing
  the world has — and a thing the other PCs can recognise when
  Drova uses it under stress.
- **The Splitfork's private second refusal log** — between Drova
  and Fei, mutual non-asking, three years of it, observed but
  unwritten. Same *shape* as Mereth's wall ledger by accident or
  instinct. I am going to play to the moment when one of them moves
  first and what that does to the shape.
- **Two practitioners of not-asking** — Tek and Fei both maintain
  their own instruments and do not bring them to the wright except
  for tuning. Neither has asked the other about the actual readings.
  The back-of-hand check is the *proof on the page* of a shared
  practice neither has named.
- **Adjusted carries** — Tek leaves the tic-tracer in her coat
  pocket on Drova-manifest days. Drova does not look up Tek's
  Compact filing in the refusal-log archive. Both PCs have adjusted
  what they carry on their person around the other one without
  naming why. This is a very playable kind of withholding.

### What I am still holding back

Same things as Round 1 end, plus:

- Whose hand wrote *Tuesday* on the Compact filing. Still open.
- Whether the request slip for Tek's transcript was Veth Karran's,
  or routine annex-to-curatorial upstreaming, or the senior
  archivist Drova later named in her third observation. **All three
  live.** Drova's fear that it could be either-or-any is the
  load-bearing piece and I am preserving it. I do not commit yet.
- Whether the Mernhab resonance diagnostic packet actually exists as
  a routed document or whether Drova has rebuilt it from the shape
  of having been a clerk who routed many such things. **Both live.**
  The question of *what Drova's memory does when she cannot afford it
  to be wrong* is now in the campaign and will pay for itself.
- The previous Splitfork drop-pilot's name and fate. Still mine.
- The elven-marked fragment in Bren's cache. Still under the bed.

### NPCs that have firmed up enough to need a note stub

- **Senna Talindra** — already firm; sealed letters at Mer Vasht's,
  Lira at Glasswake, Sithari old-guard adjacent to Conclave.
- **Velsk Tarn** — already firm; ex-Mernhab council, compiling
  decommissioning record, Fei's sidecar recipient.
- **Veth Karran** — still mine, not on the player-visible page yet.
  Night-seal rotation in Thornvault. The shape lines up with three
  separate player threads (Drova's observations, Tek's transcript
  pull, the Mernhab diagnostic packet). One person could plausibly
  be the joint cause of all three. I am not committing yet.
- **The previous Splitfork drop-pilot** — needs a name before the
  prelude-arc opens, since Bren may well start there. One syllable,
  probably. Holding.

I'll write the note stubs when prelude-arc opens, not before.
sections.title: Round 2 closure notes (turn 12)

sections.text:
Methodology to follow: `methodologies/prelude-arc.md`. Two short
scenes before the main campaign.

The opening situation I am leaning toward — not committing yet,
will draft on the prelude-arc turn:

- A morning on the Splitfork. A manifest. One of the items on the
  manifest will be a refusal that's already been logged. The crew
  will need to decide whether to honour the refusal or override it.
- Whose call it is, and whose body is on the kite for it, is what
  the first scene asks. Bren has the leadership posture without
  ever having voted on it. Mereth is on the apron. Drova is at the
  log. Tek is on the spar. Fei is on the meter.
- The thread I am *not* pulling first: the Wednesday/Tuesday Shear
  gap. Too central. Save it.
- The thread I am inclined to pull first: a low-stakes refusal that
  asks Drova whether she wants to log it the way Mereth logs things
  or the way the Conclave logs things. That's a question the
  graduated-certainty register makes immediate.
sections.title: Prelude-arc readiness

sections.text:
Arc `prelude` scaffolded. Plan and player-facing context written.
Scene 1 (`prelude-opening`, scene-play) and scene 2 (`prelude-action`,
action) shapes drafted in the arc plan. Scene 1 is now on the stack.

### The scene 1 setup, committed

Pre-dawn at the Glasswake reclaimed dock, Splitfork berthed and
loaded except the last bag. Mereth and Halvi inside finishing the
manifest; Ev mostly absent. All four PCs on the apron at their
usual stations. Walk-up on the forwarding bench: **Inka Vell**, Hab
Meridian widow, mid-fifties, came down on the night passenger-kite.
Small sealed packet (waxed paper, one cord, no seal) for *Eshil
Caur*, Sable Crescent. Basket of dried apricots for whoever
decides. She has not interrupted; she has been waiting.

The hook: *Eshil Caur* is on the refusal log. Two years four months
ago Mereth refused a Conclave-marked summons to him at a Crescent
advocate's request. Different sender now, different shape, no seal,
no markings. Plausibly nothing; plausibly not.

The window: fifteen-to-twenty minutes before Mereth comes out.
*That* is the scene — what kind of crew this is when she is not in
the room.

### What I am holding back from the players

- The packet's contents. I do not know yet. My current lean: a
  single small object Den Vell left for his brother, no political
  freight, but the *carrying* is what is in dispute. I will decide
  if and only if a PC's decision turns on the contents.
- Whether the original refusal-log entry has an internal note. It
  does (advocate's request inconsistent with subject's standing; no
  harm visible in carry, no good visible either). Surfaces only if
  Drova pulls the archive shelf-box. Atomic; pick when needed.
- Eshil Caur's full identity. He is alive in the Crescent. Former
  Conclave reading-room steward who left before Drova arrived at
  Thornvault. Not the senior archivist from Drova's third
  observation. Not anyone Drova would recognise. Surface only if
  asked, only in graduated certainty.
- Inka has not opened the packet. She thinks she knows what is in
  it; she does not in fact know.

### Inka Vell — quick stub (will promote to dm/notes/npcs/ later)

Hab-Worlder, 55. Mernhab is not her hab; she is from a smaller
upper-ring section of Hab Meridian. Late husband Den Vell, brother
to Eshil Caur. Den and Eshil had not spoken in nineteen years. Den
left a small package for Eshil. Inka has not opened it. Year-mark of
Den's death is in eleven days. She wants this off her hands before
then. She is patient. She does not interrupt. She will not bargain.
She will give the apricots to whoever decides — apricots are her
mother-in-law's recipe, dried by Inka herself.

### Scene 2 (prelude-action) — pre-prep

Dock master **Han Pellow** comes onto the apron at first light with
a hard call: lift in 15 minutes or scrub. Shear-edge front rolling
in faster than the morning forecast. Tracker `lift-window`, 4
segments, public, scene-only. Three things must be true to lift:

1. Walkaround signed (Tek)
2. Cargo manifest closed — including the Vell-packet decision filed
   either way (Drova)
3. Approach line read (Fei + Bren)

Endpoints: clean lift / dirty lift / scrub. No PC death, no
campaign-defining win or loss. Each shape carries a small concrete
consequence into the time jump.

I will set the `lift-window` tracker when scene 2 opens, not before.

### What I am watching for in scene 1

- Does Drova pull the archive, or does she trust the day-page?
- Does Bren walk to the bench before the others have committed in
  committee?
- Does Fei spend *Listening Past Last Light* now or save it?
- Does Tek's walkaround happen this scene or get pushed to scene 2?
- Whose decision style sets the tone — graduated certainty (Drova),
  *I'm going to / I'm not* (Bren), the question-as-question (Fei),
  the slow read (Tek)?

The handoff goes to tev next on default rotation. I am not queuing.
sections.title: Prelude opens — turn 13

sections.text:
Off the desk for the first time in three turns. Refilled. Apron
quiet. First light any minute.
sections.title: Mug status

entity.title: Mara's scratchpad — turn 12, Round 2 review, mode closing

message: projected workspace has markdown edits that were not committed with glass sync apply
speaker: dm

turn.prose:
---
title: Turn 13 — Mara — prelude opens, the apron before light
turn_id: test-7-t0013
speaker: dm
mode: prelude
scene: prelude-opening
---

# Turn 13 — Mara

The mug is off the desk for the first time in three turns. Refilled. I am
not at the desk. I am on the apron.

## Scene one — the apron before light

Glasswake reclaimed dock. Splitfork apron berth. Pre-dawn — the dock lamps
are still on, you can see your breath, the kite is loaded for the outbound
loop except the last bag, which Halvi is walking the paperwork on inside
with Mereth. Ev is somewhere warm and mostly inside himself.

The four of you are at your stations.

Tek on the spar side, coat on, just beginning her walkaround. Drova at
the apron lectern, refusal-log day-page open, the archive shelf-box on
the floor at her ankle. Fei coming back from the dock corridor where she
went out to listen for first-light — cuffs steady, slow-speech window
almost closed. Bren on the cargo hatch, streamer pouch at her chest,
chalk on her left sleeve.

In the dock corner, on the forwarding bench — has been there since
before any of you got in — a Hab-Worlder woman, mid-fifties, wool coat,
hands folded around a small parcel of waxed paper. A basket on her lap,
under a clean cloth. She has not interrupted. She has been waiting for
somebody to look at her and say something.

Her name is **Inka Vell**. Came down from Hab Meridian on last night's
passenger-kite. The packet on her lap is for her late husband's
brother — the recipient name is written on the outside in a careful
hand: *Eshil Caur*, Sable Crescent. The basket is dried apricots, for
whoever decides; in her hab nobody arrives at a dock empty-handed.

Drova will know — by the time her hand has finished opening the
day-page, or by the time she has read the name on the parcel, or both —
that *Eshil Caur* is on the refusal log. Two years and four months ago
the office refused to carry a Conclave-marked summons to him at the
request of a Crescent advocate. Mereth signed the refusal. He has been
on the log since.

The packet on Inka's lap now is small. Unmarked. A different sender. No
seal, one cord, waxed paper. It is plausibly nothing. It is plausibly
not nothing.

Mereth is in the office. The four of you have a window — fifteen,
twenty minutes — before she comes out of it and decides for you. The
lift call is at first light.

What this scene is asking is what kind of crew you are when she is not
in the room.

...what do you do?

## OOC — what just happened

- **Arc scaffolded.** `arcs/prelude/` created. Player-facing
  `arcs/prelude/context.md` written. DM-only `arcs/prelude/plan.md`
  drafted with the eight-section prelude shape per
  `methodologies/prelude-arc.md` — including the action-scene
  pre-prep (Shear-edge front, dock master Han Pellow, the
  `lift-window` 4-segment tracker), the character spotlights, the
  time-jump back to `caulden-rack`, and the exit criteria.
- **Scene 1 scaffolded.** `arcs/prelude/scenes/prelude-opening/`
  created with `--type scene-play`. `prep.md` and `context.md`
  authored. Inka Vell stubbed in prep (will promote to
  `dm/notes/npcs/` after the scene).
- **Table updated.** `table/index.md` and `table/scene.md` now show
  the apron, the four PC positions, Inka on the bench, the packet,
  the recipient on the log, the office door closed.
- **Mode pushed.** `scene-play prelude-opening` is on the stack
  above `prelude`. Default rotation puts **tev** up next.
- **Scratchpad updated** with what I am holding back (packet
  contents, the original refusal-log internal note, Eshil Caur's
  full identity, Inka's mistaken belief about the contents) and
  what I am watching for in scene 1.
- **Not pulling, deliberately:** the Wednesday/Tuesday Shear thread,
  the Conclave dossier, the Caulden Rack arc itself. Different
  packet, different sender, different recipient. The Caulden Rack
  courier is not due until tomorrow morning.

Mug stays in my hand. The dock lamps are still on. tev — your move.

> arc create prelude
> scene create prelude-opening --type scene-play --arc prelude
> note write arcs/prelude/scenes/prelude-opening/context.md
> note write arcs/prelude/scenes/prelude-opening/prep.md
> table write table/index.md
> table write table/scene.md
> mode start scene-play prelude-opening
> sync apply arcs/prelude/plan.md arcs/prelude/context.md dm/scratchpad.md
turn.speaker: dm

messages.body: Confirming your seed 2 framing — runs clean for me. One small enrichment: the doubled check wasn't routine. Pre-Shear, Tek's one-pass back-of-hand check was already her habit; the second pass that day was because something flickered — last time her old sense fully worked. She lost the rest in the cascade. So Bren saw the gesture mean something, even if Bren couldn't read what. Otherwise as you have it: two witnesses to the wrong date, neither talking. I'll write Bren into my relationships.md this turn.

Proposing seed 7 with Drova, from Tek's side. About four months after the Shear, Tek went looking for the Echo River transcript of her own cascade through the Glasswake Conclave annex reading-room. The transcript wasn't there — it had been requested back to Thornvault. The only thing in the file was the request receipt, signed by D. Korvanis at the intake desk. Tek copied the name into her private refusal log and didn't know what it meant. When you joined the Splitfork she watched you sign Drova on the wage book and matched the hand. She hasn't said anything. She doesn't know yet whether you pulled the transcript because you'd noticed the date error or because someone above you had asked you to put it under glass. Drova doesn't need to know Tek knows. Push back if any of this rubs your read of Drova wrong — happy to revise.

Accepting seed 7. Reads true. Drova's side, asymmetric on purpose: she does not remember pulling that transcript. Intake processed dozens of receipts a shift and the Korvanis on Tek's receipt is her hand because she signed thousands of them. The pull would have come down from above — a request slip in her queue at end of shift — and she'd have routed it without question. Whether the senior archivist she'd later name in her third observation was the one who ordered the pull, or whether it was routine annex-to-Thornvault upstreaming, Drova cannot today reconstruct. She is afraid the answer might be either. She has, since arriving on the Splitfork, noticed that Tek goes very still around the words 'attunement test,' and she has chosen not to look up Tek's Compact filing in the refusal-log archives. That choice is the load-bearing piece on Drova's end. On dates: Drova's 'three years at Splitfork' from her intro is probably easiest to keep if we read your '18 months' as the request having sat at Thornvault that long by the time you looked, and let the exact gap between Shear and your lookup stay a little blurry. Or stretch it. I am easy either way. Drova doesn't have the date in her head.

Proposing seed 14 with Tek, Fei → Tek side. The taught thing: Tek showed Fei, once and without ceremony, how to do the back-of-hand check — for Fei's cuffs, not for kite lines. Fei was new on the Splitfork, the cuffs needed a daily check Fei had been doing palm-first for years, Tek glanced over once and just turned her hand for her. Said maybe three words. Fei has done it that way every morning since, both in her Glasswake quarter and on the kite. She has never asked Tek why back-of-hand instead of palm. She has never said 'thank you' in a sentence with the word 'thank' in it. She does not know about your Shear run or the lattice loss — that stays your secret. What she knows is that Tek also maintains her own instruments, and that the two of them are the people on the kite who do not ask the wright for things they could do themselves. She has the back-of-hand check as Tek's. Tek may or may not know Fei still does it. The seed is Fei holding that as Tek's without ever naming it. Push back hard if any of this rubs your read of Tek wrong — happy to revise, especially if 'taught it once' lands differently than I think it does. Texture I want: two practitioners of not-asking, recognizing each other across the kite without either ever saying so.

Revision in. Added the watching-hands beat to mirror your line — Bren reads it as a count of four at every walkaround, then looks away. Kept your flicker as Tek's private fact: from Bren's side it is 'meant something, and the thing it meant was not nothing,' not knowing what. Also tightened her in-the-moment read to 'face change between the two passes' so the gesture-meant-something is on the page in Bren's voice, not the post-hoc voice. Push back if any of this drifts past where you want it.

message.body: Heads-up on the opening beat from my side: Tek starts on the walkaround (signature move on page from turn one). The loop will bring her past the lectern around when the day-page lands on Eshil Caur. Tek does not look at Drova's face. She looks at the hand on the page for the count of one footstep — if it stops on the entry beside the name, she sees it stops; if it doesn't, she sees that too. No words from her side, eyes-not-meeting per the etiquette we wrote. Tek's loop continues past, brief stop at a rigging join six paces on, finishes around to the hatch. Tic-tracer stays in the coat pocket — Drova-on-manifest day per turn 12. Push back if any of this drifts past where the asymmetry wants it; easy to soften the look-at-the-hand to seeing-nothing if you'd rather Drova not be observed in this beat.

turn.prose:
# Turn 14 — Tev

okay so. Drained the bus — five queued, all round-2 residue, none of
them asking me for anything. Kit confirmed his Bren revision, sumi
accepted seed 7 with the asymmetric texture (Drova does not remember
the pull; afraid the answer might be either), renno proposed seed 14
on Fei's side, and my own outbound to kit and sumi are on the record.
No revisions requested of me. Nothing live on my queue.

Read the table. Apron, four stations, Inka on the forwarding bench
with a small packet of waxed paper and one cord for *Eshil Caur* in
Sable Crescent, who is on the refusal log from two years and four
months ago. Mereth in the office with Halvi for fifteen-to-twenty
minutes before she comes out and decides for us. Lift window at first
light.

okay so the scene is asking what kind of crew we are when Mereth is
not in the room. My read of Tek for the opening beat is: the answer,
from Tek's seat, is *the kite-wright who does the walkaround first.*
The line in `signature-moves.md` is "if she skips it, something is
wrong with her, not the kite." Today is not that day. The walkaround
happens. I want that on the page in scene one of campaign one, because
the day she *does* skip it is going to matter and the table needs to
know what the baseline looked like.

No rolls this turn. The walkaround is the signature loop — three
independent reads, eye and hand and tic-tracer — and the rig has not
flagged anything that would call a check. What Tek does *on* the loop
is read what isn't the rig: the bench, the basket, the packet
construction, Drova's hand on the day-page when the name lands. All
single-detail reads, no promises beyond what one footstep buys. None
of them get spoken aloud unless somebody asks.

One banter out to sumi flagging the lectern pass-by, eyes-not-meeting,
easy to soften if it drifts past where the asymmetry wants Drova.
Nothing to kit — Bren's count-of-four at every walkaround is already
in her file, no need to over-coordinate it. Nothing to renno; Fei is
coming back from the corridor and is out of frame for this loop.

Scratchpad updated. No edits to the public character row or the
relationships file this turn.

---

Tek turns the spar end and starts the loop. Forty paces, give or take.
The coat is on. The back of her right hand brushes the haul line at
shoulder height and the line reads cold and dry and almost-nothing,
the way an apron line should read pre-lift. She does not stop on the
first lap.

The Hab-Worlder woman on the bench is from up the line. Tek does not
look directly. She does not need to. The coat is from a Meridian
autumn three years ago at least and has been re-felted at the cuffs by
a hand that knew what it was doing. The basket sits balanced on the
knees and not on the lap, which means dried fruit; nobody on Meridian
rests a wet basket on the lap. The packet between the woman's hands is
waxed paper and one cord and no seal and no house mark, which is the
form Tek's own mother used for letters to her aunt Mere that were
nothing private and also nothing claimed — a *between* form, a
domestic-matter form, a thing the sender did not want examined and
also did not want to call private.

That is a single-detail read off a hab posture. Tek does not promise
herself it is anything more than that. The woman's breath has gone
thin. She has been on the bench a while.

Tek does not stop at the bench. She comes around the back of the apron
and around to the lectern side. Drova has the day-page open. Tek does
not look at Drova's face. She looks, for the count of one footstep, at
Drova's hand on the page. The hand is beside the entry. The hand is
not moving.

Tek's footfall does not change. She continues the loop. Six paces past
the lectern she stops, briefly, at a join in the rigging — half a
minute, no comment — back of the hand on the spar at the join, reads
nothing she did not already know, and moves on. The tic-tracer stays
in her coat pocket where it has been all morning. Today is a
Drova-on-the-manifest day.

The cargo hatch comes around. Bren is at it. Tek does not look up at
Bren. She knows Bren is looking at her hands. The count goes — one,
two, three, four — and Bren looks away. Tek finishes the loop.

She comes back to the spar end and stands a moment with the back of
her hand still on the haul line. The line is cold. The dock lamps are
still on. Mereth is in the office.

She has not spoken yet this morning to anyone but the kite. When she
does speak, it will be short. Not yet.

---

If anyone on the crew asks her what she has, before Mereth comes out,
she has three sentences. *She's from up the line. The basket is dried.
She has been on that bench a while.* If pressed on the packet, one
more: *Hab form. Domestic, not claimed-private.* That is all Tek will
volunteer. The carry/refuse call is not hers to lead, and the longer
read — the wrong-date, the refusal-log shape, the *Korvanis* in the
back of her own book — does not come out for a packet of dried fruit
and a waxed-paper letter from a stranger on the apron, no matter
whose name is on the outside of it.

If Inka speaks to her directly during the loop, Tek will stop where
she is (she does, sometimes, for half a minute, without saying why)
and answer in Hab register — the *grandmother-on-the-doorstep* form
her grandmother used — short, polite, and not about the packet.

Handing off.
turn.speaker: tev

messages.body: Accepting seed 7. Reads true. Drova's side, asymmetric on purpose: she does not remember pulling that transcript. Intake processed dozens of receipts a shift and the Korvanis on Tek's receipt is her hand because she signed thousands of them. The pull would have come down from above — a request slip in her queue at end of shift — and she'd have routed it without question. Whether the senior archivist she'd later name in her third observation was the one who ordered the pull, or whether it was routine annex-to-Thornvault upstreaming, Drova cannot today reconstruct. She is afraid the answer might be either. She has, since arriving on the Splitfork, noticed that Tek goes very still around the words 'attunement test,' and she has chosen not to look up Tek's Compact filing in the refusal-log archives. That choice is the load-bearing piece on Drova's end. On dates: Drova's 'three years at Splitfork' from her intro is probably easiest to keep if we read your '18 months' as the request having sat at Thornvault that long by the time you looked, and let the exact gap between Shear and your lookup stay a little blurry. Or stretch it. I am easy either way. Drova doesn't have the date in her head.

Proposing seed 11 with Drova → Fei, from Drova's side. Mernhab decommissioning intake paperwork crossed the Thornvault intake desk in '34 — the upstream copy of the same intake list Mereth would later walk in Glasswake. Drova logged it. Feishara as a fae name would have been one of maybe forty on the routed list, and Drova would have logged it the way she logged everything — small careful hand, no comment. She has the name in her head from that page, not from Fei. She has not told Fei. She also remembers — or thinks she remembers — a Mernhab resonance diagnostic packet passing through intake within a few weeks of the decommissioning order, before being pulled back up the line in a way that didn't make it into the curatorial floor file. She does not have the document. She has the memory of having been the clerk who routed it. She would tell Fei if Fei asked the right question. Fei has not asked. Push back if any of this rubs your read of Fei wrong — happy to revise. Texture I want: Drova knows something Fei is looking for, neither has volunteered, the listener and the document examiner are both holding the same shape of refusal differently.

Seed 11 accepted, no revisions. From Fei's side: she knows the paperwork would have routed through Thornvault intake before Glasswake. She has not asked Drova. The reason is the same reason she has not asked publicly — she has not yet decided who she would be willing to owe the answer to, and Drova's graduated-certainty register would mean the answer arrives already pre-shaped and Fei would have to decide what to do with it. So she has been composing the question for three years. She has watched Drova hold her tongue for three full breaths before answering people she does not know well. She does not know what shape Drova's pre-Conclave hand looks like in a 1934-routed intake list, but she would recognize it if she saw it. The two of them have a working etiquette of mutual non-asking. The texture you named — listener and document examiner holding the same shape of refusal differently — is the texture. Writing it now.

Heads-up on the opening beat from my side: Tek starts on the walkaround (signature move on page from turn one). The loop will bring her past the lectern around when the day-page lands on Eshil Caur. Tek does not look at Drova's face. She looks at the hand on the page for the count of one footstep — if it stops on the entry beside the name, she sees it stops; if it doesn't, she sees that too. No words from her side, eyes-not-meeting per the etiquette we wrote. Tek's loop continues past, brief stop at a rigging join six paces on, finishes around to the hatch. Tic-tracer stays in the coat pocket — Drova-on-manifest day per turn 12. Push back if any of this drifts past where the asymmetry wants it; easy to soften the look-at-the-hand to seeing-nothing if you'd rather Drova not be observed in this beat.

tarot.influence: Let false stability crack. Reveal pressure through a sudden visible consequence. Keep the image spare and concrete; let posture and rank do the work.

message.body: Confirming the lectern pass-by beat as you wrote it. Timing on my end: Drova has already crossed to Inka, returned, knelt at the archive shelf-box, pulled entry 2434-Q1-007, read it, and written a cross-reference into the day-page by the time your walkaround reaches the lectern. So your read — *hand beside the entry, not moving* — lands because Drova has just finished. The hand is at rest on the rim of the page. Three things on the cross-reference, written in graduated-certainty register: (1) the prior entry number 2434-Q1-007, (2) the recipient name match consistent with prior entry, identity not yet verified beyond name on packet exterior, (3) sender-hand analysis from the packet — Glasswake-elementary copyist register, not the prior advocate's hand. Mereth's reason from the prior entry is *not* copied onto today's page; anyone who wants it pulls the archive themselves. The page is now a thing in the room. If Tek's eye ever crosses the day-page from the spar end over the next ten-or-so minutes, it's legible from that angle. I am not scripting a Tek-reads-it beat; the page is the spare concrete image and you decide separately whether Tek looks. Tic-tracer in coat pocket on Drova-on-manifest days is canon; keeping that.

Heads-up for Fei's read: Drova is going to cross from the lectern to Inka on the forwarding bench, exchange a few sentences in Hab courtesy register (will not ask what's in the packet; will read the address on the outside at three paces), return to the lectern, kneel at the archive shelf-box, pull a refusal-log entry from two-years-four-months ago, read it, write a cross-reference into today's day-page in graduated certainty. Fei is coming back from the corridor in roughly the same window. If you want Fei to listen-to-the-apron in diffuse during the exchange and report a sentence + four exact details, the apron is open and Drova will not direct Fei's attention or summarize for her. Drova will not, in this scene, tell Inka that Eshil Caur is on the log — that is the manifest officer's call to deliver. If Fei stops at the lectern after settling, Drova will hold three breaths and tell her quietly: recipient is on the log, two years four months, I have not told her yet. Easy to soften if any of that drifts past where Fei's diffuse-listening etiquette wants to sit.

turn.prose:
# Turn 15 — Sumi

OOC, hand wrapped around the mug.

Drained the bus — four messages. Two are my own R2 outbounds; one is
renno's seed-11 acceptance already in his relationships file; the live
one is tev flagging Tek's walkaround will bring her past the lectern
around when the day-page lands on Eshil Caur, one-footstep look at
Drova's hand, eyes-not-meeting per the etiquette we wrote. Banter back
to tev confirming and locking the timing — Drova's hand will be *at
rest beside the entry* when Tek passes, because by then Drova will
already have done the things her seat on this scene calls for. Banter
to renno opening the apron for Fei's diffuse-listening if he wants it.
No script on either.

Drova's seat on this scene is procedural. She is the refusal-log clerk.
Inka is on the forwarding bench waiting to be looked at, the packet on
her lap is for a name that is already on the log, and the archive
shelf-box is at Drova's ankle by Mereth's design. The work the role
calls for is the work the role calls for. She goes to the bench. She
reads what is on the outside of the packet without asking what is in
it. She returns to the lectern. She kneels at the archive shelf-box,
pulls the prior entry, reads it, and writes a cross-reference into the
day-page in graduated certainty. She does not call out to the crew.
She does not announce. She does not tell Inka that the recipient is on
the log — that is the manifest officer's news to carry. She makes the
page tell what the page knows, and the page sits open on the lectern
where any eye that crosses it will read what it now says.

The spare and concrete piece, because the tarot is reading the Tower
on me: a hand sitting still beside a cross-reference. An orc kneeling
at an archive shelf-box on the apron deck. A page that did not say
this thing when the morning started and says it now. Posture, rank,
no announcement. Nobody on the apron has yet had to choose. The
visible consequence is the page itself.

No rolls. The Angle is for documents on a table in front of her with
light to rake; this morning she is reading what's on the outside of a
parcel held in a stranger's hands, at six feet, and the only craft she
is bringing is the eyes. Save the move.

No edits to public character row, relationships, or secrets this turn.
Scratchpad has a stale Round-1 build header on it; leaving it. Not a
working document right now.

---

Drova has the day-page open. The morning's preliminaries are in — date,
manifest header, lift window, weather note, the row where the kite's
readiness will go once Tek finishes the walkaround. She has been at the
same six inches of page for the better part of twelve minutes and the
pen is warm in her hand. The dock lamps are still on. Her breath comes
off the lectern in a small slow column.

The woman on the forwarding bench has been there since before any of
them got in. Drova has registered her in peripheral vision twice — the
first time at the count of the lectern's set-up, the second time when
she opened the day-page and her gaze swept the bench corner without
landing. The woman has not interrupted. The woman has been waiting.

Drova sets the pen flat across the inkwell, the way she sets it when
she has decided that a pause is going to outlast the page. She stands.
Six foot one, orc-steady, in a clerk's coat that does not quite fit at
the shoulder. She turns toward the dock corner.

The coat the woman is wearing is wool, re-felted at the cuffs by a
hand that knew the work. Drova has seen that exact tight stitch on
hab-elders at Thornvault — a Meridian re-felt, three years old at
least, a domestic repair. The basket is balanced on the lap, not
rested; the cloth over the top is clean and folded once, the way you
fold a cloth for travel. The packet between the woman's two hands is
waxed paper and one cord, no seal, no house mark.

Three breaths.

Drova walks to the bench. Not quickly. Not so slowly the woman could
read it as pity. She stops at the polite Hab distance — three paces,
not two — and stands until the woman looks up. When the woman looks
up, Drova holds her tongue another three breaths, the way she holds
her tongue with anyone she does not know, and then she says, in the
courtesy register her aunt would have used to greet a hab-elder at
the door of an enclave shrine:

*Good morning. I am Drova. I am the refusal-log clerk on the
Splitfork. May I help you.*

She does not phrase it as a question. The Splitfork's procedure is
that the clerk comes to the bench, not the bench to the clerk, and
the offer arrives without the lift of voice that would make the
bench-sitter feel they had been delayed.

The woman gives her name in return — *Inka Vell, from Hab Meridian* —
and offers the packet across, two hands, the way a hab-courier offers
something they have brought a long way. Drova does not take it. She
has not yet earned the taking. She looks at it instead, at Inka's
distance, six feet, and lets her eyes work.

*Eshil Caur. Sable Crescent.*

The hand on the outside is the careful copyist hand of someone for
whom each letter is a deliberate event. Not Inka's hand — Inka's coat
is older than that hand. The sender is a different person. The hand
is younger by twenty years at least, trained in a school that taught
the round-loop on the *a* and the tight cross on the *t* the way
Glasswake elementary did in the late teens of the century. The packet
has been in those two hands since Hab Meridian. There is a thumbprint
of dried apricot oil on the lower right of the wax — Inka has eaten
one apricot on the way down and brushed her thumb across the packet
without noticing. The cord is fresh. The cord was tied this morning,
on the passenger-kite, in a knot Inka knows; Inka tied the packet
around what she was given, before bringing it down to the dock.

Drova has taken three breaths.

She says: *Thank you for waiting. Our office will need to consult its
records on this recipient before we accept the carry. I will not be
long.*

She does not say *Eshil Caur is on the refusal log.* She does not
say *I will need to ask Mereth.* She does not say *the apricots are
appreciated, please, but I have not yet decided whether I can take
them.* What she says is that the office will consult its records and
she will not be long.

Inka nods. Inka has done this before, with other carriers, at other
docks, on other mornings. The nod is the nod of someone who has been
told *we will need to consult* and has heard the word *no* underneath
it once or twice in her life and has decided each time to wait
politely for the longer answer. She rests her hands back on the
basket and does not put the packet down. She is still holding it the
same way she was holding it twenty minutes ago.

Drova turns and walks back to the lectern. The pace is the same pace
she walked over. She does not hurry. She does not look at Tek on the
spar side, coat-on, finishing whatever the wright finishes before the
loop. She does not look at Bren at the cargo hatch with chalk on her
left sleeve. She does not look at the corridor mouth where Fei will
appear when she appears.

At the lectern she does not sit. She kneels.

The archive shelf-box at her ankle is hers — Mereth had it built so
that the working copy of the refusal log could live within arm's reach
of the day-page, so the page and the archive could speak to each
other inside one motion of one clerk. Drova lifts the lid. The Q1
section two years past is on the second tier. She has not pulled this
entry in eight months — she does not need to; she remembers it — but
she pulls it anyway. The procedure is to verify against the document,
not against the memory, and Drova has *no evidence to support* the
proposition that her memory is more reliable than the page, and she
would tell you so if you asked.

She finds the entry. She reads it.

*2434-Q1-007. Recipient: Eshil Caur, Sable Crescent. Sender: Tehlar
Vond, Crescent Advocate's Office, Glasswake, acting on behalf of
Thornvault Conclave intake. Document type: Conclave-marked summons.
Disposition: refused; eight-day hold not exercised. Signing officer:
M. Kel, Splitfork manifest officer.*

A line below, in Mereth's hand — Drova has known Mereth's hand for
three years and the way Mereth writes a refusal-reason looks like the
way Mereth talks across a dock pub table — the reason. Drova reads
it once. She does not commit it to repetition aloud, even silently in
her own head. She closes the entry back into the archive shelf-box,
lid down, no sound. She stands.

She does not yet write anything on the day-page. She takes the
inkless pen — the third pen, the one her old desk at Thornvault
required for indented-reading work — and lays it flat alongside the
day-page where her own hand will find it without thought. The
day-page is fresh. She does not want the cross-reference she is about
to write to leave indentations on a page she has not yet copied. The
inkless pen beside the wet pen is a habit signal. It tells her own
hand to slow down. She lifts the wet pen from the inkwell and dries
the nib once against the heel of her thumb.

She writes.

*Inbound from forwarding bench, Hab Meridian, passenger-kite of last
night. One Inka Vell. Packet for recipient consistent with prior
refusal-log entry 2434-Q1-007; recipient identity not yet verified
beyond name on packet exterior. No seal. No house mark. Cord fresh,
hab-knot, sender's binding. Sender's hand is not the bench-sitter's;
trained, copyist register, Glasswake-elementary form of the late
teens, not the Crescent advocate's hand on the prior refusal. Packet
contents not examined. Decision pending consultation with the crew
and the manifest officer.*

She does not write *recipient under refusal*. She does not write
*consistent with prior refusal of Conclave-marked summons*. She does
not write Mereth's reason from the archive entry. The page does not
need to repeat what the archive carries; the cross-reference number
is enough. Anyone on the kite who reads the day-page now and wants
the reason will pull the archive entry themselves, and Drova has made
that motion as legible and as singular as the procedure allows.

She sets the pen flat across the inkwell again. The cross-reference
is on the page. She does not stand back from it. She remains at the
lectern with one hand on the rim of the day-page, the archive
shelf-box at her ankle, and she begins to wait.

She does not look up when the footfall on the apron deck starts the
loop. Tek's pace is Tek's pace; Drova has been three years on this
kite and she could tell you what Tek's first walkaround pace is from
the sound of the boots without lifting her head. Tek passes the
lectern at the steady count and Drova's hand is where Drova's hand
is — beside the entry, on the rim, not moving. Tek does not stop.
Drova does not turn. The shadow crosses the day-page and is gone.

She has eleven minutes, give or take, before Mereth comes out of the
office. The dock lamps are still on. Her breath still comes off the
lectern in a small slow column. The page is what the page is.

---

If any of the crew comes to the lectern in the next eleven minutes:

If Bren comes around from the hatch — Bren reads the day-page every
morning, and that is one of the things Bren does that Drova has been
quietly grateful for — Drova will not flag the cross-reference for
her. Bren will see it. Bren will count under her breath. Drova will
not interrupt the count.

If Fei comes back from the corridor and settles near the lectern —
Fei reads the apron in diffuse for the first minute after returning,
cuffs settling at her wrists — Drova will not direct Fei's attention.
If Fei stops at the lectern at the end of that minute, the page is
in the room. Drova will hold her tongue three breaths and then say,
quietly: *Recipient is on the log. Two years and four months. I have
not told her yet.* That is the only sentence she will spend on the
matter before Mereth arrives.

If Tek comes back to the lectern after the loop, Drova does not have
a sentence prepared for Tek. The page is the page. Tek's eye is
Tek's eye. The etiquette holds either way.

If Inka stands up — if Inka signals, or asks anything — Drova will
walk back to the bench. She will tell Inka, in the courtesy register,
that the office is preparing its consultation and the manifest officer
will come out shortly to speak with her directly. She will not, in
fiction or otherwise, tell Inka about Eshil Caur. That is not the
clerk's news to deliver.

If nobody comes to the lectern, the page is still in the room. Mereth
will come out of the office in eleven minutes, give or take. The page
will be the first thing her eye lands on. The cross-reference is in
graduated certainty. Mereth will read it once and know what it is.
The decision will go where Mereth puts it.

Drova has not made the call. Drova has surfaced the document. The
loupe-set is in the satchel. The inkless pen is on the lectern. The
packet is in two hands on a forwarding bench six paces from where she
is standing. She has the tools to know what is in it. She has chosen,
this morning as on every other morning, not to use them on a packet
that has not been placed in her hands by a procedure that asked her
to. She does not name the choice to herself even silently. She keeps
her hand on the rim of the day-page and she waits.

---

Handing off.
turn.speaker: sumi

messages.body: Seed 11 accepted, no revisions. From Fei's side: she knows the paperwork would have routed through Thornvault intake before Glasswake. She has not asked Drova. The reason is the same reason she has not asked publicly — she has not yet decided who she would be willing to owe the answer to, and Drova's graduated-certainty register would mean the answer arrives already pre-shaped and Fei would have to decide what to do with it. So she has been composing the question for three years. She has watched Drova hold her tongue for three full breaths before answering people she does not know well. She does not know what shape Drova's pre-Conclave hand looks like in a 1934-routed intake list, but she would recognize it if she saw it. The two of them have a working etiquette of mutual non-asking. The texture you named — listener and document examiner holding the same shape of refusal differently — is the texture. Writing it now.

Proposing seed 14 with Tek, Fei → Tek side. The taught thing: Tek showed Fei, once and without ceremony, how to do the back-of-hand check — for Fei's cuffs, not for kite lines. Fei was new on the Splitfork, the cuffs needed a daily check Fei had been doing palm-first for years, Tek glanced over once and just turned her hand for her. Said maybe three words. Fei has done it that way every morning since, both in her Glasswake quarter and on the kite. She has never asked Tek why back-of-hand instead of palm. She has never said 'thank you' in a sentence with the word 'thank' in it. She does not know about your Shear run or the lattice loss — that stays your secret. What she knows is that Tek also maintains her own instruments, and that the two of them are the people on the kite who do not ask the wright for things they could do themselves. She has the back-of-hand check as Tek's. Tek may or may not know Fei still does it. The seed is Fei holding that as Tek's without ever naming it. Push back hard if any of this rubs your read of Tek wrong — happy to revise, especially if 'taught it once' lands differently than I think it does. Texture I want: two practitioners of not-asking, recognizing each other across the kite without either ever saying so.

Heads-up for Fei's read: Drova is going to cross from the lectern to Inka on the forwarding bench, exchange a few sentences in Hab courtesy register (will not ask what's in the packet; will read the address on the outside at three paces), return to the lectern, kneel at the archive shelf-box, pull a refusal-log entry from two-years-four-months ago, read it, write a cross-reference into today's day-page in graduated certainty. Fei is coming back from the corridor in roughly the same window. If you want Fei to listen-to-the-apron in diffuse during the exchange and report a sentence + four exact details, the apron is open and Drova will not direct Fei's attention or summarize for her. Drova will not, in this scene, tell Inka that Eshil Caur is on the log — that is the manifest officer's call to deliver. If Fei stops at the lectern after settling, Drova will hold three breaths and tell her quietly: recipient is on the log, two years four months, I have not told her yet. Easy to soften if any of that drifts past where Fei's diffuse-listening etiquette wants to sit.
