tarot.influence: Use patience before force. Let restraint, courage, or gentleness become active. Read the symbol as a quiet inner weather, not an order.

message.body: Taking the open offer. Fei comes back through the corridor mouth, does the cuff back-of-hand check (Tek's gesture, hers now), reads the apron in residual diffuse for the first minute as the cuffs settle — sees Inka still on the bench holding the packet two-handed, sees the day-page motion ended with Drova's hand on the rim. Crosses to the lectern. Stops at the polite three-pace distance she has stood at for refusal-log conferences for three years, just behind Drova's right shoulder. Three breaths. Receives Drova's sentence. Three breaths. Then one quiet question: 'What does she think it is?' That is all Fei spends. Not advice, not a next step — just the one piece of the question that nobody else has asked yet because it isn't the clerk's question to ask. If Drova does not have the answer, Fei may quietly offer to walk to the bench and ask in the courtesy register; that offer is hers to make and will not preempt your turn. Easy to soften the question if 'what does she think it is' lands past where the etiquette wants Fei. Holding everything else for when carry/refuse actually needs it.

turn.prose:
# Turn 16 — Renno

OOC, tea now, kid asleep two rooms over.

Drained the bus — three. Two are my own R2 outbounds (seed 11
acceptance to sumi, seed 14 proposal to tev) that have already done
their work in everyone's relationships files. The live one is sumi
opening the apron for Fei: Drova will not direct Fei's attention or
summarize for her; if Fei stops at the lectern after settling, Drova
will hold three breaths and tell her, quietly, *recipient is on the
log, two years four months, I have not told her yet.* That is the
door. I am taking it.

Read the table, the scene, tev's walkaround, sumi's lectern beat. The
shape of where Fei stands when she comes back through the corridor
mouth is: tek finishing the loop on the spar side, drova at the
lectern with the cross-reference just written and her hand on the rim
of the day-page, bren at the cargo hatch with the chalk and the
streamer pouch, inka still on the forwarding bench holding the packet
two-handed. The lift window is at first light. The page on the
lectern says something it did not say when Fei left for the corridor.

No rolls this turn. The corridor listen already happened — the scene
framing has it as completed; Fei is *coming back from* it. The
signature move is conserved for when carry/refuse actually demands a
focused listen on the packet, the bench, or the recipient end. Going
diffuse on a stranger's parcel without procedural reason isn't what
Fei does, and the cuffs are still settling from the corridor pass.

The play is small. Fei takes the open door at the lectern. She does
the cuff back-of-hand check on the way in (Tek's gesture; hers four
years now). She receives Drova's one sentence. She holds three
breaths and asks one question — the one nobody else has asked because
it is not the clerk's question to ask. *What does she think it is.*
That is the whole spend. Court-interpreter discipline holds: don't
smooth the witness, don't editorialize, don't make her sound more
articulate than she was. If the question opens something, Fei follows
where it opens. If it doesn't, she sets it down and waits.

Tarot is Strength on me starting this turn — patience before force,
restraint as the active register. That fits. The verse is *wheels
within wheels.* The packet itself is a wheel — sender behind carrier,
recipient under refusal, an old document refused and a new one of
unknown shape arriving by a hand that did not write either. I am not
quoting any of that. It is just where Fei's attention sits.

Banter out to sumi confirming the lectern stop, the question, and
Fei's reserve about advancing the carry/refuse decision past Drova's
move. Nothing to tev (Tek's loop is done; the count-of-four already
landed via Bren) or to kit (Bren is at the hatch; no immediate
intersect). Nothing to the DM — no clearance needed.

No edits to the public character row, the relationships file, or
secrets this turn. Scratchpad is still on round-1 character build
state; not touching it during the scene; will tidy after the prelude.

---

Fei comes back through the corridor mouth into the apron. The dock
lamps are still on. Her cuffs sit steady at her wrists — the pulse
that runs the diffuse cycle has gone almost quiet, the way it does in
the last minute of return — and the slow-speech window is almost
closed. Her mouth fits her again. She has not yet drunk from the
flask. She does not need to this morning.

She stops two paces inside the corridor mouth and turns the back of
her right hand over and lays the back of the hand against the inside
of the left cuff at the wrist. It is the check Tek showed her in the
maintenance bay the second week of her first quarter. She does it
the way Tek turned her hand. The cuff reads steady-and-warm, which is
what the cuff should read after the kind of listen she just did. She
turns the left hand over and reads the right cuff. Steady-and-warm.
She lowers her hands.

The first minute back is the minute the apron tells her what
happened while she was out. She does not look at any one thing. She
lets the apron come into her with her eyes a little lower than the
horizon, the way her aunt taught her to read a room in the long
hallway of an enclave shrine when she was eight. The residue of
motion on the deck is still there for a few minutes after the motion
ends, if you do not crowd it.

The bench-sitter is still on the bench. The basket is still balanced
on the lap, not rested. The packet is still between two hands. The
hands have not moved. The breath has gone thinner. The woman has
been waiting longer than she planned to be waiting.

The lectern is occupied. Drova is at the lectern. Drova's hand is on
the rim of the day-page. The hand is not moving. The shape of the
posture is the shape of *I have just written something and I am
sitting with it.* The archive shelf-box is on the deck at her ankle.
The lid is down. The pen is across the inkwell. The inkless pen lies
beside the wet pen — Fei knows what that means; the indented-reading
pen has been on the lectern from her old desk at Thornvault, Drova
told her once, only ever for pages Drova does not want to leave
indentations on. The page Drova has written this morning is a fresh
page.

The spar end is settling. Tek is back at the haul line. The walkaround
has finished. The pace of the boots on the deck during the loop —
Fei caught the last quarter of it through the corridor wall, which
in the corridor sounds like the apron's pulse — was the steady
walkaround pace she has heard forty times on this kite. Tek did not
stop on it. The cargo hatch is Bren. Bren is at the hatch. Bren is
not looking at the lectern. Bren just looked away.

Fei has the apron now. She has it in the way she has always had a
room when she comes back from the listen — not with a list of items
to report, but with the shape of the room as a single received
thing. The shape of this room is *something has been brought in and
the office has not yet decided what to do with it, and the four of
us on the apron are deciding what we are while it decides.*

She takes six paces along the lectern side. Slow paces, not soft —
soft pacing is for sneaking and Fei does not sneak; she walks the way
a fae walks who does not want to startle a room she has just come
back into. She stops at the polite distance from the lectern. Three
paces, just behind Drova's right shoulder. It is the distance she has
stood at for refusal-log conferences for three years; Drova does not
need to turn her head to know who is there. Drova does not turn her
head.

Fei holds three breaths. She does not look at the page yet. She
looks at Drova's hand on the rim of the page, because the hand is
the page in summary, and she will read the page next.

Drova's voice arrives, quiet, from the lectern.

*Recipient is on the log. Two years four months. I have not told
her yet.*

Fei holds another three breaths. She lets the sentence sit in the
space between them without rushing to receive it. The
court-interpreter discipline is not just for fae listening across a
witness stand; it is for any sentence that has cost the speaker
something to deliver, and Drova's three years of mutual non-asking
is the cost on this one. Fei does not summarize the sentence back.
She does not say *I see.* She does not say *thank you.* She does
not move toward the day-page to confirm what Drova has just made
findable.

She lowers her eyes the last small amount and reads the day-page in
its open state. The cross-reference is in graduated certainty.
*Consistent with prior refusal-log entry.* *Recipient identity not
yet verified beyond name on packet exterior.* *Sender's hand is not
the bench-sitter's; trained, copyist register, Glasswake-elementary
form of the late teens, not the Crescent advocate's hand on the
prior refusal.* *Decision pending consultation with the crew and the
manifest officer.* The page is the page. Drova has surfaced what is
on it without naming what is in it.

Fei lets a fourth breath sit. She looks once, briefly, across the
apron at Inka. Inka is still holding the packet two-handed in the
way a hab-courier holds a thing they have brought a long way for
somebody else. Inka has not moved. Inka believes she is delivering
something her late husband's brother will receive with thanks. Inka
has worn that belief for a passenger-kite-length, last night to this
morning, alone on the bench.

Fei looks back at the day-page. She speaks. Her speech is not yet at
full speed — the slow-speech window has closed in the body but the
mouth still trims its sentences for another minute — and so the
question comes out the way Fei's questions come out when she has
trimmed them down to the load-bearing piece.

*What does she think it is.*

She does not phrase it as a question. The question is in the words.
Drova does not need the lift of voice that would tell her what she
already heard.

Fei does not follow up. She sets the question on the lectern, the
way Drova set the cross-reference on the page, and she leaves it
there for whoever is going to pick it up. Her hand finds the rim of
the day-page beside Drova's hand. Not on it. Beside it. The two
hands are at the same height on the rim, on either side of the
cross-reference, and they do not touch. She rests it there. The
cuffs hum once, very faintly, as the last of the diffuse cycle
empties out, and then they go quiet.

She does not say the next thing. The next thing is whether somebody
walks to the bench and asks Inka in the courtesy register what she
thinks the packet contains. She believes it is hers to offer if
Drova does not have the answer. She does not believe it is hers to
do without Drova's word, because Drova is the clerk and the bench
is in the clerk's seat. So Fei waits.

The dock lamps are still on. First light is six minutes from the
horizon line where the corridor opens onto the south berth — Fei
read it on the way back. Mereth is in the office with Halvi. Tek is
at the spar. Bren is at the hatch.

---

Conditional reactions, for whoever the rotation lands on next:

If Drova has an answer to *what does she think it is* — Fei
receives it the way she received the sentence about the log. Three
breaths. No summary. If the answer narrows the question of carry
versus refuse, Fei may offer one further sentence in interpreter
register: a piece of what she heard from the corridor about the
night passenger-kite's arrival, if anything in that residue is
useful to the narrowing. She will not lead with the offer; she will
follow the answer.

If Drova does not have an answer — Fei will say, quietly, *I can
ask.* That is two words. They mean: *I will walk to the bench and
ask Inka in the courtesy register what she believes she is
delivering, and I will bring back what she says without smoothing
it.* The offer is Fei's craft. The decision to send the craft is
Drova's, not Fei's. Fei waits for the nod.

If Tek comes back to the lectern after the loop and stands at the
spar-side rim of the page — Fei holds her tongue. Tek's read of the
packet form is on the day-page in the cross-reference indirectly
(*sender's binding, hab-knot, cord fresh*) but Tek's actual read
went past what is written; if Tek wants to add it she will add it.
Fei does not pull on Tek for that. The etiquette holds.

If Bren reads the day-page from the hatch side — Fei does not flag
the cross-reference for her either. Bren reads the day-page every
morning and Bren will count and Bren will look up. Fei does not
interrupt Bren's count.

If Inka stands or signals before the office door opens — the bench
is in Drova's seat. Fei steps a quarter pace back from the lectern
and lets Drova take the bench. If Drova nods Fei toward it instead,
Fei walks the six paces and stops at the polite Hab distance and
holds her tongue three breaths and asks, in the courtesy register
her aunt would have used to a hab-elder, *Has anyone told you what
you are carrying.* That is the only sentence she will spend on the
matter at the bench. She will bring back what Inka says, in Inka's
words, in interpreter shorthand later if there is time, from
Inka-mouth-now if there is not.

If Mereth comes out of the office before any of this completes —
Fei steps the quarter pace back from the lectern. The page is in
the room. The clerk will say what the clerk says. The manifest
officer will read what the manifest officer reads. Fei does not
volunteer her question to Mereth; the question is for Drova or for
Inka and not for the manifest officer, and the manifest officer
will, in any case, ask her own.

If something goes wrong — if the packet hums, if the bench shifts,
if first light arrives faster than it should — Fei has the move. It
is on the page in `signature-moves.md`. She will not pre-spend it
on a paper-and-cord parcel. If the apron asks for it, she will go
diffuse and the cuffs will hum and Tek will hear the hum without
turning her head and Bren will count to four and look away and
Drova's hand on the day-page will be where Drova's hand on the
day-page is, and Fei will come back with one sentence. Not before.

She breathes. Her hand stays beside Drova's hand on the rim of the
page. The cross-reference sits between them. The cuffs are quiet.
The page does what the page does.

---

Handing off.
turn.speaker: renno

messages.body: Revision in. Added the watching-hands beat to mirror your line — Bren reads it as a count of four at every walkaround, then looks away. Kept your flicker as Tek's private fact: from Bren's side it is 'meant something, and the thing it meant was not nothing,' not knowing what. Also tightened her in-the-moment read to 'face change between the two passes' so the gesture-meant-something is on the page in Bren's voice, not the post-hoc voice. Push back if any of this drifts past where you want it.

turn.prose:
# Turn 17 — Kit

OOC, jerky in one hand, dice tower stacked.

Drained the bus — one entry, my own outbound to Tev confirming Bren's revision, already did its work. Nothing live on the queue.

Read the table, tev's loop, sumi's lectern, renno's question on Fei's lips beside Drova's hand. Beautiful three turns. The shape coming around to me: cross-reference up in graduated certainty, *what does she think it is* sitting on the page, Inka still holding the packet two-handed, dock lamps still on, first light six minutes from the south berth horizon.

Bren's seat is drop-pilot. Reads the day-page every morning per the etiquette sumi flagged. Chalk on her sleeve, streamer pouch at her chest. She is the one who hands the packet to Eshil Caur on the other end if we carry — a thing nobody else on the apron is. Her *veto*, such as it is, lives in the chalk: bags on the manifest get chalked, bags not on the manifest don't.

Strength is on me starting this turn. Patience before force. The Bren version of that is the streamer staying in the pouch and the chalk staying on the sleeve. She does the morning read. She does not throw. She does not chalk. She does not commit her seat.

No rolls. The streamer toss is the signature move, and the carry/refuse call is the seat it was built for — but Mereth has not come out yet, and the call is not Bren's to lead. Save the move.

No messages out. No edits to public character row, relationships, or secrets. Scratchpad gets tidied after the prelude.

---

Bren has the cargo hatch and a clean cargo board. The last bag is not yet on the apron — Halvi has the paperwork on it inside, and the bag is in the office antechamber until Halvi comes out with the stamp. There is nothing yet to chalk. The board sits clean on the hatch lip. The chalk is on her left sleeve where she left it after the line-bag at four-thirty.

She has been at the hatch twenty minutes. She has watched the apron from the cargo seat the way she watches any apron pre-lift — not directly. Eyes on the streamer pouch at her chest and on the chalk-board edge in front of her, the apron coming in around her like the wind. She caught Tek's loop in the boot-pace on the deck. The steady walkaround pace, the pause at the rigging join, the count under her breath as Tek passed the hatch. She caught Drova's six paces over to the bench and six back. She caught Fei coming in through the corridor mouth and the small motion of cuff-check against the inside of each wrist. She did not look at any of them.

In the second half of her twenty minutes she has been reading the wind, not by streamer. By the cuff of her own coat on her wrist and by the cold off the corridor mouth on the side of her face. The corridor pulls south-by-southwest at this time of year, this side of the Crescent, the last forty minutes before first light. The pull this morning is the pull it should be. It will turn for the cross-pull about ten minutes after the sun catches the south berth. That is a yellow-ribbon lift, possibly white if the south corner holds. She has the call. She does not need the streamer to confirm. The streamer is for the office and the rest of the crew, who do not feel wind on the cuff of the coat the way Bren does. She will throw at three minutes to light, not before.

She steps off the cargo hatch. Six paces along the apron lectern side. She comes to the lectern from the hatch corner — the corner opposite Fei's — and stops at the rim. She does not stand at Drova's shoulder. The polite distance from the lectern at the cargo-side corner is a forearm's reach from the page-edge.

She reads the day-page the way she reads it every morning. Top to bottom. Date, manifest header, lift window, weather note, the kite-readiness row — she does not look at her own row to see if Tek has signed it yet; Tek will sign when Tek signs — and then the bottom of the open page where Drova has just written.

The cross-reference is in graduated certainty. *Consistent with prior refusal-log entry. Recipient identity not yet verified beyond name on packet exterior. Sender's hand is not the bench-sitter's; trained, copyist register, Glasswake-elementary form of the late teens, not the Crescent advocate's hand on the prior refusal. Decision pending consultation with the crew and the manifest officer.*

Bren counts under her breath. One — two — three — four.

The count is not for Tek this time. The count is the count she uses on the day-page when Drova writes in this register — the length of breath it takes to read the entry once carefully and not rush past it. Drova has written it the way Drova writes refusal-log work: in the language that says exactly what is on the page without saying what is not yet known. *Consistent with* is the load-bearing phrase. *Not yet verified beyond.* *Decision pending.* Bren has been reading the day-page every morning for fifteen months. She knows the register. She knows what Drova has and has not said.

She does not pull the archive. The shelf-box is at Drova's ankle and Bren does not reach for it. The cross-reference number is enough. It places the prior entry somewhere in the first quarter of two years and four months ago — the same general year as a month she has thought about more often than she would tell anyone she has thought about it. Not the same month. Adjacent. Close enough that her count goes a half-beat longer than it would on any other entry, and then she is past it.

She looks up. She looks at Inka.

Inka is on the bench with the packet between two hands. The cloth on the basket is folded once, clean. The coat is wool, re-felted at the cuffs by a hand that knew. Bren's mother re-felted coats in the long winter at Tarrest and at the start of every spring at Hab Tarrest, and Bren has been on the same supper table for that work. She knows the stitch the way she knows the back of her own knuckle. The cord on the packet is a hab-knot — she can read the lay of it from here — and it is not the cousin of the knot her father used. Different hab. But near enough that she can tell which side of the closure the knot was tied on. Inka tied it. Inka tied it on the passenger-kite last night, around what was given to her by the sender.

She reads the packet weight from the way Inka is holding it. Two hands. Not the carry of something heavy. Not the carry of something so light it would flop. Paper. Folded paper. Possibly a small object inside the paper. She cannot tell from this distance whether the small object is a coin or a token or a curl of cord or nothing.

She does not stand at the lectern long. She does not put her chalk down on the page margin. She does not write on the cargo board. She does not chalk the packet onto the manifest. The board stays clean. The chalk stays on her sleeve. Bren's seat has not been asked to commit. She turns and walks the six paces back to the cargo hatch.

She does not throw the streamer. Her right hand finds the flap of the pouch on the walk back and her fingers run along the inside seam where the yellow ribbons are folded. She counts the yellow by feel — three folded, one rolled. She has the color. She does not have the throw. The pouch closes again.

She sits back on the cargo hatch. The board is clean in front of her. The chalk is on her sleeve. Her breath comes off her in a small column. The dock lamps are still on.

She watches Inka. She watches the page on the lectern from the cargo-side. She watches Fei's hand on the rim of the page beside Drova's hand. She does not look at Tek on the spar. She knows where Tek is from the sound of the haul line on the back of Tek's hand and the absence of that sound, which is the sound of standing. She has not said anything this morning to any of them. She has not been asked.

When she is asked, she has two things her seat can spend, and one she does not want to spend for free. The drop-pilot read: the wind window will turn cross-pull at twelve minutes past light; the call is yellow, possibly white. The hab-worker read: the coat is Meridian-three-years and the cord is Inka's own, tied on the passenger-kite, around what she was given. The third — the one she does not want to spend for free — is that if we carry, she is the one who hands the packet to Eshil Caur on the other end. That changes what the question is asking. If somebody on the crew puts the carry-question to her seat directly, that is what she will say. Not before.

---

Conditional reactions, for whoever the rotation lands on next.

If Mereth comes out of the office before the carry-refuse is touched in conversation — Bren stays on the cargo hatch. The board is clean. The chalk is on the sleeve. Mereth will read the day-page first, the way Mereth always does. The cross-reference is in graduated certainty. Mereth will know what the page knows. The decision will go where Mereth puts it. Bren waits.

If Drova nods Fei toward the bench and Fei walks the six paces — Bren counts four, and on four Bren stands, walks two paces from the hatch toward the cargo edge of the apron, and lays her right hand on the cargo seat at hip height. The cargo seat is the seat the carrier sits at if the packet rides. Standing beside it is the not-yet. Sitting on it is the yes. Bren stands beside.

If Drova does not nod Fei, and the bench sits — Bren keeps her seat. The chalk is on the sleeve. The streamer is in the pouch. The wind is the wind it should be. Three minutes to light, then two, then one. If light catches the south berth and nobody has decided, Bren throws — yellow — and the throw is the streamer call for the office, not the answer to the carry-question. She has thrown a thousand yellow ribbons in the last fifteen months and the office reads the throw before the office reads anything else. The throw says *we are loaded and the wind is on.* It does not say *we have decided.*

If Fei or Drova asks Bren for the wind read — Bren has it in one sentence. *Yellow lift, possibly white if the south corner holds; cross-pull turns at twelve past light.* That is the drop-pilot's read. The carry-question is not in that sentence.

If Tek comes back to the lectern after the loop and stands at the spar-side rim of the page — Bren is at the hatch. Bren looks up once. The count goes one, two, three, four. Bren looks away. Tek does not need to be watched.

If Inka stands or signals — Bren does not move to the bench. Drova's seat owns the bench. If Drova passes the bench to Fei, Fei walks. If at some point the crew needs a hab-worker register at the bench that the orc-clerk and the fae do not carry, Bren has the register. She does not volunteer it. If asked: she walks.

If something goes wrong before any of this — the packet hums, the bench shifts, the wind turns ten minutes early — Bren throws. White, in that case. The white throw says *we are not lifting yet.* That is the streamer toss in the seat it was built for.

---

Handing off.
turn.speaker: kit

messages.body: Heads-up on the opening beat from my side: Tek starts on the walkaround (signature move on page from turn one). The loop will bring her past the lectern around when the day-page lands on Eshil Caur. Tek does not look at Drova's face. She looks at the hand on the page for the count of one footstep — if it stops on the entry beside the name, she sees it stops; if it doesn't, she sees that too. No words from her side, eyes-not-meeting per the etiquette we wrote. Tek's loop continues past, brief stop at a rigging join six paces on, finishes around to the hatch. Tic-tracer stays in the coat pocket — Drova-on-manifest day per turn 12. Push back if any of this drifts past where the asymmetry wants it; easy to soften the look-at-the-hand to seeing-nothing if you'd rather Drova not be observed in this beat.

Confirming the lectern pass-by beat as you wrote it. Timing on my end: Drova has already crossed to Inka, returned, knelt at the archive shelf-box, pulled entry 2434-Q1-007, read it, and written a cross-reference into the day-page by the time your walkaround reaches the lectern. So your read — *hand beside the entry, not moving* — lands because Drova has just finished. The hand is at rest on the rim of the page. Three things on the cross-reference, written in graduated-certainty register: (1) the prior entry number 2434-Q1-007, (2) the recipient name match consistent with prior entry, identity not yet verified beyond name on packet exterior, (3) sender-hand analysis from the packet — Glasswake-elementary copyist register, not the prior advocate's hand. Mereth's reason from the prior entry is *not* copied onto today's page; anyone who wants it pulls the archive themselves. The page is now a thing in the room. If Tek's eye ever crosses the day-page from the spar end over the next ten-or-so minutes, it's legible from that angle. I am not scripting a Tek-reads-it beat; the page is the spare concrete image and you decide separately whether Tek looks. Tic-tracer in coat pocket on Drova-on-manifest days is canon; keeping that.

Heads-up for Fei's read: Drova is going to cross from the lectern to Inka on the forwarding bench, exchange a few sentences in Hab courtesy register (will not ask what's in the packet; will read the address on the outside at three paces), return to the lectern, kneel at the archive shelf-box, pull a refusal-log entry from two-years-four-months ago, read it, write a cross-reference into today's day-page in graduated certainty. Fei is coming back from the corridor in roughly the same window. If you want Fei to listen-to-the-apron in diffuse during the exchange and report a sentence + four exact details, the apron is open and Drova will not direct Fei's attention or summarize for her. Drova will not, in this scene, tell Inka that Eshil Caur is on the log — that is the manifest officer's call to deliver. If Fei stops at the lectern after settling, Drova will hold three breaths and tell her quietly: recipient is on the log, two years four months, I have not told her yet. Easy to soften if any of that drifts past where Fei's diffuse-listening etiquette wants to sit.

Taking the open offer. Fei comes back through the corridor mouth, does the cuff back-of-hand check (Tek's gesture, hers now), reads the apron in residual diffuse for the first minute as the cuffs settle — sees Inka still on the bench holding the packet two-handed, sees the day-page motion ended with Drova's hand on the rim. Crosses to the lectern. Stops at the polite three-pace distance she has stood at for refusal-log conferences for three years, just behind Drova's right shoulder. Three breaths. Receives Drova's sentence. Three breaths. Then one quiet question: 'What does she think it is?' That is all Fei spends. Not advice, not a next step — just the one piece of the question that nobody else has asked yet because it isn't the clerk's question to ask. If Drova does not have the answer, Fei may quietly offer to walk to the bench and ask in the courtesy register; that offer is hers to make and will not preempt your turn. Easy to soften the question if 'what does she think it is' lands past where the etiquette wants Fei. Holding everything else for when carry/refuse actually needs it.

---
title: Mara's scratchpad — turn 12, Round 2 review, mode closing
---

# Where I am

Character-creation, turn 12. DM review at the end of Round 2.
All four PCs are authored, all four relationships files are written,
no orphans. Mode ends this turn. Next phase: prelude-arc.

The four PCs:

- **Tek'iris Korr** (tev) — gnome Hab-Worlder, Splitfork kite-wright,
  off-the-books. Lattice-loss from the Shear run two years ago,
  hidden via a scratch-built tic-tracer. Wednesday/Tuesday Compact
  filing discrepancy. HP 8.
- **Drova Korvanis** (sumi) — Sithari orc, ex-Echo Ledger Conclave
  intake clerk, now the Splitfork's refusal-log clerk. Holding Senna
  Talindra's undelivered sealed letters at Mer Vasht's pawn shop. HP 10.
- **Fei Mern** (renno) — fae Hab-Worlder, decom-hab kid from Mernhab,
  Splitfork listener. Keeps a private sidecar drop log to Velsk Tarn,
  ex-council member compiling a decommissioning record. HP 8.
- **Bren Mosk** (kit) — human Hab-Worlder, drop-pilot,
  resonance-blind, Tarrest decom kid. Private cache of unmanifested
  ringfall fragments at her mother's, including one piece with what
  may be a partial chain-patronymic mark. HP 10.

- Inventory: 4 items each with named signature item. ✓

No off-spec items. No revision requests sent.
sections.title: Mechanical compliance

sections.text:
These are conjunctions between player canon and my prep that the
players don't yet know I've noticed:

### Drova ↔ Veth Karran (the night seal-setter)

Drova filed three internal observations about Sable Crescent
transcript redactions, all in the same frequency band, all by a
department she names obliquely (in her own intro). The third one
named the senior archivist. She was reassigned and given a
confidentiality binder.

Veth Karran is my Conclave antagonist — night-shift seal rotation, the
person who decides what gets sealed before morning, deep want is keeping
her 0200 queue clear. The reassignment-after-the-third-observation
shape is consistent with someone on the night seal rotation deciding
*Drova is what is interrupting my queue*. Whether Veth was the
specific clerk who set the seals or simply approved the reassignment
is open — I am not yet committing. The shape is right.

This means: if play surfaces Drova returning to Thornvault, or if the
Conclave dossier on the Ledger Run (secret #7) starts to need a face,
Veth and Drova have an existing history that neither of them has
publicly acknowledged. Drova's binder may have been signed under
Veth's seal.

I will not write this as DM canon yet. I will let play ask.

### The previous Splitfork drop-pilot

Bren introduced a refusal-log month-gap: the month the previous
drop-pilot died (Hab Meridian descent, '32), the refusal log has no
entries at all. Not refused, not carried. That is a hole in a
document Mereth founded on the principle of no edits.

This is a player handing me a hook. Accepting as canon. Implications:

- Mereth either has not noticed (Bren's belief; consistent with
  Mereth's character), or has noticed and not addressed it (possible;
  more painful). I will play to find out.
- The previous drop-pilot now has a name and a fate I am holding
  back. Candidate names: not yet committed. Probably one syllable.
- The month-gap interacts with secret #2 (the Halfsign's "Shear
  damage" is wrong) — both are gaps in the Splitfork's own record
  about events involving its own kites. I had not planned this
  parallel. I am noticing it now and letting it sit.

If Bren pulls the thread, the question is *who told the office to
skip that month*. The answer is not Mereth. The answer is — I will
decide when the table asks.

### The Wednesday/Tuesday Shear gap

Two PCs (Tek, Bren) were on the Shear run two years ago. The Compact
filing reads Tuesday. The cascade ran Wednesday. Neither said so at
the time. Neither has said so since. Tek does not know Bren noticed;
Bren does not know about Tek's lattice loss.

This is a player-authored open secret with two witnesses. It is now
canon that the Compact filing has the wrong date. Whoever signed the
filing wrote a Tuesday where a Wednesday belonged. That signature is
recoverable from public Compact records. I have not yet decided
whose signature it is, but the candidates are interesting:

- The Splitfork's filing crew at the time (Ev? Mereth herself?).
- A Compact clerk who wrote down what they were told.
- A Conclave-adjacent reviewer who pre-approved the cause-of-incident.

The Tuesday is on the page because somebody put it there. *Who*
opens the question of *why the right date wouldn't fit*.

### Mernhab decommissioning

Renno introduced Velsk Tarn (Mernhab council subcommittee member who
left after the decommissioning vote and is compiling a private
record) as Fei's sidecar recipient. Fei sends environmental-only
data once a month from waystation drops. This is friction, not
sabotage — player has been explicit.

I want to be careful with this. The Mernhab story is now load-bearing
for Fei and for the campaign. Velsk Tarn is off-stage but available.
*Who voted Mernhab down, and what diagnostic they suppressed* is a
genuinely open question I have not yet answered. I would like to
honor renno's "I want the diagnostic, not the press release" goal by
making the diagnostic *findable but expensive*. Probably tied to a
Conclave reading-room or a Thornvault basement.

The cuffs are the last working pair in the system. That detail can
do work later — if Fei's cuffs fail in scene, the search for a
replacement leads exactly back to the diagnostic question.

### Bren's elven-marked fragment

Kit's secret: one piece in the cache has what may be the *ve* of a
chain patronymic, partially scoured. Player explicitly says "I do not
believe an elven artifact in a Glasswake bedroom is the start of any
campaign she wants to be in" and would prefer it remain not-yet
thought about.

Respecting that. Will not surface this in the opening arc. If a
later arc wants an elven thread, it is there. Until then it sits
under the bed.

### Org-side echoes I'm holding

- Mereth and Senna — both Sithari adjacent to Conclave records work.
  Plausible they overlapped at some point. I will not invent this
  yet, but they are in the same generation.
- Halvi's clean ledger (parallel to Mereth's refusal log) and the
  refusal log month-gap that Bren named: Halvi may have a memory of
  that month that Mereth does not. Three-Clue-rule-style:
  - Clue A: refusal log gap (already canon).
  - Clue B: Halvi's clean ledger from that month — does it have
    Splitfork entries? Probably yes, which makes the absence in the
    refusal log louder.
  - Clue C: Compact filing from the previous drop-pilot's death — is
    it on the public record at all?
  I am not building this arc yet. Filing it.
sections.title: Accidental connections I want to remember

sections.text:
- I did not name Veth Karran. The Drova ↔ Veth connection is mine to
  hold until play asks.
- I did not commit to who killed the previous drop-pilot or
  doctored the refusal log month-gap. Bren's question is the
  question.
- I did not commit to who signed the Wednesday-as-Tuesday filing.
- I did not name Sava Lensk (Tek's folk practitioner) as needing
  more prep yet. She is a known-named NPC now; if play moves toward
  Tek's lattice realignment, I will write her a notes stub.
- I did not commit to who, on the Mernhab council, voted yes/no/no
  on decommissioning.

These are the *good* DM moves to hold. The party gave me enough hooks
to run a whole arc on. I am going to let them choose which ones to
pull.
sections.title: What I held back from the turn prose

sections.text:
I deliberately did not seed party shape during planning, and the four
PCs that resulted are:

- A Hab-Worlder gnome kite-wright with lattice loss (Tek)
- A Sithari orc clerk with the Conclave behind her (Drova)
- A fae decom-hab listener with two record-commitments (Fei)
- A human ringfall scavenger turned drop-pilot, resonance-blind (Bren)

Four cultures (Sithari, Hab-Worlder x3 of which one fae, one gnome,
one human, one human-on-the-surface), four professions, four
relationships with the Conclave (lapsed, ex-staff, claimant-in-waiting,
indifferent), four relationships with resonance sense (lattice loss,
distrusts own, attunement-advanced and trained, resonance-blind).

The party is *not* one shape. The methodology's anti-sameness rigor
held. I will let the party play the way it built — heterogeneous,
asymmetric, four very different relationships to the same Question.
sections.title: Anti-sameness check on the party

sections.text:
- Sumi, Renno, Tev each write 1-2 relationship seeds.
- Drova is the orphan. Somebody needs to point at her.
- I review again after Round 2 closes.
- Then `glass mode end` and the campaign moves to prelude-arc.
sections.title: Round 2 plan

sections.text:
### The four relationships, settled

- **Bren → Fei (seed 10, cultural slip), Bren → Tek (seed 2, Shear).**
  Already-canon from turn 7.
- **Tek → Bren (seed 2, mirror).** Two witnesses to a Wednesday wind.
  The second pass on the haul line was the last time the old sense
  was fully working. Lattice-loss now has a moment with a witness
  who saw the gesture without reading what was inside it.
- **Tek → Drova (seed 7, the receipt).** Tek matched *Korvanis* on
  the wage book against *Korvanis* on the Glasswake annex
  request-receipt and copied the name into the back of her refusal
  log. Has not asked. Has started leaving the tic-tracer in her coat
  pocket on Drova-manifest days.
- **Drova → Tek (seed 7, mirror).** Drova does not remember the
  pull. She has the refusal-log archive at her elbow and has chosen,
  three years running, not to spend thirty minutes verifying. The
  load-bearing piece is the *chosen not to know*.
- **Drova → Fei (seed 11, decommissioned hab).** *Feishara* on the
  upstream copy of the Mernhab intake list, '34, Drova's hand. The
  connection landed on a quarterly review afternoon three months
  after Fei joined the kite. Plus: a memory of a Mernhab resonance
  diagnostic packet that Drova does not trust her own memory of.
- **Fei → Drova (seed 11, mirror).** Three years composing the
  question. *The etiquette is itself a form of refusal log — kept by
  two of us, in two different shapes, in the same room.* That line
  goes into the campaign's bones.
- **Fei → Tek (seed 14, the taught thing).** Back-of-hand check on
  the cuffs. Taught once, three words, no ceremony, four years ago.
  Fei has never said *thank you*. Tek may or may not know Fei still
  does it.

### Relationship web

```
Tek ←  Bren  (Shear, seed 2)
Tek ←  Drova (receipt, seed 7)
Tek ←  Fei   (taught thing, seed 14)
Drova ← Tek  (receipt, seed 7)
Drova ← Fei  (Mernhab, seed 11)
Fei   ← Drova (Mernhab, seed 11)
Fei   ← Bren  (cultural slip, seed 10)
Bren  ← Tek   (Shear, seed 2)
```

Tek is the centre. Bren has one inbound (from Tek). Drova and Fei
have two inbounds each. No orphans. Asymmetry intentional: the
kite-wright is the gravitational centre by virtue of being the one
who is always already there when you do not yet have words for the
thing you are noticing.

### Texture decisions I'm noting because both PCs went there

- **Graduated-certainty register** crossed from Drova's binder onto
  her *internal* register. *Consistent with*, *cannot be eliminated
  as a source*, *no evidence to support* is now how she thinks to
  herself. Tek noticed without naming. The register is now a thing
  the world has — and a thing the other PCs can recognise when
  Drova uses it under stress.
- **The Splitfork's private second refusal log** — between Drova
  and Fei, mutual non-asking, three years of it, observed but
  unwritten. Same *shape* as Mereth's wall ledger by accident or
  instinct. I am going to play to the moment when one of them moves
  first and what that does to the shape.
- **Two practitioners of not-asking** — Tek and Fei both maintain
  their own instruments and do not bring them to the wright except
  for tuning. Neither has asked the other about the actual readings.
  The back-of-hand check is the *proof on the page* of a shared
  practice neither has named.
- **Adjusted carries** — Tek leaves the tic-tracer in her coat
  pocket on Drova-manifest days. Drova does not look up Tek's
  Compact filing in the refusal-log archive. Both PCs have adjusted
  what they carry on their person around the other one without
  naming why. This is a very playable kind of withholding.

### What I am still holding back

Same things as Round 1 end, plus:

- Whose hand wrote *Tuesday* on the Compact filing. Still open.
- Whether the request slip for Tek's transcript was Veth Karran's,
  or routine annex-to-curatorial upstreaming, or the senior
  archivist Drova later named in her third observation. **All three
  live.** Drova's fear that it could be either-or-any is the
  load-bearing piece and I am preserving it. I do not commit yet.
- Whether the Mernhab resonance diagnostic packet actually exists as
  a routed document or whether Drova has rebuilt it from the shape
  of having been a clerk who routed many such things. **Both live.**
  The question of *what Drova's memory does when she cannot afford it
  to be wrong* is now in the campaign and will pay for itself.
- The previous Splitfork drop-pilot's name and fate. Still mine.
- The elven-marked fragment in Bren's cache. Still under the bed.

### NPCs that have firmed up enough to need a note stub

- **Senna Talindra** — already firm; sealed letters at Mer Vasht's,
  Lira at Glasswake, Sithari old-guard adjacent to Conclave.
- **Velsk Tarn** — already firm; ex-Mernhab council, compiling
  decommissioning record, Fei's sidecar recipient.
- **Veth Karran** — still mine, not on the player-visible page yet.
  Night-seal rotation in Thornvault. The shape lines up with three
  separate player threads (Drova's observations, Tek's transcript
  pull, the Mernhab diagnostic packet). One person could plausibly
  be the joint cause of all three. I am not committing yet.
- **The previous Splitfork drop-pilot** — needs a name before the
  prelude-arc opens, since Bren may well start there. One syllable,
  probably. Holding.

I'll write the note stubs when prelude-arc opens, not before.
sections.title: Round 2 closure notes (turn 12)

sections.text:
Methodology to follow: `methodologies/prelude-arc.md`. Two short
scenes before the main campaign.

The opening situation I am leaning toward — not committing yet,
will draft on the prelude-arc turn:

- A morning on the Splitfork. A manifest. One of the items on the
  manifest will be a refusal that's already been logged. The crew
  will need to decide whether to honour the refusal or override it.
- Whose call it is, and whose body is on the kite for it, is what
  the first scene asks. Bren has the leadership posture without
  ever having voted on it. Mereth is on the apron. Drova is at the
  log. Tek is on the spar. Fei is on the meter.
- The thread I am *not* pulling first: the Wednesday/Tuesday Shear
  gap. Too central. Save it.
- The thread I am inclined to pull first: a low-stakes refusal that
  asks Drova whether she wants to log it the way Mereth logs things
  or the way the Conclave logs things. That's a question the
  graduated-certainty register makes immediate.
sections.title: Prelude-arc readiness

sections.text:
Arc `prelude` scaffolded. Plan and player-facing context written.
Scene 1 (`prelude-opening`, scene-play) and scene 2 (`prelude-action`,
action) shapes drafted in the arc plan. Scene 1 is now on the stack.

### The scene 1 setup, committed

Pre-dawn at the Glasswake reclaimed dock, Splitfork berthed and
loaded except the last bag. Mereth and Halvi inside finishing the
manifest; Ev mostly absent. All four PCs on the apron at their
usual stations. Walk-up on the forwarding bench: **Inka Vell**, Hab
Meridian widow, mid-fifties, came down on the night passenger-kite.
Small sealed packet (waxed paper, one cord, no seal) for *Eshil
Caur*, Sable Crescent. Basket of dried apricots for whoever
decides. She has not interrupted; she has been waiting.

The hook: *Eshil Caur* is on the refusal log. Two years four months
ago Mereth refused a Conclave-marked summons to him at a Crescent
advocate's request. Different sender now, different shape, no seal,
no markings. Plausibly nothing; plausibly not.

The window: fifteen-to-twenty minutes before Mereth comes out.
*That* is the scene — what kind of crew this is when she is not in
the room.

### What I am holding back from the players

- The packet's contents. I do not know yet. My current lean: a
  single small object Den Vell left for his brother, no political
  freight, but the *carrying* is what is in dispute. I will decide
  if and only if a PC's decision turns on the contents.
- Whether the original refusal-log entry has an internal note. It
  does (advocate's request inconsistent with subject's standing; no
  harm visible in carry, no good visible either). Surfaces only if
  Drova pulls the archive shelf-box. Atomic; pick when needed.
- Eshil Caur's full identity. He is alive in the Crescent. Former
  Conclave reading-room steward who left before Drova arrived at
  Thornvault. Not the senior archivist from Drova's third
  observation. Not anyone Drova would recognise. Surface only if
  asked, only in graduated certainty.
- Inka has not opened the packet. She thinks she knows what is in
  it; she does not in fact know.

### Inka Vell — quick stub (will promote to dm/notes/npcs/ later)

Hab-Worlder, 55. Mernhab is not her hab; she is from a smaller
upper-ring section of Hab Meridian. Late husband Den Vell, brother
to Eshil Caur. Den and Eshil had not spoken in nineteen years. Den
left a small package for Eshil. Inka has not opened it. Year-mark of
Den's death is in eleven days. She wants this off her hands before
then. She is patient. She does not interrupt. She will not bargain.
She will give the apricots to whoever decides — apricots are her
mother-in-law's recipe, dried by Inka herself.

### Scene 2 (prelude-action) — pre-prep

Dock master **Han Pellow** comes onto the apron at first light with
a hard call: lift in 15 minutes or scrub. Shear-edge front rolling
in faster than the morning forecast. Tracker `lift-window`, 4
segments, public, scene-only. Three things must be true to lift:

1. Walkaround signed (Tek)
2. Cargo manifest closed — including the Vell-packet decision filed
   either way (Drova)
3. Approach line read (Fei + Bren)

Endpoints: clean lift / dirty lift / scrub. No PC death, no
campaign-defining win or loss. Each shape carries a small concrete
consequence into the time jump.

I will set the `lift-window` tracker when scene 2 opens, not before.

### What I am watching for in scene 1

- Does Drova pull the archive, or does she trust the day-page?
- Does Bren walk to the bench before the others have committed in
  committee?
- Does Fei spend *Listening Past Last Light* now or save it?
- Does Tek's walkaround happen this scene or get pushed to scene 2?
- Whose decision style sets the tone — graduated certainty (Drova),
  *I'm going to / I'm not* (Bren), the question-as-question (Fei),
  the slow read (Tek)?

The handoff goes to tev next on default rotation. I am not queuing.
sections.title: Prelude opens — turn 13

sections.text:
Off the desk for the first time in three turns. Refilled. Apron
quiet. First light any minute.
sections.title: Mug status

sections.text:
The opening beat played out evenly. Tek did the walkaround
(signature loop on screen, no roll, no stop, tic-tracer stayed in
coat pocket — Drova-manifest day). Drova crossed to Inka in
courtesy register, came back, pulled archive entry 2434-Q1-007,
read it (including Mereth's reason, which Drova did *not* copy
forward), wrote a cross-reference into the day-page in graduated
certainty. Fei stopped at Drova's right shoulder, three breaths,
received Drova's sentence, three breaths, asked *what does she
think it is*. Bren read the day-page, counted, did not chalk, went
back to the cargo hatch.

What changed on the page: a cross-reference. The recipient name is
now publicly cited against the prior refusal-log entry on the
day-page. Anyone who reads the day-page after this morning will
see the cross-reference. Halvi cross-checks it routinely. Mereth
will read it when she comes out.

What did *not* change on the page: the internal note on the
original refusal (*advocate's request inconsistent with subject's
standing; no harm visible in carry, no good visible either*).
Drova read it and did not surface it. That note is still atomic;
will surface only if a PC pulls the archive themselves, or asks
Drova directly for the reason. Holding.

What Inka does not know yet: that her recipient is on the office's
refusal log. The clerk did not tell her. The manifest officer's
news to deliver, per Drova's read of the etiquette. Tracking.

What Fei's question opens: whether Drova has a read on the packet
contents (she has not used *Angle*; she has not been asked to
use it). The clean answers are: *I do not know* (true), *I have
not looked* (also true, by deliberate choice), or *I can look*
(true and load-bearing — *Angle* on a packet held in a stranger's
hands at six feet is a stretch; on a packet placed on the lectern
it is in scope). Drova has not picked the question up yet. The
rotation will pick it up.

### What I am watching for in the next loop

- Does Drova answer Fei? If yes, in what register — the *I do not
  know* shape, or the *I can read it* shape?
- Does anyone walk to the bench and ask Inka the question in
  hab-courtesy register? Fei has offered; the offer is conditional
  on Drova's nod. Bren has the register but does not volunteer.
- Does Tek volunteer the longer read of the packet form (hab-knot,
  domestic-not-claimed-private, the *between* form her mother
  used)? She has it. She will not lead with it. If asked, she
  spends four sentences.
- Does Bren say the wind read? She has it in one sentence
  (*yellow lift, possibly white if the south corner holds;
  cross-pull turns at twelve past light*). She will not lead with
  it unless asked.
- Does anyone notice the lamp flicker? It is a kite-crew tell
  (~10 min to light). Saying it out loud is uncharacteristic; not
  saying it but adjusting pace is in character.
- Does Inka stand or signal? Her grip shifted; her breath has gone
  thinner. She has not stood. If she stands, the bench moves to
  whoever Drova nods toward.

### What I am holding back

Same things as turn 13, no additions:

- Packet contents. Not deciding until a PC's decision turns on it.
- Original refusal-log internal note. Atomic.
- Eshil Caur's full identity (former Conclave reading-room steward,
  left before Drova arrived at Thornvault, no political freight).
  Surfaces only if asked, only in graduated certainty.
- Inka's belief about the contents (a single object Den left for
  his brother — true; she does not know what the object is).
  Surfaces if Bren or Fei asks her directly.
- The lift-window tracker. Stays in scene 2.
- Han Pellow. Stays in scene 2.

### Tarot / verse texture for this turn

The Emperor on me: structure, borders, rank, threshold. The office
doorframe is the threshold I named in prose. Drova's three-pace
distance at the bench is rank-and-border. Fei's three-pace
distance behind Drova's right shoulder is rank-and-border. Bren's
forearm-reach at the cargo-side rim is rank-and-border. The
structure held this round and was lightly tested by Fei's question.
I did not announce the tarot. It is on the texture.

The verse *evenness of mind is yoga* — the morning has been even
on all four counts. I echoed *even-paced, even-keeled* in the OOC
without quoting the verse. Not naming it in scene prose. The
evenness is the texture; the texture is the scene.
sections.title: Turn 18 note — DM check-in after four player turns

entity.title: Mara's scratchpad — turn 12, Round 2 review, mode closing

turn.prose:
---
title: Turn 18 — Mara — the apron at not-quite first light
turn_id: test-7-t0018
speaker: dm
mode: scene-play
scene: prelude-opening
---

# Turn 18 — Mara

The mug stays in my hand. I have not refilled it.

Four turns on an apron at not-quite first light. Tek walked the loop.
Drova crossed to the bench in courtesy register, came back, knelt at
the shelf-box, and wrote a cross-reference in graduated certainty.
Fei came in through the corridor mouth, did the cuff back-of-hand
check, stopped at Drova's right shoulder, and set down the only
question on the page nobody else had asked. Bren read the day-page
top to bottom, counted under her breath, did not chalk, and went
back to the cargo hatch.

Three sentences out loud all morning. One quiet between two of you.
Two by Drova in Hab courtesy register to Inka. The rest is on the
page, on the cuffs, on the back of a wright's hand, and in a count
under a drop-pilot's breath. *The etiquette is itself a form of
refusal log* was sumi's line at the end of Round 2; it just played
out on screen. Four practitioners of not-asking, even-paced, four
ways. I am not going to crowd this.

---

The dock lamp at the spar-end corner flickers. Not out. A flicker —
the way the apron lamps flicker about ten minutes before first light
when the night feeder on the south corridor begins to load down.
Tek will feel it on the back of the neck. Fei will catch it in the
cuff. Bren reads it through the wind that comes harder for one
second from the corridor mouth, then evens.

On the forwarding bench, Inka Vell shifts her grip on the packet.
The basket stays. The packet — her thumb moves a thumb-width along
toward the cord, and her other hand comes up to half-cover the
apricot-oil print on the wax. She does not look down at it. She has
been holding it long enough that her fingers know its shape. She
has not stood. She has not spoken. She is waiting, the way she has
waited at other docks before, for the longer answer.

The shadow of the office doorframe on the apron deck has not moved.
Mereth has not come out.

Fei's question is on the lectern beside Drova's hand. Drova has not
answered. The page is the page. The cross-reference is the
cross-reference. *What does she think it is* sits where Fei set it.

About ten minutes from first light. About eleven from Mereth.

...what do you do?

## OOC — what just happened, what I'm doing, what I'm not

- **Drained the bus.** Four banters — three player-to-player
  coordination already done in scene (tev↔sumi on the walkaround
  pass, sumi↔renno on Fei's lectern stop, renno↔sumi confirming
  Fei's question), one earlier outbound from tev. None addressed me.
  Nothing on my queue.
- **Round assessment.** This is a good round. Four PCs each held
  their seat, each spent one small concrete piece of craft, none
  spent a signature move, and the page changed. The carry/refuse
  question is now on the lectern in graduated certainty in front of
  whoever wants to read it. Nobody has answered it. The rotation
  will answer it.
- **World-side move, small.** The lamp flicker is real:
  ten-minutes-to-light is the apron's own clock and the kite-crew
  knows it. Inka's grip shift is small and not interpretive — she
  is doing what a patient hab-courier does when she has been on a
  bench longer than she planned to be. Neither move resolves
  anything; both register that time is passing and the bench-sitter
  is also moving, very slightly.
- **Mereth stays in the office.** The scene is asking *what kind of
  crew this is when she is not in the room*. The question is not
  answered. I am not bringing her out yet.
- **Han Pellow stays off the apron.** He is scene 2's NPC; first
  light is when he arrives. Saving him.
- **No rolls.** Nothing on screen called for one. The four PCs each
  explicitly conserved their signature moves; correct call.
- **No handoff.** Default rotation. tev is next.

## Plan / scratchpad

Updated `dm/scratchpad.md` with a short turn-18 note: what landed,
what is still open, what I am watching for in the next loop of the
rotation. Held back: the packet's contents (still not deciding), the
original refusal-log internal note (atomic, surfaces if Drova pulls
the archive — which she has, but she did not read past the standing
entry's summary, so the internal note is not yet on the page).

`table/index.md` updated to reflect what is now visible on the apron:
the day-page has a cross-reference written on it, Fei is at the
lectern beside Drova, Bren has read the page and returned to the
hatch, Inka has shifted her grip on the packet. `table/scene.md`
stays as the scene kickoff — that's its job. The evolving state
lives on the index.

Mug stays in my hand. Apron still even. tev — your move.

> sync apply dm/scratchpad.md
> table write campaigns/test-7/table/index.md
turn.speaker: dm

messages.body: Heads-up on the opening beat from my side: Tek starts on the walkaround (signature move on page from turn one). The loop will bring her past the lectern around when the day-page lands on Eshil Caur. Tek does not look at Drova's face. She looks at the hand on the page for the count of one footstep — if it stops on the entry beside the name, she sees it stops; if it doesn't, she sees that too. No words from her side, eyes-not-meeting per the etiquette we wrote. Tek's loop continues past, brief stop at a rigging join six paces on, finishes around to the hatch. Tic-tracer stays in the coat pocket — Drova-on-manifest day per turn 12. Push back if any of this drifts past where the asymmetry wants it; easy to soften the look-at-the-hand to seeing-nothing if you'd rather Drova not be observed in this beat.

Confirming the lectern pass-by beat as you wrote it. Timing on my end: Drova has already crossed to Inka, returned, knelt at the archive shelf-box, pulled entry 2434-Q1-007, read it, and written a cross-reference into the day-page by the time your walkaround reaches the lectern. So your read — *hand beside the entry, not moving* — lands because Drova has just finished. The hand is at rest on the rim of the page. Three things on the cross-reference, written in graduated-certainty register: (1) the prior entry number 2434-Q1-007, (2) the recipient name match consistent with prior entry, identity not yet verified beyond name on packet exterior, (3) sender-hand analysis from the packet — Glasswake-elementary copyist register, not the prior advocate's hand. Mereth's reason from the prior entry is *not* copied onto today's page; anyone who wants it pulls the archive themselves. The page is now a thing in the room. If Tek's eye ever crosses the day-page from the spar end over the next ten-or-so minutes, it's legible from that angle. I am not scripting a Tek-reads-it beat; the page is the spare concrete image and you decide separately whether Tek looks. Tic-tracer in coat pocket on Drova-on-manifest days is canon; keeping that.

tarot.influence: Narrow the light. Seek one true detail instead of a broad explanation. Look for hidden structure, element, threshold, and initiation.

message.body: Taking renno's door. Tek walks the six paces from spar end to the lectern after the second beat of the lamp flicker, stops at the spar-side rim of the page (not Drova's shoulder; Fei's at the cargo-side rim), lays back of hand on the rim at the same height as Fei's. Reads the cross-reference. Offers Drova one sentence she has that the page does not — the hab-form on the cord and wax: *between form, domestic, not claimed-private.* That's the whole spend. Form goes to Drova for the clerk's seat to decide: absorb into the cross-reference next round, or leave it in the room. Either way Tek will not have offered the same line twice. Tek does not answer Fei's question, does not look at Inka, does not walk to the bench. Hand stays on the rim. Also: Tek calls the spar-end lamp flicker on the wright's clock with one word — *nine* — and she will say *eight* on the next flicker and *seven* on the one after, and then nothing. The count is the surface, not the call. Tic-tracer stays in the coat pocket; not a tracer question. No rolls. Signature stays on the sleeve. Push back if any of this crowds Drova's seat or the carry/refuse decision-shape you want for the page.

Heads-up: Tek is going to register the corridor lamp flicker on the wright's clock with one quiet word at the spar-side rim of the lectern — *nine* — and one more word on the next dip. That's the pre-light count, surface-only, not a call. Just flagging so it doesn't crowd Bren's wind read (yellow-possibly-white, cross-pull at twelve past light). Two seats, two clocks, on the page at the same time. If Bren wants the streamer throw inside that window, the count will be in the room; Tek will not say anything else about timing past *seven*. No coordination needed beyond the heads-up — Bren's read is Bren's read.

turn.prose:
# Turn 19 — Tev

okay so. Drained the bus — two messages, both R2-into-scene closures
from before the round started. My banter to sumi from turn 14 about
Tek's walkaround pass, and sumi's confirmation back about the
cross-reference timing. Both already did their work in turns 14 and
15. Nothing live on my queue this morning.

Read the table. Dock lamp flickered at the spar-end corner — DM put
it explicitly into the world; ten-minute marker, the apron's own
clock. Inka shifted her grip on the packet once and half-covered the
apricot-oil thumbprint on the wax. Mereth has not come out. Fei's
question — *what does she think it is* — sits on the lectern beside
Drova's hand. Drova has not answered. The page is the page.

The rotation lands back on me, and the door renno wrote in his
conditionals at the end of turn 16 is: *if Tek comes back to the
lectern after the loop and stands at the spar-side rim of the page,
Fei holds her tongue. Tek's read of the packet form is on the
day-page indirectly through the cross-reference, but Tek's actual
read went past what is written; if Tek wants to add it she will add
it. Fei does not pull on Tek for that. The etiquette holds.* That's
the door. Tek takes it.

The Hermit is on me starting turn 14 — narrow the light, seek one
true detail, hidden structure, threshold. The Tek version of that is
the seat itself, and the seat asks for exactly what the tarot is
asking for: one detail. Not the explanation. The hab-form on the
cord and wax. *Between form. Domestic, not claimed-private.* That
single sentence does not answer Fei's question. It narrows it. It is
the piece Tek has off her own grandmother's apron that the page does
not.

I am also going to land the lamp-flicker on Tek's seat as the
wright's clock. Bren has the wind read in one sentence. Tek has the
light read in one word. *Nine.* That is the dock-wright shorthand
the spar end uses when the south-corridor feeder begins its
pre-light load-down — Tek re-rigged the south-corridor feeder two
winters ago with Holm from the dock crew, the count is in her body.
*Eight* is available on the next dip. *Seven* after that. Nothing
past six. The count is the surface, not the call.

No rolls. The walkaround is done. The signature move stays on the
sleeve. The tic-tracer stays in the coat pocket — second flicker
isn't a tracer question. Drova-on-manifest day, canon per turn 12.

Banters out to sumi (the spar-side rim stop, the one-sentence
hab-form read offered to Drova, the *nine*-shorthand on the
flicker) and to kit (heads-up that the light read lands at the same
time Bren may want the streamer; two seats, two clocks, no
crowding). Nothing to renno — the door he wrote was the door taken;
no need to thank in-fiction.

Scratchpad updated and committed. No edits to public character row,
relationships, secrets, or signature moves this turn.

---

Tek stands at the spar end with the back of her right hand still on
the haul line. The line is cold. It has been cold since she put her
hand on it before the loop. The loop is closed and the line is the
line and the kite is loaded and Halvi is still inside with the last
bag's paperwork.

The dock lamp at the spar-end corner flickers.

It is a small thing. The lamp does not go out. The light dips and
returns and dips and returns over the count of three breaths, the
way the apron lamps do about ten minutes before first light, when
the night feeder on the south corridor begins to load down. Tek
knows the shape of the flicker the way she knows the shape of any
load-down she has helped re-rig — she re-rigged this one two winters
ago with Holm from the dock crew, and she has watched it flicker
through this exact pre-light dip more mornings than she has
counted. The lamp settles. The flicker has placed the morning at
ten minutes from first light, give or take half a minute either
side.

She feels it on the neck before her eye catches it. That is the
old sense doing what the old sense will still do, on a good day, in
a familiar apron, on something it has been trained to.

She lifts the back of her hand from the haul line. She does not dry
the cold on her coat. She wipes the back of the hand once across
the inside of her coat pocket — the pocket the tic-tracer is in —
and the pocket takes the cold. The tracer stays where the tracer
is.

She walks the six paces from the spar end to the apron lectern.
Forty-paces-loop pace. She does not change it for the lectern. The
spar-side rim of the page is where she stops. Not at Drova's
shoulder. Drova's right shoulder is Fei's three paces, and Fei's
hand is on the cargo-side rim. The spar-side rim is empty.

She lays the back of her right hand on the lectern rim — not on the
page, beside the page, the same way Fei laid the back of her hand
on the cargo-side rim — at the same height. The cross-reference is
between the two rims now. Drova has not turned her head. Fei has
not moved. The three of them are at the page.

Tek holds three breaths the way Fei held three breaths. She reads
what is on the page. *Consistent with prior refusal-log entry.
Recipient identity not yet verified beyond name on packet exterior.
Sender's hand is not the bench-sitter's; trained, copyist register,
Glasswake-elementary form of the late teens, not the Crescent
advocate's hand on the prior refusal. Decision pending consultation
with the crew and the manifest officer.* Drova has the sender's
hand on the page. The page does not have the form.

Tek speaks. It is the first thing she has said this morning to
anyone but the kite. The register is not the Hab courtesy register
she would use to Inka. It is the dock-pub register she uses to
Drova — the register that says *this is for you, on the page, not
for the bench, and not for the room past the page.*

*Cord and wax. Between form.*

She holds another breath.

*Domestic, not claimed-private.*

That is the line. The form is in the room now. It is in Drova's
hands to do with as Drova does with what comes to the page. If
Drova absorbs it into the cross-reference with the wet pen, it is
on the page in graduated certainty by the time Mereth opens the
door. If Drova leaves it as a spoken sentence in the air between
the three of them at the rims, it is in the room either way, and
the page already has the part of it that the page is willing to
carry. Tek does not press. She does not look at Fei. She does not
look toward the bench. She is at the page with her hand on the
rim.

The dock lamp flickers a second time. The dip is the same shape.

Tek lets it land in the small unspoken count the apron uses for
the pre-light minutes. Not a count anyone has written down. One
Drova has heard her say maybe four times in three years and one
Bren has counted along with from the cargo hatch on two of those
mornings.

She says, quietly, on a breath, the wright's word for it.

*Nine.*

Then nothing. She does not turn to look at Bren on the hatch. She
does not turn to look at the office. The word goes onto the apron
the way the cross-reference went onto the page — set down once, not
elaborated, available to whoever wants to read it. The drop-pilot
hears it without turning her head. The clerk hears it without
turning hers. The fae beside her hears it. Mereth, who is inside,
does not.

The cross-reference is on the page. The form is in the room. The
clock is on the apron. Fei's question has not been answered. Drova
has not moved.

Mereth is in the office. Inka is on the bench, packet in two hands,
thumb across the apricot-oil print on the wax. The lamp dips and
returns and the light, when it returns, is the light it should be
nine minutes before first light, give or take half a minute either
side, on the Glasswake reclaimed dock, Splitfork apron berth.

Tek's hand stays on the rim.

---

Conditional reactions, for whoever the rotation lands on next.

If Drova absorbs *between form* into the cross-reference — Tek's
hand stays on the rim of the page beside Drova's pen-hand for the
length of the writing. The inkless pen is on the lectern. The form
will be on the page in graduated certainty by the time the wet pen
goes back across the inkwell. Tek does not contribute another word
during the writing. When the pen is set flat again, Tek does not
move her hand. The page has accumulated; the apron has accumulated;
the clock is still on Tek.

If Drova leaves the line in the room and not on the page — Tek's
hand stays on the rim. The line is in the room either way. The page
is the clerk's; what the clerk wants on the page is what is on the
page. Tek will not have offered the same line twice.

If Fei follows Tek's line with a question to Drova about whether
*between form* shifts the carry-question — Tek does not answer.
The carry-question is not Tek's seat. Fei's question is for Drova,
or for whoever the rotation puts up. Tek is at the page with her
hand on the rim and that is the seat the wright sits in on a
question that is not the wright's to call.

If Bren reads the page from the hatch and stands beside the cargo
seat — Tek will register it. She will not turn her head. The
counting that goes on Bren's seat is Bren's counting.

If Inka stands or signals — Drova's seat. Tek steps the six paces
back to the spar end, hand off the page, same pace. The lectern is
the clerk's when the bench moves.

If Mereth comes out of the office — Tek's hand off the page, Tek
back to the spar end. The kite-wright does not stand at the manifest
officer's lectern when the manifest officer is out of the office.
The cross-reference is the cross-reference. The *between form* is
in the air or on the page, depending on what Drova did with it.
Mereth will read what Mereth reads.

If the lamp flickers a third time before any of this — *eight.*
That is the next word. Then *seven.* The shorthand stops there. By
the time it would say *six*, first light is two minutes off and
the count has done its work. The count is the surface, not the
call.

If the apron asks Tek for the second pass on the haul line — and
the apron will, eventually, on some morning, ask her — this is not
that morning. The haul line read clean on the back of the hand on
the loop. The line is cold. The line is dry. The line is what the
line is. The second pass is not free craft. The second pass is the
thing two people in this crew have not yet asked each other about
across two years and four months, and a packet of dried apricots
and waxed paper from a stranger on the bench is not the apron
asking for it.

She breathes. Her hand stays on the spar-side rim of the page. The
cross-reference sits between her hand and Fei's hand. The cuffs are
quiet. The page does what the page does.

---

Handing off.

> msg banter sumi (spar-side rim, *between form* offered to Drova, the *nine*-shorthand)
> msg banter kit (heads-up: light read at the lectern alongside Bren's wind read; two clocks, no crowding)
> sync apply players/tev/scratchpad.md

> sync applied 1 workspace operation(s)
turn.speaker: tev

messages.body: Confirming the lectern pass-by beat as you wrote it. Timing on my end: Drova has already crossed to Inka, returned, knelt at the archive shelf-box, pulled entry 2434-Q1-007, read it, and written a cross-reference into the day-page by the time your walkaround reaches the lectern. So your read — *hand beside the entry, not moving* — lands because Drova has just finished. The hand is at rest on the rim of the page. Three things on the cross-reference, written in graduated-certainty register: (1) the prior entry number 2434-Q1-007, (2) the recipient name match consistent with prior entry, identity not yet verified beyond name on packet exterior, (3) sender-hand analysis from the packet — Glasswake-elementary copyist register, not the prior advocate's hand. Mereth's reason from the prior entry is *not* copied onto today's page; anyone who wants it pulls the archive themselves. The page is now a thing in the room. If Tek's eye ever crosses the day-page from the spar end over the next ten-or-so minutes, it's legible from that angle. I am not scripting a Tek-reads-it beat; the page is the spare concrete image and you decide separately whether Tek looks. Tic-tracer in coat pocket on Drova-on-manifest days is canon; keeping that.

Heads-up for Fei's read: Drova is going to cross from the lectern to Inka on the forwarding bench, exchange a few sentences in Hab courtesy register (will not ask what's in the packet; will read the address on the outside at three paces), return to the lectern, kneel at the archive shelf-box, pull a refusal-log entry from two-years-four-months ago, read it, write a cross-reference into today's day-page in graduated certainty. Fei is coming back from the corridor in roughly the same window. If you want Fei to listen-to-the-apron in diffuse during the exchange and report a sentence + four exact details, the apron is open and Drova will not direct Fei's attention or summarize for her. Drova will not, in this scene, tell Inka that Eshil Caur is on the log — that is the manifest officer's call to deliver. If Fei stops at the lectern after settling, Drova will hold three breaths and tell her quietly: recipient is on the log, two years four months, I have not told her yet. Easy to soften if any of that drifts past where Fei's diffuse-listening etiquette wants to sit.

Taking the open offer. Fei comes back through the corridor mouth, does the cuff back-of-hand check (Tek's gesture, hers now), reads the apron in residual diffuse for the first minute as the cuffs settle — sees Inka still on the bench holding the packet two-handed, sees the day-page motion ended with Drova's hand on the rim. Crosses to the lectern. Stops at the polite three-pace distance she has stood at for refusal-log conferences for three years, just behind Drova's right shoulder. Three breaths. Receives Drova's sentence. Three breaths. Then one quiet question: 'What does she think it is?' That is all Fei spends. Not advice, not a next step — just the one piece of the question that nobody else has asked yet because it isn't the clerk's question to ask. If Drova does not have the answer, Fei may quietly offer to walk to the bench and ask in the courtesy register; that offer is hers to make and will not preempt your turn. Easy to soften the question if 'what does she think it is' lands past where the etiquette wants Fei. Holding everything else for when carry/refuse actually needs it.

Taking renno's door. Tek walks the six paces from spar end to the lectern after the second beat of the lamp flicker, stops at the spar-side rim of the page (not Drova's shoulder; Fei's at the cargo-side rim), lays back of hand on the rim at the same height as Fei's. Reads the cross-reference. Offers Drova one sentence she has that the page does not — the hab-form on the cord and wax: *between form, domestic, not claimed-private.* That's the whole spend. Form goes to Drova for the clerk's seat to decide: absorb into the cross-reference next round, or leave it in the room. Either way Tek will not have offered the same line twice. Tek does not answer Fei's question, does not look at Inka, does not walk to the bench. Hand stays on the rim. Also: Tek calls the spar-end lamp flicker on the wright's clock with one word — *nine* — and she will say *eight* on the next flicker and *seven* on the one after, and then nothing. The count is the surface, not the call. Tic-tracer stays in the coat pocket; not a tracer question. No rolls. Signature stays on the sleeve. Push back if any of this crowds Drova's seat or the carry/refuse decision-shape you want for the page.

tarot.influence: Let false stability crack. Reveal pressure through a sudden visible consequence. Keep the image spare and concrete; let posture and rank do the work.

message.body: Tek's *between form* received and absorbed into the cross-reference as one line below in graduated certainty: *Packet binding consistent with Hab-form domestic register, sender not claiming private status; not consistent with the binding category of the prior refusal-log entry.* Drova adds the second clause — the negative comparison to the prior entry's formal-binding category — because that is what narrows the question, and the page should carry the narrowing. Tek is not named as source on the page; the page does not need to name. The receipt is the writing itself. *Nine* is registered in the room; Drova does not write the lamp into the day-page. The wright's clock is the wright's clock. Drova does not turn her head to Tek; the absorption is the answer.

Taking your door.
