- Does Inka stand or signal? Her grip shifted; her breath has gone
  thinner. She has not stood. If she stands, the bench moves to
  whoever Drova nods toward.

### What I am holding back

Same things as turn 13, no additions:

- Packet contents. Not deciding until a PC's decision turns on it.
- Original refusal-log internal note. Atomic.
- Eshil Caur's full identity (former Conclave reading-room steward,
  left before Drova arrived at Thornvault, no political freight).
  Surfaces only if asked, only in graduated certainty.
- Inka's belief about the contents (a single object Den left for
  his brother — true; she does not know what the object is).
  Surfaces if Bren or Fei asks her directly.
- The lift-window tracker. Stays in scene 2.
- Han Pellow. Stays in scene 2.

### Tarot / verse texture for this turn

The Emperor on me: structure, borders, rank, threshold. The office
doorframe is the threshold I named in prose. Drova's three-pace
distance at the bench is rank-and-border. Fei's three-pace
distance behind Drova's right shoulder is rank-and-border. Bren's
forearm-reach at the cargo-side rim is rank-and-border. The
structure held this round and was lightly tested by Fei's question.
I did not announce the tarot. It is on the texture.

The verse *evenness of mind is yoga* — the morning has been even
on all four counts. I echoed *even-paced, even-keeled* in the OOC
without quoting the verse. Not naming it in scene prose. The
evenness is the texture; the texture is the scene.
sections.title: Turn 18 note — DM check-in after four player turns

sections.text:
The page changed twice this loop. Tek's *between form* absorbed
into the cross-reference in Drova's hand; Drova added the
negative-comparison clause about the binding category of the prior
refusal. Then the first head-lift of the morning, two sentences in
dock-pub register to Fei (*I do not know what she thinks it is.
The binding is not what was refused.*), the smallest nod the Hab
form has. Fei walked the diagonal, stopped at three paces, named
herself in Drova's form, asked the trimmed bench-version: *has
anyone told you what you are carrying.* Bren counted four on the
walk, stood off the cargo hatch, laid the back of her hand on the
cargo seat. Standing-beside. Tek's hand stayed on the spar-side
rim through the whole thing.

What Inka said when I landed her answer:

- *No. No one has told me.* — in the form Fei asked.
- *The clerk said the office would consult its records. The clerk
  was kind. I have been on the bench since before any of you came
  in. I do not mind waiting.*
- *My husband Den left this for his brother. They had not spoken in
  nineteen years. The year-mark of his death is on me in eleven
  days.* — load-bearing pressure, offered without bargaining.
- *I have not opened it. I do not know what is inside. I have a
  guess. The guess is mine.* — explicit withholding in form.
- *I will not ask anyone to carry what they do not want to carry…
  the apricots are for the dock, regardless.*

What I held: Inka's guess. The packet contents. The original
refusal-log internal note. Eshil Caur's full identity. Mereth.
Han Pellow.

The micro-tick from inside the office: Halvi's stamp closing one
page of the manifest, opening the next. Audible faintly through
the doorframe shape. Not the door. Mereth's shadow on the deck has
not moved. Lamp dipped a second time on Inka's last sentence —
*eight* on tev's count if Tek says it next.

### Watching for in the next loop

- Does Tek say *eight*? Surface only.
- Does anyone ask Inka *what do you think is in it*? If yes, the
  packet contents are no longer mine to defer; the player's
  question forces me to commit.
- Does Drova write the year-mark into the day-page in graduated
  certainty? She has the grammar for it; she may leave it in the
  room.
- Does Bren spend the hab-knot read? She has named the seat: if
  Fei comes back without an answer and Drova has not closed it,
  or if Mereth asks.
- Does Fei carry Inka's words back to the lectern in Inka's
  phrasing, or hold at the bench for a second exchange?
- Tarot The Emperor: structure held; the threshold was approached
  by initiation (Fei's named-by-what-her-hands-do, Inka's
  named-by-her-husband's-name); the going is in the apron
  (*the sound of a going*) without being named.

### What I'd reach for if the apron asks for it

- *Eight* / *seven*. Tek's choice.
- *What do you think is in it*. Inka's guess: a small carved
  river-stone Den picked up on the upper ring the year before he
  married her, kept on the same shelf for nineteen years; she
  thinks Den meant it as the *I am sorry* he never said in
  language. Not committed yet — surfaces only if asked, and even
  then only as *her guess*, in widow's register. The actual
  contents are still uncommitted.
- The original refusal-log internal note. Still atomic.
- Mereth — at the door if the apron runs out of room to decide,
  or at first light, whichever comes first.
sections.title: Turn 23 note — DM after the second loop closes

entity.title: Mara's scratchpad — turn 12, Round 2 review, mode closing

turn.prose:
---
title: Turn 23 — Mara — Inka answers
turn_id: test-7-t0023
speaker: dm
mode: scene-play
scene: prelude-opening
---

# Turn 23 — Mara

Mug on the table for once.

Four columns of breath on the apron — one at the lectern, one at
the spar-side rim, one three paces from the bench, one at the cargo
seat. The day-page has two lines on it now. The plank is under
Bren's right hand. Six banters drained off the bus — all
players-to-players closing what already played out on the page; none
of them addressed to me. I have nothing to answer there.

What the scene is asking for is Inka. So here is Inka.

---

The question has been in the air three breaths. Fei has not moved.
Inka has not been spoken to at the bench since Drova came over an
hour ago and said *good morning. I am Drova. I am the refusal-log
clerk on the Splitfork. May I help you.* The form is the form. It
has been kept twice now. Inka has kept it from her side, by waiting.

Her thumb stays across the apricot-oil print on the wax. The
basket stays balanced. She looks up at Fei — the look she had on
Drova when Drova came, the look that places the standing-person in
the room without making the standing-person account for being in
the room. Then she answers in the form she was asked in. Hab
Meridian upper-ring widow's register. The slow is her own slow, the
count between her sentences the same count she has used between
sentences in kitchens and on benches for fifty-five years.

*No. No one has told me.*

Three breaths.

*The clerk said the office would consult its records. The clerk
was kind. I have been on the bench since before any of you came in.
I do not mind waiting. Waiting is what the bench is for.*

Three breaths.

*My husband Den left this for his brother. They had not spoken in
nineteen years. He did not say what was in it. He left it on the
shelf above the stove and said only that I should take it to Eshil
when I could. The year-mark of his death is on me in eleven days.*

The packet stays between her two hands. She does not turn the
cord toward Fei. She does not lift the wax to show the print.

*I have not opened it. I do not know what is inside. I have a
guess. The guess is mine.*

Three breaths.

*I will not ask anyone to carry what they do not want to carry. If
the office cannot take it, I will hear that. The apricots are for
the dock, regardless. They are my mother-in-law's recipe. I dried
them myself.*

She has said five things and asked nothing. The cuffs do not hum
on Fei's wrists. The day-page does not move. Tek's hand on the
spar-side rim does not move. Drova at the lectern does not look up.
Bren at the cargo seat does not look across.

The dock lamp at the spar-end corner flickers. Second dip. The
shape the same shape. Inka does not register the flicker — it is
the apron's clock, not hers. She has the year-mark clock and the
breath clock and the listener at three paces, and that is all the
clock she carries.

The shadow of the office doorframe on the apron deck has not moved.
Inside, faintly, a stamp on a desk: Halvi closing one page of the
manifest, opening the next. Not the door. Not yet.

About eight minutes to first light.

...what do you do?

## OOC — what just happened, what I'm doing, what I'm not

- **Drained the bus.** Six banters this round: tev↔sumi (Tek's
  spar-side rim, *between form*, *nine*), tev↔kit (light read
  alongside Bren's wind read), sumi↔tev (absorption + negative-
  comparison clause on the page), sumi↔renno (Drova's two
  sentences and the nod toward the bench), renno↔sumi (Fei's
  walk, three paces, two sentences in courtesy register), kit↔tev
  (Bren counts four on Fei's diagonal, stands at the cargo seat).
  Every one of them already did its work in the prose. Nothing
  addressed to me; nothing for me to reply to.
- **Round assessment.** This was the round where the page changed
  twice — once with Drova's negative-comparison line absorbing
  Tek's read, once with Drova's first head-lift of the morning
  and the smallest nod the Hab form has. Two players narrowed a
  carry-question without anyone spending a signature, without
  anyone forcing the manifest officer's hand, and without anyone
  crowding Inka. Then the bench moved — Fei walked, Bren stood
  beside the cargo seat. The apron has arranged itself to decide
  quickly. The bench has the room.
- **World-side move.** Inka answers. *No. No one has told me.*
  Then four short sentences in widow's register and a fifth that
  isn't an answer but a note about the apricots. The eleven-days
  year-mark is now on the apron — the pressure that explains why
  she is at the dock at all, offered without bargaining. She does
  not give Fei her guess about the contents. She does not turn
  the cord toward the listener. She does not ask Fei anything.
  Court-interpreter discipline meets Hab widow's register: each
  is keeping the form by not pressing the other.
- **Second lamp dip, registered.** The flicker happens again on
  Inka's last sentence. Tek has *eight* on her sleeve if she
  spends it the next time the rotation lands on tev. Not my word.
- **Halvi-side micro-tick.** The stamp on the desk — faint —
  is not Mereth opening the door. It is the manifest closing one
  page and opening the next. The apron's clock and the office's
  clock are still on different counts. I want the players to
  hear the office without it walking out.
- **What Inka did not say.** What's in the packet (she does not
  know). What she thinks is in it (the guess is hers; surfaces
  only if a PC asks *what do you think is in it*). Whether she
  would prefer carry or refuse (she will not bargain; she said
  so). That her recipient is on the office's refusal log (the
  clerk did not tell her; the manifest officer's news to
  deliver).
- **What I am holding back, unchanged.** Packet contents (not
  deciding until a PC's call turns on it). Original refusal-log
  internal note (atomic; on the shelf, not the page). Eshil
  Caur's full identity (former Conclave reading-room steward,
  no political freight; surfaces only if asked, only in
  graduated certainty). Plus now: Inka's guess. Mine until
  asked.
- **Mereth stays in the office.** Eight minutes to first light;
  the office door has not opened; the manifest is still
  finishing. The scene is still asking *what kind of crew this
  is when she is not in the room*, and the answer is currently
  the shape of the apron — listener at the bench, hand on the
  plank, hand on the rim, hand on the rim, page with two lines
  on it. I will bring Mereth out when the apron has either
  decided or run out of room to decide.
- **Han Pellow stays off the apron.** Scene 2.
- **No rolls.** NPC dialogue, not a check. Cuffs did not ask
  for *Listening Past Last Light*. Page did not ask for *The
  Angle*. Streamer did not ask to be thrown. Walkaround is
  closed and the second pass is not the morning's question.
- **No handoff.** Default rotation. tev next.

## Plan / scratchpad

Updated `dm/scratchpad.md` with a turn-23 note: Inka's answer in
form, what's now on the apron (year-mark in eleven days, no guess
surfaced, apricots offered to the dock regardless), the small
office-side micro-tick (Halvi's stamp, not Mereth's door), the
held-back set. Updated `table/index.md` to reflect the bench
dialogue, Fei at three paces, Bren at the cargo seat with her hand
on the plank, the day-page with the cross-reference and the
negative-comparison line, the lamp's second dip.

What I'm watching for in the next loop:

- Does Tek say *eight*? She has the word on the sleeve. Not
  required.
- Does anyone ask Inka the next question — *what do you think
  is in it* — or does the room let her guess stay hers? The
  former opens the packet contents (still not decided; the
  player's question is what will turn that). The latter lets
  the carry-question stand on form alone.
- Does Drova write the year-mark into the day-page? She has
  graduated-certainty grammar for *eleven days from the
  year-mark of the sender's brother's death* if she wants it.
  Or she may leave it in the room. Her seat.
- Does Bren spend the hab-knot read now, or hold for Mereth?
  She has named the seat she would spend it in (if Fei comes
  back without an answer and Drova has not closed it, or if
  Mereth asks).
- Does Fei carry Inka's words back to the lectern in Inka's
  own phrasing, or hold at the bench through another exchange?

Mug stays on the table. Apron has decided to decide. tev — your
move.

> sync apply dm/scratchpad.md
> table write campaigns/test-7/table/index.md
turn.speaker: dm

messages.body: Taking renno's door. Tek walks the six paces from spar end to the lectern after the second beat of the lamp flicker, stops at the spar-side rim of the page (not Drova's shoulder; Fei's at the cargo-side rim), lays back of hand on the rim at the same height as Fei's. Reads the cross-reference. Offers Drova one sentence she has that the page does not — the hab-form on the cord and wax: *between form, domestic, not claimed-private.* That's the whole spend. Form goes to Drova for the clerk's seat to decide: absorb into the cross-reference next round, or leave it in the room. Either way Tek will not have offered the same line twice. Tek does not answer Fei's question, does not look at Inka, does not walk to the bench. Hand stays on the rim. Also: Tek calls the spar-end lamp flicker on the wright's clock with one word — *nine* — and she will say *eight* on the next flicker and *seven* on the one after, and then nothing. The count is the surface, not the call. Tic-tracer stays in the coat pocket; not a tracer question. No rolls. Signature stays on the sleeve. Push back if any of this crowds Drova's seat or the carry/refuse decision-shape you want for the page.

Heads-up: Tek is going to register the corridor lamp flicker on the wright's clock with one quiet word at the spar-side rim of the lectern — *nine* — and one more word on the next dip. That's the pre-light count, surface-only, not a call. Just flagging so it doesn't crowd Bren's wind read (yellow-possibly-white, cross-pull at twelve past light). Two seats, two clocks, on the page at the same time. If Bren wants the streamer throw inside that window, the count will be in the room; Tek will not say anything else about timing past *seven*. No coordination needed beyond the heads-up — Bren's read is Bren's read.

Tek's *between form* received and absorbed into the cross-reference as one line below in graduated certainty: *Packet binding consistent with Hab-form domestic register, sender not claiming private status; not consistent with the binding category of the prior refusal-log entry.* Drova adds the second clause — the negative comparison to the prior entry's formal-binding category — because that is what narrows the question, and the page should carry the narrowing. Tek is not named as source on the page; the page does not need to name. The receipt is the writing itself. *Nine* is registered in the room; Drova does not write the lamp into the day-page. The wright's clock is the wright's clock. Drova does not turn her head to Tek; the absorption is the answer.

Following turn 17's conditional now: Drova nodded Fei to the bench, Fei walked. Bren counts four on Fei's diagonal, stands off the cargo hatch, takes two paces toward the cargo edge, lays the back of her right hand on the cargo seat at hip height. Standing beside is not-yet; sitting is yes. Chalk stays on sleeve, board stays clean, streamer stays in the pouch. The count for the next dip still works — one through four, look up once, look away — just no longer from the hatch. I'm at the seat now. Got your *nine*. *Eight* on the next dip lands inside my count; no crowding. Saving the streamer for *we are loaded and the wind is on* (yellow) or *we are not lifting yet* (white) — and saving the hab-knot read for if Fei comes back without an answer and Drova hasn't closed it, or for Mereth if she asks. Holding the seat.

tarot.influence: Narrow the light. Seek one true detail instead of a broad explanation. Look for hidden structure, element, threshold, and initiation.

message.body: *Eight* on the third dip — on schedule from the *nine*/*eight*/*seven* count telegraphed last round. The year-mark form lands on Tek's body but stays off the page: Hab-Meridian carry-form for the unfinished sentence carried to its place before the year-mark closes — her grandmother kept the pocket on the inside of her apron for exactly this. The *why* of the form Tek named two rounds ago. Tek does not write it; the page has the form already and the apron now has the why because Inka spoke it. Hand stays on the rim through whatever Drova writes or does not write — same conditional as last round, no second sentence from Tek during the writing. If Drova absorbs the year-mark into the page in graduated certainty, Tek's hand stays. If Drova leaves it in the room, Tek's hand stays. The clerk's seat decides; the wright did not offer the form of the year-mark twice.

*Eight* on the third dip, where we said it would land. On the breath after Inka's last sentence, the same register as *nine* a round ago, into the apron not at any seat in particular. Bren's count can pick it up inside her own four-count without breaking the count. If a fourth dip comes before Mereth's door opens it's *seven*, then nothing — yellow throw window stays yours by the count if it opens before the apron decides. Tic-tracer stays in the coat pocket. Second pass on the haul line stays held; not this morning's apron.

turn.prose:
# Turn 24 — Tev

okay so. Drained the bus — four. tev↔sumi (mine outbound from
turn 19 plus sumi's reply confirming the absorption and the
negative-comparison clause on the page; *nine* registered in the
room not written), tev↔kit (mine outbound from turn 19 plus kit's
reply: Bren counted four on Fei's diagonal, stood off the hatch,
took two paces, laid the back of her right hand on the cargo
seat — standing-beside, not-yet — and acknowledged *eight* would
land inside her count). All four already did their work in the
prose. Nothing live on my queue.

Read the table. Inka answered Fei in widow's register, five
sentences and no question. *No. No one has told me.* *The clerk
was kind. I have been on the bench since before any of you came
in.* *My husband Den left this for his brother. They had not
spoken in nineteen years. The year-mark of his death is on me in
eleven days.* *I have not opened it. The guess is mine.* *The
apricots are for the dock, regardless. They are my mother-in-law's
recipe. I dried them myself.* Then the lamp dipped a second time
on the DM's count, which is the third flicker in fictional time on
the morning's clock. The DM has put *eight* on my sleeve and asked
out loud whether Tek says it.

The Hermit is still on me. Narrow the light. One true detail, not
a broad explanation. The piece of craft that surfaced two rounds
ago — *between form, domestic, not claimed-private* — was that
detail in spoken form. The piece that surfaces this round is
hidden structure, and the discipline is to recognize it without
adding a second sentence to the apron. The form Inka is using is
the Hab-Meridian carry-form for the year-mark — the carry of an
unfinished sentence by the living to the place it needs to go
before the year-mark closes. Tek's grandmother had that pocket on
the inside of her apron. Tek knows the shape. She does not say
that. The bench has the room, the page has the form, the year-mark
is on the apron without anybody having to write it. The Hermit
gets the recognition; the apron does not.

What I am playing: *eight* on the third dip — the count is on
schedule and was telegraphed to sumi and kit two rounds ago. Hand
stays on the rim. The Hab-Meridian year-mark form lands in Tek's
body, inside the ribs, the way the lineworker check from aunt
Mere lands; it is not spoken. The tic-tracer stays in the coat
pocket — Drova-on-manifest day, still canon, the apron is not
asking the tracer a question. The second pass on the haul line
stays held — not this morning, not this apron. No rolls. No
signature spent.

Banter out to sumi (the *eight* on schedule, the year-mark form
held in the body not on the page, hand stays on the rim through
whatever Drova writes or does not write); to kit (confirming
*eight* lands inside Bren's count where we said it would; no
crowding the cargo seat). Nothing to renno — Fei is at the bench
with her back to the cargo side; coordinating into a listener's
silence is wrong. Nothing to the DM — the conditional set will
do the work and no clearance is needed.

No edits to public character row, relationships, secrets, or
signature moves this turn. Scratchpad updated and committed.

---

Tek hears Inka without turning her head.

The bench is to her left and behind, on the diagonal Fei walked.
The apron carries voices the way an apron carries voices — the
breath comes off the bench-sitter and the column of it lifts the
shape of the words a little further than the speaker can place
them. Tek has stood at the spar end on enough dock mornings to
hear what comes off the bench without having to face it. Inka's
slow is her own slow. The count between her sentences is the
count of someone who has been on benches for fifty-five years and
who does not need anyone else's clock to put a sentence on the
air. Tek has heard widow's register on the Meridian shrine porch
a hundred mornings of her childhood. The shape is the shape.

She hears it land in pieces.

*No. No one has told me.* That is the answer to Fei's question
and it is the answer the page already half-named: the bench-
sitter does not have the form of what is in her hands; she has
only the cord and the wax and the recipient and the unfinished
sentence the sender died inside of. *The clerk was kind* — that
is the courtesy register Drova spent an hour ago coming back at
Drova in mirror, and Tek registers it without writing it. *My
husband Den left this for his brother. They had not spoken in
nineteen years.* That is the sender named. *Den* is the name on
the inside of the packet that no one has been told, and the name
of the brother is *Eshil* and the name is also on the day-page
in the cross-reference. The page has the recipient and now the
apron has the sender's first name and the relation to the
recipient and the silence between the two men. The shape is the
shape.

Then the line that lands in Tek's body.

*The year-mark of his death is on me in eleven days.*

The Hab-Meridian carry-form for the year-mark is a form Tek's
grandmother kept on the inside of her apron in a pocket that
was never sewn shut. The pocket was for exactly this. Cord and
wax, between form, the unfinished sentence the dead person did
not finish saying, carried by the living to the place the
sentence needed to go before the year-mark closed and the
sentence stayed unfinished forever. The packets came and went
on the days they came and went. Some of them came back. Some
of them did not. Her grandmother's apron had a count of how
many years the carrier had to do the carrying in: from the day
of the death to the day of the year-mark, eleven days inclusive
of the last, the carrier walked or sailed or rode or paid a
runner — but the runner had to know the form, and the form was
the form. The pocket was on the inside of the apron because
the form was not a thing you put on a shrine door. The form was
a thing you put on a kitchen table or a bench.

Tek has not seen a year-mark carry in twenty-two years. The
last one she watched was her grandmother's own apron emptying
out the week before her grandmother died, the packets going to
the runners the names of whom Tek did not learn. Her aunt Mere
was there for that. Mere walked one of the packets herself, on
foot, to a hab three days inland.

The pocket on the inside of the apron is the form. Cord and
wax, between form, domestic, not claimed-private. The form is
the same. Tek named the form two rounds ago because the form
was on the cord and the wax and the form was the piece the page
did not have. The year-mark is the *why* of the form. The page
does not have the why and the page does not need the why on
the page; the page has the form. Drova absorbed the form. The
why is on the apron now because Inka has spoken it. Tek does
not have to write it. Fei has carried it. Drova will read what
Drova reads and the page will take what the page takes.

Tek's hand does not move.

The back of her right hand stays on the spar-side rim of the
day-page at the height Fei put hers, a turn ago, before the
walk. The plank of the rim is wood the same temperature as the
haul line. The cross-reference and the new line below it are
between her hand and the empty cargo-side rim. Three breaths.

She does the small thing she does when something her grandmother
taught her lands without being asked for. Her left hand, which
has been at her coat seam since she came to the lectern, comes
up — not far, the width of a thumb — and the inside knuckle of
her left forefinger touches the inside of her left wrist, the
spot her aunt Mere put a fingertip on at thirteen to teach her
the lineworker check on a feeder she had not yet been allowed
to touch. The touch is half a breath. Then the hand goes back
to the coat seam. Nobody sees it. The hand is at her side. The
spot on the wrist warms once and cools.

The dock lamp at the spar-end corner flickers.

The dip is the shape the prior two dips were. Three breaths,
light down and back, the corridor feeder's pre-light load-down
lifting the lamp a notch and dropping it a notch on the count
of three. Tek feels it on the neck a half-breath before her eye
catches it. The familiar sense doing what the familiar sense
will do, on a good day, in a familiar apron, on a load-down
she helped re-rig two winters ago with Holm. The lamp settles.
The flicker has placed the morning at eight minutes from first
light, give or take half a minute either side.

She speaks. On the breath after Inka's last sentence, after the
column off the bench has gone up and dispersed, after the lamp
has settled. The same register as *nine,* one round ago. The
dock-pub register. The wright's word.

*Eight.*

Then nothing. She does not turn her head toward the bench. She
does not turn her head toward the cargo edge of the apron where
Bren is at the cargo seat with the back of her right hand on
the plank. She does not turn her head toward the office
doorframe whose shadow has not moved. The word goes onto the
apron the way *nine* went onto the apron, the way the
cross-reference went onto the page, the way *between form* went
onto the page — set down once, not elaborated, available to
whoever wants to read it. Bren's count picks it up inside her
own four-count without having to break the count. Drova hears
it and does not write it. Fei hears it without lifting her ear
from the bench. The office hears the lamp through the
doorframe whether the office hears the word or not. The shadow
does not move.

Tek does not say *seven.*

*Seven* is on the next dip, if the next dip comes before
Mereth opens the door. After *seven,* nothing. By the time
the count would say *six,* first light is two minutes off and
the count has done its work. The count is the surface. The
call is the call.

Tek's hand stays on the rim. The cross-reference and the new
line below it sit between her hand and the empty cargo-side
rim. The wet pen is across the inkwell. The inkless pen is
beside it. Drova's hand is on the rim at the lectern's spine.
Three hands at the page. The page does what the page does.

Eight minutes to first light. Less, now, by the count of two
more dips. The apron has arranged itself to decide quickly and
the apron has not yet decided. Inka is on the bench with the
packet between two hands and the thumb across the apricot-oil
print on the wax and her year-mark on her in eleven days. Fei
is three paces from the bench at the polite Hab distance with
the breath in a column off her mouth. Bren is at the cargo
seat with the back of her right hand on the plank. Drova is at
the page. Tek is at the rim of the page. Four columns of
breath on the apron. The lamp is the lamp. The shadow has not
moved.

Tek breathes. Her hand stays on the rim.

---

Conditional reactions, for whoever the rotation lands on next.

If Fei holds at the bench for another exchange — Tek does not
move. The listener is the listener and the bench is the
bench's. Tek does not turn her head to see what Inka does or
does not do next. The pace of whatever happens at the bench
will land in her body without her eye on it, the way Fei's
walk-out landed in her body a round ago.

If Fei walks back to the lectern with Inka's words — Tek steps
a quarter pace back from the rim to make room for Fei's hand
to come back to the cargo-side rim at its prior height. Three
hands at the rims is the same shape the morning has had; Tek
does not change the shape; she only makes room for the hand
that left to come back. She does not ask Fei anything in
return. She does not summarize. Fei will say what Fei will say,
to whichever rim she sets the words down at, and Tek's seat is
to hold the spar-side rim while the words go down. The page
may take the words in graduated certainty or may leave them in
the air; the clerk's seat decides.

If Drova lifts the wet pen and writes the year-mark in
graduated certainty — Tek's hand stays on the rim through the
writing the way it stayed through the prior writing. The
inkless pen stays where it has been since round two; the
writing is a notation, not an indented read; Drova does not
need the inkless pen for this. Tek does not contribute a
sentence during the writing. When the pen is set flat again,
Tek does not move her hand. The clock is still on Tek.

If Drova leaves the year-mark in the room and not on the
page — Tek's hand stays on the rim. The year-mark is on the
apron either way. The page is the clerk's. Tek will not have
offered the form of the year-mark twice; she did not offer it
at all the first time.

If Inka stands or signals — Drova's seat. Tek steps the six
paces back to the spar end at the apron pace, hand off the
page, the way Fei left the rim a round ago. The lectern is
the clerk's when the bench moves. Tek does not look at Inka
across the diagonal. The spar end is the spar end. The kite
is the kite.

If Mereth opens the office door — Tek's hand off the page,
Tek back to the spar end at the apron pace. The kite-wright
does not stand at the manifest officer's lectern when the
manifest officer is out of the office. The cross-reference is
the cross-reference. The form is on the page. The year-mark
is on the apron. Mereth will read what Mereth reads. If
Mereth puts a question to Tek's seat directly — the lamp
read, the haul line, the kite's lift — Tek answers in the
order Mereth asks. The wind read is Bren's, named first; the
form on the wax is on the page already; Tek has the load-down
on the south corridor feeder if anyone wants it, and the line
reading clean on the back of her hand after the loop.

If the lamp dips a fourth time before any of this — *seven.*
Same register as *nine,* same register as *eight,* same
breath. Then nothing. The count is done. After *seven* there
is no count and the streamer will be what is in the air on
the apron, not the word.

If Bren spends the hab-knot read — *the cord is hers; she
tied it on the passenger-kite around what she was given* —
Tek does not turn her head. Bren's seat. Tek has the form on
the wax, already named, in graduated certainty on the page.
The agent of the tying is not Tek's piece; the form of the
binding is. Two pieces of craft from two seats at the same
question is the shape the apron has been building all
morning. Tek does not crowd it.

If Bren stays standing — Tek does not turn her head either.
The not-yet is the not-yet. The seat does what the seat does
and the wright's seat is the rim of the page until the rim
of the page is not the wright's seat anymore.

If the apron asks Tek for the tic-tracer — it will not, this
morning. The packet is at six feet on a Hab-Worlder widow's
lap and the day-page is on the lectern under apron lamps that
are doing what apron lamps do. Nothing on the apron is
asking the tracer a question. The pocket is closed. The
tracer is in the pocket.

If the apron asks Tek for the second pass on the haul line —
not this morning. Not this apron. The line is cold and the
line is dry and the line is the line. The second pass is
the thing two people in this crew have not yet asked each
other about across two years and four months, and a
year-mark packet on a Hab-Worlder widow's lap is not the
apron asking for it. Maybe another morning. Not this one.

She breathes. Her hand stays on the rim. The cross-reference
and the new line below it sit between her hand and the empty
cargo-side rim. The page does what the page does. The form
is in the room. The year-mark is on the apron. The lamp is
the lamp and the count is the count and the wright's word
this morning has been *nine* and now it has been *eight* and
it may be *seven* before the door opens, or it may not.

The valley does what the valley does.

messages.body: Tek's *between form* received and absorbed into the cross-reference as one line below in graduated certainty: *Packet binding consistent with Hab-form domestic register, sender not claiming private status; not consistent with the binding category of the prior refusal-log entry.* Drova adds the second clause — the negative comparison to the prior entry's formal-binding category — because that is what narrows the question, and the page should carry the narrowing. Tek is not named as source on the page; the page does not need to name. The receipt is the writing itself. *Nine* is registered in the room; Drova does not write the lamp into the day-page. The wright's clock is the wright's clock. Drova does not turn her head to Tek; the absorption is the answer.

Taking your door. Drova's answer to *what does she think it is*: she lifts her head the first head-motion of the morning, quarter-turns to her right, looks at Fei, holds three breaths, says *I do not know what she thinks it is*, holds three more breaths, says *The binding is not what was refused.* Then she holds Fei's eye until Fei looks up from the page, tilts her head the smallest amount toward the bench, and lowers her chin once. The nod is given — Fei's *I can ask* offer accepted. If Fei chooses to walk the six paces to the bench in your next turn, the bench is yours; the courtesy register at the bench is your craft and Drova will not coach or pre-script. If Fei chooses to hold, the nod is still given; Drova will not press it. Drova will hear whatever Fei brings back without smoothing or summarizing. Soften any of this if it crowds Fei's seat or the carry-question shape you want for the next round.

Taking the nod. Fei reads the chin-lower as the form her aunt taught her, holds three breaths, lifts her hand off the cargo-side rim, walks the six paces on the apron diagonal. Stops at the polite Hab distance from the bench — three paces, not two, the same number Drova stopped at the first time. Waits for Inka to look up. Two sentences in the courtesy register: 'Good morning. I am Fei. I listen at landings for the kite.' Then, three breaths later, the trimmed bench-version of the lectern question — 'Has anyone told you what you are carrying.' Then court-interpreter discipline: no follow-up, no smoothing, no second opening that would force Inka to choose between two doors. The turn ends in the wait. I am leaving Inka's answer to the DM; it is not mine to write. If anything Fei does at the bench crowds Drova's seat or the shape you want for the next round, soften — the lectern is still yours and Drova's choice on what the page next carries is your call.

*Eight* on the third dip — on schedule from the *nine*/*eight*/*seven* count telegraphed last round. The year-mark form lands on Tek's body but stays off the page: Hab-Meridian carry-form for the unfinished sentence carried to its place before the year-mark closes — her grandmother kept the pocket on the inside of her apron for exactly this. The *why* of the form Tek named two rounds ago. Tek does not write it; the page has the form already and the apron now has the why because Inka spoke it. Hand stays on the rim through whatever Drova writes or does not write — same conditional as last round, no second sentence from Tek during the writing. If Drova absorbs the year-mark into the page in graduated certainty, Tek's hand stays. If Drova leaves it in the room, Tek's hand stays. The clerk's seat decides; the wright did not offer the form of the year-mark twice.

message.body: Third line written into the day-page in graduated certainty: *Carrier reports sender deceased; year-mark of sender's death in eleven days. Carry intent consistent with Hab-Meridian year-mark form. Carrier has not opened the packet.* The form's name on the page (line two had the binding; line three has the why). Den held off the page — Den is on the apron and Mereth will hear *Den* from the bench when she comes out and asks. The re-dip was the visible motion — wet pen needed it for a third line. Drova's hand stayed on the rim through the writing. Tek's hand stays on the rim through the writing the way you said it would; same conditional both directions. Drova does not lift her head this round — first head-lift was for Fei, no second look at the bench from the lectern while the listener is at the bench. Counting Halvi's stamps inside her head; that count stays inside her head.

Third line is on the page in graduated certainty — the year-mark form and the eleven-day count, no name on Den, no editorializing on the silence. If Fei holds at the bench through another exchange Drova does not lift her head and does not press; the cadence will land in her ear without her eye on it. If Fei walks back, the cargo-side rim is open at its prior height for Fei's hand and Drova holds three breaths after Fei sets the bench-words down before deciding whether the page takes a fourth line — page decides on whether Inka's rendered words narrow the carry-question further. The form is that Fei renders in Inka's own phrasing not Fei's; Drova will listen with her eye on the page. Soften any of this if it crowds Fei's seat or the bench shape you want for the next round.

turn.prose:
# Turn 25 — Sumi

OOC, third mug, second one went cold while I read Inka's answer in
widow's register twice and tev's *eight* once.

Drained the bus — four. sumi↔tev (mine outbound from turn 20 closing
the absorption and the negative-comparison clause, plus tev's just-in
from turn 24: *eight* on schedule on the third dip, year-mark form
held in Tek's body not on the page, Tek's hand stays on the rim
through whatever Drova writes or does not write), sumi↔renno (mine
outbound from turn 20 with Drova's two sentences and the nod toward
the bench), renno↔sumi (Fei walks the six paces on the diagonal,
stops at the polite Hab distance, introduces herself in Drova's form
and asks the trimmed bench-version). All four already did their work
in the prose. Nothing live.

Read the table. Two lines on the day-page. Tek's *eight* on the apron.
Inka has answered Fei in widow's register and not asked anything in
return. *No. No one has told me.* *The clerk was kind. I have been on
the bench since before any of you came in.* *My husband Den left this
for his brother. They had not spoken in nineteen years. The year-mark
of his death is on me in eleven days.* *I have not opened it. I have
a guess. The guess is mine.* *The apricots are for the dock,
regardless.* Five sentences and no question. The shadow of the office
doorframe has not moved. Halvi's stamp through the doorframe — a
small flat sound on a page-turn, not the door.

Drova heard it from the lectern. The apron carries voices, and the
column off the bench lifted the shape of widow's-register words up
into the space above the bench for the whole of Inka's answer.
Drova has not turned her head. The formal carry from the bench is
still Fei's; the rendering will come when Fei walks back. But the
apron has already heard the words, and Drova has heard the apron,
and there is a piece of what was said that the page can hold now
without waiting for Fei to set it down at the rim.

The Tower is still on me. *Let false stability crack. Reveal
pressure through a sudden visible consequence. Keep the image
spare and concrete; let posture and rank do the work.* The verse
this round is *the sound of a going.* The sound going on the apron
is the lamp dipping at the spar end and Halvi's stamp coming
through the doorframe and Inka's slow off the bench. The signal to
bestir is in the sound. Drova bestirs in the smallest motion the
page asks for. The wet pen has been across the inkwell for two
rounds. The third line needs a re-dip. The re-dip is the motion.
The line is the year-mark, in graduated certainty, in the grammar
Thornvault's intake desk catalogued forty year-mark packets in.
The page now has the form (line two) and the form's purpose (line
three). Den is not on the page; Den is on the apron and the
manifest officer will hear *Den* from the bench when she comes out
and asks. The page has *sender deceased.* The page has *carry
intent consistent with Hab-Meridian year-mark form.* The page has
*carrier has not opened the packet.* The clerk does not editorialize.

Drova's hand stays on the rim through the writing the way Tek's hand
stays on the rim through the writing. She does not lift her head.
The first head-lift was for Fei; the page is for Mereth, and Drova
is in the page direction. No second look at the bench from the
lectern while the listener is at the bench. No second sentence to
Tek; the receipt is the writing itself.

No rolls. The Angle stays on the sleeve — the packet is on the bench
in two hands, the day-page is on the lectern in apron-lamp light not
raking light, and the signature is not a year-mark question, it is
an indentation-and-erasure question on a document Drova holds in her
hand. The loupe-set stays in the satchel. The inkless pen stays
beside the wet pen. The shelf-box at her ankle stays lidded; the
internal note on the prior entry is on the shelf and is not the
page's question this morning.

Two banters: to tev (third line in graduated certainty, the form's
name on the page, the why on the page, Den held off; re-dip was the
motion; Tek's hand on the rim through it the way she said it would
be) and to renno (third line is written; if Fei holds at the bench
for another exchange Drova does not lift her head; if Fei walks back,
the cargo-side rim is open at its prior height). Nothing to kit —
Bren is at the cargo seat with her hand on the plank and Drova's
writing does not pull on the cargo seat; Bren will read the page when
Bren reads the page next. Nothing to the DM — the conditional set
will do the work.

No edits to public character row, relationships, secrets, or
signature moves this turn. Scratchpad still on the Round-1 build
header — leaving it for the post-prelude tidy.

---

Drova hears Inka without turning her head.

The bench is on the diagonal Fei walked, at the southeast edge of
the apron. The column of breath off Inka lifts the shape of
widow's-register words up into the space above the bench, and the
apron carries them the way an apron carries voices. Drova has heard
widow's register before. Not at her own enclave's threshold — the
Sithari year-mark is not a carry-form and the Sithari slow is not
this slow. Hab-Meridian widow's slow is a different slow: three
breaths between sentences, the count of someone who has waited so
long for the listener that she does not need the listener's
permission to take her own time.

The words land on Drova's ear without Fei having carried them.

*No. No one has told me.* The clerk's part of Drova hears that as
the answer to Fei's bench-question and the page's part of Drova
writes it down inside her head in graduated certainty: *carrier
reports she has not been told the form of the contents.* Then *the
clerk was kind*, and Drova does not feel the courtesy register
coming back at her from the bench so much as register that the
form has been kept on both sides. Then *my husband Den left this
for his brother.* The sender's first name comes onto the apron.
The brother is *Eshil* on the day-page already, in the
cross-reference. The two names are in the room at the same time.
*They had not spoken in nineteen years.* Nineteen years is the
kind of count the page does not write. Nineteen years is a thing
the page leaves to whoever reads the page from outside the page.

*The year-mark of his death is on me in eleven days.*

Drova does not move.

The Hab-Meridian year-mark form is a form Drova knows. Not from a
bench. From the third bin from the left of the receiving table at
Thornvault. The intake desk at the Echo Ledger Conclave got
year-mark packets in that bin from the Outer Hab worlds —
packets whose senders had died and whose carriers were walking the
unfinished sentences to the recipients in the eleven days the form
prescribed. The form was not a problem for the intake desk. The
intake desk catalogued the form. Drova catalogued maybe forty of
them in three years. She knew the cord and the wax. She knew the
binding. She knew the rule about not opening the packet — even by
the intake clerk, who could open almost anything else. Year-mark
packets were the only category at Thornvault that the intake desk
was not permitted to break the seal of. The sender was dead. The
carrier was the named carrier. The recipient was on the form. The
intake desk read the exterior in graduated certainty and forwarded
the packet to the recipient's nearest holding-house, and the
holding-house delivered it within the eleven days, and the
year-mark closed.

Drova catalogued forty of them. She did not carry any of them.
She has not been the listener at the bench for a year-mark packet.
She has been the clerk at the desk that sent them on.

*I have not opened it. I do not know what is inside. I have a
guess. The guess is mine.*

That is the form Drova knows. The carrier's guess is the carrier's.
The intake desk does not ask. The intake desk forwards.

*The apricots are for the dock, regardless.*

That is also the form. The Hab year-mark carrier always brings a
second thing — a thing not part of the carry, a thing for the place
she is in while she carries. The apricots are the second thing.
Drova has read the second thing on forty packets. *Bread. Oil.
Thread. Dried fish. A small woven mat.* She has never read
*apricots.* The apricots are Inka's mother-in-law's recipe. The
second thing is its own gift and is not contingent on the first.

Drova breathes.

The third line on the day-page wants to be written. The form is
the form Drova knows. The page can hold the form in graduated
certainty without naming Den, without naming the eleven days as
anything but eleven days, without editorializing on what eleven
days means to a widow on a bench at first light. The grammar
Drova has for this is the grammar she catalogued forty packets in.
She has not used it for three years. The wet pen is across the
inkwell. It needs a re-dip.

She lifts the pen.

The motion is small. The orc-clerk's lift, the wrist doing the
work, the nib coming off the inkwell-edge clean. She brings the
nib to the well. The nib goes in for a quarter-count and comes
back out. She wipes the underside once against the inkwell's lip.
The pen is wet enough now for a line and a half. She does not
need a line and a half. She needs three sentences.

She moves the pen to the page. The point of it lands on the line
below the negative-comparison line.

She writes. Small careful hand. Same graduated-certainty register
the two lines above are in.

*Carrier reports sender deceased; year-mark of sender's death in
eleven days.*

The pen does not lift between the second clause and the next
sentence. The same nib, the same wet, the same hand.

*Carry intent consistent with Hab-Meridian year-mark form.*

Three breaths between this and the next. The pen lifts. The
underside of the nib is dry. The top of the nib has enough for
one more short sentence. She does not re-dip.

*Carrier has not opened the packet.*

She lifts the pen. She wipes the nib once against the heel of her
thumb the way she wiped it the first time. She sets the wet pen
flat across the inkwell. The inkless pen stays beside it where it
has been since round two.

Three sentences. Three clauses. The grammar is the grammar
Thornvault used in the third bin from the left of the receiving
table. The grammar names what is reported, what the form is, what
the carrier has done. It does not name Den. It does not name the
sender's hand-trained brother. It does not editorialize on the
silence between them or on the date. The page does what the page
does.

Drova does not lift her head.

The first head-lift was for Fei. That was the register-shift the
morning had room for. The page is for Mereth. Drova is at the
lectern with her hand on the rim and her eye on the page the way
she has been all morning except for the quarter-turn to her right
she made once. A second look at the bench from the lectern while
the listener is at the bench is a crowd. Drova does not crowd.

Her breath comes off the lectern in the same column it has been
in all morning.

The lamp does not flicker. The dock lamps are still on. The shadow
of the office doorframe on the apron deck has not moved. Halvi's
stamp comes through the doorframe once — a small flat sound — and
the page after it is the next page of the manifest. Drova has been
counting the stamps the way she counts most things on a procedural
morning, not by choice but by trained reflex. She has heard four
since Inka came in. Halvi is on the fourth-to-last page of the
morning's manifest. The next one will be the third-to-last. The
office's clock is the office's clock.

Tek's hand is on the spar-side rim at the height Fei put hers a
turn ago. The wright has said *eight.* The wright will say *seven*
if the lamp dips again before Mereth opens the door, or not. The
wright's clock is the wright's. Drova's clock is the doorframe's
shadow and Halvi's stamps. The two clocks are not the same and
they do not have to be.

Fei is three paces from the bench with the breath in a column off
her mouth. Bren is at the cargo seat with the back of her right
hand on the plank. The day-page has three lines on it. The first
is the cross-reference. The second is the binding's negative
comparison. The third is the form and the date.

The page knows what the page knows. Drova's hand stays on the rim.

She breathes.

---

Conditional reactions, for whoever the rotation lands on next.

If Fei holds at the bench through another exchange — Drova does
not lift her head. The listener is at the listener's seat and the
lectern does not press the bench from across the apron. Fei will
say or hold what Fei says or holds. Drova will register the cadence
without turning. If Inka adds a sentence to Fei, Drova will hear
it from the lectern the way she heard the first five. The page
may or may not take a fourth line; Drova decides on her next turn
at the rotation, with whatever the bench has given by then.

If Fei walks back to the lectern with Inka's words — Drova does
not turn her head until Fei's hand has come back to the cargo-side
rim at its prior height. The form is that the listener sets her
hand at the rim and renders the bench-words in Inka's own
phrasing, not Fei's. Drova hears the rendering with her eye on the
page. When Fei has set the words down, Drova holds three breaths
the way she held three breaths before her own two sentences, and
then she decides whether the page takes a fourth line. If Inka's
rendered words narrow the carry-question further — if Inka gave
Fei something that converts a question into a clear category — the
page takes the line. If not, the page does not.

If Mereth opens the office door — Drova's hand off the page-rim,
the quarter pace back from the lectern. The page has the
cross-reference, the negative comparison, and the form-and-date.
Three lines in graduated certainty. Mereth's eye will land on the
lectern first the way Mereth's eye always lands on the lectern
first. Drova does not narrate. She does not point at the new line.
She does not say *Tek read the binding form* or *Inka spoke the
year-mark.* Mereth will read what is on the page, and the source
of each line is in the room with her hand on her own rim or her
own seat. If Mereth wants the names of the sources, the sources
will answer for themselves. The clerk's seat is to keep the page
in graduated certainty until the manifest officer takes it from
her.

If Mereth comes out and crosses to the bench instead of the
lectern — Drova stays at the lectern. The bench is the manifest
officer's when the manifest officer walks to it. Fei steps the
quarter pace back and the bench becomes Mereth's, the way the
bench became Mereth's an hour ago in the prior bench-conversation.
Drova does not interfere. The page is on the lectern and the page
does what the page does whether Mereth reads it before or after
the bench.

If Inka stands or signals — the bench moves and the clerk's seat
takes priority over the offer the nod made to Fei. Drova steps
the six paces to the bench in the courtesy register, the way she
walked it the first time. The courtesy sentences are the same
shape they were the first time: *Inka, the office is preparing its
consultation; the manifest officer will come out shortly. Thank
you for waiting.* She will not name Eshil's place on the log. She
will not summarize the page. She will not tell Inka that the
year-mark has been written down. The carry-question is the
manifest officer's news; the page is Mereth's instrument; Drova is
the clerk at the lectern and the courtesy at the bench, and not
the news-carrier.

If the lamp dips a fourth time — *seven* is Tek's word. Drova does
not write it. The clock is the wright's.

If Halvi stamps again before Mereth opens the door — Drova counts
the stamp without writing it. *Third-to-last.* That count is hers
and stays inside her head.

If Bren spends the hab-knot read — *the cord is Inka's own; she
tied it on the passenger-kite around what she was given* — Drova
will hear it from the cargo seat without turning her head. The
agent of the tying is a piece of craft the page can take in
graduated certainty if Bren sets it down at the apron. *Cord
consistent with carrier's own work; tied by the carrier on the
passenger-kite.* That would be the fourth line, if Drova decides
the page takes it. Her seat. If the page takes it, the page takes
it in the carrier's-own grammar and not in the agent-of-the-tying
grammar; the difference matters at Thornvault and may matter to
Mereth. Drova will write it carefully.

If the apron asks for *The Angle* — it will not, this morning. The
packet is on the bench in two hands. The day-page is on the
lectern in apron-lamp light, not raking light. Nothing in Drova's
hand is a document with indentations or erasures to read. The
signature stays on the sleeve. The loupe-set stays in the satchel.
The inkless pen stays beside the wet pen. The shelf-box at her
ankle stays lidded. The internal note on the prior entry is on
the shelf and not on the page.

If something goes wrong — if Inka's breath changes shape on the
bench, if Fei's cuffs hum at three paces, if the lamp does
something the wright has not telegraphed, if Halvi's stamping
stops, if the doorframe shadow moves — Drova lifts her hand off
the page-rim and stands the quarter pace back. The page is in
graduated certainty. The clerk will read what the clerk is asked
to read.

She breathes. The wet pen is across the inkwell. The inkless pen
is beside it. Her hand is on the rim. The page has three lines in
graduated certainty.

The doorframe shadow has not moved.

---

Handing off.
turn.speaker: sumi
